title: Vegetal matters in Thomas Hardy and Olive Schreiner: literary representations of the ontological, philosophical, and material plant
abstract: This thesis examines the representation of the vegetal in the writings of Thomas Hardy and Olive Schreiner. I argue that the vegetal has a prominent presence in their work and that they represent it as a location of vitality, change, process, and agency. In extending this to its participation in networks of causality and meaning, I demonstrate how they decentre humanity and situate meaning, knowing, and agency in wider networks that encompass all lifeforms. Developing current work within the plant humanities, this interdisciplinary study brings together the philosophical botany of Arthur Schopenhauer and Michael Marder and, recognising the vegetal as the epitome of materiality, the New Materialism of Jane Bennett and Karen Barad. I assert that although Hardy and Schreiner emphasise the evolutionary material connectivity across all lifeforms, they also represent the vegetal as ‘other’, using it to examine human modes of being. Through such comparison, they problematise the mind, viewing it as engendering a focus on self and the short-term, and through its dominance, suggesting that humanity fails to recognise the role of the body in perception and motivation. I explore their extension of literary form to embed long-term evolutionary frameworks, recognising Schreiner’s innovative and experimental form as ontologically vegetal. Schreiner conceptualises humanity as a long-term continuous body, analogous to that of the vegetal, the individual having an ethical responsibility to contribute to its progress. Hardy uses vegetal settings to establish a long-term stability while the unceasing growth of vegetation also continuously transforms and re-creates place. Bringing these two late nineteenth-century/early twentieth-century writers into conversation, I examine how their work participates in debates between material and metaphysical notions of reality; Hardy using the material vegetal to challenge metaphysical views while Schreiner incorporates these two epistemologies into models of reality. This productive vegetal approach creates new post-human ecological readings of Hardy and Schreiner, in which life is seen as a continuum, meaning is relocated to material existence, and relationships are key.

AND MATERIAL PLANT

Acknowledgements  
 
First and foremost, I must thank Lizzie Ludlow, my first supervisor, without whom there 
would be no thesis. Your constant encouragement and your confidence in me have been 
invaluable. Your high expectations combined with an unwavering faith that I could always do 
better whenever I sent you any work has made that happen. Thank you for noticing 
whenever I was struggling and couldn’t see the wood for the trees, and for knowing when to 
advise me to step back so that I could see the bigger picture. Thank you for the opportunities 
you have given me; I hope that your faith is justified. 
My thanks to Tory Young and Tristan Moyle, my second supervisors. Tory, your advice on 
writing style has been immensely helpful, and Tristan, your guidance on philosophical 
concepts and methodology has been invaluable, particularly in the early days of this project. 
Thank you to ARU library, especially during the pandemic, for always managing to get 
material quickly, and electronically, when requested. Thank you also to UEA library for 
allowing me the use of your resources.  
Thank you to my fellow PhD travellers on this journey: Edwin, Marie, and Anna who were 
there at the beginning, and more recently, Alice, Kathleen, Kimberley, and Georgia. A 
special thank you to Kathy Rees. Kathy, when I asked your advice before I began this PhD 
and you told me that doing your PhD was the best thing you’d ever done, that was what 
finally made me decide to embark upon this project.  
 
Thank you to my friends who have supported me on this journey, especially Susan and 
Meike, who have always checked how I am getting on and shared my enthusiasm. Thank 
you for understanding when I couldn’t come out to play as I was working yet again. I’m up 
and ready for everything now! 
 
Thank you to my children, Mark and Rachel, my greatest joy.  
 
Thank you all for having faith in me.  
 
And finally, thank you to the vegetation of the local woods and marshes. Whenever I’ve been 
stuck, putting on my wellies and tramping through the woods and across the marshes has 
often been the key that unlocks the next bit in my head. The willows, poplars, birches, and 
oaks. The yellow rattle, the buttercups, the rose-bay willow herb, and the orchids. They have 
been my solace. 
 
 
3 
 
Abstract  
ANGLIA RUSKIN UNIVERSITY 
 
ABSTRACT 
 
FACULTY OF ARTS, HUMANITIES  
AND SOCIAL SCIENCE 
 
DOCTOR OF PHILOSOPHY 
 
VEGETAL MATTERS IN THOMAS HARDY AND OLIVE SCHREINER:  
LITERARY REPRESENTATIONS OF THE ONTOLOGICAL, PHILOSOPHICAL, 
AND MATERIAL PLANT 
 
HILARY BEDDER 
 
MAY 2023 
 
This thesis examines the representation of the vegetal in the writings of Thomas Hardy and 
Olive Schreiner. I argue that the vegetal has a prominent presence in their work and that 
they represent it as a location of vitality, change, process, and agency. In extending this to 
its participation in networks of causality and meaning, I demonstrate how they decentre 
humanity and situate meaning, knowing, and agency in wider networks that encompass all 
lifeforms. Developing current work within the plant humanities, this interdisciplinary study 
brings together the philosophical botany of Arthur Schopenhauer and Michael Marder and, 
recognising the vegetal as the epitome of materiality, the New Materialism of Jane Bennett 
and Karen Barad. I assert that although Hardy and Schreiner emphasise the evolutionary 
material connectivity across all lifeforms, they also represent the vegetal as ‘other’, using it to 
examine human modes of being. Through such comparison, they problematise the mind, 
viewing it as engendering a focus on self and the short-term, and through its dominance, 
suggesting that humanity fails to recognise the role of the body in perception and motivation. 
I explore their extension of literary form to embed long-term evolutionary frameworks, 
recognising Schreiner’s innovative and experimental form as ontologically vegetal. Schreiner 
conceptualises humanity as a long-term continuous body, analogous to that of the vegetal, 
the individual having an ethical responsibility to contribute to its progress. Hardy uses 
vegetal settings to establish a long-term stability while the unceasing growth of vegetation 
also continuously transforms and re-creates place. Bringing these two late nineteenth-
century/early twentieth-century writers into conversation, I examine how their work 
participates in debates between material and metaphysical notions of reality; Hardy using 
the material vegetal to challenge metaphysical views while Schreiner incorporates these two 
epistemologies into models of reality. This productive vegetal approach creates new post-
human ecological readings of Hardy and Schreiner, in which life is seen as a continuum, 
meaning is relocated to material existence, and relationships are key. 
 
Keywords: Olive Schreiner; Thomas Hardy; vegetal; materiality; long-term; post-human; 
philosophical botany; New Materialism.

Introduction 
 
Metaphysics?  What metaphysics do those trees have? 
To be green and lush and to have branches 
And to bear fruit at the right time […]  
But what better metaphysics than theirs, 
The metaphysics of not knowing for what they live 
And of not knowing that they do not know it.  
(Fernando Pessoa, Obra poetica, in Michael Marder, Plant-Thinking, p.79)  
 
It matters to the world how the world comes to matter 
 
(Karen Barad, Meeting the Universe Halfway, p.380) 
 
In this thesis I explore the work of Thomas Hardy (1840-1928) and Olive Schreiner (1855-
1920) through examining their representation of the vegetal. I argue that the vegetal has a 
prominent presence in their work and that they represent it as a location of vitality, change, 
process, and agency. They further extend its reach to its participation in networks of 
causality and meaning, untethering these concepts from humanity. Reading Hardy and 
Schreiner’s work from this perspective, I offer new readings of their work in which these 
features come to the fore, demonstrating how they decentre humanity and locate meaning, 
knowing, and agency within wider networks that encompass all lifeforms. I examine how 
through their use of the vegetal and its seemingly continuous and non-individualistic 
ontology, they embed long-term and evolutionary perspectives in which life itself is important 
and the transient individual part of such a continuity. I assert that their focus on the vegetal is 
indicative of the importance they place on materiality per se. Reading the vegetal as the 
epitome of materiality, all its attributes become proper to material itself. Features that in 
humanity are situated in the mind, such as intentionality, agency, knowing, and meaning, are 
relocated to materiality. I contend that their representation of the vegetal highlights how for 
Hardy and Schreiner matter itself matters. Hardy and Schreiner emphasise how through 
materiality all lifeforms are connected. Yet they also represent the vegetal as other and offer 
it as a means of exploring the problematic nature of humanity, particularly the human mind. 
As Eduardo Kohn (2013) states: ‘attending to our relations with those beings that exist […] 
8 
 
beyond the human forces us to question our tidy answers about the human’.1 I argue that in 
doing so, they suggest that a vegetal mode of being can radically question human modes of 
being and offer lessons in how to live better in the world.  
 
The key element of the vegetal which embeds its agency is, for Hardy, its spatiality and in 
his novels, he situates this in his vegetal settings. In chapter one I explore Hardy’s The 
Return of the Native (1878), reading his representation of Egdon Heath as a place formed by 
the vegetal. I consider how its agency is located in its vegetal which also furnishes the long-
term framework of the novel. I go on to discuss how, in The Woodlanders (1887), Hardy 
uses the vegetal setting to create a world of entanglements, in which imperfection and 
complex causalities challenge an idealist metaphysic underlying reality. In my final Hardy 
chapter, I analyse a selection of his poetry in which vegetal spatiality becomes the location 
of vitality and meaning, and its alternative mode of being offers life lessons to humanity. In 
the second part of this thesis, I explore the work of Schreiner, recognising her principal use 
of the vegetal as to express continuity. I begin by examining The Story of an African Farm 
(1883) and consider how she locates meaning in the continuum of material life underneath 
which lies a metaphysical connectivity. I move on to analyse Schreiner’s posthumously 
published From Man to Man (1926), exploring her portrayal of a long-term evolutionary 
connectivity and her representation of humanity as a continuously progressing collective. In 
my final chapter, I consider three of Schreiner’s allegories. I analyse her critique of 
individualistic attitudes and her presentation of a collective mode of being and ethics. 
Overall, the thesis demonstrates how my vegetal methodology creates productive readings 
across a variety of literary forms. 
 
In what follows I discuss how, writing in the latter half of the nineteenth and early twentieth-
centuries, Hardy and Schreiner negotiate how new understandings of materiality intrude into 
 
1 Eduardo Kohn, How Forests Think: Toward an Anthropology Beyond the Human (Berkeley, University of 
California Press, 2013), p.6.  
9 
 
notions of meaning and epistemology. Through incorporating long-term evolutionary ideas, I 
explore how they extend literary form itself. I go on to outline my use of theory, particularly 
my amalgamation of philosophical botany and New Materialist perspectives. I describe the 
twenty-first century ‘plant-turn’ in the humanities and show how my work expands vegetal 
ecocriticism through incorporating notions of agency, knowledge, and meaning within the 
vegetal, as well as incorporating vitalist and New Materialist understandings.  
 
I follow this with an outline of the key findings of my thesis alongside explanations of some of 
the essential theory, divided into three topics areas. The first of these concerns Hardy and 
Schreiner’s representations of life and humanity as collective and continuous. I explore how 
they problematise the role of the individual and explain how I use vegetal ontology to 
conceptualise this. I then consider how Hardy and Schreiner deal with the phenomenal and 
noumenal worlds. I draw a distinction between Hardy’s materialist understanding and his 
representation of materiality as a challenge to the metaphysical, and Schreiner’s perception 
of the material and metaphysical as inseparable. The third major element of my thesis looks 
at how Hardy and Schreiner question the dominance of the human mind. I frame this around 
their non-dualist understanding of humanity and explore how notions of knowing as centred 
in the material rather than in a separate sphere support such an understanding. The 
introduction concludes with an overview of the chapters that follow.  
 
Before venturing further, I want to clarify my use of the term ‘vegetal’. I use this to indicate 
the entire plant kingdom. It is preferred to the alternative of ‘plant’ as that can imply a narrow 
range of vegetal beings, excluding the larger forms such as trees or the smaller like mosses 
and suchlike. However, where I do use the word ‘plant’, it should be read as equivalent to 
‘vegetal’.   
 
Putting Hardy and Schreiner into conversation makes for a productive pairing as they both 
foreground the vegetal and relocate many features conventionally assigned to the human, to 
10 
 
the vegetal and materiality. They are writing at a time when material evolutionary theories 
had altered understandings of the world framed by religious beliefs. However, as Mark 
Knight and Emma Mason comment, it is ‘easy to exaggerate’ the effect of Darwin’s Origin of 
Species (1859) on religious beliefs.2 Knight and Mason remind us to ‘remain alert to the 
mutability and plurality of belief’ in the nineteenth-century and that it rarely helps ‘to 
characterise people’s beliefs as either religious or secular’.3 Acknowledging a continuing 
dynamic between religious and material conceptions of the world, I recognise the remaining 
influence of religious ideas on their work, even though they both professed to a loss of a  
conventional Christian faith. Schreiner’s conceptualisation of the connectivity between all 
beings relies upon a noumenal lifeforce behind materiality, and the ethical stance in her work 
is overtly indebted to Christian values. Although Hardy’s work is more inherently materialist, 
he demonstrates a continuing tension between a materialist viewpoint and one that yearns 
for what that leaves out.  
 
However, Charles Darwin and evolutionary theory looms large over this thesis. I argue that 
through incorporating the long-term perspectives necessary to evolutionary understanding, 
Hardy and Schreiner broker new realisms at the end of the nineteenth-century, offering 
routes towards twentieth-century modernisms. Their use of long-term evolutionary 
frameworks within the boundaries of the narrative form shows them grappling with 
integrating time-bound stories of human individuals with a decentralising of humanity in 
favour of a wider temporal perspective and the inclusion of other lifeforms. One of the 
leading critics of the novel form in this respect is Amitav Ghosh. In The Great Derangement 
(2016) he contends that the realist novel is unable to incorporate the long-term temporality 
 
2 Mark Knight and Emma Mason, Nineteenth-Century Religion and Literature: An Introduction (Oxford, Oxford 
University Press, 2006), p.156. 
3 Knight and Mason, p.167. 
11 
 
necessary for grasping environmental reality as it has a narrative necessity for ‘boundaries, 
in time and space’.4 He asserts that: 
Within the mansion of serious fiction no one will speak of how the continents 
were created; nor will they refer to the passage of thousands of years; 
connections and events on this scale appear not just unlikely but also absurd 
within the delimited horizon of a novel.5   
 
Hardy creates a long-term framework through his representation of the continuous growth of 
the vegetal against which he contrasts what become ephemeral stories of humanity. 
Schreiner does so through her use of an experimental form in which the long-term is 
embedded in abstract sections which interrupt and yet interlace with the more short-term 
linear narrative.    
 
To support my interdisciplinary analysis of Hardy and Schreiner’s representation of the 
vegetal, I bring into service the philosophical botany of Michael Marder and Arthur 
Schopenhauer. I explore Marder’s notions in some detail later in this introduction, but here I 
want to briefly explain the rationale behind my use of Schopenhauer’s work. The vegetal is 
prominent in the philosophy of Schopenhauer. In his argument that Schopenhauer’s 
perspective is of value to vegetal ecocriticism, Timothy Morton comments that 
‘Schopenhauer makes it terribly easy to appreciate lettuces. I am a manifestation of will. A 
lettuce is a manifestation of will’.6 Schopenhauer employs plants to explain how the force of 
the will is ‘blind’ and how it functions in all beings unclouded by consciousness, asserting 
that ‘the truth that the will can exist without knowledge is apparent, we might say palpably 
recognisable, in plant life’ (WWR2 295). This is a useful perspective from which to 
understand Hardy and Schreiner’s demoting of the primacy of the human mind. 
Schopenhauer’s vitalist account of materiality, in which the will is separate to and yet 
 
4 Amitav Ghosh, The Great Derangement: Climate Change and the Unthinkable (Chicago, University of Chicago 
Press, 2016), p.59. 
5 Ghosh, p.61. 
6 Timothy Morton, ‘What Vegetables are Saying about Themselves’, in The Language of Plants: Science, 
Philosophy, Literature, ed. by Monica Gagliano, John C Ryan, and Patricia Vieira (Minneapolis, University of 
Minnesota Press, 2017), pp.173-190 (p.174). 
12 
 
manifests itself through materiality, is a useful lens for examining Schreiner’s own vitalist 
perceptions. In fact, Schreiner herself recognised that many of her ideas were similar to 
those of Schopenhauer. That Hardy’s own philosophy shares an affinity with Schopenhauer 
is a truism in Hardy studies, although this is generally considered in relation to his 
representation of character and his perceived pessimism. However, we do know that Hardy 
owned translations of Schopenhauer’s work with some of the texts marked up showing 
evidence of his reading.7  
 
Recognising how Hardy and Schreiner extend their use of the vegetal to express the value 
of matter per se, I expand my approach to draw upon elements of twenty-first century New 
Materialism. In her ‘vital materialism’, which she describes in Vibrant Matter (2010), Jane 
Bennett discounts descriptions of matter as ‘passive, mechanistic or divinely infused’ (JB xiii) 
and reconceptualises it as ‘as much force as entity, as much energy as matter’ (JB 20). For 
Bennett, matter is ‘vibrant, vital, energetic, lively, quivering, vibratory, evanescent, and 
effluescent’ (JB 112). As Hardy and Schreiner represent matter as agential and vital, this 
becomes a useful interpretive viewpoint. Bringing Bennett’s work into conversation with that 
of Marder, I perceive how both perspectives work to raise the status of matter. The vegetal 
matters because it is matter, not despite it. Bennett’s perspective is a useful counter to that 
of Schopenhauer as she refutes vitalist interpretations: a vitalist account of matter, such as 
that of Schopenhauer’s, sees its life principle as something additional whereas Bennett 
views it as intrinsic to matter itself. Reading Hardy and Schreiner through these lenses 
shows Hardy’s view to be closer to that of Bennett whereas Schreiner’s bears resemblance 
to that of Schopenhauer.  
 
 
7 Hardy owned translations of Schopenhauer’s The World as Will and Representation and On the Fourfold Root 
of the Principle of Sufficient Reason, the latter of which is marked up by Hardy. This second text contains the 
essay On the Will in Nature in which Schopenhauer devotes an entire chapter to ‘Plant Physiology’.  
13 
 
I also bring into play Karen Barad’s ‘agential realism’, outlined in Meeting the Universe 
Halfway (2007). For Barad, things are secondary, and subsequent to, relationships, or what 
she terms ‘intra-actions’. Beings emerge or materialise through intra-action and ‘agency is a 
matter of intra-acting; it is an enactment, not something that someone or something has’ (KB 
178, emphasis in original). Barad’s work sees relationships as key, and this is a useful 
perspective for considering the agential entanglements between vegetal and human, and 
between other bodies in Hardy and Schreiner’s work. Furthermore, rather than being 
contained within time and space, for Barad, these intra-actions precede, produce, and 
reconfigure temporality and spatiality itself (KB 179). For Barad ‘matter isn’t situated in the 
world; matter is worlding in its materiality’ (KB 180-1). This is a particularly useful position for 
considering Hardy’s presentation of Egdon Heath in The Return of the Native. Using Barad, I 
demonstrate how Hardy represents the vegetal as creating and continuously re-creating 
place, encoding temporality and historicity within its vegetation. Using a Baradian lens, I 
contend that Schreiner’s representation of entanglements between matter and mind work to 
reconfigure the world and humanity.    
 
I. The Plant-Turn 
 
My study is situated within the field of vegetal ecocriticism, variously termed critical plant 
studies, the plant humanities, or the ‘plant-turn’. This field has developed across the 
humanities over the course of the second decade of the twenty-first century and follows the 
lead of animal studies in challenging humancentric views. I argue that vegetal ecocriticism is 
more radical in that it also challenges a zoocentric perspective which continues to exist 
within twenty-first century ecocriticism. But even in 2018, John Charles Ryan can contend 
that the plant has been a ‘conspicuous lacuna’ within ecocriticism and that ‘studies of plant 
life in contemporary poetry are curiously few and far between’.8 I position my own study 
 
8 John Charles Ryan, Plants in Contemporary Poetry: Ecocriticism and the Botanical Imagination (London, 
Routledge, 2018), p.7. 
14 
 
within vegetal and post-human perspectives that decentre the human and question human 
exceptionalism, viewing all lifeforms as of equal value. 
 
The ’plant-turn’ refers to an interdisciplinary field that stretches across the humanities and 
the sciences. Early work in this field is disappointingly conservative. Rodopi Press’s (now 
Brill) book series, ‘Critical Plant Studies: Philosophy, Literature, Culture’, launched in 2013 
with the aim to ‘initiate an interdisciplinary dialogue’ and ‘encourage an incremental shift of 
cultural attitudes from a purely instrumental to a respectful approach to vegetal beings’.9 
However, the first book published in this series, Randy Laist’s edited collection Plant and 
Literature: Essays in Critical Plant Studies (2013), although countering the notion of plants 
as instrumental and inferior, contains too many essays that rather conventionally focus upon 
the symbolic status of plants.10 As Ryan contends, in its ‘diffuse form to date, this green-
minded specialisation has been wont to centralise the figurative potencies of flora as 
symbols, metaphors, tropes, linguistic devices, and narrative artifices’.11  
 
Over the second decade of the twenty-first century, a plethora of books have been published 
within the field of vegetal philosophy, many of them influenced by the burgeoning area of 
botanical research into plant attributes such as memory, learning, and intelligence. These 
provide the philosophical background to much of my work and books in this field include 
Matthew Hall’s Plants as Persons: A Philosophical Botany (2011), Michael Marder’s Plant-
Thinking: A Philosophy of Vegetal Life (2013), Eduardo Kohn’s How Forests Think: Toward 
an Anthropology Beyond the Human (2013), and Craig Holdrege’s Thinking Like a Plant: A 
Living Science for Life (2014).12 More popular texts that foreground plants as dynamic 
 
9 https://brill.com/view/serial/CPST (accessed 24th September 2022) 
10 Plants and Literature: Essays in Critical Plant Studies, ed. by Randy Laist (Amsterdam, Rodopi, 2013).  
11 Ryan, p.10-11. 
12 Matthew Hall, Plants as Persons: A Philosophical Botany (Albany, University of New York Press, 2011); 
Michael Marder, Plant-Thinking: A Philosophy of Vegetal Life (New York, Columbia University Press, 2013); 
Eduardo Kohn, How Forests Think: Toward an Anthropology Beyond the Human (Berkeley, University of 
California Press, 2013); Craig Holdrege, Thinking Like a Plant: A Living Science for Life (Great Barrington, 
Lindisfarne Books, 2014). 
15 
 
intelligent beings include Michael Pollan’s The Botany of Desire (2001), Richard Mabey’s 
The Cabaret of Plants: Botany and the Imagination (2015), and Peter Wohlleben’s The 
Hidden Life of Trees (2016).13 
 
Michael Marder’s Plant-Thinking has become a seminal text in this field, making an 
important contribution to advancing the plant humanities. Many later works in this field that 
utilise Marder’s ideas offer progressive perspectives. These include the edited collection The 
Language of Plants: Science, Philosophy, Literature (2017), Chauncey Maher’s Plant Minds: 
A Philosophical Defence (2017), John C Ryan’s Plants in Contemporary Poetry: Ecocriticism 
and the Botanical Imagination (2018), as well as chapters in edited books, including those by 
Hannah Stark in Deleuze and the Non/Human (2015), Ruth Livesey in Anticipatory 
Materialisms in Literature and Philosophy, 1790-1930 (2019), and Emma Mason in The 
Figure of Christ in the Long Nineteenth Century (2020).14 Such work has contributed to a 
radicalisation of this field in which earlier understandings of plants as mechanistic, passive, 
and hierarchically inferior, have been superseded by those that view the vegetal as a 
location of process and vitality, and even a site of intentionality, agency, knowledge, and 
meaning.  
 
In the second decade of the twenty-first century, work in plant humanities has widened to 
embrace an interdisciplinary perspective, bringing other literary approaches into 
conversation. This includes two recent texts that focus on the cultivated plant. Elizabeth 
 
13 Michael Pollan, The Botany of Desire (London, Bloomsbury, 2001); Richard Mabey, The Cabaret of Plants: 
Botany and the Imagination (London, Profile Books, 2015); Peter Wohlleben, The Hidden Life of Trees 
(Vancouver, Greystone Books, 2016). 
14 The Language of Plants: Science, Philosophy, Literature, ed. by Monica Gagliano, John C Ryan, Patricia Vieira  
(Minneapolis, University of Minnesota Press, 2017); Chauncey Maher, Plant Minds: A Philosophical Defence 
(London, Routledge, 2017); John C Ryan, Plants in Contemporary Poetry: Ecocriticism and the Botanical 
Imagination (2018); Hannah Stark, ‘Deleuze and Critical Plant Studies’ in Deleuze and the Non/Human, ed. by 
Jon Roffe and Hannah Stark (Basingstoke, Palgrave MacMillan, 2015), pp.180-196; Ruth Livesey, ‘Arboreal 
Thinking:  George Eliot and the Matter of Life in Adam Bede’ in Anticipatory Materialisms in Literature and 
Philosophy, 1790-1930, ed. by Jo Carruthers, Nour Dakkak, and Rebecca Spence (Cham, Palgrave MacMillan, 
2019) pp.131-151; Emma Mason, ‘Considering the Lilies: Christina Rossetti’s Ecological Jesus’, in The Figure of 
Christ in the Long Nineteenth Century, ed. by Elizabeth Ludlow (Cham, Palgrave MacMillan, 2020) pp.149-161.  
16 
 
Hope Chang’s Novel Cultivations: Plants in British Literature of the Global Nineteenth 
Century (2019) considers the commerce in cultivated plants of the nineteenth-century and 
how this is mediated through the novels of the period.15 It is interesting that in terms of 
Schreiner she discusses African Farm and not From Man to Man which includes much more 
on the cultivated plant. Sue Edney’s edited collection, EcoGothic Gardens in the Long 
Nineteenth Century: Phantoms, Fantasy and Uncanny Flowers (2020), brings the Gothic 
mode into play, examining elements of the uncanny and alien in garden writing of that 
period.16  
 
I draw upon other texts that have embraced interdisciplinary perspectives. The recent edited 
collection, Entangled Worlds:  Religion, Science, and New Materialism (2017) aims to find 
common ground between the ostensibly opposing fields of materiality and religion, viewing 
the perceived binary of mind and matter as incoherent.17 My works puts into service Jane 
Bennett’s contribution to this volume, in which she purports a materialised ‘onto-sympathy’ 
between plants and humans.18 I also incorporate elements of the emerging field of 
econarratology, that examines narrative form in relation to ecocriticism and environmental 
studies. Erin James and Eric Morel, the editors of Environment and Narrative: New 
Directions in Econarratology (2020), state their intention to overturn notions of the study of 
narrative theory as anthropogenic and consider instead how narrative can convey and foster 
environmental forms of understanding.19 I draw upon some of these insights in my analysis 
of Schreiner’s innovative and experimental narrative form. However, I inflect the 
 
15 Elizabeth Hope Chang, Novel Cultivations: Plants in British Literature of the Global Nineteenth Century 
(Charlottesville, University of Virginia Press, 2019). 
16 EcoGothic Gardens in the Long Nineteenth Century: Phantoms, Fantasy and Uncanny Flowers, ed. by Sue 
Edney (Manchester, Manchester University Press, 2020).  
17 Entangled Worlds: Religion, Science, and New Materialism, ed. by Catherine Keller and Mary-Jane 
Rubenstein (New York, Fordham University Press, 2017). 
18 Jane Bennett, ‘Vegetal Life and Onto-Sympathy’, in Entangled Worlds: Religion, Science, and New 
Materialism, Ed. by Catherine Keller and Mary-Jane Rubenstein (New York, Fordham University Press, 2017), 
pp.89-110. 
19 Erin James and Eric Morel, Environment and Narrative: New Directions in Econarratology (Columbus, Ohio 
State University Press, 2020). 
17 
 
econarratological lens through reading Schreiner’s experimental narrative form as 
ontologically vegetal which, I argue, enables her to extend her temporal and collective 
perspective. 
 
Hardy and Schreiner use the vegetal as both concrete being and as a model of a non-human 
and non-zoocentric mode of life. Cognisant of it as a continuous long-term and collective 
being, as agential and meaningful, and as having a heteronomous relationship to alterity, my 
own approach draws heavily on Marder’s Plant-Thinking. In this, Marder explores the modes 
of being appropriate to plants, recognising them as possessing the attributes of agency, 
intentionality, and expression. He also considers how they embrace a collective ontology 
and embody time. I draw upon all these features in my examination of Hardy and Schreiner’s 
vegetal. 
 
Marder’s perspective differs to that of Matthew Hall who in Plants as Persons: A 
Philosophical Botany, suggests that to be of value, the other-than-human needs to be given 
‘personhood’. Marder and Ryan take issue with such an idea, as do I. Marder asserts that it 
is neither necessary nor helpful to attribute to vegetal beings features embraced by 
personhood in order for them to be ‘respect-worthy’ (MM 55), and Ryan writes that he 
intends in his ‘phytocriticism’ ‘not to homogenize vegetal otherness through a form of 
personhood’.20 My point is that by attributing ‘personhood’ to the vegetal, one is in danger of 
implying that humans are more important as well as the sole beings who possess attributes 
such as agency, meaning, and knowledge. I argue that in viewing the vegetal as other that 
does not make it a lesser being neither does it suggest that it is incapable of being a 
participant in a world of agency, knowing, and meaning. 
 
 
20 Ryan, p.8. 
18 
 
A further aspect of Marder’s work that is pertinent to the study of Hardy and Schreiner is his 
notion that the vegetal ‘in its very being […] accomplishes a lived destruction of 
metaphysics’, its ontology deconstructing binaries such as ‘self and other, depth and 
surface, life and death, the one and the many’ (MM 11). This provides a helpful viewpoint for 
considering Hardy and Schreiner’s own responses to the metaphysical. It is particularly 
useful in my exploration of Hardy’s representation of the vegetal as a material challenge to 
metaphysical understandings of reality. 
 
Through putting Marder’s lens into conversation with other fields, specifically those of New 
Materialism and the vitalism of Schopenhauer, and engaging with interdisciplinary practices, 
my methodology extends the field of Marderesque vegetal ecocriticism. I not only widen it 
into considering materiality per se as a site of vitality, agency, causality, historicity, and 
meaning, but also recognise the continuing role of the metaphysical within the world of 
materiality, especially relevant to Schreiner studies. As such, I draw these potentially 
disparate fields into conversation and bring to light where these perspectives converge or 
diverge. This creates a broader, more complex, and productive approach for investigating 
the uses of the vegetal in literary texts, and Hardy and Schreiner’s work in particular.  
 
II. Individual and Collective  
 
In Hardy and Schreiner’s work, the individual being is less important than the continuity of 
life itself and the relationships between individuals. I conceptualise Schreiner’s 
representation of humanity as a long-term continuous and collective body. I read this, and 
the corresponding relationship between collective and individual, through the model of the 
vegetal body, drawing upon Marder’s notion of vegetal being as an ‘ontological paradox’ 
(MM 183) in that it deconstructs the binary oppositions of ‘the one and the many’ and the 
‘self and other’ (MM 11). For Marder, the plant is a ‘provisional unity of multiplicities’ which 
‘transcend[s] the distinction between “part” and “whole”’ (MM 84); its parts (shoots, roots, 
19 
 
leaves) able to act independently as well as cooperating with the whole plant. In fact, the 
whole idea of the existence of individual beings becomes virtually untenable in the plant-
world. Through such a lens, individuality becomes a zoocentric notion but as Merlin 
Sheldrake suggests, ‘if we only have words that describe neatly bounded autonomous 
individuals, it is easy to think that they actually exist’.21  
 
Barad also demotes the importance of the individual, claiming that ‘existence is not an 
individual affair. Individuals do not preexist their interactions; rather, individuals emerge 
through and as part of their entangled intra-relating’ (KB ix). In reading Barad alongside 
Marder, I recognise these conceptualisations as plant-like, in that the individual parts of 
plants are only viable in relation to the other parts. Approaching Schreiner’s representation 
of the relationship between individual and collective through these perspectives, suggests 
that the individual’s importance is in relation to their contribution to collective life. In fact, in 
From Man to Man, Schreiner explicitly uses plant analogies to illustrate humankind as 
‘intimately in its nature a solidarity and a whole with all its parts reacting on one another’ (FM 
188). The way that Hardy problematises interpretations of humans as autonomous is 
reflective of his awareness of their integral relationship to alterity. In the two Hardy novels I 
explore in this study, characters who construct themselves as autonomous come to bad 
endings or learn to reframe themselves as heteronomous and part of a wider framework of 
entanglements and relationships.    
 
Across her work, Schreiner extends her framing of the relationship between the individual 
and the collective to consider ethics. In From Man to Man and her allegories, her critique of 
hegemonic societies is centred around their focus on egoic individuality. In examining her 
ethics of responsibility towards others through Marder’s vegetal ontology, I establish that the 
ethical stances of Schopenhauer and Barad are modelled in vegetal being. For Marder, plant 
 
21 Merlin Sheldrake, Entangled Life: How Fungi Make Our Worlds, Change Our Minds, and Shape Our Futures 
(London, The Bodley Head, 2020), p.103. 
20 
 
modules are ‘both members of a plant and independent entities in their own right' (MM 84), 
with each part, although independent, contributing to the growth and survival of the whole. 
Schopenhauer develops his theory of the will into an ethical position based upon the fact 
that our ‘intuitive knowledge […] recognises in another’s individuality the same inner nature 
as in one’s own’ (WWR1 367-8), meaning that it should engender virtuous responses. I 
argue that this bears similarity to the relationships between the vegetal modules of a plant. In 
her life of Schopenhauer, Helen Zimmern identifies how Schopenhauer’s ethics reflect those 
of the gospel. She notes that his understanding that 'the fundamental basis of morality [is] 
love of one's neighbour' shows that 'his ethics were thus in unison with those of the New 
Testament, and might be defined as its scientific expression'.22 Schopenhauer’s perspective 
becomes particularly useful for examining how Schreiner’s ethics reflect those of the gospel, 
especially since she overtly calls this to our attention in her Trooper Peter allegory. 
Furthermore, as Schopenhauer’s ethics embed temporal relationships, this is a useful 
perspective for exploring Schreiner’s notion of a long-term responsibility to the future of 
humankind.  
 
 
Bringing into service Barad’s ethics, I recognise how this is predicated on a similar 
understanding of the inherent relationships between beings. Barad conceptualises this as an 
‘incarnate relation that precedes the intentionality of consciousness’ (KB 392). I assert that 
such an a priori understanding is implicit in Schreiner’s notion that ethical responsibility 
precedes and overrides socio-political context. Moreover, reading Schreiner through Barad’s 
notion that through intra-actions, one is engaged in ‘bringing forth new worlds’ (KB 170) and 
therefore that ‘the becoming of the world is a deeply ethical matter’ (KB 185), is a useful 
perspective for exploring Schreiner’s call to ethical action in aid of the creation of a better 
future. 
 
22 Helen Zimmern, Arthur Schopenhauer: His Life and Philosophy (London, Longmans, Green, and Co. 1876), 
p.154. (I draw upon Zimmern’s book on Schopenhauer as evidence shows that Schreiner read this. I explain 
this in more detail later in this introduction.) 
21 
 
 
I propose that Schreiner’s experimental and innovative use of form in African Farm and From 
Man to Man, in which she combines realist narrative and abstract sections, can be read as 
ontologically vegetal as it models a similar relationship between the individual and the 
collective. The realist narrative sections articulate the stories of individuals while the abstract 
sections universalise such accounts. Moreover, the way that Schreiner blurs the boundaries 
between these narrative forms is redolent of plant ontology. 
 
Through such use of form, I contend that Schreiner also embeds a vegetal temporality into 
the narrative. She uses the abstract sections to create a long-term framework which also 
incorporates cyclicities and reiterations. This impersonates the growth of the vegetal in which 
a linear accumulation of material is interrupted by cyclical seasonal responses. Marder’s 
understanding of how seasonal reiterations are not simply repetitions is useful here. For 
Marder, each leaf, say, is different to any previous leaf. I draw upon this notion to examine 
Schreiner’s cyclical reiterations which, rather than mere repetitions, are each distinctive. 
Marder further claims that repetition ‘achieves the exact opposite of deadening; it stakes out 
the being of everything that is living’ and that ‘there is no life without iterability’ (Marder 113-
16). Reading Schreiner’s structure through this lens, I suggest that her complex temporal 
structures reflect the temporality of life itself.    
 
I demonstrate how Hardy also uses the vegetal to create a long-term framework. In the two 
Hardy novels, I consider his vegetal settings through Marder’s notion that plant spatiality is 
time itself. For Marder, through growth, plants ‘spatially express time’ and therefore embody 
a ‘temporalisation of space and spatialisation of time’ (MM 96). Read through this lens, 
Hardy’s settings embed a long-term temporality against which humanity and their stories 
become ephemeral. But the vegetal’s continuous growth also enshrines endless change and 
Hardy’s representation of the vegetal emphasises its vitality and adaptation. Through 
22 
 
comparison, Hardy represents humankind as a species in decline: a direct contrast to 
Schreiner’s representation of humanity as in continuous evolutionary development. 
 
Both Hardy and Schreiner consider the transience of life and how it is a necessary, if difficult, 
reality for humanity to address. In his poetry, Hardy overtly draws on the vegetal’s seasonal 
transience as a comparison to human responses to death. I read this through Marder’s 
notion that vegetal growth ontologically deconstructs the binary of life and death (MM 11). 
For Marder, the continuously growing modular plant becomes almost immortal as the 
‘continual death of used organs has replaced death of the plant itself’ (MM 122). Through 
such a lens, I argue that Hardy positions death as central to an understanding of life itself as 
continuous. Schreiner’s representation of transience embraces a similar notion. Her 
portrayal of humanity as a continuous body enshrines an acceptance of the transience of the 
individual.  
 
III. Material and Metaphysical 
 
I read Hardy and Schreiner’s work as concerned with the relationship between the 
phenomenal material world and a metaphysical worldview. I establish that for Hardy this 
relationship is antagonistic whereas for Schreiner these epistemological viewpoints are 
interdependent. I bring this divergence to light through considering their representation of 
vegetal materiality, of entanglements between beings, and a lifeforce. 
 
In The Return of the Native, I read Hardy’s emphasis on the darkness of the material vegetal 
as a challenge to the enlightenment of reason and the metaphysical biblical creation in which 
light overcomes darkness. In The Woodlanders Hardy extends such a challenge through 
creating a phenomenal world, based on the spatiality of the arboreal, in which complexity is 
integral and counters metaphysical notions of the ideal. Entanglements between beings, 
human and vegetal, create networks of causality with unforeseen consequences. I combine 
23 
 
the approaches of Marder’s vegetal spatiality and New Materialist notions of entanglements 
to analyse this world of relationships. Bennett suggests that things or bodies become 
‘entangled’ with one another, forming ‘assemblages’ that themselves generate ‘emergent 
properties’ (JB 24). Furthermore, these entanglements mean that ‘instances of efficient 
causality’ become ‘impossibly rare’ (JB 32). I argue that the complexity of Hardy’s web in 
The Woodlanders, in which even the vegetal becomes entangled in the plot, counters 
straightforward notions of causality. Moreover, Hardy’s web illustrates Bennett’s relocation of 
agency to the assemblage, rather than individuals and a ‘mind capable of choice and 
intention’ (JB 32). Extending these notions through Barad, I suggest that Hardy’s web 
becomes of greater importance than the nodes (characters, events, and suchlike) it 
connects, further demoting the importance of the individual and individual agency. His 
evolutionary ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ (W 48) embeds imperfection as a principle of life, one that 
illustrates Schopenhauer’s notion that nature only exists because of competition between 
beings (WWR1 146-7).  
 
In The Woodlanders, Hardy blurs the ontological boundaries between lifeforms. His 
emphasis on their common materiality reconceives relationships between them as horizontal 
rather than hierarchical. Schreiner evokes similar material connections across lifeforms, but 
her explicit models of connectivity, unlike Hardy’s, merge the material with the metaphysical. 
She purports that the lifeforce, for want of a better word, that connects all beings is both 
material and metaphysical.  
 
For Schreiner, the lifeforce is something additional to materiality. I read it as bearing an 
affinity with a vitalist perspective, such as that of Schopenhauer, and I draw upon him to 
explore this aspect of Schreiner’s work. I prefer to use Schopenhauer’s vitalist philosophy in 
preference to other vitalists, as Schreiner herself recognised the affinity between her ideas 
and those of Schopenhauer. She writes in a letter that:  
24 
 
I have been looking at that life of Schopenhauer to-day. If I had ever read him, 
or even knew before I came to England that such a man existed, one would say 
I had copied whole ideas in the African Farm and From Man to Man from him. 
There is one passage of his on the search for philosophic truth that reads like a 
paraphrase of my allegory in the African Farm. There’s something so beautiful 
in coming on one’s very own inmost thoughts in another.23  
 
The ’life of Schopenhauer’ to which she refers is that of Zimmern’s Arthur Schopenhauer: His 
Life and Philosophy (1876).24 In a later letter to the Librarian of the London Institution she 
asks, ‘would you very kindly send me any of Schopenhauer’s work that you have except the 
“World as Will & Idea” which I do not want’.25 One might speculate whether the reason for not 
wanting Schopenhauer’s magnum opus is because she has already read it. 
 
For Schopenhauer ‘matter is the mere visibility of the will, but not the will itself’, which is the 
additional ‘vital force’.26 I read Schreiner’s lifeforce as similarly that which manifests in 
materiality, with the underlying connection between all beings as metaphysical. I am not the 
first to use Schopenhauer in ecocritical readings. As this connective force embraces all 
lifeforms, his philosophy has increasingly been appreciated and brought into service within 
ecocriticism. Morton asserts that Schopenhauer transcends the 'normative anthropocentrism 
of Western thought' in his taking seriously of nonhuman beings.27 John Gray suggests that 
Schopenhauer presents ‘the first and still unsurpassed critique of humanism’ and that ‘for 
Schopenhauer we are at one with other animals in our innermost essence’.28 Greg Pritchard 
 
23 Olive Schreiner, letter to Havelock Ellis, 2nd March 1885, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 29th August 
2022].  
24 Zimmern. 
25 Olive Schreiner, letter to London Institution, 15th December 1888, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 29th 
August 2022], Schreiner’s underlining. (Schopenhauer’s The World as Will and Idea is the same text as The 
World as Will and Representation.)  
26 Schopenhauer, ‘On Philosophy and Natural Science’, in Parerga and Paralipomena: Short Philosophical 
Essays, Vol. 2, trans. by E.F.J. Payne (London, Clarendon Press, 1974), p.107, emphasis in original. 
27 Timothy Morton, ‘What Vegetables are Saying about Themselves’, in The Language of Plants: Science, 
Philosophy, Literature, ed. by Monica Gagliano, John C Ryan, and Patricia Vieira (Minneapolis, University of 
Minnesota Press, 2017), pp.173-190 (p.174). 
28 John Gray, Straw Dogs: Thoughts on Humans and Other Animals (London, Granta Books, 2002), p.38-41. 
25 
 
and Laurence Wright view the Schopenhauerian will as that which connects all beings and 
therefore instils a respect for the whole natural world.29 
 
Reading Schopenhauer through Marder, I recognise how he anticipates materialist 
explanations of the lifeforce, both negating mechanistic understandings of materiality. 
Marder’s own explanation makes use of Merleau-Ponty’s phenomenological intentionality, in 
turn an extension of Husserl’s (MM 160). Marder suggests that the growth of the plant 
indicates a ‘becoming-spatial and a becoming-literal of intentionality’ (MM 37). Particularly 
pertinent for present purposes, he claims that this intentionality supplants the metaphysical 
idea of the will.30  
 
My use in this study of Schopenhauer’s and Marder’s interpretations of the lifeforce provides 
a productive friction for my analysis of this aspect of Hardy and Schreiner’s work and 
highlights where their conceptualisations converge or diverge. In Schreiner’s work, the 
awareness of a metaphysical connectivity embeds a further meaning in materiality. Marder’s 
perspective is more useful for examining Hardy’s materialist account of vegetal vitality and 
agency. But there are elements of Hardy’s vegetal agency that seem close to a metaphysical 
force, but bearing in mind his materialist credentials, I find Bennett’s notion of an ‘onto-
sympathy’ that is rooted in an ‘overlap between materialities of humans and plants’ a cogent 
and legitimate reading of such kinship.31

Coetzee and who we are in the world’, in Toxic Belonging? Identity and Ecology in Southern Africa, ed. by Dan 
Wylie (Newcastle, Cambridge Scholars Publishing, 2008). 
30 Marder, ‘Plant Intentionality and the Phenomenological Framework of Plant Intelligence’, Plant Signalling 
and Behaviour, 7.11 (2012), 1365-1372 (p.1367). 
31 Jane Bennett, ‘Vegetal Life and Onto-Sympathy’, chapter in Entangled Worlds: Religion, Science, and New

pp.89-110 (p.90-1).

IV.   The Problematic Dualism of Humanity 
 
Hardy and Schreiner problematise the human possession of mind and my exploration of this 
is a key element of this study. It is therefore incumbent at this point to clarify my use of terms 
such as ‘mind’, ‘consciousness’, and ‘reason’. For the purposes of this study, I recognise 
‘mind’ as having two components: that of intelligence and reason, and that of consciousness 
(or subjective experience). Where I use the word ‘mind’ I do so to refer to both elements. 
Hardy and Schreiner, however, often use such terms more loosely and, where necessary, I 
draw attention to this.    
 
Hardy and Schreiner present the possession of mind as that which creates existential 
burdens and an associated necessity to create meaning within the individual life. For Hardy 
in particular, thought, emotion, and consciousness are presented as responsible for human 
unhappiness. They both suggest that by generating a focus on the individual, the self, and a 
concomitant short-termism, the mind impedes a longer-term and wider awareness of being 
part of a continuity of beings that have developed over evolutionary time. I explore how they 
explicitly and implicitly contrast this to the connectedness and continuity inherent in the 
vegetal.  
 
In his autobiographical ‘Life’, Hardy comments directly on the questionable benefit of a mind:  
A woeful fact – that the human race is too extremely developed for its corporeal 
conditions, the nerves being evolved to an activity abnormal in such an 
environment. Even the higher animals are in excess in this respect. It may be 
questioned if Nature, or what we call Nature, so far back as when she crossed 
the line from invertebrates to vertebrates, did not exceed her mission. This 
planet does not supply the materials for happiness to higher existences.32   
 
 
32 Thomas Hardy, The Life and Work of Thomas Hardy, ed. by Michael Millgate (London, Macmillan, 1984), 
p.227 (entry dated 7th April 1889). (This ‘Life’  has become known as Hardy’s ‘autobiography’. It was first 
published as a biography under the name of his second wife, Florence Hardy. However, in subsequent years it 
has been recognised as largely penned by Hardy himself and referred to as the ‘Life’. It is, however, written in 
the third person and consists largely of dated entries.) Further references to this text are indicated as Thomas 
Hardy, Life. 
27 
 
In his poems ‘Before Life and After’ and ‘The Aerolite’, both of which I address in Chapter 
Three, Hardy explores the notion that the evolution of the human mind is ‘abnormal’. 
Elsewhere in his poetry, he expresses an envy of the vegetal’s lack of mind. In this sense, he 
anticipates Marder’s suggestion that humanity can learn lessons from the vegetal’s ‘healthy 
existence that does not problematise itself and therefore does not become an issue for itself’ 
(MM 79)  
 
I argue that Hardy and Schreiner write against a dualistic conception of mind as separate to 
body. For Hardy in particular, characters who rely too heavily on reasoning are represented 
as dissatisfied, conflicted, or misguided. Across Hardy’s work, he presents the light of reason 
as damaging and that which engenders a notion of humanity as non-corporeal. Through his 
characters, he implies that when understanding and cognition is understood as embodied, 
humanity gains a wider understanding or ‘insight’. For Schreiner, dreams, intuition, and 
feelings all enable an embodied understanding and I contend that characters that recognise 
these modes of understanding are portrayed as vegetal-like in their embrace of corporeality. 
In her allegories Schreiner extends such a non-dualist notion of humanity to suggest that 
transcendence itself is corporealized.  
 
I explore such notions through Barad and Marder’s negation of the separation between 
being and knowing. Barad uses an echinoderm, the brittlestar, as her model for this, 
asserting that ‘”I think, therefore I am” is not the brittlestar’s credo’ (KB 379). For Barad, as 
the brittlestar’s entire body is a ‘visualising system’, there is no mediator (such as a lens) 
between the subject body and the materiality of the outside world. Its visual system is 
‘materially enacted’. For the brittlestar then, ‘being and knowing, materiality and intelligibility, 
substance and form, entail one another’ (KB 375, emphasis in original). 
Brittlestars literally enact my agential realist ontoepistemological point about the 
entangled practices of knowing and being. They challenge our Cartesian habits 
of mind […] Knowledge making is not a mediated activity […] Knowing is a 
28 
 
direct material engagement, a practice of intra-acting with the world as part of 
the world (KB 379, emphasis in original). 
 
In a similar vein, Marder uses the plant as a lifeform that connects being and knowing: 
ontophytology overflows the strict confines of traditional ontology […] there is no 
reason why this excess should not be reflected in a corresponding 
epistemophytology – the epistemology of plants – that similarly oversteps the 
bounds of the conventional theories of knowledge (MM 152).  
 
Without detracting from Barad’s coherent and fascinating analysis of the brittle star’s 
ontoepistemology, it seems to me that the embodied quality of the brittle star has a striking 
similarity to Marder’s vegetal. In my view, Barad could equally have used a plant to illustrate 
her point. However, the correspondences between Marder and Barad’s integration of 
material being and knowing suggests that this is common to all life and that mind or intellect 
is not necessary or essential for knowing. For Marder, consciousness is not ‘an 
epistemological flaw’ but a move from ‘material knowledge to the formal knowledge of 
intelligence’ (MM 154-5). Hardy might suggest that this is where life developed too far.  
 
I use these insights to explore Hardy and Schreiner’s embodied humanity. I contend that it is 
through an acceptance of their own materiality, or a reliance on embodied modes of 
knowing, that their characters gain a better understanding of their kinship with other 
lifeforms, as well as embrace a more heteronomous mode of life for themselves. In this way, 
they both counter notions of human exceptionalism based upon the possession of mind.  
 
In a challenge to mind-based sources of meaning, I argue that Hardy and Schreiner purport 
a meaning to material existence, reflecting Marder’s vegetal which is ‘laden with meaning in 
all its spatiality, materiality, and finitude’ (MM 76). For Hardy, this is particularly explicit in 
his poetry where he writes against Wordsworthian subjectivity and relocates meaning to 
mere existence. In Schreiner’s African Farm, a post-faith search for significance is resolved 
through finding it within a rootedness amongst other lifeforms, albeit with an awareness that 
this connection is metaphysical as well as material. 
29 
 
 
 
V. Overview of chapters 
 
In Chapter One I consider Hardy’s The Return of the Native and begin by exploring his 
representation of the setting of Egdon Heath. Rather than topographical feature, I argue that 
Egdon Heath is a place created by the vegetal and that Hardy uses the continuous growth of 
the vegetal to embed a long-term temporality against which humanity and the plot become 
ephemeral. I assert that Hardy uses the darkness of the heath vegetation to counter the light 
of the sun and in doing so, also rewrites the biblical creation. Yet I also recognise the vegetal 
setting as a location of continuous vitality and explore how Hardy agentially entangles it with 
character and plot. In the second part of the chapter, I examine Hardy’s problematisation of 
the human mind, in which he views thought as a ‘disease of flesh’ (RN 151) and that which 
creates a sense of autonomy. I read Hardy as writing against dualist conceptions of 
humanity, viewing the mind as embodied, and embodied insight a challenge to intellectual 
enlightenment. Finally, I explore how in presenting the emotions as embodied and their 
workings unconscious to the rational mind, Hardy allies the human body to that of the 
vegetal. 
 
In Chapter Two I argue that in The Woodlanders Hardy develops his use of the vegetal 
setting as a challenge to human abstractions, the challenge now being to an idealist 
metaphysics underlying reality. The world of The Woodlanders is one of imperfection, 
illustrated through the evolutionary ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ (WL 47) which inscribes the 
impossibility of any preconceived ideal. I explore how through his ‘great web’ (WL 17), Hardy 
creates a complex world with entanglements across beings, vegetal and human, in which 
labyrinthine agencies and causalities result in unintended consequences. I contend that, as 
in Return of the Native, Hardy deposes the human, questioning the role of the imagination 
and spreading agential capacity across all lifeforms. I demonstrate how for Hardy, humanity 
30 
 
is dominated by the vitality, change, and adaptability that he locates within vegetal spatiality, 
and how he therefore counters human-derived notions of hierarchy and ownership.  
 
My final Hardy chapter analyses a selection of his poetry, including five poems in which the 
vegetal is prominent. I explore Hardy’s explicit framing of the vegetal as other, and his 
implication that its freedom from the burden of mind is a preferable mode of being. He 
purports that the vegetal can offer a discontented humanity, lessons in how to live and 
extends this to the vegetal’s heteronomous mode of life and embrace of constant change 
and transience. I argue that in his poetry Hardy explicitly writes against Romantic 
subjectivity, problematising anthropocentric notions that meaning is located within the human 
mind, repositioning it to material existence. I explore how in these poems he replaces the 
human speaker’s perspective with the vegetal’s own voice and agency, and how his use of 
poetic form, allows the vegetal to spatially dominate. I conclude the chapter by exploring two 
poems in which Hardy explicitly questions the benefit of human consciousness and suggests 
that non-consciousness is normal.  
  
In Chapter Four, I explore Schreiner’s presentation of the search for meaning in a material 
world in The Story of an African Farm, and how she locates this within the continuum of life. 
For Schreiner, reasoning creates a barrier to the perception of an unexplainable underlying 
metaphysical connectivity, and I analyse the two models she creates of an interconnected 
material and metaphysical reality. I consider Schreiner’s further suggestion that in 
engendering a focus on the individual self and its survival, the mind hinders understanding of 
the connection between beings and the relative insignificance of the individual, creating 
existential dissatisfaction. I also explore Schreiner’s use of a forward-looking experimental 
form that interpolates abstract sections and linear narrative. I offer a reading of this as 
ontologically vegetal in the way these two forms juxtapose individuality and the collective, as 
well as combining a long-term temporal linearity with cyclicities and repetitions.   
31 
 
 
In Chapter Five I move on to discuss what I consider to be Schreiner’s magnum opus: her 
From Man to Man. I analyse how the connectivity that she portrays in African Farm is further 
developed in From Man to Man through her use of a long-term framework that repositions 
the individual within evolutionary progression, further decentralising humanity. I examine the 
development from African Farm of her innovative use of form and how in this novel she more 
fully contextualises the abstract sections within the realist narrative. I argue that this enables 
her to entangle theory and practice, process and matter, and abstract and concrete, blurring 
the dividing lines between these, and demonstrating how each pairing in these binaries 
contributes to progress. I discuss how the use of a botanist as her main character enables 
her to overtly use the ontology of the vegetal to analogise and critique hierarchical 
hegemonic society and demonstrate the role of the individual in creating progress across 
humanity. Finally, I analyse how Schreiner characterises the human creative force as 
analogous to the lifeforce, both having the ability, through the entanglement of matter and 
mind, to connect across time.   
 
My final chapter concerns Schreiner’s allegories, a form that she repurposes to examine and 
critique contemporary society. I assert that in her allegories she conceptualises humanity as 
a continuous temporal body, and I explore this through its resemblance to vegetal ontology. I 
argue that through this continuous body, Schreiner links past, present, and future, and 
demonstrates how individual action in the present must be in service to the long-term 
collective progress of humanity, and how meaning then becomes located within the 
relationship and contribution of the individual to the progress of that collective body. 
Although ethical concerns are important across Schreiner’s work, I demonstrate that in her 
allegories, she places ethics at the centre of her vision, explicitly critiquing the ethics 
inherent in hierarchical hegemonic societies. I recognise her ethical standpoint as having 
similarity to a vegetal ethics, in which the role of the individual is in service to the collective 
32 
 
and explore how this ethical stance resembles other models that embrace relationships and 
responsibility rather than individuality.   
33 
 
Chapter One - Material Darkness and Symbolic Light: The Vegetal 
Darkness of Egdon Heath in Hardy’s The Return of the Native 
 
Matter isn’t situated in the world; matter is worlding in its materiality 
 
(Karen Barad, Meeting the Universe Halfway, p.180-1) 
 
Whereas humans remember whatever has phenomenally appeared in the light, 
plants keep the memory of light itself  
(Michael Marder, Plant-Thinking, p.156) 
 
In this chapter I argue that the dominating presence of Egdon Heath in The Return of the 
Native shows the importance of materiality to this novel. It is in the vegetal of the heath that 
Hardy embeds many of its characteristics as well as the long-term temporal framework of the 
novel. In the spatiality of this vegetal Hardy locates features more conventionally ascribed to 
humanity: face, voice, and agency, as well as the capacity for its involvement in the plot. I 
contend that his focus in The Return of the Native on materiality extends to his 
representation of humanity. He views humankind as material beings and suggests that the 
domination of the mind prevents a recognition of the importance of human corporeality, 
resulting in dissatisfaction, the unawareness of the embodied basis of many motivations, and 
a dismissal of our material kinship to all other lifeforms, including the vegetal.  
 
Previous scholarship has considered Hardy’s Egdon Heath as landscape feature. However, I 
read his representation of the heath as vegetal. I contend that not only is it a vegetal setting, 
but that it is a place that has been created by the vegetal. It is through the vegetal’s ability to 
create place that Hardy historicises the heath and, read through the lens of Karen Barad, 
means that Egdon is constantly being re-created. Although Hardy uses it as an expression of 
long-term endurance, the vitality of its vegetation makes it a location of perpetual change 
and adaptation. Even scholars who examine Hardy’s representation of the vegetal of the 
heath, such as William Cohen who, in his article on its faciality refers to it as ‘perhaps the 
most famous landscape description in British fiction’, or Sinéad Garrigan Mattar who uses 
34 
 
Hardy’s description of heath-bells to illustrate Hardy’s tendency to animism, fail to fully 
consider the material and spatial implications of Egdon as a construction of the vegetal. 33 
Such a lacuna is perhaps understandable. Although many landscape terms are derived from 
the vegetation that constructs those places: think of wood, copse, or prairie, we often turn 
these into terminologies of landscape. Such semantic conflations are seen in Hardy’s own 
writing: he uses the term ‘heath’ to refer both to ‘Egdon Heath’ and to the vegetation: ‘the 
heath and fern had insidiously crept in’ (RN 53).  
 
I demonstrate how a failure to understand Egdon as vegetal has resulted in a corresponding 
failure to recognise many of the heath’s features as located within its vegetation. In this 
chapter I argue that the heath’s darkness, its human-like capacities, its agency, and its 
impact on humanity, are all situated within the vegetal. Readings of Egdon as a ‘character’ 
are, for me, a misnomer and reminiscent of approaches such as Matthew Hall’s in which to 
be of value the non-human must be given personhood (see p.17). Rather than necessitating 
humanising the heath, through reading it as vegetal, I recognise the active presence of the 
heath in this novel as embedded within the vegetal’s own capacities. Marder’s 
conceptualisation of the vegetal as a lifeform that has its own vitality, agency, communicative 
ability, and meaning, all embedded within its spatiality, is a useful lens for exploring these 
aspects of Hardy’s Egdon. 
 
In this chapter I also press into service the materiality of the vegetal to examine Hardy’s 
representation of humanity as non-dualist and embodied and his problematisation of the 
notion of the separate mind. Many scholars have recognised that for Hardy it is the body and 
emotions, rather than the rational mind, that motivate humanity. George Levine claims that 
 
33 William A Cohen, ‘Faciality and Sensation in Hardy’s The Return of the Native’, PMLA, 121(2) (2006), 437-452 
(p.446); Sinéad Garrigan Mattar, ‘The Return of the Native: Animism, Fetichism, and the Enchanted Heath’, The 
Thomas Hardy Journal, 24 (2008), 4-22. 
35 
 
for Hardy ‘rationality cannot trump all.  Lives are shaped by desire and chance’.34 Phillip 
Mallett argues that for Hardy ‘we are as much motivated by instinct and the body as by 
reason and the intellect’.35 Hardy’s presentation of the emotions as motivational, and yet 
embodied, and that ‘the springs of human action may be inaccessible even to the actors’, 
means that, for humanity, life must be fundamentally difficult and perplexing.36 But my 
reading of The Return of the Native from the perspective of the vegetal extends these views. 
I assert that for Hardy, the domination of a separate interior sphere of mind separates 
humanity from a recognition of our materiality and our fundamental evolutionary relationship 
to all lifeforms, even the vegetal. For Hardy, thought is destructive. It creates dissatisfaction 
and the need to search for purpose and meaning, rather than understanding that these are 
to be found within existence itself.  
 
Through the importance and efficacy of materiality and corporeality, I argue that Hardy 
undermines the abstract and the metaphysical. In The Return of the Native, light and 
darkness are used as symbolic oppositions: light associated with the abstract, and darkness, 
particularly that of the heath vegetation, with materiality. I contend that through his 
description of the heath, Hardy imagines a materialist rewriting of the metaphysical biblical 
creation. Rather than light displacing the darkness, the material darkness of the heath 
continues to dominate light. For Hardy, light is also symbolic of the problematic mind, 
whether that be the metaphysical light of the spirit or the symbolic light of reason; darkness 
is opposingly corporeal. Furthermore, the internal light of the intellect is challenged by the 
insight of the body. Rather than enlightening, the ‘parasite’ thought blinds humanity into 
assuming that we are rational beings rather than motivated by our corporeality. Such a 
blindness correspondingly prevents a recognition of kinship with the world of all beings and 
 
34 George Levine, ‘Hardy and Darwin: An Enchanting Hardy?’ in A Companion to Thomas Hardy, ed. by Keith 
Wilson (Chichester, Wiley-Blackwell, 2009), pp.36-53 (p.45). 
35 Phillip Mallett, ‘Hardy and Philosophy’ in A Companion to Thomas Hardy, ed. By Keith Wilson (Chichester, 
Wiley-Blackwell, 2009), pp.21-35 (p.30). 
36 Mallett, p.30. 
36 
 
engenders a sense of superiority. In contrast, Hardy implies that the roots of our being are 
as embodied as the vegetal of the heath. The opposition between the metaphysical and the 
material is extended in The Woodlanders which I examine in the next chapter.  
 
The remainder of this chapter is divided into two parts: the heath and the human. I begin 
Part I by reading Hardy’s Egdon Heath as a place created by the vegetal. I consider how 
Hardy uses the heath vegetation to embed The Return of the Native’s long-term temporality, 
while also portraying it as a site of a seemingly paradoxical continuous change. I go on to 
analyse how Hardy uses heath vegetation to generate a darkness that dominates both light 
and time, challenging metaphysical constructions and human interpretations of temporality. I 
conclude Part I by exploring how Hardy relocates features usually attributed to the human to 
the spatiality of the heath vegetal. This includes faciality, voice, and awareness, ultimately 
establishing agency and awareness within the vegetable, the heath becoming an active 
participant in the plot. In the second part of the chapter, I consider Hardy’s problematisation 
of humanity. I begin by exploring how Hardy presents the domination of the intellect in 
humanity as putting the mind in conflict with the body and disconnecting humanity from 
corporeality. I go on to demonstrate how humankind’s ensuing assumption of autonomy is 
misguided and how a vegetally heteronomous mode of being might be beneficial. I trace the 
journey of the returning native, Clym Yeobright, from an autonomous mode of life to one that 
embraces heteronomy, predicated on a literal blinding caused by his focus on the intellect. 
Such a blinding allows his corporeal insight to emerge and symbolically unblind him. I 
conclude the chapter with an examination of how Hardy’s portrayal of humanity as irrational 
and motivated by the bodily emotions recognises our similarity to the vegetal, and thereby all 
other lifeforms.  
 
 
 
37 
 
I:  EGDON HEATH  
 
I(i) 
 
Egdon Heath’s Vegetal Temporality: Permanence and Change  
 
The Return of the Native is the only one of Hardy’s novels in which the first chapter is solely 
devoted to landscape. In fact, John Blackwood’s criticism of Hardy’s manuscript includes the 
comment that ‘there is hardly anything like what is called Novel interest’ in the first part of the 
story.37 As part of his argument that fiction plays little attention to place other than as setting, 
Lawrence Buell claims that although Hardy presents Clym Yeobright as influenced by his 
environment, ‘the heath is ancillary to Clym’s story […] Return of the Native is about people 
in place, not about place itself’.38 On the contrary, my analysis suggests that Hardy’s focus 
on Egdon Heath in the first chapter is indicative of its importance to the novel. Hardy’s 
suggestion that the first book edition include a sketch-map of the scene of the novel as its 
‘unity of place […] affords an opportunity of doing so’, serves to further suggest the centrality 
of the location.39  
 
The title of the first chapter, ‘A Face on Which Time Makes but Little Impression’, announces 
Hardy’s use of the heath to create his long-term temporal framework. For the heath, 
‘civilisation was its enemy; and ever since the beginning of vegetation its soil had worn the 
same antique brown dress, the natural and invariable garment of the particular formation’ 
(RN 10). I argue that it is the vegetation that clothes the heath that makes it of ‘antique’ 
temporality. Moreover, reading the heath as created by its vegetation, makes the vegetal the 
location of the challenge to civilisation.   
 
 
37 Simon Gatrell, ‘The Textual History of The Return of the Native’, in The Return of the Native, ed. by Phillip 
Mallett, 2nd edn. (New York, Norton & Co., 2006), pp.343-363 (p.346). 
38 Lawrence Buell, The Environmental Imagination: Thoreau, Nature Writing, and the Formation of American 
Culture (Cambridge (Massachusetts), The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 1995), p.255. 
39 Thomas Hardy, letter to Messrs Smith & Elder, in Thomas Hardy, Life, p.125-6. 
38 
 
In this respect, one could contend that Hardy gestures towards human constructions of place 
as usurping the vegetal’s natural ability to do so through its inherent materiality. It is useful to 
read this through Marder’s notion that spatiality is ‘the exclusive province of vegetal life’ (MM 
72) and plant spatiality an encoding of time. Hardy’s embedding of the longevity of Egdon 
Heath in its vegetation brings into service Marder’s notion that plants ‘spatially express time’ 
(MM, 96). They transform space into place. Moreover, rather than the vegetal being a 
passive inhabitant of place, for Marder ‘place dynamically emerges from the plant’s living 
interpretation of and interaction with its environment’.40 Reading Hardy’s vegetal through this 
lens, it has an active role in the historicising of Egdon Heath. 
 
Hardy uses such vegetal longevity to historicise the heath through his description of it as like 
the vegetation found there in the Domesday Book where it is described as a ‘healthy, furzy, 
briary wilderness’. He extends this resemblance by alluding to the Domesday Book’s 
‘“Turbaria Bruaria” – the right of cutting heath-turf’ (RN 10), an occupation that continues to 
be carried out by the natives in the novel. He also indicates that the modern-day bonfires on 
the heath are ‘lineal descendants from jumbled Druidical rites and Saxon ceremonies’ (RN 
18). In a later text, he also links the heath to the long-term through literary history: in his 
short story, ‘The Withered Arm’ (1888), he writes that it is ‘not improbably the same heath 
which had witnessed the agony of the Wessex King Ina, presented to after-ages as Lear’.41 
 
Hardy further lengthens the long-term temporality of the heath to a pre-human deep time. I 
have already indicated that its vegetation has clothed the heath ‘ever since the beginning of 
vegetation’ but Hardy extends this by hundreds of millions of years through characterising its 
vegetation as that of the carboniferous period:  
The ferny vegetation […] was quite uniform: it was a grove of machine-made 
foliage, a world of green triangles with saw-edges, and not a single flower […] 
 
40 Michael Marder, ‘Plant Intentionality and the Phenomenological Framework of Plant Intelligence’, Plant 
Signalling and Behaviour, 7.11 (2012),1365-1372 (p.1367). 
41 Thomas Hardy, Wessex Tales (London, Macmillan, 1976), p.79. 
39 
 
The scene seemed to belong to the ancient world of the carboniferous period, 
when the forms of plants were few, and of the fern kind; when there was neither 
bud nor blossom (RN 174-5). 
 
Reading this description through Schopenhauer’s understanding that plant form is permanent 
whereas its material changes over time (WWR1 277), Hardy’s ‘antique brown dress’ is only 
similar to that of the carboniferous period in terms of form. The vegetation being ‘uniform’, 
emphasised through the description of its ‘machine-made […] triangles with saw-edges’, 
enables Hardy to use it as an expression of permanence, whereas materially it is entirely 
different. 
 
As well as characterising it as permanent, Hardy also draws attention to the vegetal’s 
constant change and adaptation. Paths are constantly reclaimed by the vegetal: ‘a little 
patch of shepherd’s-thyme intruded upon the path’ (RN 241), and areas cleared for farming 
‘after holding on for a year or two, had receded again in despair, the ferns and furze-tufts 
stubbornly reasserting themselves’ (RN 149). 
 
Barad’s ‘agential realism’ provides a useful alternative lens for reading what could be seen 
as Hardy’s oxymoronic combination of permanence and change in the heath’s vegetation. 
Barad contends that temporality and spatiality are produced and reconfigured through intra-
actions, rather than intra-actions happening within time and space (KB 179). For Barad, 
matter ‘is generative […] in the sense of bringing forth new worlds, of engaging in an 
ongoing reconfiguring of the world’ (KB 170, emphasis in original). Reading Hardy’s Egdon 
vegetation through Barad draws attention to how its continuous growing and adaptation 
constantly creates and re-creates this place. Rather than fixed or immutable, as many have 
characterised Hardy’s Egdon Heath (encouraged perhaps by Hardy’s own language, such 
as his description of the heath as ‘from prehistoric times as unaltered as the stars overhead’ 
(RN 11)), I argue that Hardy encodes time through his representation of perpetual change 
within that permanence.  
40 
 
 
Through using the vegetal to represent time, Hardy embeds process and vitality within 
permanence. The impression of permanence, the ‘Face on Which Time Makes but Little 
Impression’, is due to the vegetal’s incessant growth being largely imperceptible to humans 
due to its duplication of form and, from a human perspective, the slowness of its growth and
