change. In fact, Hardy makes the point that although the vegetation might appear in ‘the 
repose of actual stagnation’, this is an ‘apparent repose of incredible slowness’. It might 
‘exhibit the inertness of the desert’ but it is ‘exercising powers akin to those of the meadow, 
and even of the forest’ (RN 14-15). As Francis Hallé points out, the vegetal cannot be in a 
condition of stasis. It is not ‘that plants can grow indefinitely; they must grow, to the extent 
that they die if so impeded’.42 Perhaps Jane Bennett’s suggestion that ‘the claim to a vitality 
intrinsic to matter itself becomes more plausible if one takes a long view of time’ is illustrated 
in Hardy’s long-term, constantly changing, heathland vegetation (JB 10-11). 
 
Hardy’s evocation of continuous change includes his recognition that the long-term survival 
of the heath is predicated upon competition and adaptation. Vegetal beings constantly 
explore and respond to the environment through plasticity and modular growth. Hardy’s 
heath vegetal competitively reclaims the space that had previously been itself reclaimed by 
cultivation – Barad’s dynamic matter’s ‘worlding’ in action (KB 180). Although Captain Vye’s 
paddock was ‘bearing evidence of having once been tilled’ the ‘heath and fern had 
insidiously crept in, and were reasserting their old supremacy’, their competitive success 
emphasised through Hardy’s aggressive anthropomorphic language. Although ‘hedges will 
not grow because of the wind and the wilderness’, the banks surrounding Vye’s paddock are 
topped with ‘disconnected tufts of furze, standing upon stems along the top, like impaled 
 
42 Francis Hallé, In Praise of Plants (Portland and London, Timber Press, 2002), p.97, emphasis in original. (For 
many of the botanical explanations in this study I am indebted to Francis Hallé’s book. It is particularly useful 
for this study as it directly compares the plant and animal kingdoms. Hallé also addresses the roots of the 
asymmetry, bias, and injustice inherent in our zoocentrism and anthropocentrism.) 
 
41 
 
heads above a city wall’, suggesting a need for defensive fortifications to keep out the heath 
vegetal enemy (RN 53-55). Its success is perhaps demonstrated by the fact that its extent in 
the Domesday book shows that ‘the area of Egdon down to the present day has but little 
diminished’ (RN 10).  
 
In this respect, it is interesting that in Hardy’s self-confessed earliest poem, ‘Domicilium’, he 
makes the point that the real heath upon which Egdon is based, has reduced in size. 
Although the ‘garden-plots | And orchards’ had been ‘uncultivated slopes’, the road had been 
‘shut in by ferns’, and ‘so wild it was’, it is no longer so (CP 3-4). It seems that in the 
fictionalised Egdon Heath of The Return of the Native Hardy was keen to emphasise its 
extent as continuous from at least the time of the Domesday Book rather than accept its 
actual diminution. I suggest that his fictionalisation of the real demonstrates the importance 
of the heath to his creation of his long-term temporal framework in The Return of the Native.  
 
Reading the heath through Barad also draws attention to how Hardy makes the heath itself a 
place of historicity, not simply through linking it to human history, but by historicising the 
vegetal itself. Barad argues that it is not just the human, language and culture that have 
historicity (KB 132), but that through its world-making capacity, matter is also ‘an ongoing 
historicity’ (KB 151). Reading Egdon Heath through this lens, as created and re-created by 
the vegetal, Hardy extends its historicization. After all, as Timothy Morton claims, ‘what we 
call Nature is really just solidified history’.43 Moreover, Hardy suggests that ancient human 
intrusions become natural through ageing processes: ‘an aged highway, and a still more 
aged barrow […] almost crystallised to natural products by long continuance’, implying that 
the natural is at least as historicised as the human-made (RN 11).  
 
 
43 Timothy Morton, The Ecological Thought (Cambridge, Massachusetts, Harvard University Press, 2010), p.42. 
42 
 
In his article on Hardy’s use of ‘scalar shifts’ in his novels, Benjamin Morgan argues that 
Hardy makes interactions between ‘divergent timescales […] a structuring feature of 
narrative form, such that the novel acquires the formal capacity to induce layered modes of 
durational awareness’.44 In The Return of the Native, Hardy’s dominant timescale is that of 
the heath and against this long-term temporality, the tragic plot, that of the love triangle 
between Eustacia Vye, Clym Yeobright, and Damon Wildeve, becomes ephemeral. It is an 
interruption, both to the heath itself, and the lives of the heath natives. Although Diggory 
Venn had (wrongly) assumed that the marriages in the novel would be ‘good for at least 
twenty years to come’, Clym’s marriage to Eustacia is a ‘chasm’ (RN 311, 244). Eustacia 
and Wildeve, the disruptive outsiders, thrive on change and modernity, whereas the heath 
‘gave ballast to the mind adrift on change, and harassed by the irrepressible New’ (RN 11). 
The fact that the central story of Eustacia and Wildeve’s disruptive influence lasts exactly 
one calendar year perhaps serves to emphasise it as a mere interlude. 
 
Drawing attention to the short-term nature of his plot, Hardy also gestures towards its 
artificiality. The final book of the novel is entitled ‘Aftercourses’ and begins: 
The story of the deaths of Eustacia and Wildeve was told throughout Egdon, 
and far beyond, for many weeks and months.  All the known incidents of their 
love were enlarged, distorted, touched up, and modified, till the original reality 
bore but a slight resemblance to the counterfeit presentation by surrounding 
tongues (RN 314).  
 
Through highlighting how stories can be exaggerated, Hardy implies their inauthenticity, and 
their abstract nature can perhaps be contrasted to the concrete reality of the heath. 
Moreover, Hardy shows an awareness here of the fictional story as a discontinuity in time, 
and an understanding of the necessity for time boundaries around stories – the very notion 
that causes Amitav Ghosh to assert that the realist novel cannot encompass the long-term 
and instead ‘conjure up worlds that become real precisely because of their finitude’ (see 
 
44 Benjamin Morgan, ‘Scale in Tess in Scale’ in Novel: A Forum on Fiction, 52.1 (2019), 44-63 (p.53). 
43 
 
p.10-11).45 Hardy’s suggestion that his long-term heath outlives his plot, somewhat counters 
Ghosh’s criticism of the temporal limitation of the realist novel form.    
 
Hardy even muses on the potential ephemerality of his own fictional text. Referring to the 
surviving barrows as the ‘records’ of the ancient inhabitants of the heath, he contrasts those 
on the ‘cultivable tracts’ which are ‘as writers on paper beside writers on parchment’ whose 
‘records had perished long ago by the plough’ (RN 316). Like the records of agriculture, his 
own story, written on paper, is ephemeral and will perish, in contrast to the concrete human 
landmarks on the heath. 
 
I(ii) 
The Darkness of the Vegetal as a Challenge to Light 
 
As well as using the spatiality of the vegetal to create place and temporality, Hardy uses it to 
establish a darkness that challenges not only the light of the sun but also that of the biblical 
creation. The predominant feature of Hardy’s heath vegetal is darkness. Rather than 
presenting it in aesthetic terms, through focussing on its spatial or structural aspects (its 
biomass), Hardy uses it to emphasise a vegetally-constructed darkness. He minimises any 
reference to flowers or colour. The crimson flowers of the heather only appear in one of four 
seasons, and even then, they are only ‘gorgeous’ when the sun shines in that season (RN 
201). He nowhere mentions the yellow flowers of the furze, and neither are the purple heath-
bells seen in flower. The dominating form of vegetation, the carboniferous fern, is non-
flowering and its structure, that even persists after its death - ‘between thickets of tall and 
dripping bracken, dead, though not yet prostrate’ (RN 301) - provides density.  
 
Hardy uses the vegetally-produced darkness to embed a materialist rewriting of the biblical 
account of creation. For comparison, I place here the pertinent verses from Genesis followed 
immediately by Hardy’s description of the darkness of the heath as a challenge to light:   
 
45 Ghosh, p.61.  
44 
 
In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth. And the earth was 
without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep.  And the 
Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters. And God said, Let there be 
light: and there was light. And God saw the light, that it was good: and God 
divided the light from the darkness. And God called the light Day, and the 
darkness he called Night. And the evening and the morning were the first day 
(Genesis 1. 1-5, emphasis in original). 
 
The heaven being spread with this pallid screen and the earth with the darkest 
vegetation, their meeting-line at the horizon was clearly marked. In such 
contrast the heath wore the appearance of an instalment of night which had 
taken up its place before its astronomical hour was come: […] The distant rims 
of the world and of the firmament seemed to be a division in time no less than a 
division in matter. The face of the heath by its mere complexion added half an 
hour to evening; it could in like manner retard the dawn, sadden noon (RN 8). 
 
In the Genesis account, darkness is original and displaced by light and time. Hardy also 
presents darkness as original, preceding light, although his darkness is material. The 
darkness of the heath creates, or at least dominates, time. It enforces night before its 
‘astronomical hour’ and retards or dims the brightness of daytime. Such darkness is explicitly 
located in the vegetal: the ‘darkest vegetation’ which is the ‘complexion’ of the heath’s face. 
Rather than the face being supplanted by the metaphysical ‘Spirit of God’, Hardy’s material 
face continues to have effect, reminding the reader of the chapter title: ‘A Face on Which 
Time Makes but Little Impression’. Reading this through Barad’s notion of a continuous 
‘worlding’, rather than an absolute change, Hardy’s vegetal face is continuously re-
challenging the light. Hardy also implies that it is more efficacious than astronomical time: at 
night, the sky’s darkness is ‘incomplete […] in comparison with the total darkness of the 
heath below it’ (RN 49). 
 
Hardy extends the material as a challenge to light through its association with other forces of 
darkness. The dark heath acts in ‘sympathy’ with the night: 
when night showed itself an apparent tendency to gravitate together […] The 
sombre stretch of rounds and hollows seemed to rise and meet the evening 
gloom in pure sympathy […] in a black fraternisation towards which each 
advanced half-way (RN 8).  
 
45 
 
Such ‘black fraternisation’ suggests a defiant threat to opposing forces. The heath also 
colludes with storms: ‘the storm was its lover, and the wind its friend’ (RN 10) and Hardy 
suggests that the low-growing vegetation of the heath is adapted for stormy conditions. Trees 
just off the heath undergo ‘amputations, bruises, cripplings, and harsh lacerations’ yet ‘on the 
open heath, how ineffectively gnashed the storm! Those gusts which tore the trees merely 
waved the furze and heather in a light caress. Egdon was made for times such as these’ (RN 
178). Such a triumphant phrase alongside the ‘caress’ demonstrates the heath’s accord with 
such material forces of darkness, reinforcing its opposition to the forces of the metaphysical 
light.  
 
Hardy also makes the point that the heath vegetation acts as a coolant. As the ground 
‘uncarpeted with vegetation’ reflects ‘from its surface […] the heat which it had imbibed 
during the day’, it reminds us of how the vegetal absorbs light (RN 245). Of course, it is in 
doing so, that through the processes of photosynthesis, it produces new materiality. In the 
context of my reading of Hardy’s vegetal darkness as a challenge to light, the vegetal can 
use light to defy light, reinforcing materiality as the location of the challenge. 
 
I(iii) 
Vegetal Faciality, Voice, and Agency 
 
Hardy situates features usually located within the human, including faciality, voice, and 
agency, within the spatiality of the vegetal. There are countless references to Egdon as a 
‘face’: it ‘had a lonely face’, Rainbarrow has a ‘lowering forehead’ and its vegetation is 
frequently described as hair with paths ‘like the parting-line on a head of black hair’ (RN 10, 
48, 12). It also has its own sensory capacities: voice, observational ability, and a tactile 
awareness.  
 
46 
 
Perhaps it is these capabilities that have led to claims that in The Return of the Native Egdon 
Heath is a ‘character’.46 Cohen refutes such interpretations, as do I. However, I take issue 
with Cohen’s alternative reading of the heath. Although he recognises ‘the correspondence 
between human and landscape faces’, he reads Hardy’s description of the heath as filtered 
through the sensory capacities of its human characters, conceptualising the consequent 
relation between heath and human as ‘symbolic, embodied, and continuous’.47 For me, this 
is an anthropocentric interpretation, potentially predicated through Cohen’s reading of the 
heath as landscape feature rather than as vegetal. If the heath is viewed as vegetal, its face, 
voice, perception, and agency, can more credibly be located within its own vitality without the 
necessity for anthropocentric interpretations. In my reading, it is not that Hardy likens human 
and heath, but that he relocates human features to the heath, widening the capacity of the 
vegetal. 
 
Hardy represents the vegetal’s ‘voice’ as produced through its spatiality. Wildeve hears ‘the 
intonation of a pollard thorn […] the breezes filtering through its unyielding twigs as through 
a strainer. It was as if the night sang dirges with clenched teeth’ (RN 77). Although the sound 
is caused by the breeze filtering through twigs, it is the specific qualities of the species that 
produces the distinctiveness of the voice. Pollarding is carried out to create vigorous new 
growth, and it is this that gives it its ‘unyielding twigs’. Rather than anthropomorphising the 
sound, Hardy’s comparison of it with a ‘strainer’ and ‘dirges with clenched teeth’, draws 
attention to its consequent dense network of twigs.  
 
These distinctive voices of different vegetal species enable a listener ‘acoustic pictures’ of 
the scenery:  
 
46 Cohen, p.446-7, provides some examples of these, including Jean Brooks in ‘The Return of the Native: A 
Novel of Environment, in Thomas Hardy’s The Return of the Native, ed. by Harold Bloom (New York, Chelsea, 
1987), and William H Rutland in Thomas Hardy:  A Study of His Writings and Their Background (New York, 
Russell, 1962). See Cohen footnote 17. 
47 Cohen, p.446-7. 
47 
 
they could hear where the tracts of heather began and ended; where the furze 
was growing stalky and tall; where it had been recently cut; in what direction the 
fir-clump lay, and how near was the pit in which the hollies grew; for these 
differing features had their voices no less than their shapes and colours (RN 78). 
 
Although it is humans who perceive the differences in these voices, they are enabled to do 
so because the disparate voices are produced through the distinctive spatial qualities of each 
species. The furze even has a different voice depending upon whether it is ‘stalky and tall’ or 
‘recently cut’. 
 
Vegetal spatiality almost becomes a language. Hardy describes the heath-bells’ ‘worn 
whisper, dry and papery’ as caused by ‘neither stems, leaves, fruit, blades, prickles, lichen, 
nor moss. They were the mummied heath-bells of the past summer […] dried to dead skins 
by October suns’ (RN 50). Hardy makes the point that rather than the sound created by 
these heath-bells coming from the vitality of its life, it is produced through the structural 
spatiality formed by the dead parts. This structural voice suggests to me that Marder’s 
interpretation of vegetal spatiality and posture being a plant’s ‘language’ might be a useful 
lens. For Marder, a plant meaningfully ‘expresses itself’ through its spatiality and he is 
insistent that in interpreting this as ‘language’, rather than using a metaphor, he is treating 
language through the ‘hyper-materialist tradition’ that ‘is alive to the spatial relations and 
articulations between beings, animate or inanimate’ (MM 75). Through this perspective, 
Hardy’s vegetal spatiality can be read as communicative and meaningful.  
 
This contrasts with two anthropocentric interpretations of the heath-bells’ voice that Hardy 
speculates upon:  
“The spirit moved them.” A meaning of the phrase forced itself upon the 
attention; and an emotional listener’s fetichistic mood might have ended in one 
of more advanced quality. It was not, after all, that the left-hand expanse of old 
blooms spoke, or the right-hand, or those of the slope in front; but it was the 
single person of something else speaking through each at once (RN 50).  
 
48 
 
The ‘emotional listener’ in ‘fetichistic mood’ hears the distinct sounds of different areas. The 
second listener is of ‘more advanced quality’ and hears a single voice. Mattar reads this 
passage as an enactment of ‘the evolution from a fetichistic to a theological mode’ that is 
‘monotheistic’.48 Although she reads Hardy in The Return of the Native as at times veering 
towards ‘fetichism’ and ‘animism’, she also perceives the connection between such beliefs 
and scientific materialist thinking, claiming that both are rooted in an understanding ‘that 
more metaphysical systems of thought lack’, that ‘humanity is part of the larger organism of 
nature’.49 That being so, for me even an animistic interpretation is problematic as it fails to 
take into account the fact that this sound is created by the dead parts of the heath-bells. My 
reading of this passage recognises that both the fetichistic and metaphysical interpretations 
by the listeners on the heath are imagined interpretations. In suggesting that ‘the spirit 
moved them’ is a notion that is forced upon their attention, Hardy highlights the fact that such 
an idea is rooted within the listener and that these are therefore anthropocentric 
interpretations. In contrast, the narrative voice makes it clear that the cause is definitively 
material, but, I argue, this does not mean that it is any less lacking in meaningful 
communication.  
 
Hardy extends such vegetal capacity to the possession of awareness and agency. The 
heath ‘became full of a watchful intentness now; for when other things sank brooding to 
sleep the heath appeared slowly to awake and listen’ (RN 8-9). Like the ‘black fraternisation’ 
of vegetal darkness and the night, this sinister vegetal alertness seems threatening. Hardy 
implies that the vegetal has agential capacity and this is reinforced through its spatiality 
enabling its participation in the plot. Such agency works to drive the plot forward, benefiting 
the natives and furthering the transgressions of the outsiders Eustacia and Wildeve, which 
eventually lead to their deaths. The threat is to the outsiders on the heath. 
 
 
48 Mattar, p.15. 
49 Matter, p.14. 
49 
 
This is particularly evident when tracing the symbolic and literal role of paths, these being 
places where the vegetal has been destroyed. For the outsiders, the wayfinding functions of 
paths are necessary; to the natives they are less essential. As Wildeve walks Eustacia home 
after the dance that they have attended while married to other partners, ‘the irregularities of 
the path were not visible’, with the result that ‘Wildeve occasionally stumbled; whilst Eustacia 
found it necessary to perform some graceful feats of balancing whenever a small tuft of 
heather or root of furze protruded itself through the grass of the narrow track’. As a result, 
Wildeve must give her his hand, furthering their transgressive association. In contrast, Venn, 
in his role as a deus ex machina, is easily able to follow Wildeve by crossing ‘the pathless 
portion of the heath’ (RN 222-3). Natives like him have a more developed ‘sense of touch in 
the feet’ so that ‘a difference between impact on maiden herbage, and on the crippled stalks 
of a slight footway, is perceptible through the thickest boot or shoe’ (RN 52), the spatial 
qualities of the vegetal recognised by the natives. 
 
Even when the paths are followed by natives, they forward the disruptive plot. When Mrs 
Yeobright attempts to visit her son, Clym, newly married to Eustacia, ‘she tried one 
ascending path and another, and found that they led her astray’ (RN 231). Leaving without 
seeing him, ‘in her anxiety to get out of the direct view of the house she had diverged from 
the straightest path homeward’ (RN 238). This leads to her death, Clym and Eustacia’s 
separation, and onwards to Eustacia and Wildeve’s tragic ending. Later, when Clym is 
presented with three paths outside his house, he chooses the path to Johnny Nunsuch’s 
house, who reveals the information that leads to Clym’s understanding of Eustacia’s role in 
the death of his mother.  
 
Hardy also stresses how the whiteness of paths distinguishes them from the darkness of the 
heath. In the twilight ‘the white surface of the road remained almost as clear as ever’ (RN 
11), whereas moonlight is ‘powerless to silver such sable features’ of ‘the masses of furze 
and heath’ (RN 114). Such whiteness, and its relationship to light, furthers Hardy’s 
50 
 
problematisation of light in favour of materialised darkness, as through such whiteness, 
Hardy symbolically links paths to Eustacia and Wildeve’s transgressions. As they dance, 
they make the vegetation under their feet more reflective as ‘the hard, beaten surface of the 
sod, when viewed aslant towards the moonlight, shone like a polished table’. Then as they 
walk home, the moonlight acts upon them and makes their faces appear ‘like two pearls on a 
table of ebony’. Paths and their corresponding whiteness become symbols of how their 
actions on the night of the dance cause them to break social codes of fidelity to their 
marriage partners: ‘the dance had come like an irresistible attack upon whatever sense of 
social order there was in their minds, to drive them back into old paths which were now 
doubly irregular’ (RN 219-222). 
 
Like paths, the bonfires are places of vegetal erasure and further the tragic plot. They act as 
romance signals between Eustacia and Wildeve: their first meeting in the novel is expediated 
by Eustacia’s signalling bonfire, and her bonfire one year later precipitates their final tragedy. 
Their story therefore becomes framed by the destruction of vegetation. Moreover, the 
specific material qualities of the different vegetal species also play a role. Of the fifth of 
November bonfires at the beginning of the novel, Eustacia’s is ‘steady and persistent’ (RN 
47) as its fuel is ‘hard pieces of wood […] the knotty boles of old thorn trees’ (RN 53) and 
therefore able to attract Wildeve’s attention, whereas the other bonfires, made of furze or 
straw and beanstalks, are less long-lasting. Likewise, the bonfire one year later that again 
attracts Wildeve, is built with the long-lasting material of ‘furze stumps, thorn-tree roots, and 
[…] other solid materials’ (RN 281). Destruction of the vegetal, whether paths or bonfires, 
advances the plot, eventually ridding the heath of these disruptive outsiders.  
 
 
 
51 
 
II: HUMANITY 
 
II(i) 
Thought as a ‘disease of flesh’ 
 
In the second part of this chapter, I turn to Hardy’s representation of humanity. I thereby 
echo Hardy’s structure in which humanity does not appear until chapter two, after the 
description of the heath in chapter one. Hardy’s structure is another reflection of the 
chronology of the biblical creation: only after creating land and the vegetal, does God make 
humankind. Through entitling this second chapter ‘Humanity Appears upon the Scene, Hand 
in Hand with Trouble’, Hardy indicates that unlike the ‘imperturbable countenance’ of the 
heath, humanity will be the cause of disquiet (RN 268). 
 
It is the human mind that Hardy pinpoints as problematic, particularly a dualistic conception 
of it as a separate sphere that is in conflict with the body. Marder compares humanity with 
the vegetal in terms of the possession of such a separate inner sphere. For the vegetal there 
is no separate ‘inner essence that would be hidden behind the surface level of its 
phenomenal appearances’ (MM 80). In contrast, for humankind, he paraphrases Aristotle in 
claiming that ‘what makes humans human is not their physical shape but logos’ (MM 77). In 
many of his novels Hardy presents a life based on the mind alone as detrimental, 
dramatizing this through characters such as Clym Yeobright in The Return of the Native, 
Edred Fitzpiers in The Woodlanders, and Angel Clare in Tess of the d’Urbervilles. In Clym, 
Hardy explicitly illustrates the conflict between intellect and body through describing his face 
as ‘ruthlessly overrun by its parasite, thought’. For Clym, thought is a ‘disease of flesh’ (RN 
120). 
 
Hardy entitles the chapter in which he outlines Clym’s intellectual development as ‘My Mind 
to Me A Kingdom Is’, and it is this domination of the mind that Hardy presents as 
problematic. Both Schopenhauer and Marder consider this over-developed human mind. 
52 
 
Schopenhauer views the intellect as ‘designed for comprehending those ends on the 
attainment of which depend individual life and its propagation’ (WWR2 284). The intellect’s 
function is to ensure the survival of the individual. Both Schopenhauer and Marder consider 
the brain as a development from earlier stages of evolution. Marder views a continuum from 
the irritability of plants to the sensibility of animals, evolving eventually into a brain, and in a 
similar way to Schopenhauer, he sees its primary purpose as survival: ‘the brain and the 
central nervous system do not invent a new function but offer a novel solution to the old 
problem of life’ (MM 156-7). Reading Hardy’s Clym through these lenses suggests a 
character whose intellect has become divorced from the practicality of survival. It has 
become too dominant and used for purposes for which it was not intended.  
 
Barad’s reconceptualization of knowing as integral to being, a notion I discussed in the 
introduction, locates knowing in a materiality that is in direct engagement with the rest of the 
material world. But Clym’s knowing, or thought, is separate to the materiality of his body, 
illustrated through the thought-marks on his face being representations of thought rather 
than thought itself. Clym’s mind uses the face as a ‘mere waste tablet’ that becomes 
‘overlaid with legible meanings’ (RN 120). His face is represented as a recording device 
separate to the physical body. It is seen not ‘as a picture, but […] as a page; not by what it 
was, but by what it recorded’, and these recordings are mere ‘symbols’ (RN 143). This is not 
Barad’s knowing as being but knowing as separate from being. It is also a reflection of 
Marder’s contention that thinking ‘is a defect or disease’ that is ‘an anomaly or a gap, within 
the order of existence’ (MM 79). 
 
Marder extends this point to suggest that thinking is a cause of dissatisfaction with life, 
stating that it demonstrates a ‘disconnect between life and formal knowledge, between 
existence and the brooding about its meaning or purpose’ (MM 80). Hardy does indeed show 
Clym’s dominating intellect as that which causes him unhappiness. He describes Clym’s face 
as showing ‘the view of life as a thing to be put up with, replacing that zest for existence 
53 
 
which was so intense in early civilisations’ (RN 143), implying that the intellect displaces 
enjoyment in mere life itself. It takes the reader back to his comment in the Life that I 
discussed in the introduction: that the extremity of development in humankind means that 
there can be no ‘happiness’ for ‘higher existences’.50 
 
I have already considered how Hardy portrays an opposition between symbolic and 
metaphysical light and material darkness, and how he associates the transgressive Eustacia 
and Wildeve with light. I now want to extend this analysis of Hardy’s use of the light/dark 
antithesis through his representation of the separate internal sphere of the mind as a 
symbolic light. Hardy portrays the mind as using or feeding off the body. He suggests that 
‘mental luminousness must be fed with oil of life, even though there is already a physical 
need for it’. The separate inner sphere, characterised here as light, must parasitically use the 
life that is already required to support the body. This damages Clym and his face shows ‘the 
pitiful sight of two demands on one supply’ (RN 120). 
 
It is instructive to compare Hardy’s representation of a symbolic light as destructive to 
humankind with the vegetal’s use of light. Humanity’s face is marred by light whereas the 
vegetal transforms light to create its own dynamically changing face. For Marder, ‘whereas 
humans remember whatever has phenomenally appeared in the light, plants keep the 
memory of light itself' (MM 156). For Clym, light is destructive; for plants it is constructive. 
They absorb light to use in photosynthetic processes to construct more life and more 
material. Through my reading, this produces more darkness that then competes with light. In 
the vegetal then, materiality can use and dominate light; in Hardy’s construction of humanity, 
light feeds off materiality.  
 
 
50 Thomas Hardy, Life, p.227 (entry dated 7th April 1889). 
54 
 
Hardy’s use of humanity’s symbolic light serves then to separate us from materiality. The 
vegetal’s use of light is embodied, whereas humanity’s inner light isolates us from 
embodiment. In considering how humanity uses vegetal movement towards light to 
symbolise a movement towards the light of the heavens, Marder suggests that we are 
elevated from the rootedness of the earth but that in the process, the literal plant is devalued 
(MM 57). My reading of The Return of the Native proposes that Hardy recognises the 
importance of materiality and uses the symbolism of the inner light within humanity, to 
denigrate dualistic models of humankind.   
    
II(ii) 
Autonomy, Heteronomy and the Returning Native 
 
Another way of exploring how Hardy problematises the mind is through the opposing modes 
of being of autonomy and heteronomy, or self-determination and lack of. It seems to me that 
the human yearning for autonomy is a theme that many Hardy scholars gesture towards in 
their analysis of how Hardy dramatizes a life framed through self-determination as 
detrimental. Mallett asserts that ‘Hardy’s most thoughtful characters […] appear wounded by 
the very act of trying to “mould” themselves’ and he includes Clym in this category.51 In 
Plant-Thinking, Marder explores the differences between heteronomous and autonomous 
modes of being. He argues that the vegetal’s lack of self-determination has contributed to its 
denigration (MM 67-8), suggesting that to be autonomous is to be judged as superior. Using 
the vegetal’s heteronomous mode of being as a contrast to humanity’s perceived autonomy 
becomes a productive perspective for considering this aspect of humanity. 
 
The vegetal’s relationship to light is heteronomous, light being necessary to its existence and 
growth. However, humanity’s attachment to a symbolic inner light ‘emasculates [our] integral 
connection to the environment’ (MM 168). Norman Wirzba suggests that in nineteenth 
 
51 Mallett, p.30. 
55 
 
century literature, emancipatory themes were becoming important, with freedom seen as a 
way of transcending the constraints of material life. Consequentially, humanity leaves behind 
material entanglements with the natural world.52 In many respects, this is Hardy’s very point 
with respect to Clym. Hardy suggests that a more heteronomous mode of life that befits our 
vegetal inheritance, would result in a less alienating and more connected relationship to the 
other. 
 
In characterising both Clym and Eustacia as ‘original’, Hardy gestures towards their   
predilection for autonomy and individuality. He contrasts such characters to the natives who 
are portrayed as more heteronomous and as a collective. Similarly, and highlighting the 
connection between the natives and the heath vegetal, Hardy represents the vegetal en 
masse. The sound created by the heath-bells is produced by a multitude rather than 
individual flowers. It is the ‘united products of infinitesimal vegetable causes’, the collective 
emphasised through describing them as ‘myriads’ and ‘combined multitudes’ (RN 50). Unlike 
the natives and the vegetal, the tragedy of The Return of the Native is predicated upon the 
motivations of individual autonomy.53  
  
It is Eustacia’s yearning for autonomy that makes her such an outsider on and to the heath. 
Hardy individualises her as a ‘divinity’ and a ‘goddess’, suggestive of a being that surpasses 
an earthbound nature. But the heath limits her impact. Her ‘celestial imperiousness, love, 
wrath, and fervour had proved to be somewhat thrown away on netherward Egdon. Her 
power was limited’ (RN 60-2). To live on the heath, implies Hardy, it is necessary to be in a 
heteronomous relationship with it: ‘to dwell on a heath without studying its meanings was like 
wedding a foreigner without learning his tongue’. He continues, pointedly contrasting this to 
 
52 Norman Wirzba, ‘Transformation of Practical Life in the Nineteenth Century’, unpublished paper delivered at 
the conference ‘Ecology and Religion’ (multi-site conference: Atlanta, Seattle, Waco, Washington DC, 
Lancaster, 18th-21st September 2019). 
53 The contrasting modes of individuality and the collective are important themes in the work of Schreiner, and 
I explore these modes more fully in the second part of this thesis. 
56 
 
Eustacia’s nature: ‘to have lost the godlike conceit that we may do what we will, and not to 
have acquired a homely zest for doing what we can, shows a grandeur of temper’. Eustacia 
is determinedly autonomous, unable to accept ‘compromise’ (RN 65-6), surely an attribute of 
heteronomy. 
    
It is, however, through Clym, the returning native, that Hardy more fully compares 
autonomous and heteronomous modes of life. Clym’s trajectory is from autonomy to 
heteronomy and then, problematically, a limited reversion to autonomy. Comparing The 
Return of the Native to eighteenth-century fiction, Gillian Beer notes that Hardy ‘moves the 
return of the native from the end of his story to relatively near its beginning’ and that this then 
‘becomes the generative act of his fiction’.54 She therefore reads it as a challenge to a 
nineteenth-century emancipatory agenda. Hardy presents Clym as one of whom others 
expected autonomy: ‘he had been a lad of whom something was expected […] he would not 
stand still in the circumstances amid which he was born’ (RN 143-4). But after his stint as 
manager to a diamond merchant in Paris, Clym bucks such expectations: aiming to be ‘less 
useless’, he returns to Egdon with an ambition to educate the natives (RN 146). However, 
although Clym frames this aspiration as a service to his community, his response shows, as 
Mallett suggests, an ‘undercurrent of egotism’.55 His motivation remains framed through his 
own intellect. Although he might seem to be rejecting personal success and his ethics 
collective, the motivation remains autonomous. 
 
Through becoming partially blinded, Clym’s plans are, however, thwarted. He is ‘transformed 
into an invalid’ with ‘all thought of pursuing his work […] given up for a long time to come’ 
(RN 208). His partial sightedness has been caused by intensive intellectual work. The 
symbolic inner light causes his literal partial blindness. 
 
54 Gillian Beer, ‘Can the Native Return?’, Open Fields: Science in Cultural Encounter (Oxford, Clarendon Press, 
1996), pp.31-54 (repr. in Thomas Hardy, The Return of the Native ed. by Phillip Mallett, 2nd edn. (New York, 
Norton & Co., 2006), pp.504-524 (p.509).  
55 Mallett, p.27. 
57 
 
 
But it is through such partial blindness that Clym gains a greater insight, discovering truths to 
which, until now, he has been blind, particularly the role played by Eustacia in his mother’s 
death. Hardy’s chapter titles explicitly link this insight to enlightenment: they move from 
‘Wherefore is Light Given to Him That Is in Misery’ (quoting Job 3.20), to ‘A Lurid Light 
Breaks in upon a Darkened Understanding’. These chapters conclude with Clym’s insight 
becoming embodied:  
his mouth had passed into the phase more or less imaginatively rendered in 
studies of Oedipus […] Instead of there being before him the pale face of 
Eustacia, and a masculine shape unknown, there was only the imperturbable 
countenance of the heath […] defied the cataclysmal onsets of centuries (RN 
268). 
Hardy’s reference to the self-blinded Oedipus explicitly connects such blindness to a wider 
insight. Rather than focusing upon what is immediate, Eustacia and her lover, Clym’s 
perspective expands out to encompass the heath’s long-term temporality. Furthermore, 
through comparison with Clym’s mother’s ‘intuition’, Hardy suggests that such insight is 
embodied, further denying the superior efficacy of the separate intellect. He describes Mrs 
Yeobright as having ‘a singular insight into life’, and his examples of others who, like her, are 
‘without clear ideas of the things they criticise’, are literally blind (RN 162). Representing 
Clym and Mrs Yeobright as in possession of an embodied insight, characterises them as 
participating in Barad’s knowing as located within being, and as such, in tune with all material 
beings. 
 
Forced to abandon intellectual pursuit, Clym begins work as a furze and turf cutter, finding a 
commonality with his fellow natives, many of whom have similar occupations. Rather than 
merely returning to live on the heath, Clym’s real homecoming seems to be this more integral 
relationship with the heath, returning to his childhood being when he was ‘inwoven with the 
heath’ (RN 144):   
He was permeated with its scenes, with its substance, and with its odours. He 
might be said to be its product. His eyes had first opened thereon […] first 
58 
 
images of his memory […] his estimate of life had been coloured by it; his toys 
had been the flint knives and arrow-heads which he found there […] his flowers, 
the purple bells and yellow furze; his animal kingdom, the snakes and croppers; 
his society, its human haunters (RN 148). 
 
Hardy emphasises Clym’s bodily relationship to the heath. He is ‘permeated’ with its 
‘substance’ and is its ‘product’ as much perhaps as is the vegetal. Even his mind had been 
formed by it, and his return is therefore understood as both a physical and mental return. He 
is akin to the ants that have a continuous existence on the heath, ‘those of the old times […] 
the ancestors of these which walked there now’ (RN 241). Reading Clym as ‘product’ of the 
heath through Barad’s notion that beings emerge through ‘intra-action’, with ‘“environments” 
and “bodies” […] intra-actively co-constituted’, Clym’s formation becomes relational, and his 
subsequent separation from the heath, becomes a period of severance from naturally-formed 
relationships, and the damage that ensues inevitable (KB 170). His return becomes a return 
to the materiality of his very being.   
 
Hardy’s description of Clym in his new occupation becoming part of the heath’s ecology is 
therefore apposite:  
The silent being who thus occupied himself seemed to be of no more account in 
life than an insect. He appeared as a mere parasite of the heath, fretting its 
surface in his daily labour as a moth frets a garment, entirely engrossed with its 
products, having no knowledge of anything in the world but fern, furze, heath, 
lichens, and moss (RN 231). 
 
For Marder, the loss of reason, the ‘distinguishing element’ of humanity, makes humans 
‘ontologically akin to non-human beings’ (MM 77). Hardy presents Clym here as a mere 
‘being’. His silence and the reduction of his knowledge to that only of his vegetal 
surroundings dehumanises him, emphasised through this description focalised through Mrs 
Yeobright who at this point fails to recognise her own son. Describing Clym as a ‘parasite’ 
recalls Hardy’s earlier description of the effect of thought as a parasite upon Clym’s face. 
Now he is a ‘parasite’ on the face of the heath. But his parasitic destructiveness is reduced to 
‘mere’. Compared to the earlier ‘ruthlessly overrun’ of the ‘parasite, thought’ on Clym’s face, 
59 
 
alongside his ‘fretting’ of the heath’s surface, Hardy suggests that his destructiveness as a 
furze cutter is minimal. Abstract thought seems more damaging than physical destruction. 
 
The dehumanised Clym becomes an integral part of the natural world of the heath:    
His daily life was of a curious microscopic sort, his whole world being limited to 
a circuit of a few feet from his person. His familiars were creeping and winged 
things, and they seemed to enroll him in their band. Bees hummed around his 
ears […] The strange amber-coloured butterflies which Egdon produced, and 
which were never seen elsewhere, quivered in the breath of his lips, alighted 
upon his bowed back […] Tribes of emerald-green grasshoppers leaped over 
his feet […] Huge flies […] buzzed about him without knowing that he was a 
man (RN 211). 
 
Through defining the butterflies as those only ever seen on Egdon Heath, Hardy emphasises 
Clym’s limited world. However, his blindness reduces this even further, to a ‘microscopic’ 
area of only a ‘few feet’. Clym becomes embedded in an ecological niche, becoming enrolled 
in a band with what might be viewed as insignificant beings. But Hardy’s prepositional 
language constructs Clym’s importance not just as non-human, but non-animal. Like the 
heath, furze and fern that spatially create this ecological niche in which the animals move 
and act, Clym’s function is spatial: the bees ‘hummed around his ears’, the butterflies 
‘quivered in the breath of his lips’ and ‘alighted upon his bowed back’, the grasshoppers 
‘leaped over his feet’ (my italics). The flies even buzzed ‘about him without knowing that he 
was a man’. Clym is not just vegetally heteronomous, but vegetally structural.  
 
Hardy further portrays Clym as part of the heath vegetal through colour. He is described as ‘a 
brown spot’ (RN 211) and ‘of a russet hue, not more distinguishable from the scene around 
him than the green caterpillar from the leaf it feeds on’ (RN 231). He has become part of the 
heath’s ‘antique brown dress’ and, by implication, as timeless as the vegetal. Whereas Clym 
has ‘rough and brown’ hands and a ‘rusty look’, in comparing him with Wildeve, ‘elegantly 
dressed in a new summer suit and light summer hat’, Hardy recalls the antonymic whiteness 
60 
 
of the heath’s paths and the transgressive whiteness of Eustacia and Wildeve on the night of 
the dance (RN 235). 
 
Such a vegetal appearance allies him to the other natives. Hardy describes the furze-cutters 
for whom ‘every individual was so involved in furze by his method of carrying the faggots that 
he appeared like a bush on legs’ (RN 16). Venn, concealing himself with turves to overhear 
Eustacia and Wildeve’s conversation, also becomes vegetal-like. As he drags them over him, 
‘the turves, standing upon him with the heather upwards, looked precisely as if they were 
growing. He crept along again, and the turves upon his back crept with him’ (RN 76). 
 
Clym’s relationship with light is also heteronomous, linked to the external light of the sun 
rather than the internal light of reason. Like the other natives, he becomes, in Marder’s 
terms, vegetally hetero-temporal – plant temporality being derived from the time of alterity. 
When furze-cutting, the rhythm of his day is determined by the sun: ‘he rose with the sun […] 
to work from four o’clock in the morning till noon; then when the heat of the day was at its 
highest, to go home and sleep’ (RN 211). This seems beneficial: Wildeve envies Clym’s 
‘sweet sleep’ (RN 237). Furthermore, being a furze-cutter, Clym’s time is wedded to the 
material growth of the vegetal, controlled by the time embedded within its spatiality.  
 
But if we read Clym’s heteronomous relationship with the heath as his true homecoming, 
Hardy implies that this is an impossible return for humanity due to the demands of the 
intellect. At the end of the novel Clym rejects a heteronomous way of being and reverts to a 
life based on intellectual pursuit, becoming an itinerant preacher and planning on keeping a 
night-school. One can surmise that there might be something of Hardy himself written into 
Clym here. Both Clym’s and Hardy’s boyhood homes are just off the heath that becomes 
Egdon in this novel. Like Clym, Hardy is a returned native, writing The Return of the Native 
61 
 
while living back in rural Dorset after periods in London. Also like Clym, Hardy fails to 
maintain his returned native status with his subsequent move back to London.56  
 
Clym also struggles to accept an equality with other lifeforms. When he observes the heath’s 
‘oppressive horizontality’ which ‘gave him a sense of bare equality with, and no superiority to, 
a single living thing under the sun’, the modifier ‘oppressive’ implies that a sense of equality 
is burdensome and dispiriting (RN 177). In contrast, at the end of the novel Clym preaches 
from the highest spot on the heath, the ‘commanding elevation of Rainbarrow’ (RN 335). 
Such positioning surely reinforces some sense of perceived superiority of humanity in 
possession of an intellect.    
 
II(iii) The Vegetal Roots of the Emotions 
 
Although Clym struggles to perceive an equality with other living beings, through 
representing the emotions as embodied, Hardy does link humanity with other lifeforms. 
Recognising that for Hardy the emotions are embodied and are the motivating force within 
humanity is no new insight. I have already indicated in the chapter introduction Mallett and 
Levine’s views on this. Mallett contends that, for Hardy ‘feeling rather than reason governs 
human life’, and he indicates an entry in Hardy’s own literary notebooks taken from Comte: 
‘Feeling – the great force of human life.57 However, I extend this to argue that in The Return 
of the Native, through such embodiment, Hardy connects humanity to all other lifeforms, 
specifically the vegetal of the heath.   
 
To emphasise their embodied source, Hardy infers a similarity between emotion-led human 
utterances and the voice of the vegetal. After the sound of the heath-bells, he describes the 
involuntary sounds made by Eustacia: 
 
56 In later years, Hardy does return to live in Dorset for the rest of his life, to a house built within miles of his 
childhood home. 
57 Mallett, p.21; p.25. 
62 
 
there mingled […] a sound which modulated so naturally into the rest that its 
beginning and ending were hardly to be distinguished […] her articulation was 
but as another phrase of the same discourse as theirs. Thrown out on the winds 
it became twined in with them […] What she uttered was a lengthened sighing 
[…] There was a spasmodic abandonment about it as if, in allowing herself to 
utter the sound, the woman’s brain had authorized what it could not regulate 
(RN 51). 
 
Eustacia’s sighing is ‘twined’ with and is the ‘same discourse’ as the sounds of the heath-
bells. Her similarity to the heath at this point is emphasised as ‘the night revealed little of her 
whose form it was embracing, for the mobile parts of her countenance could not be seen’ 
(RN 52). With her own face indistinct, she becomes less human and part of the material face 
of the heath. In fact, in terms of her physicality alone, Eustacia is often allied with the heath. 
When first seen by Venn, she is ‘much like an organic part of the entire motionless structure’ 
of the heath, and it is not until she moves that he realises that the form is a human figure 
(RN,15). Other descriptions of her physicality ally her with the darkness of the heath. Her 
hair is that in which ‘a whole winter did not contain darkness enough to form its shadow: it 
closed over her forehead like nightfall extinguishing the western glow’ (RN 61). 
 
The sound Eustacia makes is further vegetal-like in that it is unmediated by rationality. It had 
a ‘spasmodic abandonment about it’ which her brain ‘could not regulate’. Hardy reinforces 
this irrationality when, later that same evening and in response to the appearance of 
Wildeve, ‘a low laugh escaped her’. This is the third of three ‘utterance[s]’: ‘the first […] had 
expressed anxiety; the second […] had expressed impatience; the present was one of 
triumphant pleasure’. All these sounds are led by emotion. They are inarticulate and 
spontaneous: they ‘escaped’ her. Furthermore, when she does eventually reply to Wildeve, 
she ‘repress[es] herself’ and speaks ‘artificially’, Hardy going on to describe her mouth as 
‘formed less to speak than to quiver’ (RN 56-61). Language moderated by reason seems 
alien to Eustacia. As vegetal language, in Marder’s terms, is spatial and material, her 
utterances are similarly corporeal. 
 
63 
 
The emotions and the vegetal are also allied in the way they fuel the narrative. The tragic 
plot is framed by the bonfires then driven forwards by the emotions. The vegetal provides 
fuel for the bonfires and, through describing the embodied emotions of love, passion, and 
anger through a vocabulary of inflammation and heat, they are characterised as providing 
fuel for the body and the language redolent of the bonfires. Eustacia and Mrs Yeobright are 
‘two people with inflammable natures’ who give back ‘heat for heat’ (RN 205-8). In Eustacia 
‘indignation spread through her like subterranean heat’ and for Wildeve ‘the curse of 
inflammability is upon me’ (RN 58-9). In places, Hardy even more explicitly links the bonfires 
and the emotions. The feelings Eustacia inspires in Wildeve are as ‘the revived embers of an 
old passion’, her own soul is ‘flame-like’ from which ‘sparks […] rose into her dark pupils’ 
(RN 60-1), and she responds to Wildeve with a ‘suppressed fire’ (RN 78). When Eustacia 
dreams of Clym as ‘excellent fuel’ for ‘newly kindled fervour’, reading the bonfire as romance 
signal seems apposite (RN 104). 
 
Jennifer Gribble’s feminist reading of The Return of the Native is useful here. She reads the 
bonfires on Egdon Heath as one of the heath’s ‘traditional fertility rituals’ and goes on to refer 
to Eustacia’s bonfire as her ‘sexual call’ to Wildeve. She asserts that Eustacia uses this 
‘silent language of the heath’ as ‘a way of expressing feelings that cannot readily be 
articulated in any other language’.58 Reading the bonfires as ‘language’ recalls Eustacia’s 
own inarticulate emotion-led sounds, and almost validates this use of a fellow inarticulate 
language to communicate her feelings. Moreover, returning to Marder’s notion of vegetal 
language, this suggests that the destruction of the vegetal in the bonfire, releases its 
spatially inflected language as it colludes with Eustacia on a path to mutual destruction.   
 
Hardy also uses the colour red to link the bonfires and the embodied emotions. As Mrs 
Yeobright argues with Clym over his relationship with Eustacia, he ‘reddened like fire’ (RN 
 
58 Jennifer Gribble, ‘The Quiet Women of Egdon Heath’, Essays in Criticism, 46(3) (1996), 234-57 (repr. in The 
Return of the Native, ed. by Phillip Mallett, 2nd edn. (New York, Norton & Co., 2006) pp.524-542 (p.529-531). 
64 
 
165), and Eustacia’s face ‘burnt crimson’ when she conceals Wildeve’s presence from 
Clym’s mother (RN 226). Redness linked to emotions can also be traced through the 
romance of the reddleman, Diggory Venn, and Thomasin Yeobright. Although Venn’s colour 
is due to his reddle-dealing, Hardy pointedly informs the reader that Venn has no mercantile 
reason for being on the heath as it contains no sheep that need marking with said reddle 
(RN 129). Instead, his red presence is due to his attachment to Thomasin. It is only when 
late in the novel he returns to the heath with his skin and clothes washed of reddle that 
Thomasin accepts his proposal of marriage. Such removal of redness and tragic emotions 
seem part of the restoration of normality on the heath and allied to the deaths of Eustacia 
and Wildeve. 
 
I have already explored how Hardy represents rationality as light and as that which blinds 
Clym. But Hardy also characterises the emotions as a cause of blindness. Mrs Yeobright 
refers to Clym’s developing attachment to Eustacia as being ‘blinded’ (RN 165), and he 
gradually grows ‘accustomed to the first blinding halo kindled about him by love and beauty’ 
(RN 171). However, it is not the emotions themselves that cause blindness, but that the 
rational mind is blind to the workings of the emotions, buried as they are in the darkness of 
the body. Hardy presents this conflict in Eustacia. Wildeve’s effect on her is as ‘a sigh which 
shook her like a shiver. Whenever a flash of reason darted like an electric light upon her 
lover’ (RN 60). Rationally, Eustacia knows that Wildeve is not good for her, but her feelings 
clash with this reasoned judgement and dominate her response. 
 
It is instructive to read this through Schopenhauer’s interpretation of the emotions that drive 
reproduction as related to the ‘unconsciousness of the plant […] [which] remains the 
foundation’ or ‘the original and natural condition of all things’ (WWR2 142), therefore allying 
the emotions and the body. Schopenhauer identifies our ‘digestion, circulation, secretion, 
growth, and reproduction’ as ‘functions of our body which are not guided by knowledge’ 
(WWR1 115), and as the ‘vegetative part of our own life’ (WWR1 275). Furthermore, in his 
65 
 
article on Schopenhauer and Darwinism, David Asher claims that Schopenhauer views 
sexual love as an expression of the will, suggesting that Nature implants an illusion in 
humankind that we perceive as ‘love’, with the aim of securing the next generation.59 From 
this perspective, Hardy’s characters are blind to the working of love and passion, such a 
drive being unconscious. The problem is that our minds hoodwink us into assuming that we 
are rational beings.  
 
I am not claiming that Hardy’s notion of the over-riding motivation of love is influenced by 
Schopenhauer’s view, and instead concur with Mallett’s view that although many of Hardy’s 
views at this point might bear similarity to those of Schopenhauer, that does not mean that 
they are influenced by him. But it is not inconceivable that Hardy might have come across 
such Schopenhauerian ideas through the work of Darwin. Hardy’s knowledge and 
appreciation of Darwin’s ideas is well-known, and Schopenhauer’s notion that the ‘aim of all 
love intrigues […] is really of more importance than all other ends in human life’ (i.e. the 
‘composition of the next generation’), is outlined in Darwin’s Descent of Man.60 In fact, 
Darwin quotes these lines directly from (and attributes them to) Asher’s 1871 article on 
Schopenhauer.61 Be that as it may, in The Return of the Native, Hardy shows that love 
cannot be overcome by mere rationality. Eustacia desires ‘to be loved to madness’ and 
longs ‘for the abstraction called passionate love more than for any particular lover’ (RN 64). 
But in the narrative comment that ‘if she had had a little more self-control she would have 
attenuated the emotion to nothing by sheer reasoning’ (RN 104), Hardy suggests a helpless 
and deluded rather Schopenhaurian love illusion. Moreover, through entitling the chapter in 
which Eustacia and Clym meet for the first time ‘The First Act in a Timeworn Drama’, Hardy 
presents love and sexuality as perpetual instinctive human drives.   
 
 
59 David Asher, ‘Schopenhauer and Darwinism’, The Journal of Anthropology, 1(3) (Jan 1871), 312-332 (p.323).  
60 Charles Darwin, The Descent of Man and Selection in Relation to Sex, (London, John Murray, 1901), p.893. 
61 Asher, p.323. 
66 
 
Hardy’s representation of humanity as non-dualistically embodied, driven by bodily drives, 
could almost be read as a lesson to humankind. Bennett suggests that a recognition of one’s 
own embodiment might be beneficial to humanity as well as to our relationship with other 
lifeforms. She claims that ‘to raise the status of the materiality of which we are composed’ 
would contribute to ‘human health and happiness’ and that ‘a newfound attentiveness to 
matter and its powers […] can inspire a greater sense of the extent to which all bodies are 
kin’ (JB 12-13, emphasis in original). Clym in particular would perhaps gain from such a 
perspective. It would not only allow him to recognise the role of his body, but also perhaps 
engender a respect for the materiality of other lifeforms, and allow him to perceive his 
‘equality’, rather than superiority, with beings that function without conscious reasoning.  
 
 
 
 
67 
 
Chapter Two - Entanglement, Imperfection and the ‘Unfulfilled 
Intention’: The Vegetal Challenge to the Metaphysical in Hardy’s 
The Woodlanders 
 
In its very being the plant accomplishes a lived destruction of metaphysics. 
(Michael Marder, Plant-Thinking, p.11)  
 
The FAILURE of THINGS to be what they are meant to be, lends them, in place 
of the intended interest, a new and greater interest of an unintended kind. 
(Thomas Hardy, Life, p.127, emphasis in original) 
 
In this chapter I explore how in The Woodlanders, published nine years after The Return of 
the Native, Hardy extends his challenge to the metaphysical. I read the novel as, in part, an 
exploration of the relationship between an underlying metaphysical idealism and a complex 
phenomenal reality. As in The Return of the Native, Hardy situates the challenge to the 
metaphysical in the vegetal, but rather than locate this in its darkness, he positions it in its 
complex spatiality. He extends this through creating entanglements between the vegetal and 
humanity which result in complex networks of beings and events, in which causality 
becomes non-linear. Evolutionary competition becomes part of this complexity in which all 
beings impact upon one another. Through locating the metaphysical viewpoint in the 
outsider character of Edred Fitzpiers, Hardy provides a mouthpiece for this perspective and 
through undermining this character, offers a critique of the efficacy of the metaphysical 
perspective.  
 
I argue that Hardy uses the networked structure of the vegetal as a model for the webs and 
entanglements embedded in the structure of The Woodlanders. The vegetal’s complex 
spatiality is predicated on the multiplicity, adaptability, and continuousness of its growth, 
which problematises a metaphysical notion of oneness and ideality. Reading it through the 
lens of Michael Marder’s claim that the vegetal 'in its very being […] accomplishes a lived 
destruction of metaphysics' (MM 11), entitling the second chapter of Plant-Thinking, ‘The 
Body of the Plant; or, The Destruction of the Metaphysical Paradigm', vindicates such a role 
68 
 
for the vegetal. Furthermore, its heteronomous mode of being means that it cannot be 
isolated from alterity. Its plasticity and continuous adaptation to external conditions means 
that for the vegetal there can be no ideal being. Through using an woodland setting, Hardy 
makes use of the complex spatial relationships between vegetal beings and the human 
inhabitants of the wood to entangle the vegetal in the plot. Through the representation of 
evolutionary and ecological relationships, I argue that Hardy’s resulting ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ 
(W 48) embeds imperfection and suggests that life itself is the result of such relationships.  
 
Hardy’s plot interweaves the worlds of vegetal and human. Entanglements between the 
vegetal and human worlds complicate and problematise notions of linear causality and 
agency, and embed unintended and unforeseen long-term consequences. In this novel, 
relationships are key and reading these through Marder’s vegetal ontology, Bennett’s 
assemblages, and Barad’s intra-actions, highlights how these all foreground relationships 
and therefore challenge a metaphysical idealist notion of perfection. Through the use of the 
arboreal as the main representative of the vegetal, Hardy embeds a long-term stability. 
However, that is not to say that this also embeds immutability. On the contrary, Hardy 
represents the trees and other vegetal species as in a state of continuous vigorous growth 
and change and, as such, their vitality dominates that of humanity. Through blurring the 
boundaries between vegetal and human, not least in the title of the novel, Hardy denies 
human pre-eminence and highlights the material continuties between lifeforms.   
 
There are some similarities between the characters and themes of The Return of the Native 
and The Woodlanders, namely that both are about the return of a native. In The Return of 
the Native, it is the returning native who is dominated by the intellect. In The Woodlanders 
the intellectual character is the outsider Fitzpiers, and it is through his intrusive impact on the 
’woodlanders’ that Hardy problematises both the intellect and his idealist metaphysics. Hardy 
also denigrates him through his failure to understand the vegetal world, and in this respect, 
pointedly contrasts him to the native, Giles Winterborne’s economic and sympathetic 
69 
 
relationship to the trees, his knowledge of the arboreal, and his vegetal-like characteristics. 
The returning native, Grace Melbury, poised between these two suitors, must choose 
between the two sides of her own nature: intellectual and cultural interiority, or her native 
rootedness located in materiality and externality. 
 
The remainder of this chapter is divided into five parts. In Part I, I consider how Hardy 
juxtaposes two different ways in which the connectivity of the world is revealed: the 
phenomenal and the metaphysical. I argue that, by centring the metaphysical perspective 
within an unfavoured character, he undermines that view in favour of a material 
understanding. I continue to explore Hardy’s notion of material connectivity in Part II through 
examining his presentation of the close ontological similarities between all beings, 
predicated on materiality and exteriority. I also consider how he demotes the imagination by 
highlighting its illusory, contingent, and anthropocentric nature. In Part III, I consider Hardy’s 
representation of the woodland setting as the location of energy and change, and how this 
enables it to dominate humanity and problematise human notions of ownership and 
hierarchy. In Parts IV and V, I go on to explore the complexity that Hardy embeds in the 
phenomenal world. In Part IV, I examine how he uses the complex networks of the vegetal to 
illustrate his web of entanglements between humans and non-humans. I assert that these 
webs problematise straightforward chains of causality and metaphysical notions of oneness. 
I also show how Hardy uses these networks to symbolically block the metaphorical realm of 
ideas. Finally, in Part V, I discuss Hardy’s evolutionary ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ in which all 
beings impact on other beings. I argue that Hardy creates a world in which life is the 
resulting imperfections, refuting philosophical idealist notions of an underlying perfection and 
foregrounding the importance of relationships. For Hardy, there is no perfection and his 
characters have to learn to live in a world of constraints. 
 
 
70 
 
I. Metaphysical and Phenomenal Realities  
 
In The Woodlanders Hardy uses a metaphorical web as his key organising principle. He 
suggests that the ostensibly ‘isolated’ and ‘self-contained’ lives of the ‘woodlanders’ are part 
of a larger system in which ‘their lonely courses formed no detached design at all, but were 
part of the pattern in the great web of human doings then weaving in both hemispheres from 
the White Sea to Cape Horn' (W 20). Such a comment reflects his remark in the Life, from 
the period in which he was at work on The Woodlanders, that 'the human race to be shown 
as one great network or tissue, which quivers in every part when one point is shaken, like a 
spider's web if touched'.62 Although he retrospectively relates this comment to his later work, 
The Dynasts (1904-08), I contend that it is highly indicative of the ‘great web’ of The 
Woodlanders.  
 
One of the important questions for me is where Hardy locates this ‘web’: in an underlying 
metaphysical reality or in the world of the phenomenal? At the time of writing The 
Woodlanders, Hardy seems to have been reading and reflecting upon an abstract reality 
behind the phenomenal. After his comment in the Life on humanity as a ‘network’ or ‘tissue’, 
he continues: ‘the Realities to be the true realities of life, hitherto called abstractions. The old 
material realities to be placed behind the former’.63 This implies the recognition of an 
underlying metaphysical ‘reality’. In January 1887, the month before he completed The 
Woodlanders, in a comment on landscape paintings, he writes that ‘I want to see the deeper 
reality underlying the scenic, the expression of what are sometimes called abstract 
imaginings’.64 Again, Hardy seems to acknowledge an underlying metaphysic. Then in 
February 1887, shortly after completing the novel, he reinforces this understanding, writing 
that ‘the material is not the real – only the visible, the real being invisible optically. That it is 
 
62 Hardy, Life, p.183 (entry dated 4th March 1886). 
63 Hardy, Life, p.183 (entry dated 4th March 1886). 
64 Hardy, Life, p.192 (entry dated January 1887). 
71 
 
because we are in a somnambulistic hallucination that we think the real to be what we see 
as real’.65  
 
If we read The Woodlanders against this background, Hardy’s ‘web’ might quite justifiably be 
recognised as located in the metaphysical. However, I argue that the novel should be read, 
at least in part, as more an exploration of the relationship between an underlying 
metaphysical reality and the phenomenal material world than a portrayal of the influence of 
an invisible connecting force. However, if there is any weighing up of these perspectives, 
through locating the philosophical perspective in the outsider character of Fitzpiers and 
representing the world of the material as dominant, energetic, and complex, Hardy 
undermines the importance of the metaphysical.  
 
Hardy centres the philosophical point of view in the character of Fitzpiers who is ‘primarily 
[…] an idealist’ (W 120). I read him as a character who enables Hardy to examine how 
metaphysical ideas hold up in the world of the phenomenal. Lennart A Bjork suggests that in 
Fitzpiers, Hardy ‘allows a generally not very attractive character to be an authorial 
spokesman’.66 Fitzpiers is indeed unlikeable, which serves to undermine the metaphysical 
mindset that he espouses. But can we categorise him as ‘authorial spokesman’? We would 
perhaps do well to heed Hardy’s own warning, published in an essay the year after The 
Woodlanders:  
a writer whose story is not a tract in disguise has as his main object that of 
characterising the people of his little world. A philosophy that appears between 
the inverted commas of a dialogue may, with propriety, be as full of holes as a 
sieve if the person or persons who advance it gain any reality of humanity 
thereby.67  
 
 
65 Hardy, Life, p.192 (entry dated 13th February 1887). 
66 Lennart A Bjork (ed) The Literary Notebooks of Thomas Hardy, Volume 1 (New York, New York University 
Press, 1985), p.261. 
67 Thomas Hardy, ‘The Profitable Reading of Fiction’, Forum, March 1888, 57-70 (repr. in Thomas Hardy, The 
Return of the Native, (Norton & Co., 2nd edition, 2006)), p.394. 
72 
 
For Hardy, what is important is characterisation, not a consistent philosophy. The temptation 
to brand Fitzpiers as representing a particular philosophical perspective, let alone be an 
‘authorial spokesman’, should therefore be tempered by an awareness of the exigencies of 
characterisation.  
 
As well as making him unlikeable, Hardy undermines Fitzpiers through his status as an 
intellectual and an outsider. In describing his wide variety of intellectual interests – alchemy, 
poesy, astrology, astronomy, German literature, and metaphysics (W 111) - rather than 
praising this breadth of curiosity, Hardy constructs him as a dilettante. As an intellectual and 
outsider, he is alienated from the woodland and judged as strange by the woodlanders. 
Grace views him as ‘a tropical plant in a hedgerow, a nucleus of advanced ideas and 
practices which had nothing in common with the life around' (W 46). The exoticness of 
vegetation is reinforced by his own sense of himself as 'a different species from the people 
who are working in that yard' (W 161). The reader’s antagonism towards him is further 
exacerbated through his competition for Grace’s affections with the favoured woodlander, 
Winterborne. In contrast to the steadfast Winterborne, Fitzpiers views Grace as an 
amusement to while away his time at Little Hintock and he even admits to, in the past, 
having had many ‘infatuations at the same time’ (W 189). 
 
Hardy further problematises Fitzpiers’s philosophical outlook through his division of the world 
into subjects and objects. Fitzpiers states that 'there's only Me and Not Me in the whole 
world' (W 45).68 He illustrates this in his contention that 'human love is a subjective thing […] 
it is joy accompanied by an idea which we project against any suitable object', going on to 
state that 'I am in love with something in my own head, and no thing-in-itself outside it at all' 
(W 106).69 As well as reinforcing his flippant attitude to Grace, Fitzpiers here also illustrates 
 
68 According to this edition of The Woodlanders, this is an allusion to the idealism or transcendentalism of Kant 
and Spinoza. (See also the following footnote.) 
69 Hardy himself explicitly links this point to Spinoza. 
73 
 
something of Schopenhauer’s understanding of love as a subjective delusion (see p.64-5). 
Although Hardy recognises that this is a characteristic of humanity rather than solely of 
Fitzpiers, through presenting an awareness of it through Fitzpiers, and by Fitzpiers using 
these ideas to comment specifically on Grace, Hardy extends his vilification of this character 
and undermines his perspective.   
 
Fitzpiers’s subject/object viewpoint is further problematised through the alternative point of 
view of the ‘woodlander’ characters of Winterborne and Marty South. Approaching this 
through the work of Karen Barad, Fitzpiers’ view of the world is one in which there are gaps 
between ‘human and nonhuman, subject and object, mind and body’ (KB 185). In contrast, 
Winterborne and Marty’s viewpoint, presented through their understanding of the vegetal, is 
non-objectifying. They perceive through the ‘conjuror's own point of view' rather than that of 
the 'spectator', the ‘conjurer’ seemingly Nature itself. Their understanding is a 'clear gaze' (W 
297-8) rather than filtered through a subjectivity, and much closer to Barad’s 
conceptualisation of the relationship between knowing and being than is Fitzpiers’. 
 
Barad asserts that ‘knowing is a matter of part of the world making itself intelligible to 
another part […] we don’t obtain knowledge by standing outside the world; we know because 
we are of the world’ (KB 185, emphasis in original). The trees are ‘intelligible’ to Winterborne 
and Marty as they too are part of that same world. For Barad, ‘knowing is a matter of 
differential responsiveness […] to what matters’ (KB 149) and for Winterborne and Marty it is 
necessary to their living to understand the state of the trees. Their reading of the 'alphabet' 
of Nature, is through the touch of twigs or the sound of wind through the boughs. They can 
learn the state of health of a tree through its external appearance and from its upper twigs 
how far the roots have grown downwards (W 298). Their knowledge is of the tree’s being 
and this non-objectifying viewpoint is rooted in a natural experiential understanding of the 
material. In contrast, Fitzpiers’ understanding remains filtered through the subjective mind: 
74 
 
once he knows that 'the trees, the hills, the leaves, the grass' are owned by Mrs Charmond 
they become, to him, 'endowed and quickened with a subtle light' (W 176).  
 
But there is further significance in Winterborne and Marty’s viewpoint being presented 
through their understanding of the vegetal. Marder identifies the vegetal itself as a challenge 
to an objectifying perspective. He contends that the plant, bereft of interiority and self-
identity, and with a heteronomous relationship to alterity, has a non-objectifying relationship 
to the world and that ‘learning from plants is in the first instance unlearning the objectifying 
approach to the world’ (MM 71). Reading Hardy’s vegetal through this lens means that the 
setting itself is a challenge to Fitzpiers’ relationship to alterity. In this respect, his alienation 
from the woodland is appropriate whereas Winterborne and Marty’s perception is vegetal-
like. 
 
II: 
The Material Connections Between All Beings 
 
Hardy further confronts the metaphysical perspective through his emphasis on externality 
and materiality. For Marder, the vegetal’s ‘absolute exteriority’ (alongside its heteronomy) 
means that it is a ‘living reversal of metaphysical values’ (MM 55-56, emphasis in original). I 
argue that Hardy uses his vegetal setting to extend his problematisation of Fitzpiers’ idealist 
philosophy by foregrounding externality and a corresponding lack of interiority. As George 
Levine suggests, in The Woodlanders ‘Hardy’s own scepticism about philosophical thought’ 
is subjected ‘to the hard force of the material world’.70  
 
As well as the vegetal, Hardy positions humanity within this material world. Although the 
vegetal is only materiality or externality, by placing humanity within a similar material realm, 
 
70 George Levine, ‘The Woodlanders and the Darwinian Grotesque’, in Thomas Hardy Reappraised: Essays in 
Honour of Michael Millgate, ed. by Keith Wilson (Toronto, University of Toronto Press, 2006), pp.174-198 
(p.190/1). 
75 
 
Hardy purports a similarity between vegetal and human. Such a similarity is not simply that 
of symbol or analogy, but ontological and material, as recognised by William A. Cohen and 
John Heaney.71 Heaney refers to Hardy's presentation of a resemblance between species as 
a 'blurring of boundaries' and an 'ontological continuity'. Although I concur with his idea of 
'ontological continuity', I take issue with his claim that Hardy’s anthropomorphisation of the 
trees, rather than diminishing humanity, works to elevate the organic realm to the human 
level.72 I argue that rather than elevating the vegetal to the human level, Hardy denies 
hierarchical superiority per se and equalises vegetal and human.  
 
It is partially through descriptions of their externality that Hardy characterises Marty and 
Winterborne as vegetal-like. Marty's hair is 'chestnut' and to Barber Percomb (who wants it 
to create a hairpiece for Mrs Charmond) she is merely this tree-like hair; everything else is 'a 
blurred mass' (W 10-11). After she has cut it off, Winterborne views her head as 'an apple 
upon a gatepost' (W 21) and, to herself, she is a 'stubby poll', resembling a pollarded tree (W 
37).  
 
Like Clym Yeobright of The Return of the Native, Winterborne is vegetal-like in his economic 
dependence upon seasonal trades and the necessary hetero-temporality is reflected in his 
appearance: 
He looked and smelt like Autumn's very brother, his face being sunburnt to 
wheat-colour, his eyes blue as corn-flowers, his sleeves and leggings dyed with 
fruit stains, his hands clammy with the sweet juice of apples, his hat sprinkled 
with pips (W 185). 
 
His staining is due to his apple trade, but Hardy’s description of the colouration of his face 
and eyes further link him to the vegetal. Even his name links him to seasonality: 
 
71 William A Cohen, ‘Arborealities: The Tactile Ecology of Hardy’s Woodlanders’, Interdisciplinary Studies in the 
Long Nineteenth Century, 19 (2014); John Heaney, ‘Arthur Schopenhauer, Evolution, and Ecology in Thomas 
Hardy’s The Woodlanders’, Nineteenth-Century Literature, 71(4) (2017), 516-545. 
72 Heaney, p.519-527. (This is part of Heaney’s argument that Hardy’s vegetal, ‘ontological continuity’ and 
evolutionary paradigm is Schopenhaurian which I consider more fully later in this chapter.) 
76 
 
‘Winterborne’ being indicative of streams that flow only during the winter months.73 When he 
is later described as ‘the fruit-god and the wood-god in alternation’ (W 249), Hardy not only 
reinforces his vegetal hetero-temporality through reference to his two seasonally-enforced 
trades, but gestures towards the pagan blurring of boundaries between human and nature, 
reinforcing the notion of Winterborne as materially vegetal.  
 
Hardy extends Winterborne’s materiality, and concomitant lack of internality, when he 
describes him climbing Mr South’s elm tree to cut down branches. Enclosed in tree spatiality, 
and in response to Grace telling him that she is breaking off their engagement, Winterborne 
climbs higher, 'cutting himself off more and more from all intercourse', eventually becoming 
an 'unconscious figure' with an 'enfeebled voice', who remains 'motionless and silent'. Those 
features that make him exclusively human or animal: consciousness, verbal facility, and 
movement, are all diminished as he climbs further away from humanity and becomes 
increasingly embedded in the materiality of the tree. His exteriority and curtailing of mind is 
emphasised by his subsequent decision that he 'would not allow this incident to affect his 
outer conduct' (W 85-87). 
 
Hardy further ties together vegetal and human through his language choices. When Hardy 
describes Winterborne as he sat upon a bough and ‘placed his elbow in a fork, and rested 
his head upon his hand' (W 86), he is not only enclosed by the tree's spatiality, but the 
language also intimately links them. His elbow is in a ‘fork’ and this not only physically allies 
him to tree spatiality, such arboreal morphology has earlier been itself described as an 
‘elbow’: when Grace and her father walk in the woods, Hardy describes their progress as 
'elbowed old elms and ashes with great forks', the syntax here obscuring whether 'elbowed' 
is a verb describing their progress or a noun describing the shape of the tree (W 48). If the 
 
73 Hardy would have been aware of this derivation of the word ‘winterborne’. He was born and spent much of 
his life in close proximity to the series of villages in Dorset that are named after these streams: Winterbourne 
Monkton, Winterbourne St Martin, and Winterbourne Abbas to name but a few. 
77 
 
second is assumed, this suggests that Winterborne’s elbow is in a tree's elbow. The fact that 
he rests his head upon his hand also gestures towards Winterborne’s mind being
