burdensome. When he finally drops out of the tree, it 'seemed to shiver, then to heave a 
sigh', suggesting that it is losing a material part of itself (W 97).  
 
In The Woodlanders, humans are not only tree-like, but trees are human-like, but I find this 
converse analogy more problematic. Hardy’s characterisation of humans as tree-like is 
based upon a common materiality. However, the idea of trees being human-like is built upon 
shakier ground, as it necessitates trees possessing mind. 
 
Hardy himself foregrounds the illusory nature of the imagination. When the young women of 
Hintock woods partake in rituals on Midsummer Eve that derive from the pagan, Hardy notes 
that the 'imagination could trace amid the trunks and boughs swarthy faces and funereal 
figures' (W 129-130). Such imaginary perception is contingent upon the state of the individual 
mind. When separated from all familiar contact, alone in Winterborne's rudimentary shelter 
on a stormy night, Grace had 'never before been so struck with the devilry of a gusty night in 
a wood', and she imagines a low swaying bough about to 'smite the roof in the manner of a 
gigantic hand smiting the mouth of an adversary' (W 277). Likewise, feeling guilty after 
having knocked Fitzpiers off his horse, Mr Melbury sees tree trunks and large branches 'in 
the forms of sentinels, gigantic candelabra, pikes, halberds, lances, and whatever else the 
fancy chose to make of them' (W 230). Anna Burton asserts that in this second passage 
Hardy pleads with the observer to imaginatively read the space and that the imagination is 
necessary to read this landscape.74 However, I interpret Hardy as suggesting here that it is 
the anthropomorphisation of the arboreal that is fanciful. 
 
Hardy contrasts these illusions with Winterborne and Marty's understanding of the woods:     
 
74 Anna Burton, Trees in Nineteenth-Century English Fiction: The Silvicultural Novel (London, Routledge, 2021), 
p.152-3.  
78 
 
They had been possessed of its finer mysteries as of commonplace knowledge; 
had been able to read its hieroglyphs as ordinary writing; to them the sights and 
sounds of night, winter, wind, storm, amid those dense boughs, which had to 
Grace a touch of the uncanny, and even of the supernatural, were simply 
occurrences whose origin, continuance, and laws they foreknew (W 297-8).  
 
Winterborne and Marty demystify notions of an imaginative 'uncanny' or 'supernatural' world, 
reading the woodland space through ‘commonplace knowledge' and straightforward events. 
Moreover, they can de-cipher it in terms of ‘hieroglyphs’: pictorial rather than representative 
writing, in which appearance is meaningful without the necessity for any further interpretative 
act. Drawing on Marder’s notion that vegetal spatiality is its self-expression or even its 
language (see p.47), this suggests that Winterborne and Marty can understand such vegetal 
spatial communication. Furthermore, Barad’s notion that ‘knowing is a direct material 
engagement […] with the world’ bears some similarity to Marder’s point here, both 
suggesting that representational modes of interpretation are unnecessary (KB 379). 
 
Barad’s ‘direct material engagement’ and Marder’s concept of vegetal language, is 
anticipated in Winterborne and Marty’s ability to not just understand vegetal language but to 
share this communication. Their ‘intelligent intercourse with Nature’ suggests a two-way 
process. As they 'could speak in a tongue that nobody else knew […] the tongue of the trees 
and fruits and flowers themselves' (W, 297-8), they not only articulate the meaning of the 
vegetal or Nature itself, but also illustrate Marder’s notion that the vegetal participates in 
language. 
 
Yet Hardy does not fall into the trap of denying the otherness of the vegetal. Theirs is an 
‘intercourse’ with Nature, and the ‘tongue’ they speak during this communication belongs to 
that of the vegetal. It does not require any further meaning to be imposed by the human 
mind. Hardy perhaps makes a similar point in his description of mosses.  After comparing 
them to other beings: 'moss like little fir-trees, like plush, like malachite stars', he seems to 
79 
 
correct his anthropomorphism in his affirmation that they are 'like nothing on earth except 
moss' (W 280). Their otherness becomes absolute.  
 
Hardy suggests additional close ontological connections between human and vegetal. He 
describes a sympathetic relationship between tree and human that is predicated on the 
recognition of a material connection. In describing Winterborne’s ability to promote tree 
growth, Hardy explains that:  
He had a marvellous power of making trees grow. Although he would seem to 
shovel in the earth quite carelessly there was a sort of sympathy between 
himself and the fir, oak, or beech […] so that the roots took hold of the soil in a 
few days [ …] Winterborne's fingers were endowed with a gentle conjurer's 
touch in spreading the roots of each little tree, resulting in a sort of caress under 
which the delicate fibres all laid themselves out in their proper directions for 
growth (W 58-9).  
 
Winterborne’s ‘marvellous power’ could be understood as based upon his knowledge and 
experience. However, the notion of ‘sympathy’ seems deeper than this. Andrew Radford 
argues that it indicates a ‘quasi-mystical correspondence between woodlander and trees’ 
and that Winterborne has ‘a magical possession of the vibrant physical milieu’.75 Phillip 
Mallett suggests that in this ‘sympathy’ ‘the spiritual connection between humankind and 
nature, pushed out by materialist philosophy, is allowed to edge its way back’.76 While 
recognising how Hardy’s use of words such as ‘conjurer’ might imply the esoteric, I offer a 
more materialist reading, that calls into service Jane Bennett’s notion of ‘onto-sympathy’. 
Drawing upon Thoreau, Bergson, and Darwin, Bennett considers a ‘recognition’, ‘affinity’, or 
sense of ‘kinship’ between humans and plants to which she, tentatively, gives the name 
‘onto-sympathy’. Such a connection is, for Bennett, engendered through an ‘overlap between 
materialities of humans and plants’.77 As Hardy’s sympathy seems to move in two directions: 
 
75 Andrew Radford, ‘Dethroning the High Priest of Nature in The Woodlanders’, in A Companion to Thomas 
Hardy, ed. by Keith Wilson (Chichester, Wiley-Blackwell, 2009), pp.313-327 (p.316-320). 
76 Mallett, p.27. 
77 Jane Bennett, ‘Vegetal Life and Onto-Sympathy’, in Entangled Worlds: Religion, Science, and New 
Materialism, ed. by Catherine Keller and Mary-Jane Rubenstein (New York, Fordham University Press, 2017) 
pp.89-110 (p.90-94). 
80 
 
the sympathy is ‘between’ man and roots, and the roots respond to Winterborne, rather than 
Winterborne alone being in possession of some form of magical understanding, I consider 
that a material ontological connection, recognised by both man and tree, is a convincing and 
less anthropocentric interpretation.   
 
This materialist interpretation is perhaps validated through Hardy’s representation of another 
close material relationship: the material transfers across bodies. In excusing the presence of 
a slug on Grace’s plate at Winterborne's party, Robert Creedle makes two rationalisations. 
Firstly, that her winter greens 'twas his native home', and secondly, of slugs and caterpillars, 
that 'they were born on cabbage, and they've lived on cabbage, so they must be made of 
cabbage' (W 72). It is helpful to read this ontological connection through Stacey Alaimo's 
notion of material transcorporeality. For Alaimo, transcorporeality conceptualises the 
continuous exchanges of substances across bodies, such that all beings, including 
humanity, are intermeshed.78 These interchanges of material suggest that the slug or 
caterpillar is corporeally linked, even the same as, the material upon which it feeds. 
Moreover, through vocalising this through Creedle, Winterborne’s rustic servant, Hardy 
undercuts any potential interpretation of this as expressing an underlying metaphysical unity. 
Rather, it is a straightforward explanation of an exchange of substances. Extended to the 
wider novel, through Winterborne’s ‘sympathy’ and Creedle’s transcorporeality, Hardy 
establishes a radical ontological continuity between all beings. Read in this way, humans are 
not like trees, but are the same as trees.   
 
III: 
Adaptability and Vitality in the Woods  
 
As well as denying human superiority through material continuity, Hardy elevates the vegetal 
by making it the locus of vitality. He suggests that it has a greater energy, adaptability, and 
 
78 Stacey Alaimo, Bodily Natures: Science, Environment, and the Material Self (Bloomington, Indiana University 
Press, 2010). 
81 
 
ability to change, than humanity. Burton suggests that the trees in The Woodlanders are 
'almost ahistorical in their seemingly perennial immutability'.79 However, although writing 
about Hardy's Under the Greenwood Tree, Elizabeth Carolyn Miller’s understanding of 
arboreal instability is, I think, a better reflection of how Hardy uses the vegetal background in 
The Woodlanders. Miller asserts that Hardy calls attention 'to the way that trees in particular, 
and the natural world more generally, are erroneously represented as a stable, unchanging 
backdrop against which the drama of human life plays out'.80 In The Woodlanders, Hardy’s 
vegetal shows the continuous change of growth, regeneration, competition, adaptation, and 
decay.  
 
Helen Macdonald writes that winter woods are ‘places of interpolated timeframes’ that are 
'able to show you the last five hours, the last five days, the last five centuries, all at once'.81 
Hardy’s vegetal shows such a variety of temporalities of growth. Short-term growth is shown 
in the ‘arms of ivy […] pushing in with such force at the eaves as to lift from their supports the 
shelves that were fixed there' (W 23-4).  This almost intentional vigorousness seems redolent 
of Schopenhauer’s ‘blind irresistible urge’ of the will (WWR1 275). Longer-term growth is 
illustrated when Grace becomes lost in the woods. As she had not ‘traversed this the wildest 
part of the wood since her childhood […] the transformation of outlines had been great’ (W 
216). Trees have been felled or blown down and what were small bushes are now larger. 
And then there are the changes that indicate much longer-term growth. Although these might 
be impossible to perceive from the limited temporal perspective of humanity, they are present 
in Hardy’s descriptions of disfigurements that are the result of long-term competition for light 
and resources: ‘branches disfigured with wounds resulting from their mutual rubbings and 
 
79 Burton, p.151. 
80 Elizabeth Carolyn Miller, ‘Dendrography and Ecological Realism’, Victorian Studies, 58(4), 2016, 696-718 
(p.709). 
81 Helen Macdonald, Vesper Flights (London, Jonathan Cape, 2020), p.72. 
82 
 
blows. It was the struggle between these neighbours’ (W 280). Even the references to 
‘elbows’ and ‘forks’ allude to growth processes that occur over decades or centuries.    
 
Hardy uses the vegetal’s ability to retain the past in its spatiality to comment on human social 
relationships, the vegetal expressing a greater adaptability than humanity. Suspecting illicit 
meetings between Fitzpiers and his wife, Timothy Tangs sets a mantrap for Fitzpiers. He 
places the trap between 'a pair of young oaks which, rooted in contiguity, grew apart upward, 
forming a V-shaped opening between; and, being backed up by bushes, left this as the only 
course for a foot-passenger'. Although it is Grace who wends this way and becomes the 
trap's victim, it is the spatial kinship architecture between these 'sister oaks' that provides the 
space for this to happen (W 318-20). But as well as providing spatial agency, these oaks 
provide a contrast to Fitzpiers and Grace’s relationship. In what follows I draw on Ruth 
Livesey's analysis of two inosculated beeches in George Eliot's Adam Bede (1859). She 
argues that through the eponymous Adam paying attention to 'a curious large beech' which 
may be 'two trees wedded together', Eliot 'breaks open' the narrative and provides a 'counter-
narrative' that is 'an alternative to the inequality and violence that characterises human 
generative desire'.82 Hardy's two oaks, however, 'grew apart upward' although remain 'in 
contiguity' at their roots. Their youth is retained in the continued connection at their base 
while adaptation to conditions has enabled them to grow into more beneficial spaces. 
Although Fitzpiers and Grace's marriage continues to tie them together (at the roots, as it 
were), underscored by the fact that while Tang is placing his trap, Grace is re-reading 
through the marriage ceremony, unlike the oaks, they cannot adjust to their new situation. 
The vegetal can grow and adapt to circumstances; the social law continues to tie Fitzpiers 
and Grace together.   
 
 
82 Ruth Livesey, ‘Arboreal Thinking: George Eliot and the Matter of Life in Adam Bede’, in Anticipatory 
Materialisms in Literature and Philosophy: 1790-1930, ed. by Jo Carruthers, Nour Dakkak, Rebecca Spence 
(Cham, Palgrave MacMillan, 2019), pp.131-151 (p.145-6). 
83 
 
Similarly, Hardy uses seasonal change to comment on the linear trajectory of human 
relationships. Later that same evening, Fitzpiers and Grace wander into the ‘great green 
vase’ of an ‘encircled glade’. In describing this glade, Hardy calls attention to vegetal 
cyclicity: it ‘was just that transient period in the May month when beech-trees have suddenly 
unfolded large limp young leaves’ (W 323). But, of course, in the autumn these leaves will 
die, the glade will be less green and the boughs, currently heavy with leaves, will rebound. 
Hardy suggests, however, that Grace and Fitzpiers' commitment will not similarly cyclically 
rebound but lead onwards to unhappiness.   
 
Through these changes, linear and seasonal, Hardy suggests that the vegetal has a greater 
vitality, enabling it to dominate humanity. Spring growth is described as a sudden bursting 
forth of energy, when the ‘rush of sap in the veins of the trees could almost be heard’. Such 
a 'sign of great undertakings on the part of vegetable nature' is contrasted to people who ‘are 
not undertaking much themselves’ (W 121). The new growth of leaves in the spring ‘cast 
green shades, which disagreed with the complexion of the girls’ (W 129). The boughs enable 
raindrops to ruin the seed plots in the gardens that the woodlanders have ‘snipped out of the 
woodland’ (W 7). Even the road has had to be diverted around the growth of a large 
chestnut-tree (W 227). In considering the modifiers she uses to describe matter: 'vibrant, 
vital, energetic, lively, quivering, vibratory, evanescent, and effluescent’ (JB 112), Bennett 
critically reflects upon 'the default grammar of agency […] that assigns activity to people and 
passivity to things' (JB 119). In The Woodlanders, Hardy reverses this, locating activity in the 
vegetal and passivity in the human.  
 
The vegetal dominates Mrs Charmond, the owner of much of Hintock woods, and her house:  
To describe it as standing in a hollow would not express the situation of the 
manor house; it stood in a hole […] The ashlar of the walls, where not 
overgrown with ivy and other creepers, was coated with lichen of every shade 
[…] below the plinth, it merged in moss. Above the house to the back was a 
dense plantation, the roots of whose trees were above the level of the chimneys 
[…] The situation of the house, prejudicial to humanity, was a stimulus to 
84 
 
vegetation, on which account an endless shearing of the heavy-armed ivy went 
on, and a continual lopping of trees and shrubs […] its hollow site was an ocular 
reminder by its unfitness for modern lives, of the fragility to which these have 
declined […] It was vegetable nature's own home' (W 53).  
 
The vegetal usurps the supposedly superior position of humanity. The location of the house 
is ‘prejudicial to humanity’ and yet is a 'stimulus to vegetation', and covered with ivy, 
creepers, lichen, and moss. The vegetal’s continuous growth requires constant ‘shearing’ 
and ‘lopping’: this is Francis Hallé’s vegetal that must continue to grow or else will die.83 
Moreover, the contour of the land means that even the roots of the trees are above the level 
of the house: the house is not merely lower, it is 'in a hole'. Through this positioning, 
alongside the necessity for the continual cutting back of the vegetation, Hardy makes 
comment on the insufficiency and conceit of the notion of human ownership of the vegetal. 
Mrs Charmond might own the trees, but she can neither control nor dominate them.  
 
Hardy extends such problematic notions of ownership through Mrs Charmond being neither 
part of the community, nor having any understanding of the very trees that she “owns”. As 
Grammer Oliver states, 'she's the wrong sort of woman for Hintock – hardly knowing a beech 
from a woak' (W 222).84 For Mrs Charmond, 'this place oppresses me' (W 55) and her lack of 
rootedness is epitomised by the fact that after her death, as she had only a 'life-interest' in 
the estate, the relative to whom it passes wants 'to blot out every vestige of her' (W 301). 
Appropriately, even her body is not returned to Hintock and its soil. Unlike the sessile rooted 
vegetal, rather than her home, this is ‘vegetable nature’s own home’. Its relationship to 
alterity is also different to that of Mrs Charmond. Marder asserts that as plants strive towards 
exteriority, rather than returning to an inward self, they ‘let the other pass through them 
without detracting from the other’s alterity’, thereby showing an ‘inherent respect for alterity’ 
(MM 42). Mrs Charmond’s attempt at ownership is conversely an attempt to control alterity. 
 
 
83 Hallé, p.97. 
84 ‘woak’ is dialect for oak. 
85 
 
Hardy suggests that the only way for humanity to dominate the vegetal is to be destructive, 
albeit a necessity in an economy that is dependent upon these practices. But he represents 
such destruction as humanising the trees, with a felled tree reduced to mere human stature: 
‘the weakest idler that passed could now set foot on marks formerly made in the upper forks 
by the shoes of adventurous climbers only’ (W 94). In preparation for this felling, the cutting 
of lower twigs and moss is 'comparable to the "little toilette" of the executioner's victim', and 
as the bark is subsequently removed, he asserts that 'if a fine product of vegetable nature 
could ever be said to look ridiculous it was the case now, when the oak stood naked-legged, 
and as if ashamed' (W 122). The ravaged trees are reduced to a humiliating vulnerability.   
  
In writing about the position of Hintock House, Hardy suggests that modern humanity is 
fragile and has 'declined' since the house was built, suggesting human enervation compared 
to the vigorous vegetal. Such enfeeblement is emphasised by the fact that there are few 
kinship relationships in the contemporary world of this novel. There are only two parent/child 
relationships (Marty South and Mr South, Grace Melbury and her parents) but these offspring 
are now adults and there are no young children. There are also no siblings; these exist only 
in the world of the vegetal with the ‘sister oaks’ and Winterborne characterised as ‘autumn’s 
very brother’. Conversely, there are frequent references to the ancestors of characters, 
reinforcing humanity as being in decline. Even the aged trees have a vitality denied to aging 
humans. As Fitzpiers goes to visit Mrs Charmond, the 'stout-trunked trees, that never rocked 
their stems in the fiercest gale', are still green even though they are 'wrinkled like an old 
crone's face and antlered with dead branches'. In contrast, after weeping, the ageing Mrs 
Charmond turns away from Fitzpiers as she fears the impact of him seeing her face (W 177-
78).  
 
 
 
86 
 
IV: 
The Complex and Entangled Networks of Vegetal and Human 
 
A further aspect of the vegetal that Hardy presses into service in his problematisation of the 
metaphysical perspective is its complex spatiality. For Marder, the modular growth of plants, 
that makes them a ‘collective being’ or an ‘assemblage of multiplicities’ (MM 85) with no 
central control, contributes to the vegetal being a living critique of a metaphysical oneness. 
This network-like ontology is reflected in Hardy’s ‘network of boughs’ (W 212) that is both 
that of individual trees and the wood as a whole. This vegetal network reminds us of his 
metaphorical web: remember that in the Life, he explicitly allies networks and his analogic 
web. But Hardy’s vegetal network is no mere metaphor. The participation and entanglement 
of the vegetal in the plot physically manifests his web and in vegetal and human interaction, 
reveals the complexity of the phenomenal world.    
 
Although Hardy frames his web as focused on 'human doings', he represents all beings as 
participants, including the vegetal. Darwin’s own use of the image of the web, in which all 
species are entangled in an ‘inextricable web of affinities’ cannot be discounted as an 
influence, particularly as Hardy knew his work well.85 Gillian Beer points out that through 
such imagery, Darwin emphasises the ‘complex interdependency’ of relationships in the 
natural world: ‘plants and animals, most remote in the scale of nature are bound together by 
a web of complex relations’.86 Hardy recognises the evolutionary and ecological relationships 
between different species and kingdoms. However, in terms of the plot, his web of 
entanglements between vegetal and human, as well as being economic, are predicated 
upon vegetal spatiality. I have already considered how the spatiality of the ‘sister oaks’ is 
implicated in the plot. But when on Midsummer eve, Fitzpiers 'captured' Grace as she 'flew 
 
85 Darwin, Charles, On The Origin of Species, Chapter XIII. 
86 Gillian Beer, Darwin’s Plots: Evolutionary Narrative in Darwin, George Eliot and Nineteenth-Century Fiction 
(Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 2000), p.158-9. Quotation taken from Darwin, Origin of Species, 
Chapter III. 
87 
 
round the fatal bush where the undergrowth narrowed to a gorge' (W 133), it is the wider 
spatial network of the vegetal that creates this opportunity. 
 
The vegetal network even imposes network-like behaviour onto humanity. After arguing with 
Mrs Charmond over Fitzpiers, Grace becomes lost in the woods. As a result, she ‘zigzagged 
about’ and struck out ‘hither and thither in random courses’ with ‘no ideas as to direction at 
all’, whereas ‘the ground she had covered if in a straight line, must inevitably have taken her 
out of the wood’. Embedded in vegetal spatiality, Grace herself is forced into network-like 
patterns. There is even a sense that vegetal time is driving her. As she walks a track that is 
‘screened and roofed in from the outer world’ by the ‘network of boughs’, it ‘led her slowly on 
till in time she had left the larger trees behind her’ (W 211-17). Reading this through 
Marder’s notion that vegetal growth embeds time itself (MM 104), Grace seems to be 
conscripted into the temporality of the vegetal. 
 
Pushing this analogy a little further, it can also be read as a critique of the philosophy of 
Fitzpiers and his non-vegetal-like being and behaviour. Grace’s agitated state, the cause of 
her network-like ramblings, is, at least partially, caused by Fitzpiers’s lack of 
straightforwardness in relation to herself and Mrs Charmond. In this sense, he is the cause 
of her becoming more vegetal-like. Moreover, his own duplicity suggests that his behaviour 
is at odds with his avowed interest in pure reasoning. Although in his own garden the 
vegetation is clipped to create straight paths with designated areas for specific species (W 
101-2), symbolic of his idealist thinking, his actual behaviour, and that that he foists on 
others, seems closer to the entangled networks of the woodland vegetal. His purity of 
thinking cannot be maintained in the world of the phenomenal. 
 
The entanglements between human and vegetal contribute to a web of causalities that are 
more complex than those Hardy creates in The Return of the Native. As an example, one of 
the most complex of these causal webs connects the mature elm tree outside Mr South's 
88 
 
house, Mr South's life, his death, and the events that follow. From the unwell Mr South’s 
point of view, the elm tree and he are entangled in a malevolent relationship. He thinks that 
the elm, that 'sprouted up when he was born on purpose to rule him’, will now kill him by 
falling onto his house. It does, in fact, kill him but not in the way that he expects. Due to its 
impact on his mental stability, the tree is cut down and it is through being now able to see a 
'vacant patch of sky in place of the branched column so familiar to his gaze', that Mr South 
‘paralysed by amazement', dies (W 93-4).  
 
If this appears to be a straightforward chain of events, when the wider plot is considered, it 
appears more web-like than linear. Through the loss of the tree and consequent death of Mr 
South, the houses held on life-leases, the last life being that of Mr South, revert to Mrs 
Charmond, the estate owner. Winterborne's house being one of these, this results in him 
losing it, and his lack of shelter leads onwards to his death. Winterborne's death leads to 
Grace's eventual reunion with her estranged husband, Fitzpiers, who is Mrs Charmond's 
former lover. Fitzpiers is the very doctor who had ordered Winterborne to cut down the tree 
to save Mr South's life, but that unintentionally kills him. It is useful to read this through 
Bennett’s conceptualisation of the entangled bodies of the assemblage, the entanglement 
here being between the elm tree, Mr South, Winterborne, Grace, Mrs Charmond, life-leases, 
and Fitzpiers, as well as other characters. For Bennett, the properties that emerge from such 
an assemblage mean that 'efficient causality […] will be impossibly rare' (JB 32). Indeed, 
Hardy’s assemblage is a multifactorial and labyrinthine network of beings and events, in 
which causality is impossible to disentangle and from which emerge unforeseen and 
unintended consequences.   
 
If these complex causalities are read through Marder’s vegetal, it suggests a further critique 
of metaphysics. Marder contends that due to the plant’s infinitely growing and modular body, 
it has no beginning or ending, and it therefore becomes a 'concrete refutation of the 
metaphysical yearning for the pure origins and first causes of life' (MM 86). Hardy’s most 
89 
 
complex causal webs, like Marder’s vegetal, lack straightforward originating causes. Marty's 
letter to Fitzpiers informing him that she was the original owner of the hair that Mrs 
Charmond claims as her own, and that was intended as an act of revenge, reaches further 
backwards and forwards in time than Marty intends or realises. We can even question if 
there is a beginning to such a chain of events. Does it originate in that fact that her father's 
illness causes her to sell her hair to earn money? Or further back when the copyholds were 
exchanged for life-leases? Or even further, in the germination of the elm sapling? These 
events also lack any conclusion and the consequences become distanced and unforeseen. 
Under the influence of Marty’s letter, Fitzpiers parts from Mrs Charmond just before her 
death and therefore escapes 'being dragged into the inquiry'. Hardy suggests that this letter 
is therefore ‘the tiny instrument of a cause deep in nature' (W 301). As well as highlighting 
the impossibility of teasing out any straightforward beginning or ending of such chains of 
causality, Hardy here surely gestures back to his musings on the nature of reality. But the 
phenomenality of his web, however complex it is, indicates that such connections lie within 
the world of the concrete rather than in a hidden underlying reality.  
 
Hardy also uses the networked spatiality of the vegetal as a challenge to light itself. In the 
previous chapter I explored how in The Return of the Native Hardy uses the darkness of 
vegetal materiality to defeat the light of the sun, the biblical creation, and reason. In The 
Woodlanders, Hardy once again uses the vegetal to challenge the light of the sun as well as 
the symbolic light of reason and metaphysics. However, rather than the darkness of the 
material vegetal, in The Woodlanders, it is the vegetal’s spatial network that Hardy 
emphasises as the challenge: in the summer, the density of the network blocks the sun with 
‘scarce a crevice existing by which a ray could get down to the ground’ (W 268). 
 
Hardy suggests that exposure to the sky is antagonistic to the woodlanders. When the trees 
are in full leaf, glimpses of the sky through the canopy are malevolent: there are ‘strange 
faces and figures […] that had somehow wandered into the canopied obscurity’ and ‘cloven 
90 
 
tongues’ on the tips of the bough (W 268-9). When Winterborne’s house is destroyed a 
‘piece of sky […] where his walls and chimneys had formerly raised their outlines' (W 168) is 
revealed, and this is part of the chain of events that leads onwards to his death. Likewise, 
the death of Mr South is caused by replacing the tree with a view of the sky and, 
significantly, it is Fitzpiers, the metaphysician, who had ordered the tree to be cut down.  
 
In blocking the light of the sun and view of the sky, the woodland also blocks the realm of 
ideas and the location of metaphysics. Through Fitzpiers’ house being positioned on a 
hillside above the woods, he is symbolically nearer to this realm and above the woods that 
block it. This is reinforced by the 'solitary point of light' that radiates from his house 
symbolising the single-minded certainty of reason. His activities are also out of temporal 
sync with the natural world with the light produced by his nocturnal studying 'dissociated' 
from the 'normal sequences' in Little Hintock that are the result of the 'regular terrestrial roll' 
(W 44). Furthermore, if Fitzpiers is connected to the realm of ideas in the sky, Winterborne 
is, in contrast, ‘arisen out of the earth’ (W 186), the source of his humanity being materiality. 
Marder argues that the 'metaphysical project [is] bent on leaving the darkness of mere life 
behind' but that this ‘undercuts the conditions of its own existence’ (MM 177). From such a 
perspective, it is this from which Fitzpiers is detached, undercutting his ‘own existence’ and 
contributing to his downfall. Or perhaps this is better expressed as his fall to earth as he 
eventually realises that he must adapt to living in the material world.  
 
V: 
Evolution, Imperfection and the ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ 
 
What must inevitably follow from the complex, networked and entangled causalities that 
Hardy portrays in The Woodlanders, is a lack of perfection for the individual. In fact, Beer 
suggests that ‘maladaptation […] obsesses Hardy’.87 Hardy indicates that this theme will be 
 
87 Beer, p.232. 
91 
 
key on the very first page of The Woodlanders: 'the contrast of what is with what might be' 
(W 5). Ostensibly this comment is used to compare the deserted highway to its potential 
busyness. However, I interpret it as having a wider significance in this novel and an issue 
with which Hardy's characters are forced to tussle. Such a perspective is the antithesis of 
Fitzpiers’ idealist thinking, believing ‘that behind the imperfect lay the perfect’ (W 120). It is 
perhaps significant that the light from Fitzpiers’s window, symbolising the light of reason, 
‘blinked as the tree-twigs waved to and fro before its beams’ (W 44), materiality intruding 
upon his reasoning. Hardy’s complex phenomenal world thwarts the realisation of perfection.   
 
Hardy manifests this imperfection in a vegetal image: 
Here, as everywhere, the Unfulfilled Intention, which makes life what it is, was 
as obvious as it could be among the depraved crowds of a city slum. The leaf 
was deformed, the curve was crippled, the taper was interrupted; the lichen ate 
the vigour of the stalk, and the ivy slowly strangled to death the promising 
sapling (W 48).  
 
Hardy illustrates here the effect of the competition amongst beings for resources. Leaves 
compete for light, as do branches which become ‘crippled’ and their growth ‘interrupted’. 
The lichen, striving to survive, feeds on the stalk, and the ivy, using the sapling to grow 
towards the light, strangles it.  
 
Although it is widely accepted that Hardy’s ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ is a statement related to 
evolutionary competitiveness, interpretations vary. Beer reads it as an expression of 
Darwin’s emphasis on ‘imperfect adaptation’ referring to his comment in Origin of Species 
that ‘natural selection will not produce absolute perfection’.88 Levine also interprets it as 
Darwinian, framing it within a discussion of the contrast in The Woodlanders between the 
material world and the world conceived by consciousness. For Levine, Hardy sees the 
woods ‘against an ideal’: ‘there are […] the material woods […] but there is too the human 
dream of the fullest fruition […] In the ideal woods, each organism comes to full growth 
 
88 Beer, p.232. Quotation taken from Darwin, Origin of Species, Chapter VI. 
92 
 
without encroaching on its neighbours’.89 In Hardy’s phenomenal world of complex causal 
entanglements, with all beings impacting on one another, whether through evolutionary 
competition or through other forms of impact, there can be no perfect being.  
 
Although my own interpretation of Hardy’s ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ is Darwinian, Heaney 
contends that it is closer to Schopenhauer’s evolutionary paradigm, and he certainly offers 
some compelling evidence. He suggests that Hardy chose to use vegetal nature as the 
principal metaphor for evolution because in Schopenhauer's evolutionary system 'vegetable 
nature is allotted a central significance'.90 This is indeed so, but I argue that more important 
is the fact that due to the vegetal being able to preserve visual evidence of earlier growth 
processes, and therefore competitive pressures, Hardy is enabled to use it to illustrate 
evolutionary effects. Heaney also draws attention to the energy and vitality that Hardy 
embeds within the vegetal. For Heaney, this is evidence of Schopenhauerian evolutionary 
processes that recognise the driving force as being the internal force of the metaphysical 
will, as opposed to Darwin’s mechanistic model in which it is external forces, namely a 
changing environment, that provides the impetus for change.91 I would concur that Hardy 
presents the vegetal as vigorous and energetic and recognise the temptation to interpret this 
as Schopenhauerian.   
 
However, I do take issue with Heaney’s argument that Hardy’s use of the word ‘Intention’ 
implies a Schopenhauerian ‘teleological understanding of the life process’ that is evoked in 
his, albeit non-conscious, purposive will.92 While being aware of Schopenhauer’s rather 
tortuous rationalisation of the will as teleologically purposive, as well as recognising that 
Hardy’s use of the term ‘Intention’ could perfectly logically be interpreted as similarly 
 
89 Levine, ‘The Woodlanders and the Darwinian Grotesque’, in Thomas Hardy Reappraised: Essays in Honour of 
Michael Millgate, ed. by Keith Wilson (Toronto, University of Toronto Press, 2006), pp.174-198 (p.185). 
90 Heaney, p.520.  
91 Heaney, p.525. 
92 Heaney, p.527. 
93 
 
purposive, Hardy himself, in an admittedly much later poem, refutes a teleological purpose to 
his use of this word. In ‘A Philosophical Fantasy’ (1928), when the first cause is questioned 
as to the meaning of ‘unfulfilled intention’, it states that its creation was a ‘purposeless 
propension’ (CP 893-7, 18; 142, emphases in original). Nevertheless, Hardy’s ‘Unfulfilled 
Intention’ does suggest an incomplete or a flawed existence. 
 
Hardy’s suggestion that this ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ ‘makes life what it is’ calls to mind 
Schopenhauer’s claim that life only exists because of the contest between beings:  
everywhere in nature we see contest, struggle, and the fluctuation of victory […] 
Every grade of the will’s objectification fights for the matter, the space, and the 
time of another […] This contest can be followed through the whole of nature; 
indeed only through it does nature exist (WWR1 146-7, my emphasis). 
 
Reading Hardy’s description from such a Schopenhauerian perspective suggests that the 
very existence of the trees, lichen, and ivy is due to evolutionary competition.  A later image 
of ‘trees close together, wrestling for existence’ (W 280) likewise evokes the struggle as 
emblematic of existence.  
 
Hardy’s competitive diverse wood is predicated on relationships and by reading this through 
Schopenhauer and Barad I establish a connection between their relationship-based insights. 
Schopenhauer asserts that life exists because of the contest. Barad contends that matter 
‘entails entanglements – that is its very nature’ (KB 160) and ‘individuals emerge through 
and as part of their entangled intra-relating’ (KB ix). Both Schopenhauer and Barad’s 
conceptualisations are predicated on relationships rather than individual existence, and I 
argue that this is reflective of Hardy’s point of view, not just amongst the contest in the 
natural world, but throughout the whole web, including humanity. His ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ is 
‘as everywhere’ and ‘was as obvious as it could be among the depraved crowds of a city 
slum’.93 Life is everything impacting on everything else.  
 
93 Hardy makes a similar point in his poem, ‘In a Wood’ which I consider in detail in the following chapter. 
94 
 
 
If Hardy’s wood is re-read from the perspective of life as a whole rather through than the lens 
of the individual being, instead of being a world of imperfections, it becomes a biodiverse, 
and therefore successful and thriving, ecological community that exists because of the 
competition for resources. Andrew Radford notes that Hardy’s description of the wood 
immediately before the ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ passage is one of 'pastoral prettiness' only to 
degenerate into 'myriad disturbing features'.94 This interpretation is of course 
anthropomorphic – nature itself is neither pretty nor grotesque. But Hardy’s description of the 
wood immediately preceding and including the ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ passage is of a thriving 
community that includes a variety of species: moss, elm, ash, fungi, lichen, and ivy (W 48). 
In a later description of the woods Hardy includes ‘yellow fungi […] and tall fungi with more 
stem than stool’, and ‘rotting stumps’ of dead trees ‘rising from their mossy setting like black 
teeth from green gums’ (W 280). Although this could also be anthropocentrically interpreted 
as a grotesque image, it is one in which decay is part of a burgeoning ecosystem.  
 
Through the vegetal Hardy also evokes a continuity in life. An old and damaged beech tree 
nevertheless remains alive even though it has ‘great pocket holes in its sides, where 
branches had been removed in past times’ (W 280). The loss of branches is not catastrophic 
for the tree. As plants are modular, and the modules 'not internally related to each other, let 
alone to the individual vegetal being they comprise, and that are mutually indifferent to each 
other's existence' (MM 132), the loss of a module is merely that. The individual tree can 
survive and the ability to survive such loss attests to the beech’s success rather than its 
failure. Hardy goes on to suggest that the dead branches were ‘scattered about like 
ichthyosauri in a museum’ (W 280). Rather than an image of extinction and death, I read this 
as indicative of the long-term continuousness of life. However, Fitzpiers interprets this 
damage differently. Discontented with his marriage to Grace and having begun a secret 
 
94 Andrew Radford, Thomas Hardy and the Survivals of Time (Aldershot, Ashgate Publishing Ltd., 2003), p.148. 
95 
 
affair with Mrs Charmond, when he observes a large limb that had snapped off a beech-tree, 
he exclaims, 'my good God […] This is life!' (W 198), relating it to his own self-inflicted 
diminished life. His focus is upon the individual life whereas Hardy’s beech trees show the 
survival of life as a continuum. They also embody a further challenge to his idealist views, 
showing that there is no perfect unchanging tree but continuous adaptation to conditions. 
This is further shown in the apple trees in Winterborne’s garden: ‘even now retaining the 
crippled slant to north-east given them by the great November gale of 1824' (W 167). 
 
Hardy extends such a whole life perspective with its inevitable and necessary imperfections 
to that of humanity. In the Life, he notes that 'a perception of the FAILURE of THINGS to be 
what they are meant to be, lends them, in place of the intended interest, a new and greater 
interest of an unintended kind' (emphasis in original).95 He illustrates this in The 
Woodlanders (and in his other novels) with the story or ‘greater interest’ predicated on 
imperfect relationships. In fact, at the point in the novel where Hardy considers the 
‘Unfulfilled Intention’, Grace, promised to Winterborne, has just become entranced by 
Fitzpiers, and so begins the triangular plot, that results in the 'unfulfilled intentions' of many 
characters. Grace even, like Hardy, allies the imperfection in the human situation to that in 
the vegetal. As she watches Fitzpiers ride to an illicit meeting with Mrs Charmond, she 
observes acorns and chestnuts and notes that 'some kernels were unsound as her own 
situation, and she wondered if there were one world in the universe where the fruit had no 
worm, and marriage no sorrow' (W 184).  
 
In The Woodlanders, all the main characters need to learn to accept the constraints of the 
world, even Fitzpiers. Like everyone and everything else, he must experience the ‘Unfulfilled 
Intention’. In considering marrying Grace, and comparing himself with the woodlanders, 
Fitzpiers surmises that 'the secret of happiness lay in limiting the aspirations; these men's 
 
95 Hardy, Life, p.127 (entry dated 1st January 1879). 
96 
 
thoughts were conterminous with the margin of the Hintock woodlands, and why should not 
his be likewise limited'. He realises that being limited by circumstances might not, in fact, be 
unfulfilling. Such a limitation is even situated within the materiality of the woodland, dispelling 
his antipathy to the material. As he later ‘dreamed and mused till his consciousness seemed 
to occupy the whole space of the woodland round, so little was there of jarring sight or sound 
to hinder perfect mental unity with the sentiment of the place’ (W 123-6), Hardy suggests 
that Fitzpiers’ consciousness becomes bounded within the material confinement of the 
woods. By the end of the novel, he even leaves behind his single-minded philosophical 
beliefs and becomes a 'practical man' (W 310).  
 
Grace also changes as the novel progresses, increasingly accepting constraints and a 
vegetal-like rootedness. Hardy uses vegetal imagery to describe these changes, reinforcing 
the vegetal as location of change as well as showing how the woodlands and woodlanders 
revitalise her. He describes her earlier education as 'cultivation' and refers to her being 
forced into unnatural ways as being 'mentally trained' (W 40) and 'tilled into foreignness of 
view' (W 100). But as she comes to realise the value of the woodland community, she tells 
her father that 'cultivation has only brought me inconveniences and troubles' (W 201) and 
comes to crave even the 'defects and inconveniences' of the 'homely sylvan life' (W 212). Her 
responses to the woodlander, Winterborne, change as she empathetically also becomes 
vegetal as 'her heart rose from its late sadness like a released bough, her senses revelled in 
the sudden lapse back to Nature unadorned' (W 186). Moreover, when she wants to escape 
from her estranged husband Fitzpiers, her childhood ‘Daphnean instinct' (W 268) is revived 
and she transforms into a tree to escape his amorous intentions. 
  
By the end of the novel, both Grace and the reader must accept an imperfect resolution, 
settling for Hardy’s flawed existence. As Beer writes, in Hardy’s world of imperfection 'the 
97 
 
shadow plots of achievement and joy […] can never fully manifest themselves'.96 At the end 
of the novel, Grace’s situation is somewhat reminiscent of Clym’s at the end of The Return of 
the Native. Clym leaves behind his renewed pastoral life and returns to intellectual pursuit. 
Likewise, with Winterborne's death and her renewed acquaintance with Fitzpiers, for Grace 
the woods once again become 'uninteresting'. She retreats indoors and becomes 'quite a 
student, reading more than she had done since her marriage' (W 309). Fitzpiers' influence is 
again in the ascendant and Winterborne's diminished. If Winterborne is viewed as tree-like 
and read through Marder's contention that 'the loss of every tree […] equals the passing 
away of meaning bound up with the particular spatial extension of that very tree’ (MM 75-6), 
then Winterborne's death means that this vegetal model is lost to Grace. With Winterborne’s 
death, even the two most suitably matched characters, Winterborne and Marty, cannot be 
united, and the novel ends with Winterborne's ‘true complement in the other sex’ (W 297) 
able only to tend his grave. All intentions remain unfulfilled. 
 
 
 
96 Beer, p.232. 
98 
 
Chapter Three - Life Lessons from Hardy’s Vegetal Poetics 
 
The mortal moan | Begot of sentience  
(Thomas Hardy, ‘The Aerolite’ (CP 769-70) 
I do not expect much notice will be taken of these poems: they mortify the 
human sense of self-importance by showing, or suggesting, that human beings 
are of no matter or appreciable value in this nonchalant universe.  
(Thomas Hardy, Life p.408-9) 
 
 
In this chapter I explore the vegetal poetics of Hardy and argue that he purports the vegetal 
mode of being as in many ways preferable to that of humanity. Rather than emphasising the 
connections between vegetal and human, as he does in the two novels I have explored, it is 
the vegetal as ‘other’ that is prominent in his poetry, and he implies that its different ontology 
might offer us lessons in how to live. He recognises that the vegetal has its own vitality, 
agency, and voice, and does not require humanity in the form of poetic speakers to impose 
these attributes onto it. In his problematising of consciousness, Hardy seems to admire, or 
envy, the vegetal’s lack of a separate internal sphere which, he suggests, is what causes 
humanity so much trouble and heartache: existential worries, the ability to exist outside the 
temporal present, and the awareness of burdensome feelings. In recognising a continuing 
need to account for meaning and purpose, he relocates it to life itself. Life is meaning, the 
vegetal indicates, and this is one of the lessons that it offers humanity: that meaning is not 
limited to that that can be found in human existence, in the mind, and in the memory. In 
‘Yell’ham Wood’s Story’ he perhaps expresses this in its most straightforward terms: ‘we 
come to live, and are called to die’ (CP 298-9, l.10).  
 
I consider seven of Hardy’s poems in detail, and my selection is based upon those in which 
Hardy uses the vegetal to show that there is an alternative, and perhaps better, mode of 
existence than that of humankind; a mode of being in which material existence is sufficient 
and the human mind framed as a problematic and complicating addition. I begin with three 
poems that Hardy explicitly links to The Woodlanders and that extend some of the ideas 
99 
 
from that novel. I commence by exploring ‘In a Wood’, published in Hardy’s first volume of 
poetry (CP 64-5). I argue that in this poem Hardy’s speaker fails to find a Wordsworthian-
type solace in vegetal nature and instead is faced with a disillusioning material embodiment 
of evolutionary competition. I go on to consider ‘The Pine Planters (Marty South’s Reverie)’ 
in which this character from The Woodlanders is given space in which to voice her own 
concerns (CP 271-3). However, my reading of this poem problematises how she attempts to 
impose her voice and an existentialist perspective onto the trees she plants. I contend that 
Hardy resists such appropriation by interpolating the vegetal’s own ontological agency. I next 
explore how, in ‘Yell’ham-Wood’s Story’, Hardy returns to a similar idea to that of the 
‘Unfulfilled Intention’ of The Woodlanders (CP 298-9). In this poem, Hardy’s representation 
of the vegetal shows that organic transience means that although life must always be 
‘thwarted’, meaning is in material existence. Following this, I examine ‘A Backward Spring’, 
recognising Hardy’s appreciative presentation of vegetal hetero-temporality (CP 498). I 
argue that he contrasts this with the human ability, through memory, to exist non-
contemporaneously, framing this as detrimental to human equanimity. My fifth poem, ‘At the 
Royal Academy’ is, I assert, a critique of how landscape paintings falsely represent 
permanence (CP 627-8). I examine how Hardy suggests that rather than avoiding the fact of 
mortality, humanity should reconcile itself to transience and change. Finally, I consider two 
poems about consciousness, ‘Before Life and After’ and ‘The Aerolite’ (CP 277; 769-70). 
Although these poems do not comment on the vegetal, Hardy’s direct questioning of the 
benefits of consciousness in them, continues the argument that I have established, as does 
his suggestion that non-consciousness is both normal and a preferable mode of being.  
 
Free from the exigencies of narrative form, in his poetry Hardy engages with his ideas and 
philosophy more directly. The fact that most of the poems were written after he had stopped 
writing novels, his first volume of poetry being published in 1898 and his final novel, The 
Well-Beloved, in 1897, suggests that many of his poems represent his mature thinking. 
However, this must be tempered by the knowledge that Hardy wrote poetry throughout the 
100 
 
time when he was writing and publishing his novels and so we cannot assume that the 
drafting or final versions of many of the earliest published poems in particular, post-date the 
novels. Indeed, many do not. The suggestion in his novels that vegetal ontology makes it a 
better or preferable mode of being is more prominent and directly addressed in his poetry. 
Furthermore, with no necessity for characterisation, his vegetal poetics reveal a much less 
anthropocentric worldview. 
 
Hardy wrote over nine hundred poems that were published in eight volumes between 1898 
and 1928 and his subjects range widely from war poems, relationships and lost loves, to 
poems about place, landscape and nature. Although he chose to publish some of his poetry 
in thematic groupings within the volumes, in his Apology in Late Lyrics and Earlier with Many 
Other Verses (1922) he claims that many juxtapositions are unintended, and in his 
Introductory Note in Winter Words (1928) states that ‘no harmonious philosophy is attempted 
in these pages – or in any bygone pages of mine, for that matter’ (CP 559; 834). As I have 
outlined in previous chapters, scholars have long recognised Hardy’s exploration of the 
disadvantages of consciousness in his poems but, with little scholarship on his vegetal 
poetics, few have investigated how Hardy juxtaposes the vegetal and human. However, 
through analysing his poems that foreground the world of vegetation, I establish that a 
cohesive vegetal poetics is evident. 
 
I contend that in his vegetal poetics, Hardy writes against Wordsworthian conceptions of 
nature as a place of consolation and rejuvenation. I am not the first to read Hardy’s poetics 
in this way. As part of his argument about Hardy’s modernity, John Paul Riquelme suggests 
that Hardy’s ‘Nature’s Questioning’ ‘specifically rejects the consolation that Wordsworth’s 
speaker experiences’ in the ‘Immortality Ode’.97 Addressing the same Hardy poem, Linda 
Shires asserts that the participants are ‘non-Wordsworthian’ and show that there is ‘no 
 
97 John Paul Riquelme, ‘The Modernity of Thomas Hardy’s Poetry’, in The Cambridge Companion to Thomas 
Hardy, ed. by Dale Kramer (Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 1999), pp.204-223 (p.208). 
101 
 
reason to link man and nature’.98 I too argue that Hardy writes against Wordsworth’s notion 
of nature as restorative, such as is to be found in poems such as ‘Lines Written a Few Miles 
Above Tintern Abbey, on Revisiting the Banks of the Wye during a Tour, 13 July 1798’ 
(hereafter ‘Tintern Abbey’). In this poem, nature provides ‘tranquil restoration’ (l.31), 
encouraging his ‘purest thoughts’ (l.110) and informing ‘The mind that is within us’ (ll.126-
7).99 In contrast, for Hardy, nature is not a consolatory resource or a place of escape and 
renewal. The speaker of ‘In a Wood’, retreating into Nature, finds ‘no grace […] Taught me of 
trees’, instead, to him, they are ‘combatants all!’ (CP 64-5, ll.33-34; l.20). 
 
There are nearly fifty years between Wordsworth’s final published poems and Hardy’s first. 
Jennifer Gribble suggests that Hardy’s ‘historical moment’ is where ‘the wisdom of 
Wordsworth’s Nature confronts the impassive indifference of Darwin’s’.100 
Wordsworth’s poems in some ways respond to the visual picturesque that preceded them, 
whereas Hardy’s poetics are influenced by the scientific, particularly evolutionary, 
developments of his own age and so the divergence between his and Wordsworth’s vegetal 
poetics cannot be unexpected. That is not to say that Wordsworth’s representation of nature 
negates or devalues its materiality. Wordsworth recognised the value of lifeforms other than 
the human and his representation of human responses to nature, although mediated through 
the imagination, are emotional and felt, and therefore also corporeal. My point is that Hardy’s 
late nineteenth-century poems work to decentre humanity and in his framing of nature and 
other lifeforms, he attempts to minimise the voice of the intercepting mind; such that Keats 
critically terms Wordsworth’s ‘egotistical sublime’. However, for Hardy, a search for meaning 
persists in a world that lacks any consolatory transcendent significance and in which nature 
 
98 Linda Shires, ‘Matter, Consciousness, and the Human in Wessex Poems’, Studies in English Literature, 55(4) 
(2015), 899-924 (p.904). 
99 William Wordsworth, ‘Lines Written a Few Miles above Tintern Abbey, on Revisiting the Banks of the Wye 
during a Tour, 13 July 1798’, in Romanticism, An Anthology, ed. by Duncan Wu (Oxford, Blackwell Publishers 
Ltd, 1994), pp.240-244.  
100 Gribble, p.527. 
102 
 
is indifferent to humanity. Hardy finds this meaning in a material existence that spreads 
across all lifeforms, including the vegetal. 
 
Scholars have diverged between reading Hardy’s poetry as linked to the Victorian age, to the 
fin de siècle, or to an early modernism. This is hardly surprising when we consider the period 
over which Hardy was writing poetry which stretches from about the 1860s to the late 1920s. 
I argue that his poetry certainly possesses elements of a nascent post-humanism that looks 
forwards to modern perspectives. Riquelme recognises Hardy’s non-anthropocentric poetics 
as developing towards modernism and locates this in his refusal to humanise nature through 
personification, the lack of human comfort in his elegies, and his use of ambiguity and 
multiple viewpoints.101 Likewise, in her article on Hardy’s Wessex Poems, Linda Shires sees 
his use of images in association with the poems’ text as ‘witnessing a retreating humanness’, 
one that is ‘not hierarchically superior to the rest of the universe’.102 In her revised edition of 
Victorian Poetry: Poetry, Poetics and Politics (2019), Isabel Armstrong locates his poetry 
within an ‘alternative fin de siècle’, reading in it ‘the ache of modernism’ (making use of 
Hardy’s own term from Tess of the d’Urbervilles). She sees this in his Darwinism and in his 
representation of a world where ‘human agency is not at the centre’.103 I extend these 
arguments by showing how by turning his perspective to the vegetal and stressing its 
inherent value, he moves our viewpoint away from the human and encourages us to read in 
a less anthropocentric mode. Jane Bennett suggests that it is a habit that we view matter as 
dull and passive (JB vii), and I suggest that reading Hardy’s poetry challenges this manner of 
thinking and invites us to recognise the vibrancy of the vegetal. When, on the publication of 
Moments of Vision and Miscellaneous Verses (1917), he writes that ‘I do not expect much 
notice will be taken of these poems: they mortify the human sense of self-importance by 
 
101 Riquelme, p.206; p.208; p.217. 
102 Shires, p.920. 
103 Isabel Armstrong, Victorian Poetry: Poetry, Poetics and Politics, 2nd edn. (London, Routledge, 2019), p.461. 
103 
 
showing, or suggesting, that human beings are of no matter or appreciable value in this 
nonchalant universe’, I suggest that he recognises the challenge.104 
 
Hardy’s poems can seem almost obsessed by time, particularly its physical and emotional 
effects upon humankind: he is the ‘time-torn man’ of his own poetic phrasing.105 But in his 
vegetal poems he contrasts human and vegetal experiences of temporality. In ‘A Backward 
Spring’ Hardy recognises the vegetal’s integral temporal relationship with alterity and he 
contrasts this to the problems caused by the non-contemporality of humanity. Through the 
human capacity for memory and imagination, we can become non-contemporaneous with 
the present and experience what Marder terms ‘temporal dissociation’ (MM 104). In contrast, 
the hetero-temporal vegetal has no separate interiority in which to disassociate from its 
environment. Hardy’s vegetal poetics evoke the alienation caused to humans by such non-
contemporality. In ‘Freed the Fret of Thinking’, he suggests that humans would be better off 
if they could be ‘Measuring but the moment’, as then ‘Loosed from wrings of reason, | We 
might blow like flowers’ and ‘Sense of Time-wrought treason | Would not then be ours’ (CP 
753-4, l.12; ll.15-18). For Hardy, vegetal reliance upon the temporality of alterity is beneficial 
rather than restrictive.  
 
In terms of form, I argue that Hardy structures some of his vegetal poems to allow vegetal 
vitality to dominate at the very centre of them. For Marder, the way that plants grow in two 
directions at once: upwards towards the light and downwards towards the darkness, means 
that the middle is the location of their vitality (MM 63). In ‘In a Wood’, it is at the centre of the 
poem that vegetal competition is at its most combative. In ‘The Pine Planters’, the seed is 
positioned where it can grow up and down through the poem. In ‘At the Royal Academy’, the 
sap is placed where it can rise up through the poem. In all three poems, this vegetal vitality, 
 
104 Hardy, Life, p.408-9 (entry dated 1917). 
105 The phrase ‘time-torn man’ is taken from Hardy’s poem ‘A Broken Appointment’, published in Poems of the 
Past and Present (1901). 
104 
 
located as it is in the middle of the poem, reinforces the efficacy of vegetal agency, and 
drives human agency to the outer edges of the poems. 
 
I: 
The Competitive World of ‘In a Wood’ 
 
‘In a Wood’ was published in Hardy’s first volume of poetry, Wessex Poems and Other 
Verses (1898) (CP 64-5). In this poem, Hardy presents an enervated speaker who has left 
his urban environment in a quest to find peace within the natural world of the wood. 
However, contrary to expectations, what he discovers is an intensely competitive 
environment with vegetal species battling for the available space and light. By the end of the 
poem the speaker returns with relief to his fellow humans. 
 
Hardy dates this poem ‘1887:1896’. It seems that 1896 alludes to the year the poem was 
written. 1887 was the publication date of The Woodlanders and Hardy subtitles the poem: 
‘See “The Woodlanders”’. There are certainly features in this poem that speak directly to The 
Woodlanders, not least the representation of woodland and competition between vegetal 
species. In this poem, Hardy undermines the assumption, such as Wordsworth presents in 
‘Tintern Abbey’, that nature will carry out ‘psychological work’ for ‘alienated urban man’.106 
Instead, Hardy emphasises the vitality of vegetal competition. The outsider character, like 
Fitzpiers in The Woodlanders, is again in conflict with the reality of the material world.  
 
Hardy uses his speaker to illustrate the movement from an idealised imaginary Romantic 
view of the natural world to one of material and evolutionary reality. In the first stanza he 
expresses the speaker’s bewilderment, asking why the trees ‘mar sweet comradeship’ by 
‘blighting’ (ll.6-7) each other. In stanza two the reader comes to understand his 
misapprehension. ‘City-opprest’ (l.10), he has projected his own needs onto the wood, 
 
106 Jonathan Bate, The Song of the Earth (London, Picador, 2000), p.146. 
105 
 
assuming that the wood would be ‘as to a nest’, and ‘Dreaming that sylvan peace | Offered 
the harrowed ease’ (ll.12-14). The expectation of the urban speaker reflects the language of 
Wordsworth’s ‘Tintern Abbey’, in which in ‘lonely rooms, and mid the din | Of towns and 
cities’ (ll.26-27), the speaker recalls nature and the ‘sylvan Wye’ (l.57) which provides him 
with ‘tranquil restoration’ (l.31).107 Through using such similar language, Hardy suggests that 
the speaker has been influenced by a romanticised and anthropocentric view of nature’s 
beneficence. However, unlike for Wordsworth’s speaker, in stanzas three and four of Hardy’s 
poem, the speaker’s expectations are dashed by the material reality of vegetal competition. 
Through Hardy’s representation of the moment of the speaker entering the wood, there is no 
distancing through memory. The reader experiences his shock at the contrast between his 
romanticised expectation and material actuality, reflective of a nineteenth-century adjustment 
to the devastating reality of an evolutionary understanding of the natural world.  
 
In the very middle of the poem, Hardy’s description of the trees’ competition for space 
creates a claustrophobic and crowded atmosphere: 
But, having entered in,  
 
     Great growths and small 
 
Show them to men akin –  
 
     Combatants all! 
 
Sycamore shoulders oak, 
 
Bines the slim sapling yoke, 
 
Ivy-spun halters choke 
 
     Elms stout and tall.  (ll.17-24) 
 
The speaker who hopes for ‘comradeship’ is faced, in the line at the very epicentre of the 
poem, with ‘Combatants all!’. The destructive active verbs that describe the vegetal: 
‘shoulders’, ‘yoke’ and ‘choke’, represent aggressive competition, and the syntax that 
reduces grammar to subject – verb - object: ‘sycamore shoulders oak’ and ‘ivy-spun halters 
choke | elms’, creates a forceful forward-moving rhythm. Bennett suggests that we tend to 
view the world as consisting of ‘active human subjects who confront passive objects’ (JB 
 
107 Wu, p.241. 
106 
 
xiv). In this poem Hardy counters this idea by locating the action in the vegetal. The centre of 
the poem is dominated by the vegetal’s competitive vitality, such spatiality reflecting its 
assertive take-up of space in the wood. It also symbolically reflects the central position of 
materiality in new scientific understandings of life: humanity and an anthropocentric 
perspective has lost its central position and been pushed to the edges of the poem. 
 
In the final stanza of the poem, the speaker returns to the human world with an altered 
perspective, although not the one for which he had hoped: 
 
Since, then, no grace I find 
 
     Taught me of trees, 
 
Turn I back to my kind, 
 
     Worthy as these. 
 
There at least smiles abound, 
 
There discourse trills around, 
 
There, now and then, are found 
 
     Life-loyalties.  (ll.33-40) 
 
Rather than finding relief from ‘men’s unrest’ in the wood (l.16), the speaker is brought to the 
realisation that human relationships are, in fact, less combative than those between vegetal 
species. Humans are now as ‘worthy’ as the trees. There ‘at least smiles abound’ and 
‘discourse trills around’ with loyalties ‘now and then’. In ‘Tintern Abbey’, Wordsworth’s 
speaker discovers that his understanding of human weaknesses - the ‘evil tongues’, ‘rash 
judgements’, and the ‘dreary intercourse of daily life’ (ll.129-132) - are mollified by nature’s 
teachings, as, through nature, he is brought to realise the ‘little, nameless, unremembered 
acts | Of kindness and of love’ (ll.35-36).108 In Hardy’s poem, however, rather than being 
restored by nature, the speaker finds that he prefers humans and human relationships. 
Unlike Wordsworth’s speaker, he finds ‘no grace […] Taught me of trees’. He moves from a 
position in which the vegetal and human are ‘akin’, to one in which only humanity is now ‘my 
kind’. He rejects the evolutionary position and returns to a safer world of human separation.  
 
 
108 Wu, p.241-243. 
107 
 
Yet the human relationships seen weak in comparison with those of the vegetal. Hardy uses 
energetic and vigorous verbs to describe the relationships between vegetal species. The 
vegetal species ‘shoulder’, ‘yoke’, ‘choke’, and ‘sting’, whereas the human relationships are 
diminished by modifiers: ‘at least smiles abound’, and the ‘discourse’ merely ‘trills’. Even the 
‘life-loyalties’ with which Hardy ends the poem are minimised: they are only to be found ‘now 
and then’. Moreover, these human relationships are of the mind and dwarfed by the huge 
spatial relationships of the vegetal. Hardy suggests that material spatiality is more powerful 
than the mere mind. Furthermore, it is this mind that has produced the misguided Romantic 
ideals that are undermined in this poem. This is all structurally reinforced through the weak 
human relationships being pushed to the end of the poem by the dominant spatial vitality of 
the vegetal at its centre. 
 
In describing the active and energetic vegetal, Hardy’s speaker uses the language of 
intentionality, removing this from being located exclusively within humankind. The vegetal is 
made to seem intentionally malicious: the ash is described as a ‘wych’ and its ‘Touches’ will 
‘Sting you like scorn!’ (ll.25-6). The language is also aggressive and warlike, vegetal species 
being ‘combatants’ and the ‘rival’ poplars line up in ‘rank’ (l.29-30). This could be read as 
anthropomorphic, but it is also reflective of both Marder’s vegetal ‘non-conscious 
intentionality’ (MM 153) and Schopenhauer’s ‘blind irresistible urge’ (WWR1 275) in which 
‘everywhere in nature we see contest, struggle, and the fluctuation of victory’ (WWR1 146). 
In fact, Hardy’s description of vegetal competition notably resembles that of Schopenhauer: 
‘on the banks of the Missouri one sometimes sees a mighty oak with its trunk and all its 
branches so entwined, fettered, and interlaced by a gigantic wild vine, that it must wither as if 
choked’ (WWR1 147-8). Hardy even co-opts the rain into the vegetal’s intentional 
competitive strategy. As it rains, he suggests that the trees are ‘blighting’ their competitors 
with ‘poison-drip’; the ‘poison’ sounding deliberately malevolent. The positioning of these 
drops at the end of a line in the first stanza allows them to drip down through the poem, 
further destroying any notion of benevolent relationships. The length of this line also enables 
108 
 
these drops to destroy any potential for ‘neighbourly spray’ (ll.7-8) in the following shorter 
line. Rather than mutually supportive, Hardy’s neighbourliness is indicative of rivalry.  
 
Although the speaker seems to recognise vegetal intentionality, what he fails to recognise is 
that in comparing the relationships within the wood with those within humanity, he is not 
comparing like with like. The human relationships are intra-species; the vegetal relationships 
are inter-species with Hardy listing beech, pine, sycamore, elms, ash and poplars, ivy, and 
bine climbers. These cannot be expected to demonstrate even the weak intra-species 
connections that the speaker calls ‘life-loyalties’. In How Forests Think (2013), Eduardo 
Kohn claims that the human mind works through creating generalities and categories.109 I 
suggest that it is the anthropocentric categorisation embedded in the term ‘wood’ that leads 
the speaker astray. Such a term suggests a singular concept, and perhaps camouflages the 
many-species complexity that not only is the wood but that has created (and continues to 
create) the wood. It is the speaker who alliteratively joins ‘pale beech’ and ‘pine-tree blue’ in 
one line and characterises them as ‘set in one clay’. Whereas the speaker sees the 
competitive processes as destructive, in an ecological sense the wood is successful 
because of the competition. As Schopenhauer asserts, it is only through competition that 
‘nature exist[s]’ (WWR1 147) (see p.93). If the viewpoint is changed to one that views the 
wood as a whole rather than merely individual beings and their imperfections, or the 
‘Unfulfilled Intention’ to use Hardy’s language from The Woodlanders (and his subtitle surely 
encourages the reader to do so), the success of the biodiversity in this wood can be 
appreciated. 
 
The speaker does, however, recognise that vegetal beings are processes rather than static 
things. He perceives what Karen Barad terms the process of ‘worlding’ (KB 179). Rather 
than simply recognising the snapshot of vegetal life as it is at that moment, the speaker 
 
109 Kohn, p.157. 
109 
 
perceives the processes that have created that moment. Hardy shows this altered 
perspective through using the present tense for describing vegetal competition (‘shoulders’, 
chokes’), suggesting that these are happening in the moment, whereas in reality, the 
speaker can only perceive the spatial consequence of these processes: the sycamore is now 
in the space that should be due to the oak. Hardy anticipates and poeticises Marder’s notion 
of the vegetal as time itself, and its ability to embody the past in the present, suggesting that 
the speaker can read the vegetal’s spatial language. In this sense, Hardy places the reader 
within vegetal temporality. 
 
II: 
Existential Suffering in ‘The Pine Planters (Marty South’s Reverie)’ 
 
In ‘The Pine Planters (Marty South’s Reverie)’ (hereafter ‘Pine Planters’), published in Time’s 
Laughingstocks and Other Verses (1909), Hardy again returns to The Woodlanders (CP 
271-273). Marty South from The Woodlanders is the speaker and is therefore given a voice 
in which to express her feelings; a voice that she was rather denied in the novel. However, I 
argue that in this poem she projects her own feelings onto the vegetal, therefore denying its 
own voice. Although the poem therefore has an anthropocentric perspective, Hardy inserts 
elements of vegetal subjectivity and agency, problematising the speaker’s attempt to impose 
upon it her own existentialist woes.  
 
‘Pine Planters’ revisits the episode in The Woodlanders in which Marty South helps Giles 
Winterbourne plant trees. It is divided into two parts. The poem begins with a human focus: 
South ruminating upon her unreciprocated love for Winterbourne. The second part, still 
spoken by South, is ostensibly an elegy for the plight of one pine-tree that will suffer simply 
through being alive. The first part is in ballad form with eight quatrains and a predominantly 
regular rhythm and a regular abcb or abab rhyme scheme. However, although the second 
part largely maintains this rhythm and rhyme, the stanzas of twelve lines each are longer, 
110 
 
creating a denser and more sombre mood, reflective of the coniferous plantation that the 
tree represents. These longer stanzas also embody the height of the tree, and the varying 
line lengths visually resemble its silhouette, reflecting that the focus in this second part is on 
the tree. It is this second part of the poem that is most interesting from a vegetal perspective, 
and it is this part on which I concentrate. 
 
In the first part of the poem, to indicate her love to Winterborne, South wonders ‘Shall I not 
sigh to him’ (l.25) but instead decides to ‘bear it ever’ (l.31). However, in part two, once the 
tree is planted, South projects onto it her repressed despair and hears it ‘sighing’ (l.41). In 
part one she feels like sighing but does not; in part two she appropriates the voice of the tree 
in which to articulate her sighing. She can perhaps be read as the archetypical nineteenth-
century woman who must repress her feelings and voice; indeed, this is her position in The 
Woodlanders, and she can only release them in indirect ways. It is significant that she does 
this through the tree whose voice is also dismissed by a patriarchal culture. 
 
South imposes her suffering onto a being that cannot itself suffer. For South, it is her 
imagination that causes her anguish as she thinks about Winterbourne’s love for another. 
Marder suggests that the vegetal, with its ‘freedom from the constraints of selfhood’, has an 
‘ontological freedom’. With no separate interiority, it is ‘unperturbed and indifferent’, free from 
such concerns, and he reminds us that ‘across the philosophical and literary traditions, the 
idealisation of the indifference of plants has been rampant’ (MM 132-33). Indeed, in many 
poems, Hardy seems to rather envy plants this lack of consciousness and therefore 
suffering. In ‘To a Tree in London’ (1928), Hardy suggests that the tree is satisfied with its 
life in an urban landscape because it cannot imagine existence anywhere else: it is ‘blind’ 
(CP 867-868, l.22). South’s ‘subjective interiority’ (MM 32) enables her to hide her feelings. 
She can decide to simply ‘bear it’ and ‘make no sign’ (ll.31-2), needing to displace her 
111 
 
feelings onto a being that cannot hide any such needs. Its own sighing can be only external 
and spatial.   
 
South further extends the sorrow that she imposes onto the tree to a wider plight: an 
existentialist melancholy engendered through merely being alive. The tree suffers the ‘fear | 
Of Life unreckoned’ (ll.38-39). This reflects her comment in The Woodlanders that ‘it seems 
to me […] as if they sigh because they are very sorry to begin life in earnest – just as we be’ 
(W 59). In the poem she expands this to all time as the tree will:  
[…] sigh in the morning, 
   Will sigh at noon, 
At the winter’s warning, 
   In wafts of June; (ll.45-48) 
For South, the tree voices all existentialist sorrow. However, if we read this from the 
perspective of the vegetal, as Michael Pollan asks us to do in The Botany of Desire (2001), 
we can recognise the pine-tree as gaining rather than suffering from its planting.110 Pollan 
views plants as active subjects that have (in a non-conscious and evolutionary sense) used 
humanity to create their own success. Through this lens, pine trees spread and colonise 
areas through what we might otherwise view as merely human economic gain from their use 
of timber. Through being useful to humankind, pine trees increase their chances of success. 
 
South’s anthropocentric perspective also fails to recognise the different ontology of the 
vegetal. Although South has a knowledgeable and non-objective relationship to the trees in 
The Woodlanders, in this poem her interiority overrides this. However, Hardy overcomes this 
by inserting the vegetal’s own agency and subjectivity into this poem even though it is 
ostensibly ‘Marty South’s reverie’. He calls attention to the double beginning of the life of the 
tree. Although the human planters have set the tree in place, they are merely transplanting 
 
110 Michael Pollan, The Botany of Desire (London, Bloomsbury, 2001). 
112 
 
what is already a sapling as they ‘take each tree’ (l.34). It is an anthropocentric notion to 
imagine that they are ‘Beginning’ (l.40) the life of the tree when they merely transplant the 
saplings. They cannot create vegetal life. Hardy reminds us of the real beginning of the life of 
the tree through his reference to the pine-tree ‘seed’. This he embeds in the very centre of 
part two of this poem, positioning the seed where it can grow bi-directionally upwards and 
downwards through the poem. Read through Marder, this middle position also shows vegetal 
being as denying the importance of the human planting, positioned at the top of part two of 
the poem. As such, it also symbolises South erroneous assumption that the tree can suffer 
existentialist woe, located in the mind at the top of the human body. It is only South who 
needs to ‘shun the welter | Of things without’ (ll.53-4). The tree can remain ‘unknowing | For 
whom or what’ (ll.57-8) it is there. 
 
Read through the perspective of vegetal ontology, South’s assumption that the tree is 
disadvantaged by its positioning is also an anthropocentric point of view. Being a sessile 
organism, once the human planters have decided upon its position, South sees the tree as 
condemned to ‘this bleak spot’ as it is ‘Unable to leave here’ (l.60-63). This is emphasised in 
this second part of the poem: through the repeated use of the word ‘here’, especially when 
rhymed with ‘fear’, South suggests how difficult it must be to remain in one place. It is 
instructive to return to the episode in The Woodlanders to which this poem alludes in which 
South herself is portrayed as sessile. As she stands still to support the trees while 
Winterbourne plants, her pose is tree-like with ‘her outstretched hand […] chill as a stone’ 
(W, 59). To recover, she must be mobile: ‘Mr Winterbourne, can I run down the lane and 
back to warm my feet?’ (W 59). But this reflects an animal’s need for movement, so however 
much her sessility equates her to the tree and allows her licence to transfer her feelings, she 
neglects to recognise the tree’s ontological embrace of sessility. Furthermore, conifers are 
adapted to such ‘bleak’ conditions. It is only South whose positioning, in her closeness to 
Winterborne, is the cause of woe.    
113 
 
 
Hardy suggests a further reason why the trees might be more successful than humanity 
when he compares their lifespan to that of South and Winterbourne. At the end of the poem, 
South mourns that the pine-tree will be unable to: 
 
[…] tell the story 
 
     Of us today 
 
When, halt and hoary, 
 
     We pass away.  (ll.65-68) 
 
South’s mourning is less that she and Winterborne will pass away, but that the tree will not 
pass on their story into the future. Through ending the poem with the loss of their story, 
Hardy implies its relative unimportance. The tree will outlive them, and their story will be 
forgotten. South’s appropriation of the voice of the tree to tell her story is, in the end, denied. 
 
III: 
The Meaning of Life in ‘Yell’ham-Wood’s Story’ 
 
In ‘Yell’ham-Wood’s Story’ (hereafter ‘Yell’ham-Wood’s’), published in Hardy’s third volume 
of poetry, Time’s Laughingstocks and Other Verse (1909), Hardy questions the very 
meaning of Life (I follow Hardy’s own capitalisation of ‘Life’ here) (CP 298-9). In fact, Dennis 
Taylor contends that one of the ‘great Hardyesque questions which he helps make so central 
to the later Victorian period’ is ‘what is the meaning of human life in a universe which has
