lost its moorings?’.111 Although he is specifically writing about ‘Neutral Tones’ which was 
published in Wessex Poems (1898), I would argue that this question continues to be relevant 
to Hardy’s later poetry and certainly to ‘Yell’ham Wood’s’. It is the vegetal that answers this 
question and the reply is that there is meaning in simply being alive. In ‘Pine Planters’, a 
human speaker imposes a voice on the vegetal and expects the trees to tell her story, 
whereas in ‘Yell’ham-Wood’s’ it is the vegetal’s own voice that speaks and, through its own 
story, offers this lesson to humanity.  
 
111 Dennis Taylor, ‘Hardy as a nineteenth-century poet’, in The Cambridge Companion to Thomas Hardy, ed. by 
Dale Kramer (Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 1999), pp.183-203 (p.190). 
114 
 
 
The poem begins with two different woods explaining what ‘Life’ is. In the first line, in which 
‘Coomb-Firtrees say that Life is a moan’, Hardy establishes a jaunty rhythm, and this, along 
with the single syllable words, makes this beginning seem story-like, reflecting the poem’s 
title. The affirming ‘And Clyffe-hill Clump says “Yea!”’ of the following line reinforces such a 
tone. However, the third line beginning with ‘But’ indicates that ‘Yell’ham’ will undercut this, 
and here the tone becomes more sombre. Hardy goes on to withhold Yell’ham’s own 
pronouncement for the following six lines, using this space to reveal only what it does not 
assert. It does not confirm that Life is ‘“Gray, gray’ (l.4), as Coomb-Firtrees has claimed, and 
the reader might therefore expect a more optimistic explanation of Life after the tension of 
waiting for the response.  
 
It is not until after the stanza break that Hardy reveals Yell’ham’s answer and the whole of 
stanza two is devoted to this, suggesting that it is of greater importance and complexity than 
the responses of the other two woods: 
 
It says that Life would signify 
 
     A thwarted purposing: 
That we come to live, and are called to die 
 
     Yes, that’s the thing 
 
     In fall, in spring, 
 
     That Yell’ham says:- 
“Life offers – to deny!”  (ll.8-14) 
 
Yell’ham says that Life is a ‘thwarted purposing’. Hardy goes on to rework this complex 
phrase twice. The first rephrasing clarifies that it means ‘That we come to live, and are called 
to die’, affirmed by the satisfied ‘Yes, that’s the thing’. The second clarification is that ‘“Life 
offers – to deny!”’. This bitterness of this short explanation, ending in ‘deny’, is reinforced 
through this being the final line of the poem. The potential optimism of ‘Life offers’ is cut off 
by the dash and the final ‘deny!’ is crushing.   
 
115 
 
This ‘thwarted purposing’ bears some resemblance to the ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ of The 
Woodlanders. In his ‘A Philosophical Fantasy’ (1928) (a poem I also considered in the 
previous chapter) Hardy explicitly links his ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ to the idea of ‘thwarting’ (CP 
893-7). The speaker asks the God-like first cause about ‘this unfulfilled intention’ (l.18, 
emphasis in original). He asks ‘why […] let your aim be thwarted, | Its glory be diminished, | 
Its concept stand unfinished?’ (ll.22-25), to which the first cause responds that he fails to see 
it as ‘unfulfilled intention’ (l.126) as had been ‘unconscious in my doings’ (l.128). He claims 
that his creation was of ‘purposeless propension’ and ‘not intention’ (ll.142-143, emphasis in 
original). Such a clarification removes purpose from life, and even from ‘intention’.  
 
Reading this notion in ‘A Philosophical Fantasy’ through Schopenhauer is helpful, 
particularly as Hardy’s ‘purposeless propension’ seems reflective of Schopenhauer as does 
his reference to the first cause as ‘“blind force persisting”’ (l.52). In this poem Hardy seems 
to gesture at a teleological understanding of life, albeit one that is ‘thwarted’, and yet he also 
seems to refute it, and in some ways, this is similar to Schopenhauer’s own ambiguous 
teleology. Schopenhauer asserts that ‘every individual act has a purpose or end’ and yet that 
‘willing as a whole has no end in view’. The will has an ‘absence of all aim, of all limit’; it just 
exhibits an ‘endless striving’ which ‘can always be impeded only, never fulfilled or satisfied’ 
(WWR1 164). Although the will moves towards ends, it does not foresee those ends.  
 
In returning to ‘Yell’ham Wood’s’ through such Schopenhauerian notions, Hardy’s ‘thwarted’, 
as well as his clarifications that one lives then dies, reflects Schopenhauer’s notion that 
although each individual manifestation of the will might have an end or purpose, this can 
only be transitory. Hardy’s statement at the end of stanza one, that Yell’ham says ‘Nor that 
Life is for ends unknown’ (l.7), reflects the ambiguity of Hardy’s own teleology in this poem. 
The double negative deliberately obscures any definite meaning. Is Yell’ham saying that 
there are ends even though they are unknown or that there are no ends? It seems that 
116 
 
twenty-two years after the ambiguity of the ‘Unfulfilled Intention’ of The Woodlanders, Hardy 
seems no closer to resolving this.  
 
Reading Hardy’s ‘thwarted purposing’ through Bennett’s vital materiality offers an alternative 
interpretation. Bennett argues that her conception of matter denies any purposive dimension 
or notion of a blind mechanism as it embraces the unexpected: it envisages ‘paths that could 
not have been foreseen, for they are governed by an emergent rather than a linear or 
deterministic causality’ (JB 112). Using this as a lens suggests that his ‘thwarted purposing’ 
reflects an unexpectedness or randomness in nature, in which ‘ends’ must indeed be 
‘unknown’. This would justify a lack of teleology or purpose. I suggest that Hardy’s ambiguity 
in this poem permits both Schopenhauerian and Bennettian interpretations. It also allows us 
to see the bifurcation between a deterministic Schopenhauerian and an emergent New 
Materialist conception of nature. 
 
It is the transience in nature that Hardy uses to imply that life is ‘thwarted’ or that ‘we come 
to live, and are called to die’. Yell’ham Wood is deciduous and can therefore show seasonal 
cycles of renewal and decay ‘In fall, in spring’. In fact, its full name, ‘Yellowham Wood’ (and 
Hardy is using real woods in this poem), reflects the appearance of senescent leaves in 
autumn. In contrast, ‘Coomb-Firtrees’ and ‘Clyffe-hill Clump’ are coniferous. Their assertion 
that Life is ‘a moan’ (invoking their continuous sound) or ‘gray’, suggests that the non-
transience of the coniferous is deadening, perhaps reminding the reader of Hardy’s earlier 
poem, ‘Neutral Tones’ in which ‘gray’ is suggestive of the lack of any renewal (CP 12). 
Through using the cycles of renewal and senescence that are expressed in Yell’ham’s 
seasonality, Hardy suggests that transience demonstrates vitality. 
 
The symbolic use of seasonality in vegetation to express transience is common. As Marder 
notes, ‘various discussions of the philosophical problem of time have elevated the plant and 
its “life-cycle” to the status of exemplarity’ (MM 94). Using ‘Yell’ham Wood’s’ as an example, 
117 
 
Adrian Tait comments on how leaf fall provides Hardy with ‘the perfect metaphor for life’s 
transience’ but that there is also a ‘counterbalancing recognition that trees themselves do 
not die with each leaf fall. Rather, that leaf fall becomes part of a larger cycle of renewal’.112 
Indeed, the seasonal transience of the vegetal cannot strictly be compared to human life and 
death. It reflects a different ontology and, in fact, deconstructs a life/death dualism (MM 11). 
Unlike animals which are individually either dead or alive, for the vegetal, a ‘continual death 
of used organs has replaced death of the plant itself’.113 They are changes, not deaths. 
Using this to reflect human transience must therefore be a flawed analogy that is, as of all 
analogies, filtered through human interpretation.  
 
However, my reading of ‘Yell’ham Wood’s’ suggests that Hardy is doing something more 
than merely using the vegetal as an analogy for transience. That he not only extends vegetal 
transience to Life’s meaning – that meaning is to be found in the acceptance of transience – 
but that being alive is itself meaning. I propose that in the rather intrusive three-syllable 
‘signify’ (‘It says that life would signify’ (l.8)), Hardy gestures towards Macbeth’s soliloquy on 
Life’s meaning. Upon hearing of the death of his wife, Macbeth bitterly comments that ‘Life’s 
but a walking shadow’ that tells ‘a tale’ that is ‘signifying nothing’.114 In contrast, Hardy’s 
vegetal messenger, instead of asserting that life signifies nothing, suggests that life is 
meaning. Hardy’s following this with ‘we come to live, and are called to die’ becomes a 
positive rather than negative message. As Hardy affirms, ‘Yes, that’s the thing’. Merely being 
alive is what counts. For Barad, ‘matter and meaning are mutually articulated’ and this is 
Hardy’s point (KB 152). That as ‘we are a part of that nature that we seek to understand’, in 
being alive, like the plants, we too have meaning (KB 26). 
 
 
112 Adrian Tait, ‘Seeking Signs Amongst ‘Sylvan Phenomena’: Trees in the Work of Thomas Hardy’, Green 
Letters: Studies in Ecocriticism, 25(4) (2021), 377-389 (p.385). 
113 Hallé, p.122. 
114 William Shakespeare, Macbeth, Act 5, Scene 5. 
118 
 
IV: 
Hetero-temporality in  ‘A Backward Spring’ 
 
Hardy’s ‘A Backward Spring’ (hereafter ‘Backward Spring’) was published in the collection 
Moments of Vision and Miscellaneous Verses (1917) (CP 498). In this poem Hardy 
considers the hetero-temporality of plants, inextricably connected as they are to external 
temporality. However, rather than using its temporal dependency as an analogy for human 
temporality and meaning as he does in ‘Yell’ham Wood’s’, in ‘Backward Spring’ Hardy 
expresses a sympathy with the vegetal’s lack of self-determination in its reliance upon 
externality. But he also recognises that the vegetal’s lack of a conscious memory is an 
advantage and therefore suggests that hetero-temporality might also be of benefit. 
 
In ‘Backward Spring’, Hardy considers the responses of various plant species to a late 
Spring. In the first stanza, the vegetal is fearful of the inclement weather: the trees are 
‘afraid’; the grass shows ‘timidity’; the barberry is ‘fret’ (ll.1-5). But however much they might 
delay their spring growth, Hardy does not suggest that they will not grow at all. Though the 
barberry is ‘fret’, it is merely ‘waiting to bloom’. He makes this clear in the following stanza 
where the snowdrop ‘betrays no gloom’ and ‘the primrose pants in its heedless push’ (ll.7-8). 
All plants must grow to survive. 
 
Hardy only mentions each of these species once. However, he devotes at least five lines in 
this poem to the myrtle. Its struggle to cope with this tardy Spring is perhaps reflective of its 
Mediterranean heritage, in contrast to the better adapted snowdrop and primrose: 
 
Though the myrtle asks if it’s worth the fight 
 
     This year with frost and rime 
 
     To venture one more time 
 
On delicate leaves and buttons of white 
 
From the selfsame bough as at last year’s prime,  (ll.9-13) 
 
The myrtle’s response to this late Spring is one of weighing up options, veering between 
hesitancy and bravery. After asking if ‘it’s worth the fight’, the following couplet presents two 
119 
 
options: hesitancy or fortitude. Hardy suggests its fragility in its ‘delicate leaves and buttons 
of white’, reinforced through the skipping rhythm of this line after the slow hesitancy of the 
decision-making couplet. This is then contrasted in the following line where exhaustion is 
evoked in the triple stresses of ‘selfsame bough’ and ‘last year’s prime’ expressing a heavy 
weariness. If we view the poem as gesturing towards the form of the Shakespearian sonnet 
with its three quatrains followed by a couplet (apt for the myrtle as symbol of love), the 
extending of this third near-quatrain to five lines, with no punctuation, extends and exhausts 
the sonnet structure, drawing attention to the myrtle’s tiredness and continuing battle. It 
seems an almost insurmountable hurdle to add further growth onto the already extended 
‘selfsame’ branch from ‘last year’s prime’. The length of the bough is reflected in this line 
being visually the longest of the poem, visually illustrating Marder’s conceptualisation of 
linear plant growth as the ‘temporalisation of space and spatialization of time’ (MM 96). 
 
This long line represents linear growth, but the myrtle’s response to the Spring is also 
cyclical – it must generate this year’s leaves and flowers. For Marder, vegetal temporality is 
‘both linear, insofar as it tends to infinity, and cyclical […] as occurring in the midst of, and 
permeated by, the changing of the seasons’ (MM 112-113). In ‘Backward Spring’, Hardy 
evokes the complex trade-off between continuous growth and cyclical seasonal renewal. 
The myrtle must risk growing in the inclement conditions due to its necessity for spatial 
increase. But it must also respond to this year’s specific conditions – the late Spring, 
enduring the ‘frost and rime’ to ‘venture one more time’. 
 
Reading this as a sonnet, the extended third quatrain causes a delay to the volta, creating 
tension and expectation. The volta responds to this with a sudden change in tone, 
suggesting that this poem is not simply about plant responses to the late Spring. But the final 
rhyming couplet is problematic in terms of its ambiguity: 
And never to ruminate on or remember 
 
What happened to it in mid-December.  (ll.14-15)  
120 
 
 
The prominent ‘never’ early in the first line creates an ominous tone, as does the drawn out 
‘ruminate’ and the falling metre of ‘remember’, making this first line of the couplet the longest 
line in the poem. In terms of meaning, as Hardy has evoked both cyclical and linear time in 
this poem, the reader is unsure whether what ‘happened to it in mid-December’ is a regular 
cyclical occurrence or a specific event. If it is read as representative of cyclicity, obviously 
mid-December’s short days are difficult for plants and would be particularly so for the 
Mediterranean myrtle. But perhaps Hardy is indicating in this portentous volta that something 
more specific occurred last December?   
 
There is a further level of ambiguity in that the syntax makes it unclear as to whether the 
couplet is about the myrtle or about humanity. If it is continuing the myrtle’s question begun 
five lines earlier: ‘the myrtle asks’, the lack of a question mark at the end is problematic. But 
this would invite a reading in which these bitter final lines become a statement and one that 
is reflective of a human sensibility. The ‘ruminate’ and ‘remember’ are also suggestive of 
human consciousness; even if plant research suggests that plants have some form of 
memory, it is unlikely that they ‘ruminate’!  
 
I propose that this volta should be read as a comment on humanity and one that reflects 
Marder’s notion of human ‘temporal dissociation’ (MM 104): that through the imagination, 
humans can exist outside of the present time. Hardy’s ‘ruminate’ suggests that this causes 
discomfort through awakening sombre memories that impinge on the present. This is also 
implied in the poem’s title: as well as the tardiness of a late Spring, it may indicate a 
springing backwards in time with ‘spring’ used as a verb rather than a noun. Such a reading 
reinforces my argument that aspects of Hardy’s poetry show him writing against a 
Wordsworthian veneration of the recalling of experiences in the imagination. For Hardy, such 
121 
 
‘temporal dissociation’ causes suffering rather than ‘tranquil restoration’ (l.31), to once again 
quote ‘Tintern Abbey’.115 
 
Through reading this volta as relating to humanity, a further reading is possible. Hardy dates 
this poem ‘April 1917’. Although Moments of Vision contains a section entitled ‘Poems of 
War and Patriotism’ in which this poem is not included, a work of poetry published in 1917 
and specifically dated to 1917 cannot but be tinged with war themes. In fact, Armstrong 
claims that ‘Hardy was a war poet from the beginning’.116 Furthermore, April 1917 may not 
even merely be a general reference to World War One but more specific. The previous ‘mid-
December’ (precisely 15th December 1916) is the date of the beginning of the final offensive 
of the Battle of Verdun, one of the costliest battles in terms of human life of the whole War. 
This would certainly be a cause for rumination. 
 
Whether this poem is read as a comment on humanity or merely about the vegetal, Hardy 
does recognise the vegetal’s hetero-temporality and its corollary lack of Marder’s ‘temporal 
dissociation’. The vegetal can only grow and regenerate in relation to the temporality of 
alterity. If it is read as a comment on humanity or the war, there is a sense in which Hardy 
views the vegetal’s regenerative ability, without the encumbrance of consciousness or 
memory, as an asset. Its existence only in the present, rather than a hindrance, is 
advantageous. 
 
V: 
Static Representations of Reality in ‘At the Royal Academy’ 
 
‘At the Royal Academy’ (hereafter ‘Academy’) was published in Late Lyrics and Earlier with 
Many Other Verses (1922) and concerns landscape paintings of summer vegetation (CP 
627-8). It is another poem in which Hardy addresses the problematic human relationship to 
 
115 Wu, p.241. 
116 Armstrong, p.460. 
122 
 
transience and death. I read this poem as an extension of the point he makes in ‘Yell’ham 
Wood’s’: that humankind struggles to understand the meaning of transience and, to avoid 
this reality, we create false images of permanence. In fact, the poems printed immediately 
before and after ‘Academy’ in Late Lyrics, ‘The Children and Sir Nameless’ and ‘Her 
Temple’, also concern failed attempts to defeat death through the construction of permanent 
memorials (CP 627; 628). In ‘The Children and Sir Nameless, in years to come children ask 
“Who was this old stone man beneath our toes?” (l.24), while in ‘Her Temple’, ‘None now 
knows’ the name of the man who built it (l.8). In ‘Academy’ then, Hardy contrasts the vitality 
of reality with such deadening representations. Like in ‘In a Wood’ and ‘Pine Planters’, he 
shows that mediated through human consciousness, the vegetal becomes static and 
therefore unreal. He forces the reader to reject such illusory permanence and recognise that 
life and vitality is to be found in the transient. 
 
Structurally, the poem consists of five tercets with rhyming triplets and an iambic pentameter 
rhythm. Such conventional regularity reflects the formality of the Royal Academy’s landscape 
paintings. But Hardy creates a tension between this regularity and an alternative mode, 
embedded in his vocabulary, of temporal fluctuations (‘season’, ‘last noonday’, ‘yester’s’, 
‘last year’, ‘May to Michaelmas term’) which enact change, demonstrating a clash between 
the representational and actual plant vitality. The first four tercets of the poem concern the 
paintings and although Hardy leads the reader on to assume that they contain fresh 
greenery, they do not. He delays this revelation until the end of the fourth tercet and ‘real’ 
transient vegetation is only present in the final stanza. 
 
In the first three stanzas, the use of a single sentence evokes the fullness of herbage at its 
summer peak:  
These summer landscapes – clump, and copse, and croft –  
 
Woodland and meadowland – here hung aloft, 
123 
 
 
Gay with limp grass and leafery new and soft, 
 
 
Seem caught from the immediate season’s yield 
 
I saw last noonday shining over the field, 
 
By rapid snatch, while still are uncongealed 
 
 
The saps that in their live originals climb; 
 
Yester’s quick greenage here set forth in mime 
 
Just as it stands, now, at our breathing-time.  (ll.1-9) 
 
The abundance in the paintings is intensified by the listing of different vegetal landscapes 
and freshness is evoked in the ‘leafery new and soft’. But already there are signs that all is 
not as it appears, such an impression initiated by the tentative ‘seem’ at the beginning of the 
second stanza. The ensuing ‘caught’, implying entrapment, adds to feelings of unease, and 
the aggressive ‘rapid snatch’ two lines hence, although potentially depicting the spontaneity 
of painting, also gestures towards something being stolen.  
 
Indications of the unreality of the paintings continue. The enjambment at the end of the 
second stanza leaves the meaning of ‘uncongealed’ hanging. It can be read as indicating 
that the oils of the painting are still fresh and yet will congeal and become immobile. But in 
the following stanza, Hardy reveals that the ‘uncongealed’ relates to the vegetal ‘saps’. As 
part of the ‘live originals’ that will ‘climb’, rather than congeal, these will continue to flow. The 
word ‘climb’ being positioned at the end of the line, while also adding an extra syllable to the 
iambic pentameter, gives the ‘saps’ the potential to rise through the poem, suggesting that 
such vegetal movement cannot be contained within the paintings’ static regularity. Like the 
vitality of the vegetal in the middle of ‘In a Wood’ and the seed in the middle of the tree 
section of ‘Pine Planters’, Hardy inserts vegetal vitality into the middle of this poem. 
Moreover, once the oil paint congeals the paintings will become permanent while the saps 
will be renewed every year. The paintings will become immobile while the vegetal retains its 
vigour.  
 
124 
 
Although stanza four begins with ‘but’, it then seems to continue with the pretence that the 
vegetation described is vital and fresh, with the ‘young foils so fresh upon each tree’ (l.10), 
whereas this is, in fact, still about the vegetal within the paintings. It is not until the final line 
of this stanza that Hardy abruptly puts an end to such misconceptions in the short clause 
‘Are not this summer’s,’ (l.12). The reader realises they have been somewhat hoodwinked. 
As the line continues, Hardy makes this clear: the vitality represented has been false: ‘they 
feign to be’ (l.12). In the paintings, the vitality of the vegetal is stilled and the representation 
static. 
 
These landscape paintings alter our understanding of what is, in reality, a process of 
constant change. Barad claims that ‘discursive practices are causal intra-actions’ that 
participate in reconfiguring the world. She asserts that ‘in its causal intra-activity, part of the 
world becomes determinately bounded and propertied in its emergent intelligibility to another 
part of the world’ (KB 149, emphasis in original). Although she is discussing discourse here, I 
argue that this could equally apply to visual representations. They too have impact on how 
we view the world, and in the case of ‘Academy’, Hardy addresses the consequence of such 
a misconception. In The Old Ways (2012), Robert MacFarlane writes of how ‘landscape’ as 
a concept is ‘often understood as a noun connoting fixity, scenery, an immobile painterly 
decorum’.117 I suggest that, likewise, vegetation is also often perceived as fixed rather than 
dynamic. In fact, this is partially what causes the misunderstanding of the speaker of ‘In a 
Wood’. Although in ‘Academy’ Hardy uses poetic structure to mislead his reader, in doing so 
he shocks them into feeling animosity at the falsity of the paintings and an understanding as 
to how such paintings reconfigure our mental image of the world.  
 
By the time the reader gets to the final stanza of ‘Academy’, they are prepared for the reality 
of transience, but it is a harsh reality that Hardy presents:   
 
117 Robert MacFarlane, The Old Ways, (London, Penguin, 2012), p.255. 
125 
 
Last year their May to Michaelmas term was run, 
 
Last autumn browned and buried every one, 
 
And no more know they sight of any sun.  (ll.13-15) 
 
This sobering stanza is the only one in the poem that fully focuses on real vegetation but, 
ironically, in contrast to the imitative summer vegetal of the paintings, it is dead. The leaves 
are now ‘browned and buried’ and their lack of chlorophyll means that they can no longer 
convert sunlight into materiality. This, as well as their positioning at the base of the poem, 
means that they ‘no more know the sight of any sun’. Hardy implies that their deaths bear 
relation to that of humans: the leaves are ‘buried’, human notions of individuality are implied 
in ‘every one’, and the single syllable words of the final line slow down the rhythm and create 
a mournful tone, Hardy reminds us that life for all living beings must end in death. 
 
Yet vegetal transience is not comparable to that of humanity. In my exploration of ‘Yell’ham 
Wood’s’, I pointed out how this is due to the vegetal’s ability to constantly renew itself. In 
‘Academy’ the difference is that the decomposing leaves can materially contribute to new 
growth. The sunlight that is buried at the bottom of the poem, embedded in their materiality, 
will become part of a ‘material “resurrection”’ (MM 67). The paintings that are ’hung aloft’ at 
the top of the poem cannot suppress the vitality of the real vegetation, even when it is 
symbolically located at the base of the poem, within, and part of, the darkness of the soil. 
Furthermore, each vegetal regeneration is new. Drawing upon the ideas of Derrida, Marder 
makes the point that each reiteration is a singular event ‘with ‘inexorable alterations’ (Marder 
116). The leaves in the paintings might ‘Seem caught from the immediate season’s yield’ but 
Hardy makes it clear that they ‘are not this summer’s’. Each leaf that grows is the first 
iteration of that leaf. However, this year’s leaves might incorporate the regenerated 
materiality of the leaves that are represented in the paintings.  
 
 
126 
 
VI: 
The Problematic Possession of Consciousness in ‘Before Life and 
After’ and ‘The Aerolite’ 
 
To conclude this chapter, I want to examine two poems in which Hardy explicitly 
problematises human consciousness: ‘Before Life and After’ (hereafter ‘Before Life’), 
published in Time’s Laughingstocks and Other Verses (1909), and ‘The Aerolite’ (hereafter 
‘Aerolite’), published in Human Shows, Far Phantasies, Songs and Trifles (1925) (CP 277; 
769-70). Although in these two poems Hardy does not compare or contrast vegetal and 
human, his recurring use of the vegetal in his poetry to highlight the advantage of a lack of 
consciousness makes these poems of interest to this study. In both poems, Hardy considers 
the development of human consciousness, the problems it causes, and concludes that a 
return to a state of ‘nescience’ or ‘normal unwareness’ would be beneficial. 
 
‘Before Life’ consists of four quatrains, the first three of which are about life on Earth ‘Before 
the birth of consciousness’ (l.3). These stanzas evoke a rather paradisal life. In stanza two, 
Hardy establishes a world that is free from suffering and pain. Using the anaphoric ‘none’: 
‘None suffered’, ‘None knew’, ‘None cared’, Hardy lists the feelings from which no one 
suffers distress, including ‘love’, ‘loss’, ‘regret’, ‘starved hope’, and ‘heart-burnings’ (ll.5-7).  
 
It is not that nothing harmful occurs, but that there is no conscious awareness of these 
harms and therefore no emotional response to them. It is not that there was no ‘sickness’ or 
‘crash or cross’ or that ‘something winced and waned’ but that without feelings ‘none 
suffered’, ‘no heart was wrung’, ‘no sense was stung’ (ll.4-11). Even mortality brings no pain: 
‘if something ceased, no tongue bewailed’ (l.9). In the lack of ‘regret’ and ‘starved hope’ 
Hardy also suggests that humanity’s ability to divorce itself from the present, its ‘temporal 
dissociation’ that I discussed in relation to ‘Backward Spring’, is also a cause of distress.  
 
127 
 
With the first three-quarters of the poem set in a world that lacks consciousness, Hardy 
normalises nonconsciousness. But the anaphora and listing create a tension or expectation 
which is resolved in the final stanza where Hardy presents the world as it is. It is here where 
he specifically pinpoints ‘feeling’ as the problem:  
 
     But the disease of feeling germed, 
 
And primal rightness took the tinct of wrong; 
 
     Ere nescience shall be reaffirmed 
 
          How long, how long?  (ll.13-16) 
 
Rather than thought as a ‘disease of flesh’ (RN 120) as Hardy suggests in The Return of the 
Native, now it is feelings that are the ‘disease’. This is a theme that Hardy repeatedly 
addresses in his tragic novels and that I explored in relation to The Return of the Native, but 
here he explicitly explores the destructive nature of the emotions. 
 
In this final stanza, Hardy expresses a yearning for a return to what he frames as normality: 
‘nescience’. In this respect, he seems to concur with Schopenhauer that ‘unconsciousness is 
the original and natural condition of all things’ (WWR2 142). Schopenhauer suggests that 
before the evolutionary development of animals, the world was a place of a ‘long and 
profound peace’ until the development of animals put an end to ‘that plant paradise’.118 
Perhaps it is this ‘plant paradise’ that the speaker of ‘In a Wood’ hoped he might encounter. 
But there is a mixture of optimism and pessimism in Hardy’s expectation that ‘nescience 
shall be reaffirmed’. The use of the auxiliary verb ‘shall’ implies that this will happen but the 
yearning tone of the following and final ‘How long, how long?’ with its repeated question, 
creates a sense of desperation and, of course, returning to ‘nescience’ is impossible.  
   
Sixteen years after the publication of ‘Before Life and After’, Hardy returns to this theme in 
‘The Aerolite’. This poem is framed as a thought experiment, stanza one beginning with ‘I 
thought’ and a parenthetical ‘(Or so I dreamed)’ at the end. This lack of realism allows Hardy 
 
118 Arthur Schopenhauer, ‘On Philosophy and Natural Science’, in Parerga and Paralipomena: Short 
Philosophical Essays. Vol 2, trans. by E.F.J Payne (Oxford, Clarendon Press, 1974), p.143. 
128 
 
to develop an even more fantastical proposition than in ‘Before Life’: that ‘Consciousness’ 
arrived on Earth via an ‘aerolite’ (a meteorite) from ‘some far globe’. 
 
Although there are many similarities between these two poems, in ‘Aerolite’ Hardy 
emphasises a slightly different aspect of the effect of the emotions, as well as expanding his 
problematisation of consciousness to other aspects of the mind. He suggests that it is not 
the emotions per se that are the problem, but that they reveal the difficult conditions of life on 
Earth. On the ‘far globe’, consciousness and the emotions existed, it is just that the 
conditions there were ideal and so there was ‘no distress’ (l.4). As a result, ‘the power to feel’ 
was ‘a gift uncloyed’ (l.7) and the only ‘emotions’ felt are those of being ‘loved, desired, 
enjoyed’ (l.10).  
 
However, when consciousness arrives on Earth, where there are ‘stains and stingings’ (l.18), 
consciousness ‘showed to us the worm | That gnaws vitalities native here’ (ll.14-15), and the 
‘mortal moan | Begot of sentience’ (ll.33-34) commences. Hardy returns here to the 
imperfect nature of life on Earth, as he has done in The Woodlanders, where Grace 
wondered ‘if there were one world in the universe where the fruit had no worm, and marriage 
no sorrow' (W 184). Perhaps this world could only be on the ‘far globe’ that Hardy imagines 
here. The other development that Hardy makes in this second poem is that he problematises 
other aspects of the mind. Although he begins the poem by stating that it is ‘consciousness’ 
that arrives on the aerolite, he later includes other elements of the mind. In referring to the 
world’s previous ‘ignorance’ (l.17) and lack of the ‘opined’, as well as to things that ‘tax 
intelligence’ (ll.19-20), he also seems to include reason as detrimental to humanity. It maybe 
that he is using such concepts connected with the mind quite loosely here, allying these with 
consciousness, but he has specifically criticised these other aspects of the mind in The 
Return of the Native and in others of his novels, so he maybe deliberately widening his 
criticism of the human mind here. 
129 
 
 
In both of these unrealistic poems Hardy tries to grapple with the problem of consciousness. 
Although he self-proclaims that he was ‘as a young man […] among the earliest acclaimers 
of The Origin of Species’, in these poems there is a sense of difficulty in accepting that 
consciousness has developed through evolutionary processes.119 In the ‘earth’s testimonies 
tell’ (l.2) of ‘Before Life’, Hardy refers to the deep time of epochs on earth before humanity, 
or perhaps before any life. In writing that consciousness or ‘the disease of feeling germed’, 
he seems to indicate its subsequent evolutionary development. But in these poems, he also 
indicates that this is a difficult development to acknowledge. Of course, this could be 
attributed to his proximity, at least earlier in his life, to the radical and disturbing impact of 
evolutionary theory. But as Michael Pollan points out, even when one’s views are consistent 
with an evolutionary viewpoint, a materialistic acceptance often breaks down when 
considering human consciousness.120 Although consciousness is not an exception and there 
is no evidence of any break in the evolutionary chain, Hardy suggests that in the 
development of consciousness, life has extended itself too far. In the introduction I referred 
to his comment in the Life that ‘the human race is too extremely developed for its corporeal 
conditions’ and that ‘this planet does not supply the materials for happiness to higher 
existences’.121 This seems to me an apt summary of his thinking in these two poems and 
indicates why he feels the need to create an alternative, perhaps consolatory, explanation 
for consciousness: that it might be an aberration and ‘unwareness’ (‘Aerolite, l.35) normality. 
The sudden and fantastic nature of the arrival of the ‘untoward gift’ (l.27) of consciousness in 
‘Aerolite’ enables an avoidance of perceiving it as a natural evolutionary development and 
instead its origin becomes an unaccountable mystery.   
 
 
119 Hardy, Life, p.158 (entry dated 1882). 
120 Pollan, p.165. 
121 Hardy, Life, p.227 (entry dated 7th April 1889). 
130 
 
Another explanation for consciousness is of course a religious or teleological one and Hardy 
gestures towards this in both these poems. In ‘Before Life’ the ‘primal rightness’ that ‘took 
the tinct of wrong’ (l.14) implies the disruptive impact of knowledge in the Genesis story, as 
does the ‘unblind | Earth’s old-established ignorance’ of ‘Aerolite’ (ll.16-17) which the ‘seer’ 
wishes to ‘oust’ (ll.21-22). The ‘worm | That gnaws vitalities native here’ (ll.14-15), recalls the 
serpent that tempts Eve to eat of the Tree of Knowledge, removing humanity’s ignorance 
and blindness. If we read these poems from this perspective, Hardy suggests that our 
awakening to the nature of humanity is also destructive to humanity’s equanimity. Although 
having lost his own faith, Hardy cannot find any consolation in a religious perspective, the 
fact that in ‘Aerolite’ consciousness has been sent ‘For ends unknown’ (l.28) suggests that 
although humanity may not know their end, there is at least a teleological destination. Hardy 
returns us here to the exact same phrase that he had used in ‘Yell’ham Wood’s’ but where 
any notion of a teleological end was more ambiguous. The final line of ‘Aerolite’: ‘Normal 
unwareness waits rebirth’ (l.35) does suggests some form of renaissance or renewal, and in 
this way, Hardy can avoid the bitter conclusions of evolutionary theory – that there is no 
ameliorating teleological end for humanity.   
 
Although Hardy is not satisfied with dualistic notions of humanity – he is in essence a non-
dualist – he admits to struggling with the materiality, and material development of, 
consciousness. In a late poem, ‘Our Old Friend Dualism’, published in Winter Words in 
Various Moods and Metres in 1928, Hardy’s final volume of poetry that was published in the 
year of his death, Hardy addresses the persistence of dualistic understandings of humanity 
(CP 892). He refers to dualism as ‘A tough old chap’ (l.1) even though ‘We pound him with 
our ‘Truth’ (l.3). The personified Dualism asserts that ‘I must live; for flamens plead I am all 
that’s worth believing!’ (l.6). In ‘Before Life’ and ‘Aerolite, as well as elsewhere in his work, 
Hardy articulates the struggle to maintain a materialist viewpoint that does not have the 
ameliorating consolatory beliefs of religious, teleological or fantastical explanations. 
Consciousness, for Hardy, only makes life difficult and bleak, and he fully understands the 
131 
 
temptation to surmise that mind and consciousness might be located somewhere other than 
the physical. But the solution he purports in both poems is equally untenable: to return to 
‘nescience’ or ‘unwareness’. Ultimately, he has no resolution for such a dilemma. From this 
perspective, his envy of the vegetal’s non-conscious, mind-free life seems understandable.     
 
 
132 
 
Chapter Four - The search for meaning: Individual Transience and 
the Universal Life in Schreiner’s The Story of an African Farm 
 
We walk in the great hall of life, looking up and round reverentially. Nothing is 
despicable – all is meaningful; nothing is small – all is part of a whole.  
(Olive Schreiner, African Farm, p.154) 
 
Mankind is only an ephemeral blossom on the tree of time; there were others 
before it opened; there will be others after it has fallen […] We are sparks, we 
are shadows, we are pollen, which the next wind will carry away.  
(Olive Schreiner, African Farm, p.217) 
 
In this chapter, I consider how The Story of an African Farm (hereafter African Farm) 
addresses the themes of multiplicity, connectivity, and the human search for meaning. 
Schreiner represents the individual as relatively insignificant in comparison to the 
continuousness of life as a whole. She positions individual characters within a world of 
multiple lifeforms and deep time and implies that the progress of life is of greater importance 
than the transient individual. For Schreiner, all lifeforms are connected and of value. She 
presents humankind’s search for meaning and suggests that it is to be found in a recognition 
of the connectedness and continuousness of life. However, as individuals are trapped in 
their own consciousness, humanity is unable to perceive this wholeness. She identifies 
reasoning and consciousness as a barrier to such a perception and purports that it is only 
through other modes of perception that the underlying metaphysical connectivity across life 
can be accessed and meaning restored to life. 
 
It is useful to compare and contrast Schreiner and Hardy’s representation of some of these 
issues. Although they both consider how the individual relates to the whole, this is a more 
prominent theme in the work of Schreiner and it is embedded into her notion of progress 
across life over an extended temporal perspective. Hardy presents materiality as a challenge 
to a metaphysical perspective and that connectivity is to be found solely within the 
phenomenal. Schreiner, however, develops a model of connectivity that combines the 
material and metaphysical and asserts that it is through this that meaning is to be found. 
133 
 
Schreiner’s problematisation of consciousness and an overdependence on the reasoning 
mind is in some ways comparable to Hardy’s. They both view these aspects of the mind as 
fostering an existential search for meaning, as well as separating humanity from material life. 
For Hardy the mind is in conflict with the emotions that he locates in corporeality whereas for 
Schreiner, the conflict is between reasoning and embodied dreams, intuitions and feelings, 
and she suggests that these modes of perception engender a greater understanding of how 
humanity and individuals are connected to the material world as a whole.   
 
In earlier chapters I explored how Hardy uses his vegetal settings to embed his notions of 
connectivity, entanglement, and the importance of materiality. Although Schreiner also uses 
the literal vegetal in African Farm to establish some corresponding ideas, its use as setting is 
less important. However, I argue that a vegetal mode of being is a productive model for 
examining how Schreiner represents the relationship between the individual and the 
collective and Marder’s understanding of how the plant ‘transcend[s] the distinction between 
“part” and “whole” (MM 84) is a useful lens for exploring this. I use the vegetal’s transient 
mode of being as a model to reflect upon Schreiner’s understanding of the necessity for 
death for the continuance of life as a whole. In fact, in African Farm Schreiner herself uses 
the ontological vegetal as a model for her notion of a continuous connected life when she 
represents the connectivity across all life across all time through a ‘tree of life’ image. 
Schopenhauer’s notion of the survival of the will across successive individual manifestations 
encompasses some similar features and a Schopenhauerian perspective adds the notion of 
progress across successive generations to the vegetal model and is therefore helpful for 
exploring Schreiner’s proposition that life and humanity progress as a whole and that this 
progress is ultimately of greater importance than is the individual life.  
 
The chapter is divided into four parts. In the first, I examine Schreiner’s use of form and 
structure. I consider how she expands the parameters of the realist novel by inserting 
abstract philosophical sections that break with the linear narrative. These enable Schreiner 
134 
 
to universalise the stories of the individual characters and model her approach to the 
relationship between individual and whole. In Part II, I explore how Schreiner articulates the 
relative unimportance of the individual in favour of the multiple and the collective. I examine 
her portrayal of the difficulty for humanity of accepting the transience of the individual but 
how this can be ameliorated through an understanding of a metaphysical and material force 
that connects all beings. Part III links these ideas to Schreiner’s depiction of character. I 
predominantly examine how through the character of Waldo, Schreiner portrays how a mode 
of being framed by consciousness causes dissatisfaction, and how a more vegetal mode of 
life brings content. Finally, in Part IV, I examine Schreiner’s portrayal of connectivity across 
the natural world. I argue that she purports that the human search for meaning is thwarted 
by a reliance on rational analysis, and that meaning in life can be found through an intuitive 
understanding of a metaphysical connectivity behind material connectivity. 
 
While downplaying the role of the individual, African Farm is a novel and so must be about 
individual characters. The main narrative drive is shared amongst three characters: Waldo, 
Lyndall, and Em. I suggest that Waldo is the character who best reflects Schreiner’s 
concerns about the position of the individual and the search for a meaning behind life. Much 
scholarship on African Farm has focused upon the character of Lyndall and her struggle for 
female emancipation. It is this element of the novel that led to W T Stead’s 1894 evaluation 
of it as the ‘forerunner of all the novels of the Modern Woman’ and subsequent scholarship 
that has read it as a New Woman novel, and this continues in the twenty-first century.122 
Nevertheless, there are many other potential readings of African Farm and scholarship has 
widened over the latter years of the twentieth and early twenty-first centuries to consider 
aspects such as Schreiner’s representation of landscape and colonialism, her early use of 
 
122 W.T. Stead, ‘The Novel of the Modern Woman’, The Review of Reviews, 10 ( July 1894), p.64.  
https://www.proquest.com/docview/3915306/3A78643956C84A44PQ/9?accountid=8318&imgSeq=1 
[accessed 31st December 2022]. 
135 
 
literary modernist techniques, and the influence of evolutionary, Transcendental, and 
philosophical ideas. My own reading of the novel is closest to these latter interpretive 
stances. 
 
In African Farm, Schreiner presents Waldo’s development from a child to his early death. At 
the beginning of the novel, this humble herder of sheep experiences a crisis of faith and, 
after much mental struggle alongside close observation of nature, develops a quasi-scientific 
understanding of material connectivity. Following his father’s death and his subsequent 
mistreatment by the new overseer of the farm, Waldo leaves to explore the wider world. His 
existential journey, however, only brings him dissatisfaction. He returns to the farm and 
learns of the death of Lyndall. Struggling to come to terms with this, he eventually arrives at 
an understanding of the necessity for material death in a world of connectivity that embraces 
both the material and metaphysical. 
 
Waldo shares many similarities with Clym Yeobright of The Return of the Native. Both go on 
existential journeys, although perhaps Waldo’s can be more securely defined as existential 
reflecting the proto-modernism of Schreiner’s text. Both returning natives move from a 
position of relative autonomy and self-determination to a realisation of the value of a vegetal-
like heteronomous and rooted mode of life, and their connection to all other lifeforms. Unlike 
Clym, who in the end returns to a more intellectual mode of being, Waldo recognises that a 
heteronomous and rooted life is beneficial and meaningful, rather than restrictive. 
 
Schreiner extends her representation of the importance of the whole in relation to the 
individual at the level of form and structure. She creates an innovative and experimental 
structure in which the realist linear narrative is radically interrupted by two abstract sections: 
the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter, the first chapter of Part II, and the Hunter allegory 
136 
 
embedded in the following chapter. These abstract sections do not further the linear 
narrative about individual characters but instead depict a collective humanity. They are 
therefore separate from the linear narrative and yet inextricably linked to it. Although the 
‘Times and Seasons’ chapter is an account of the development of thinking within a 
universalised individual, many elements of it echo Waldo’s story. The Hunter allegory traces 
the progressive development of humanity’s understanding through a series of individuals but 
is embedded within the linear narrative through being told by a ‘stranger’ to Waldo. Waldo 
tells the stranger afterwards that ‘all my life I have longed to see you’ (AF 170) and it has 
some, if amorphous, influence on Waldo’s subsequent development. I argue that reading 
such formal and structural patterns through the lens of Marder’s vegetal ontology 
demonstrates their resemblance to elements of vegetal spatiality and growth and that these 
patterns are therefore embedded within the very structure of this novel.  
 
I. Experimental Form as Ontologically Vegetal 
 
Schreiner’s use of a radical fragmentary form expands the capacity of the linear realist novel. 
Such a strategy has been read as modernist and demonstrative of the openness of the novel 
form. Jed Esty contends that despite its nineteenth-century publication date, African Farm 
anticipates many modernist techniques.123 Jade Munslow Ong asserts that Schreiner 
exploits the ‘openness of the novel form to integrate radical modernist allegory as part of her 
‘cultural resistance.’124 In describing Schreiner’s use of form as in the vanguard of modernist 
techniques that push the novel genre forwards into the twentieth-century, Doris Lessing 
suggests that Schreiner ‘does the work of an earthworm “making new soil where things can 
grow”’.125 Lessing’s description characterises the novel form as organic, able to adapt to new 
 
123 Jed Esty, ‘The Colonial Bildungsroman: The Story of an African Farm and the Ghost of Goethe’, Victorian 
Studies, 49(3) (2007), 407-430 (p.407).  
124 Jade Munslow Ong, Olive Schreiner and African Modernism: Allegory, Empire and Postcolonial Writing 
(London, Routledge, 2017), p.14. 
125 Ruth Parkin-Gounelas, ‘Afterword’ in Olive Schreiner, The Story of an African Farm (London, Virago Press, 
1989), p.286. 
137 
 
circumstances. This is perhaps echoed in Erin James’ later assessment of the ability of 
narrative ‘to shift and change as does the world in which it is produced’.126 It is not such a 
stretch then to read Schreiner’s use of form and structure through a vegetal lens, modular 
vegetal growth enabling the plant to constantly adapt to changing environmental conditions. 
Her portrayal in the linear narrative of the stories of individuals and of collective experience 
in the abstract sections emulates a vegetal blurring of the boundaries between individual and 
collective and enables her to universalise human experience.   
 
Through my organic vegetal interpretation of Schreiner’s use of form and structure, I 
demonstrate how Schreiner’s novel undermines ecocritical assessments of the novel form as 
unable to incorporate ecological and environmental issues. Dominic Head criticises the 
‘tendency of the novel to focus on personal development, and on social rather than 
environmental matters’.127 I assert that Schreiner’s use of form means that she can 
generalise rather than solely individuate human experience. Amitav Ghosh extends such 
criticism to the novel’s focus on short-term temporality (see p.10-11).128 I have already 
explored how Hardy embeds the long-term in his novels, and I argue here that Schreiner’s 
use of abstract sections enables her to extend the temporal viewpoint further.  
 
Before I take this argument further, I want to make the point that it is not only in the abstract 
parts of the novel that Schreiner extends her temporal framework. In the linear narrative 
Waldo observes cave paintings, fossil evidence of life before humankind, and earlier 
geomorphological changes. He even recognises the perspective of the stars which will have 
‘seen the earth when it was young’ (AF 120). Schreiner also stretches temporality into a 
future beyond humanity. Lyndall suggests that ‘mankind is only an ephemeral blossom on 
 
126 Erin James, ‘Narrative in the Anthropocene’, in Environment and Narrative: New Directions in 
Econarratology, ed. by Erin James and Eric Morel (Columbus, Ohio State University Press, 2020), pp.183-202 
(p.186). 
127 Dominic Head, ‘Problems in Ecocriticism and the Novel’, Key Words: A Journal of Cultural Materialism, No.1, 
1998, 60-73 (p.65). 
128 Ghosh, p.61. 
138 
 
the tree of time; there were others before it opened; there will be others after it has fallen’ 
(AF 217). The use of a vegetal image here is key. This ‘tree of time’ conceives vegetal 
growth as continuous over an extended period while humankind is ephemeral – mere 
‘blossom’. This not only diminishes the importance of the individual, but also humanity itself. 
Humankind merely becomes part of the continuousness of life. Timothy Morton contends 
that to think ecologically, we need to expand our idea of space and time.129 Schreiner’s 
perspective seems to come at this from the other way around: through using an extended 
perspective, one is compelled to observe life as a whole.  
 
In the abstract sections, Schreiner embeds a more radical temporal framework, one that 
even appears to be outside of time itself. In the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter, she rejects 
anthropocentric timescales and asserts that as ‘in the world to come time is not measured 
out by months and years’ so the universal journey of the soul in this world has ‘seasons of its 
own; periods not found in any calendar, times that years and months will not scan’ (AF 137). 
Such temporality gestures towards eternity. Likewise, in using an allegory to portray the 
history of humanity’s search for meaning, Schreiner utilises that form’s inherent 
timelessness, appropriate for a story that tells of an eternal metaphysical search. These 
sections are therefore not only conceptually separate from the linear narrative; they are also 
separate from all concrete temporal frameworks and reach towards a metaphysical 
timelessness. Schreiner’s temporality in this novel therefore embraces both an extended 
material and metaphysical continuousness.  
 
Until the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter, African Farm follows a linear trajectory focused on 
character development. Then at the beginning of Part Two, with the insertion of the ‘Times 
and Seasons’ chapter, Schreiner marks a sudden change with this universal account of a 
 
129 Timothy Morton, The Ecological Thought (Cambridge, Massachusetts, Harvard University Press, 2010), 
p.135. 
139 
 
child’s ‘individuation’.130 The chapter beings with Waldo’s situation three years after the 
events of Part One. The reader might logically think that this chapter will follow his story. But 
after two sentences, Schreiner indicates a change by using a more generalised statement: 
‘they say that in the world to come’ (AF 137). The chapter then outlines over seven parts 
with no mention of Waldo, the development of a soul from infancy until a mature 
understanding of connectivity across life. The account seems both impersonal and particular: 
‘there rises, perhaps a warm summer’s evening; we are seated on the doorstep’ (AF 137). 
The first-person plural suggests specificity but the conditional ‘perhaps’ counters this. Some 
of the experiences of the anonymous child are similar to those of Waldo and they are also 
like Waldo in their introverted and curious nature: ‘suddenly it strikes us, Who are we? This I, 
what is it?’ (AF 139, emphasis in original). However, this self-recognition is also presented 
as a universal stage of development.   
 
Is the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter then an account of the universal development of all 
individuals or specifically Waldo’s? Dorothy Driver alludes to the ‘Times and Seasons’ 
chapter as one which ‘shows Waldo as a child in a spiritual crisis’.131 However, Voss’s view, 
that it ‘recapitulates Part I as one child’s individuation’, blends the individual Waldo of Part I 
with a generalised ‘child’.132 If we interpret it from Marder’s notion of the plant as both 
individual and collective, a ‘provisional unity of multiplicities’ (MM 84), the question perhaps 
becomes a moot point and in this sense I concur with Voss’s view. As vegetal modules are 
independent, ‘each shoot and part of root pursues its own trajectory of intentionality’, and yet 
are part of the whole plant, this chapter is both separate to the linear narrative and yet 
intrinsically related to it, blurring the boundaries between these two modes.133 Furthermore, 
 
130 Tony Voss, ‘”How Are These Things Related That Such Deep Union Should Exist Between Them All?”: The 
Textual Integrity of The Story of an African Farm’, Journal of Literary Studies, 27(1) (2011), 43-64 (p.59). 
131 Dorothy Driver, ‘Invoking indigeneity: Olive Schreiner and the poetics of plants’, The Journal of 
Commonwealth Literature, 56(1) (2021), 61-76 (p.66). 
132 Voss, p.59. 
133 Michael Marder, ‘Plant Intentionality and the Phenomenological Framework of Plant Intelligence’, Plant 
Signalling and Behaviour, 7.11 (2012), 1365-1372 (p.1370). 
 
140 
 
as elements of Waldo’s story are reiterated in the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter, this 
emulates the vegetal’s repetition of form with each material reiteration being different (see 
p.21). Schreiner draws attention to this within humanity:  
As man differs from man, so differ these souls’ years. The most material life is 
not devoid of them; the story of the most spiritual is told in them. And it may 
chance that some, looking back, see the past cut out after this fashion (AF 137).   
 
Only ‘some, looking back’ see the development of the soul as Schreiner articulates in this 
chapter and yet all will experience something akin to this, even ‘the most material life’. Such 
a juxtaposing of same and yet different reflects Schreiner’s emphasis at the end of the 
chapter on the similarities between beings: ‘all is part of a whole’ (AF 154).    
 
The Hunter allegory in the following chapter, extends this vegetal-like theme of same but 
different. It reiterates some of the themes of the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter but it is also 
different. Like the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter, it combines individual and collective 
development. But it is different in that it presents the gradual discoveries of humanity as a 
collective endeavour that progresses over time, achieved through the efforts of individual 
after individual. The relationship between the two can perhaps best be expressed by Lyndall: 
one can ‘trace the analogy there always is between the progress and development of one 
individual and of a whole nation, or again, between a single nation and the entire human 
race’ (AF 198). Moreover, the Hunter allegory is both part of African Farm and yet has its 
own independent meaning, evident in the fact that it was also published separately in 
Schreiner’s first collection of allegories (1890). 
 
In the Hunter allegory Schreiner explicitly articulates that collective achievement is of greater 
importance than that of the individual, although that for the individual, this is difficult to 
appreciate. The allegory portrays humankind’s progression, stage by stage, in its discovery 
of the ‘Truth’. A series of ‘hunters’ cut steps into the mountainside, each step representing 
141 
 
the progression of knowledge. Upon these steps the following man can mount and add his 
contribution, and so on, and so on: 
Where I lie down worn out other men will stand, young and fresh. By the steps 
that I have cut they will climb; by the stairs that I have built they will mount. 
They will never know the name of the man who made them […] they will mount, 
and on my work; they will climb, and by my stair! […] And no man liveth to 
himself, and no man dieth to himself (AF 168, emphasis in original). 
 
The allegorical anonymity of these hunters stresses the relative unimportance of the 
individual and yet the repeated and emphatic ‘my’ of this specific ‘Hunter’ shows a human 
urge to proclaim his own achievement. But the final recourse to St Paul (Romans 14.7) 
implies that he can justify the sacrifice of his body in aid of the progress of the collective body 
of humanity. Fittingly, Schreiner is using an allegory to articulate what is inherent in the form 
itself: that through the story of individuals, it represents a greater meaning. 
 
Schopenhauer’s philosophy is a useful lens for reading the relationship between individual 
and collective in the Hunter allegory. In fact, Schreiner recognises a passage of 
Schopenhauer’s work as reading ‘like a paraphrase of my allegory in the African Farm’ and 
this seems refer to the Hunter allegory.134 In this letter, Schreiner is commenting on a 
passage in Helen Zimmern’s life of Schopenhauer and the passage to which it is likely that 
she is referring is ‘on the search for philosophic truth’, in which Schopenhauer characterises 
philosophy as ‘an alpine road’ with a ’precipitous path’. As the philosopher climbs, ‘the 
lonelier, the more desolate grows the way’ but he must ‘know no fear’ and ‘leave everything 
behind him’, having ‘to cut his own path’ until he ‘stands amid clear fresh air’ where he can 
‘behold the sun when all beneath is still shrouded in the blackness of night’.135 This 
resembles Schreiner’s lonely Hunter who climbs until he ‘stepped into the broad sunshine’ 
while ‘far below rolled the white mist over the valleys of superstition’ (AF 166). Schreiner 
 
134 Olive Schreiner, letter to Havelock Ellis, 2nd March 1885, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 29th August 
2022]. 
135 Zimmern, p.37. 
142 
 
recognises Schopenhauer’s philosopher as akin to her hunters who strive through hardship 
to attain truth and extend humanity’s knowledge.   
 
The Hunter allegory can also be read as an allegorical reworking of Schopenhauer’s 
development of the will. Schopenhauer asserts that the will progresses through a series of 
individual manifestations, the individual being merely a conduit for it. In Schreiner’s allegory, 
each successive individual hunter contributes to the progress of the ‘will’, but what is of prime 
importance is the progress of the whole. Reading the Hunter allegory from this perspective 
highlights how Schreiner conceptualises a linear progression over time across a series of 
individuals. Schopenhauer’s notion of the will as a ‘blind’ force that has no knowledge of itself 
is also a useful perspective. In the Hunter allegory, the figure of Wisdom states that although 
each hunter is ‘the child of The-Accumulated-Knowledge-of-Ages’, in striking out ‘a path for 
himself […] my voice he hears no more.’ (AF 163). The blind hunter cannot foresee or benefit 
from his contribution to truth, he can only participate.   
 
Through her use of form and structure Schreiner incorporates complex structural patterns 
throughout African Farm that include linearity, cyclicity, and repetition. Scholars have 
variously interpreted such patterns. In his article on the ‘textual integrity’ of African Farm 
(2011), Voss says that the sequences that he traces ‘are offered as illustrations of the 
integrity of the text, that the novel has a plot’, taking on board critique of its structure as 
‘desultory’.136 Cherry Clayton views African Farm’s structure as ‘built on a pattern of 
repetition and variation, parallels and contrasts, at every level’.137 I extend this to argue that 
these patterns can be read as ontologically vegetal, reflective of Marder’s conceptualisation 
of vegetal growth as both linear and cyclical, with repetitions that are yet different each time.  
 
 
136 Voss, p.59. 
137 Cherry Clayton, Olive Schreiner (New York, Twayne Publishers, 1997), p.48. 
143 
 
These structural patterns exist within the linear narrative and within the abstract sections as 
well as forming complex patterns that cut across these two narrative modes. The seemingly 
linear narrative of Waldo’s development incorporates cyclicities. The development of his 
thinking is not a straightforward journey from loss of faith to a recognition of material 
connectivity across all lifeforms but incorporates loops and diversions. On returning to the 
farm after his journey, his relationship to the material world is different to that of before in 
that it now incorporates a more mature understanding of connectivity. The linear 
development of his thought through the novel is partially recapitulated in the final two 
chapters, and yet this is different in that it is rooted in Lyndall’s death and extends further. 
Waldo and Lyndall’s stories contain elements that are similar to each other and yet are 
different. Both are dissatisfied with life’s limitations, go on journeys to search for a better way 
of life, and perish. Yet their dissatisfactions, journeys and deaths are distinctive: Lyndall’s 
journey is that of a feminist striving for emancipation whereas Waldo’s is a search for the 
truth.  
 
Across the linear narrative and abstract sections, Schreiner interweaves events, themes, 
and ideas. Waldo comes to a similar conclusion in the penultimate chapter about the relative 
unimportance of the individual in favour of the whole that Schreiner has already articulated in 
the Hunter allegory. The development of Waldo’s thinking, with its cyclical back and forth 
between spirituality and materiality is similar to that of the universalised soul of the ‘Times 
and Seasons’ chapter. In both accounts it is not until an understanding is reached that 
incorporates both material and metaphysical connectivity that any satisfaction is 
experienced. Yet these models of unity are different. I could continue: such an account of the 
cyclicities and reiterations in African Farm is by no means exhaustive. All is connected yet all 
is singular.  
 
Although Schreiner uses an omniscient narrator, she also employs other voices. Waldo’s 
stranger tells the Hunter allegory, Waldo tells the story of his journey in a letter, Gregory 
144 
 
Rose tells the story of Lyndall’s final days and within that story, Lyndall’s landlady tells some 
of the story to Gregory Rose. I read these voices as emulating Marder’s vegetal’s ‘dispersed 
intentionality’ in which each vegetal module has its own intentionality and yet contributes to 
the unity of the whole plant.138 Schreiner’s use of multivocality enables her to complicate 
simple notions of truth: each voice presents its own individual truth as each growing part of 
the plant pursues its own course. It is only when these intentionalities are unified by the 
whole book (or plant), that an overarching unity is achieved.  
 
Another structural feature of African Farm that reflects vegetal spatiality is the importance of 
the middle. Schreiner positions the two abstract sections at the centre of the novel. Although 
they begin Part Two, they are chapters fourteen and fifteen of twenty-nine chapters and as 
such, occupy the very middle of the novel. I have already explored the importance of the 
vegetal middle in Hardy’s poetry. I now offer a reading of African Farm in which the structural 
middle also emulates vegetal growth.  
 
It is not causally necessary that these sections are placed in the middle of the novel. The 
events of the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter do not lead on sequentially from events at the 
end of Part One. While this chapter does reiterate Waldo’s development up to this point, it 
also goes on to foreshadow his subsequent progress. The concluding material and 
metaphysical image of the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter is not reflected in a comparable 
image in the linear narrative until the penultimate chapter. The Hunter allegory with its 
message of the importance of collective endeavour is likewise not narratively essential at 
this specific point in the novel.   
 
 
138 Michael Marder, ‘Plant Intentionality and the Phenomenological Framework of Plant Intelligence’, Plant 
Signalling and Behaviour, 7.11 (2012), 1365-1372 (p.1369).  
 
145 
 
Schreiner’s positioning of these sections at the middle of African Farm permits a more 
organic and less linear reading of the novel which works from the central axis outwards. The 
two abstract sections can be seen as a trunk from which the story branches in two 
directions. In Part One of the novel, Waldo moves from religious faith towards materiality. In 
Part Two, his development is in the opposite direction, moving away from a marked reliance 
on materiality towards a synthesis of materiality and metaphysics. Reading from the centre 
outwards then, these branches extend from materiality towards the metaphysical beginning 
and end of the book (albeit with much back and forth along the way). The pivotal ‘Times and 
Season’s chapter at the centre reiterates this movement in a universalised individual and the 
rest of the novel shows how this plays out in the individual life of Waldo. Consistent with this 
interpretation is Schreiner’s placing of her tree of life image at the very centre of the novel, at 
the end of the first of the two abstract sections. Moreover, this image itself considers the 
connectivity across life as ‘the fine branches of one trunk’ (AF 153). I examine this tree of life 
image in detail later in this chapter, but for current purposes I note that it is a model of 
connectivity that amalgamates the material and the metaphysical. Schreiner therefore 
structurally reinforces this fusion at the centre of African Farm. The Hunter allegory likewise 
embeds at the centre of her novel her other central concern: the relationship between 
individual and collective.  
 
II. Death, Transience, and the Individual 
 
In the Hunter allegory, Schreiner asserts the relative unimportance of the individual life in 
comparison with the collective human body. Akin to this, in the linear narrative Schreiner 
portrays Waldo’s journey towards an acceptance of the necessity for life to be transient. In 
African Farm death is pervasive. In the first chapter Waldo lies awake at night obsessed by 
the sheer number of human deaths that have occurred throughout human history, and the 
constantly ticking clock reminds him of the relentless movement towards finality. There are 
many individual deaths: the two heroes, Waldo and Lyndall; Waldo’s father; Lyndall’s baby. 
146 
 
Before the novel begins many close relatives have died: Lyndall’s parents, Waldo’s mother, 
Em’s father, and Tant’ Sannie’s two husbands. Death also pervades the natural world. The 
rocks ‘are built up of a life that was’ (AF 290), the ground spider’s enemy falls into its trap 
(AF 152), and the dung beetle is killed by Waldo’s dog (AF 107). Yet the movement in 
African Farm is towards an acceptance of these individual deaths, gained through an 
understanding that for life as a whole to continue, the individual must die.  
 
In the penultimate chapter Schreiner portrays Waldo’s struggle to reconcile himself to the 
death of Lyndall. He contemplates the ‘dreams’ (belief systems) through which humanity has 
tried to accept the horror of death. The first two of these metaphysical ‘dreams’ reflect 
Christian notions of life after death and a third is that of the ‘Transcendentalist’s high answer’ 
(AF 288).139 These all assure Waldo that ‘you shall see her again’ (AF 288). But the problem 
for Waldo is that these dreams locate the dead Lyndall in the metaphysical realm. An 
immortal spiritualised Lyndall is not sufficient for the bereft Waldo; he craves her material 
manifestation. For Waldo, ‘change is death’ and ‘immortality is annihilation’ (AF 289). Even 
the biblically evoked material flow between living organisms, the notion that ‘the body never 
dies, because it turns again to grass and flowers’, is problematic as it is as much a 
transformation as the doctrine that ‘the spirit never dies’ (AF 289).   
 
At this point in Waldo’s story, his perspective is material. He struggles to accept that Lyndall 
can no longer participate in the cycles of the material world: ‘in none of them all would she 
have part! […] She would exist no more’ (AF 287). His quasi-scientific understanding of the 
 
139 The transcendentalist, Ralph Waldo Emerson, was an important influence on the young Schreiner. It is 
generally assumed that the name ‘Waldo’ is rooted in this influence and Schreiner’s decision to publish African 
Farm under the nom de plume of Ralph Iron may also show Emerson’s impact. Many scholars have traced his 
ideas in Schreiner’s work generally, and African Farm in particular, and these include: Jade Munslow Ong in 
Olive Schreiner and African Modernism: Allegory, Empire and Postcolonial Writing, (Abingdon and London, 
Routledge, 2017); Ruth Knechtel in ‘Olive Schreiner’s Pagan Animism: An Underlying Unity’, English Literature 
in Translation, 53(3) (2010), 259-282; and Simon Lewis in ‘The Transnational Circulation of Dissent: Olive 
Schreiner and the Colonial Counter-flows of Unitarian Freethinking’ The Journal of South African and American 
Studies, 14(1) (2013), 1-15. 
147 
 
world, gained through close observation of materiality, means that he struggles to accept the 
ameliorating ‘dreams’ of immortality due to the lack of evidence for them. It is therefore 
unsurprising that his attitude to these metaphysical dreams is that they ‘blind reason, and 
crush out thought’ as they ‘take any lie, any creed’ to provide a ‘Bridge over Death’ (AF 287). 
To accept such dreams would mean that he would have to reject his scientific rationalism.   
 
He concludes that humans have created a ‘hereafter’ as, trapped within the perspective of 
their own lifespan, they cannot perceive any value in transience: 
There must be a hereafter because a man longs for it? […] There must be a 
Hereafter because we cannot think of any end to life. Can we think of a 
beginning? […] where were we ninety years ago? (AF 289-90). 
 
Humankind’s limited temporal perspective blocks their ability to imagine a longer-term 
perspective and traps them into thinking that the beginning and end of life is framed by their 
own existence.  
 
However, the scientifically inclined Waldo is eventually able to rationalise the necessity for 
death. Although he has previously rejected material flows, now he reassesses such ideas 
through scientifically rational thought based upon material evidence: 
All dies, all dies! the roses are red with the matter that once reddened the cheek 
of the child; the flowers bloom the fairest on the last year’s battle-ground; the 
work of death’s finger cunningly wreathed over is at the heart of all things, even 
of the living […] The rocks are built up of a life that was (AF 290). 
 
In connecting the redness of the roses with the redness of the child’s cheek, Waldo accepts 
the flow of material elements between vegetal and human. Cognisant of the fact that some 
flowers flourish on battle-grounds, he appreciates the vegetal’s ability to reuse matter and 
perform, what Marder terms, “‘material “resurrection”’ (MM 67). So instead of seeing change 
as death, Waldo now acknowledges that change is essential to life and that change involves 
death: ‘the work of death’s finger […] is at the heart of all things’. Schreiner here anticipates 
148 
 
Morton’s reflection that ‘all life forms are the mesh, and so are all dead ones, as are their 
habitats, which are also made up of living and non-living beings’.140  
 
This acknowledgement of the necessity for change is, however, still not enough to reconcile 
Waldo to Lyndall’s death. But eventually, in extremis, as ‘his soul passed down the steps of 
contemplation’ and ‘loses all consciousness of its little self’, Waldo is enabled to leave 
behind pure rationality and acknowledge the efficacy of such dreams. However, material 
evidence is not entirely discounted. For Waldo, what is important is that ‘the dream be not in 
too glaring discord with the thing they know’ (AF 290).  
 
The penultimate chapter concludes with one of the clearest accounts across all of 
Schreiner’s work of her conception of ‘Universal Unity’. Waldo finally understands that it is in 
the synthesis of the phenomenal and the noumenal that unity can be found, and through this 
he is enabled to realise: 
the old mystery of Universal Unity that surrounds it. “No death, no death” […] 
there is that which never dies – which abides. It is but the individual that 
perishes, the whole remains. It is the organism that vanishes, the atoms are 
there. It is but the man that dies, the Universal Whole of which he is part re-
works him into its inmost self […] that of which he is but the breath has 
breathed him forth and drawn him back again. That abides – we abide. For the 
little soul that cries aloud for continued personal existence for itself and its 
beloved, there is no help. For the soul which knows itself no more as a unit, but 
as a part of the Universal Unity of which the Beloved also is a part; which feels 
within itself the throb of the Universal Life; for that soul there is no death (AF 
290).  
 
This model of connectivity and an immortality that is not of the individual, is both material and 
metaphysical. The movement of atoms describes material flows between lifeforms and is 
therefore acceptable to the scientific Waldo. But Waldo goes further and recognises a 
metaphysical realm behind such flows. As such, Schopenhauer’s philosophy which also 
amalgamates materiality and metaphysics, is a helpful lens for interpreting Schreiner’s own 
 
140 Morton, p.29 
149 
 
synthesis. For Schopenhauer, the metaphysical will is the underlying force behind reality, 
and material phenomena its manifestation. The death of the individual provides the matter 
necessary for the continuation of life, and the will, in successive manifestations (WWR1 277). 
The will exists separately to materiality, and yet supplies the metaphysical impetus behind all 
existence. Schreiner also purports a metaphysical force, but hers is in the realm of the ‘soul’ 
and the ‘Beloved’. Rather than a continuous force that moves on from one life to the next as 
does Schopenhauer’s, Schreiner’s metaphysic seems located in a region, the ‘Universal 
Whole’, to which the soul returns after death. This is the Schreiner who, having lost any 
conventional faith, retains a belief in a God-like noumenal; Schopenhauer famously 
dispenses with the need for such a figure in his philosophy.141     
 
It is only through leaving behind consciousness and self that Waldo is enabled to perceive 
the noumenal behind the material. In this sense, he becomes more vegetal-like: without 
consciousness or self. There is some closeness here between Hardy and Schreiner’s 
problematisation of consciousness. In both The Return of the Native and The Woodlanders, 
Hardy infers that the mind is a barrier to an understanding of our material connectivity to 
other lifeforms. Schreiner suggests something comparable although for her, what it prevents 
is the understanding of a noumenal connectivity. It is only when there is no ‘personal 
existence’ or seeing oneself ‘as a unit’, that one can recognise a plant-like continuity and 
connection across life. However, even then, Schreiner contends that we can only ‘almost’ 
gain access to the ‘mystery’ of ‘Universal Unity’. It remains tantalisingly out of reach of the 
thinking mind.  
 
In an analogous point to that made by Hardy in ‘At the Royal Academy’, Schreiner purports 
that art and books are attempts to transcend human ephemerality. It is through books that 
Waldo discovers that others in the past have thought like him, appreciating their ability to 
 
141 I explore Schreiner’s conception of God in more detail in the following chapter. 
150 
 
communicate over time. Likewise, the continued existence of the Bushman paintings 
continues to communicate. However, Schreiner’s emphasis on the materiality of books 
serves to undermine their permanence. When Waldo finds a store of books, before reading 
them, ‘he felt them, thrust his fingers in among the leaves, and crumpled them a little’ (AF 
109). Lyndall reminds us that ‘the books that our grandmothers fed on the mould is eating’ 
(AF 217), and when Blenkins and Tant’ Sannie burn Waldo’s book, they do so to ensure that 
it has ‘gone out of existence’ (AF 114).  
 
The image of book pages as ‘leaves’ emphasises their materiality and allies them to the 
vegetal. But the vegetal is also used metaphorically with respect to books. Waldo surmises 
that ‘when great men blossom into books […] the world of men too opens beautifully, leaf 
after leaf’ (AF 299). In this way, Schreiner links the materiality of the leaves of the book with 
the metaphorical image of the gradual development of the ideas that these leaves embed. 
Both images, the literal leaf and metaphorical blossom, embed transience. And yet their 
materiality enables the ideas they contain to be transmitted over periods of time longer than 
the human life. Schreiner therefore characterises the book as vegetal-like in its blurring of the 
boundary between ephemerality and permanence.  
 
Schreiner further implies that the relative longevity of books, as compared to their authors, 
can also be problematic in that they preserve ideas that would perhaps be better
