superseded. Lyndall asserts that ‘we know that no man’s work is great and stands for ever.  
Moses is dead, and the prophets […] they are milestones that the world has passed’ (AF 
217). Schreiner suggests that the ‘dreams’ of humankind are and should be temporally 
bound: ‘so age succeeds age, and dream succeeds dream’ and ‘the generation that follows 
will have its own’ (AF 291). Knowledge and ideas progress and the ‘milestones’ become 
supplanted. Vitality lies in transience and progress, and the perishable nature of books is 
perhaps apt.  
 
 
151 
 
III. Waldo Becomes Rooted and Heteronomous 
 
In Part II I considered how, through the death of Lyndall, Waldo realises the lack of 
importance of the material individual and the ‘Universal Unity’ to be found in a material and 
metaphysical wholeness. In this part, I consider another journey towards understanding 
undertaken by Waldo. The journey is both physical – he leaves the farm to discover the 
wider world – and an existential search for meaning. However, what he comes to understand 
is the importance of rootedness and his place within the material connectivity across all 
lifeforms. 
 
Waldo’s journey incorporates elements of the bildungsroman genre. However, his 
explorations bring him no answers, his experience of the wider world is consistently 
negative, and at the end he rejects society rather than finding his place within it. Other 
scholars have also assessed Waldo’s journey as a problematic bildungsroman. Elizabeth 
Hope Chang suggests that Waldo and Lyndall’s ‘unnaturally elongated youth terminated by 
abrupt death marks a world unreceptive to […] progressive futures’.142 Esty reads it as a 
modernist revision of the ‘protocols of the nineteenth-century bildungsroman’ that 
demonstrates the lack of progress of the subject peoples of imperial capitalism, being 
unready for self-government.143 I concur with both Chang and Esty that Schreiner’s 
bildungsroman embodies a lack of individual progress, but argue that Schreiner’s use and 
undermining of this genre reflects a more radical position. Waldo’s journey leads to him 
eventually finding his place within the wider world of connectivity across all living beings and 
through his story, Schreiner critiques the very notion of individualistic progress, particularly 
that of the isolated human. Furthermore, I read Waldo’s journey as one in which he moves 
towards finding satisfaction in a vegetal mode of being in which rootedness and a 
heteronomous relationship with alterity takes the place of egoistic ambition. I suggest that 
 
142 Elizabeth Hope Chang, Novel Cultivations: Plants in British Literature of the Global Nineteenth Century 
(Charlottesville, University of Virginia Press, 2019), p.129. 
143 Esty, p.407; pp.410-15. 
152 
 
Schreiner offers this mode of being as an alternative to human desires to strive for individual 
progress and development, values inherent in the genre of the bildungsroman.  
 
Even before his existential journey, Waldo is presented as vegetal in his rootedness at the 
farm, in materiality, and within nature. His role as a herder means that he spends most of his 
time outside. In his first appearance in Part Two of the novel, he is represented as 
surrounded by materiality: sand, thorn-trees, ostriches, and the ‘brown serene earth’. This is 
intensified through physical contact: he ‘lay on his stomach on the red sand’. Prepositions 
also embed him within this material world: ‘the small ostriches he herded wandered about 
him’; ‘on his right lay the graves; to his left the dam; in his hand was a large wooden post’; 
Doss lay before him’ (AF 155, my emphases). In this same chapter, the stranger recognises 
the value of Waldo’s rooted existence, noticing that ‘it seemed that as the fellow’s dark eyes 
looked across the brown earth they kindled with an intense satisfaction’ (AF 157). In 
contrast, he bitterly describes his own rootless existence as ‘a man who believes nothing, 
hopes nothing, fears nothing, feels nothing […] no criterion of what you should be who lives 
here among your ostriches and bushes’ (AF 159). 
 
Rootlessness in African Farm is also associated with colonialism as a cause of displacement 
from the land. Em, the only one of the three main characters who remains on the farm 
throughout the novel, and who might be viewed as illustrating the benefits of rootedness, is a 
beneficiary of colonialism. She will inherit the farm ‘and everything that is upon it for [her] 
own’ (AF 46). Such ownership has displaced or uprooted, the indigenous population and the 
black characters in the novel exist only in servitude to the colonial population. Most of the 
other characters are also colonials or immigrants. Waldo and his father are German, Tant’ 
Sannie is a Boer, Gregory Rose is English, and Em herself is at least half-English. In fact, 
Schreiner satirises exaggerated claims to nationality with the absurd and yet contemptible 
Bonaparte Blenkins changing his nationality to suit circumstances, and the ridiculing of 
153 
 
Gregory Rose’s noble descent. However, all the rootless immigrants seem to lead rather 
dissatisfying lives. 
 
We could even read humanity as orphaned from the land. If we rethink rootedness in terms 
of genetic connections, no family is intact and the three main characters are or become 
orphans: Lyndall is an orphan, Em only has a step-parent, and by chapter eight Waldo is 
also an orphan. Surnames are missing with only those of Blenkins and Rose disclosed. 
Schreiner’s humanity is therefore unrooted in more than one sense. It is only when Waldo 
returns home, that rootedness is presented as a source of satisfaction. Yet as Waldo is 
himself an immigrant, from a colonial perspective this remains problematic.  
 
Allied to rootedness is heteronomy and I have already explored a heteronomous mode of 
being with respect to Clym’s return to Egdon Heath in Chapter One. It is productive to 
similarly assess Waldo’s journey from self-determination to heteronomy. Before his journey, 
Waldo is dissatisfied with life at the farm. The stranger advises him that ‘you are happy to be 
here! Stay where you are’, but Waldo wonders ‘was his delightful one ridiculing him? Here, 
with this brown earth and these low hills, while the rare wonderful world lay all beyond. 
Fortunate to be here!’ (AF 171-2). Marder asserts that ‘the human sets itself over and 
against its environment, driving a wedge in the unit of survival, wherein it participates’, and 
therefore ‘undermines itself’, resulting in ‘alienation’ (MM 167). Consciousness indeed 
alienates Waldo from an appreciation of alterity and causes dissatisfaction with what he sees 
as his limited existence on the farm. As a result, he uproots himself in search of experience, 
meaning and truth. The ensuing failure of this physical and existential journey and his 
cyclical return to the farm is framed through his growing understanding of the value of a 
rooted and heteronomous relationship with that environment and his connection to all other 
lifeforms.   
 
154 
 
The wiser, but no less alienated, Lyndall articulates this plight. She sees Waldo’s yearning to 
gain knowledge and an understanding of life’s meaning as a ‘poison, not a food’ (AF 217). 
She recognises the same problematic yearning within herself even though her own journey 
is to escape from patriarchal norms. Before she leaves the farm for the second and final 
time, she kneels at Otto’s grave and laments: ‘There is light, there is warmth, […] why am I 
alone, so hard, so cold? I am so weary of myself! It is eating my soul to its core – self, self, 
self! […] Will nothing free me from myself?’ (AF 241). Her focus on ‘self’ prevents her from 
accessing the ‘light’ and ‘warmth’ that is available in her surroundings, and which a vegetal 
being could and must access. 
 
It is not until Waldo has tried and failed to satisfy the demands of consciousness, that he 
realises that self-determination does not create satisfaction, and on his return to the farm, 
has learnt the value of a more heteronomous and connected mode of being. In the final 
chapter, he becomes part of the scene of chickens, flowers, butterflies, and goats, as he 
‘looked at it all and smiled’. He also experiences joy in the nowness of hetero-temporal 
existence: ‘to live on so, year after year, how well! Always in the present; letting each day 
glide’. As he ‘chuckled with intense inward satisfaction as the old hens had done’, he also 
rejects any sense of superiority and accepts equality with the materiality that surrounds him. 
He offers a ‘friendly greeting’ to the ice-plant and characterises the chickens as ‘brother life’ 
(AF 297-300).  
 
He also observes humanity’s material similarities to the natural world. He sees how in the 
thorn-pods ‘the little seeds suck their life through the delicate curled-up string, and how the 
little embryo sleeps inside!’. In looking ‘deep into the hearts of flowers’, he observes ‘how 
lovingly the pistil and the stamens nestle there together’ (AF 299). Rather than reading these 
as anthropomorphic, they show Waldo’s recognition of the same material processes across 
lifeforms. 
 
155 
 
Waldo therefore comes to a different ending than does Clym in The Return of the Native. 
Clym struggles to disregard his superior position in a hierarchy of lifeforms, reflecting the 
difficulty of this for humankind. However, Waldo becomes able to accept this. Perhaps it is 
that Waldo’s awareness of both a material and metaphysical connectivity enables a more 
profound understanding of the equality of all living beings. Certainly, unlike Clym, he is able 
‘to raise the status of the materiality of which we are composed’ (JB 12, emphasis in 
original). In this sense, Waldo’s death at the end of this chapter is also a completion as he 
joins the long-term continuity of life itself.  
 
In fact, Lyndall has always recognised Waldo as allied to the vegetal. Although he is judged 
by most other characters as of little worth, Lyndall perceives his value and characterises him 
as ‘like a thorn-tree, which grows up very quietly, without any one’s caring for it, and one day 
suddenly breaks out into yellow blossoms’ (AF 231). Blossom can be viewed as the 
culminating achievement of the tree and Marder reflects upon how such analogies have 
often been used to link the flower, spatially positioned at the top of a plant, to idealism, 
spirituality or even consciousness (MM 61). But Marder also reminds us that these are 
anthropocentric interpretations (MM 62) and likewise, I read Lyndall’s image differently. For a 
plant the flower is no more important than any other part of its lifecycle. The flower will 
produce seeds which will produce a new plant which will produce flowers, and so on ad 
infinitum. When Lyndall’s image is read as a whole, it is reflective of process and continuous, 
often unnoticed, growth. In this sense, ‘blossom’ is reconceived as a verb - ‘to blossom’ - 
rather than a noun that indicates attainment. Lyndall’s image sees Waldo as part of the 
material world’s continuing process rather than enacting a culminating achievement. Such a 
reading also reflects Schreiner’s commitment to collective processes rather than individual 
achievement.    
 
Schreiner suggests, however, that Lyndall, the feminist, cannot thrive through a 
heteronomous relationship to alterity. For women, alterity is a repressive patriarchal society. 
156 
 
In her diatribe against such societal oppressions, Lyndall links adaptability to the 
environment to the female plight:  
It is not what is done to us, but what is made of us […] that wrongs us. No man 
can be really injured but by what modifies himself. We all enter the world little 
plastic beings […] and the world tells us what we are to be, and shapes us by 
the ends it sets before us. To you it says – Work! and to us it says – Seem! […] 
And so the world makes men and women (AF 188, emphasis in original).   
Schreiner uses the idea of plasticity to articulate how humans are shaped and modified by 
the social world. For the vegetal, modular growth enables plasticity and adaptation to 
environmental conditions. But for women, a heteronomous relationship to social externality 
only serves to restrict as it does not supply the conditions under which to flourish. In fact, her 
subsequent use of the terms ‘atrophied’ and ‘compressed’ (AF 189) to describe women’s 
responses to oppression, recalls the response of plants to poor environmental conditions. In 
this sense, vegetal being and heteronomy is untenable for Lyndall. 
 
IV. Restoring Meaning to the Material 
 
In both Waldo’s story and the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter, Schreiner addresses humanity’s 
search for meaning. In both contexts, after the character rejects a conventional Christian 
faith, they find solace in the recognition of a connectivity across material nature. But what 
this fails to replace is meaning. I have already explored how Hardy grapples with this, and 
how, for him, meaning becomes located within the material and the recognition of material 
connectivity without the need for anything more. However, for Schreiner, it is through a 
recognition and understanding of the lifeforce as that which connects all beings that she 
comes to realise that there is meaning in materiality. 
 
In the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter the processes that connect all lifeforms are noticed. The 
corresponding shapes of rock patterns, blood vessels and tree shapes are understood as 
formed by growth processes. The ‘delicate silver tracery’ in the rocks resembles ‘leaves and 
157 
 
branches.’ Blood vessels possess similar shapes. When a gander is dissected, its blood 
vessels seem like ‘a trunk, bifurcating and rebifurcating’, resembling ‘that same exact shape 
and outline [as] our thorn-tree seen against the sky in mid-winter’ (AF 152-3).   
 
Close observation reveals ecological relationships between lifeforms. Plants are seen as 
dwelling places for insect species. The heart of the bitto flower that had been ‘a mere blur of 
yellow’ is now seen to be not only ‘a hundred perfect flowers’ but also ‘the homes of the tiny 
black people with red stripes, who move in and out of that little yellow city’. Likewise, ‘every 
bluebell has its inhabitant’. On a ‘karroo-bush a green fly is laying her silver eggs’. The 
ground-spider must ‘make its trap, bury itself in the sand, and then wait for the falling in of its 
enemy’ (AF 152). These ecological interactions show the processes of reproduction, 
nourishment, and death that occur across lifeforms. 
 
Process becomes synonymous with life. Bennett’s exploration of two meanings to ‘nature’ is 
useful here. She draws upon Spinoza’s distinction between natura naturata and natura 
naturans. The first of these characterises matter as passive, as thing, and the second, as 
active, and that which creates new forms. Bennett refers to this second meaning as 
‘universal creativity’ and affirms that it is akin to her own concept of ‘vibrant matter’ (JB 117). 
Schreiner’s recognition of the processes occurring across nature demonstrate her 
understanding to be closer to Spinoza’s natura naturans and Bennett’s ‘vibrant matter’ than 
passive conceptualisations of matter.  
 
Such a reading is reinforced in the stranger’s comment to Waldo. He states that ‘whosoever 
should portray truly the life and death of a little flower – its birth, sucking in of nourishment, 
reproduction of its kind, withering and vanishing – would have shaped a symbol of all 
existence’ (AF 169). It is useful to read this image through Marder. Like Bennett, Marder 
characterises ‘nature’ as active. For Marder, the term expresses the ‘internal determination 
of the natural entity’ and he argues that in this sense, existence becomes ‘an emergence out 
158 
 
of itself’. Extending this understanding, he contends that a plant is a ‘synecdochic 
instantiation of universal growth’, or a ‘miniature mirror’ of nature as process (MM 120). This 
is the point that Waldo’s stranger is expressing by using the image of a flower to show that 
its processes of birth, nourishment, reproduction, and death constitute ‘a symbol of all 
existence’. Material processes therefore become the foundation of existence, rather than this 
being located within a ‘higher’ realm. In fact, in this illustration he reflects Schopenhauer’s 
own use of plant life to illustrate how the will can exist without knowledge. 
 
But a conception of existence as merely materiality and material process with no need for a 
‘higher’ realm, and therefore potentially no meaning, is difficult for Schreiner. Although she 
perceives a lifeforce, how it is expressed in materiality seems meaningless. This is evident in 
her description of an interaction between Waldo’s dog (Doss) and a beetle which she 
interprets as purposeless: 
The beetle was hard at work trying to roll home a great ball of dung it had been 
collecting all the morning; but Doss broke the ball, and ate the beetle’s hind 
legs, and then bit off its head. And it was all play, and no one could tell what it 
had lived and worked for. A striving, and a striving, and an ending in nothing 
(AF 107). 
 
The bitterness of Schreiner’s understanding of the lack of purpose or meaning in these 
interactions is extended through her use of the latter part of this (‘And it was all play […] A 
striving, and a striving, and an ending in nothing’) as an epigraph for Part Two of African 
Farm. 
 
Explanations of the lifeforce by both Marder and Schopenhauer also use the word ‘striving’. 
Marder characterises the vegetal’s ‘ceaseless striving’ as reflective of the ‘restlessness of life 
itself’ (MM 162) and Schopenhauer writes that plants reveal a ‘restless, never satisfied 
striving, a ceaseless activity’ (WWR1 309). Whereas Schopenhauer’s conception of such a 
force incorporates the metaphysical will, Marder’s remains fully material. In developing 
Merleau-Ponty’s phenomenological idea that ‘beneath the intentionality of representations 
159 
 
[there is] a deeper intentionality, which others have called existence’ (MM 160), Marder 
develops a notion of corporeal intentionality and sees in vegetative processes a ‘non-
conscious intentionality’ within existence (MM 153). 
 
However, for Schreiner, an explanation of the ‘lifeforce’ that is rooted in materiality is 
insufficient. In the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter she articulates how difficult it is for one to 
dismiss everything previously taught and that the loss of a religious faith leaves a gap in 
meaning. Having previously seen Nature in relation to a ‘deity’ as ‘a poor plastic thing’, she 
now asks ‘was it possible […] to see Nature as she is – the flowing vestment of an 
unchanging reality?’ (AF 150). But Nature as material reality is not enough. When a scientific 
growth experiment is attempted, although it is successful and a plant grows from a seed until 
‘it is higher than our heads’, the problem is that ‘we find no reason for that upward starting’ 
(AF 152-3). It is this lack of purpose in materiality or material processes that remains 
problematic. 
 
Schreiner recognises humankind’s need to find meaning in life and yet, through Waldo, she 
portrays existential search as detrimental to contentment. Lyndall characterises Waldo as 
one that has ‘the desire to become, to know, to do’ which ‘is driving us mad’ (AF 217). He 
later characterises himself as like the sea which ‘never rests; it is always wanting, wanting, 
wanting […] It is always asking a question, and it never gets an answer’ (AF 259). The 
reader is left wondering what route is left. 
 
In this respect, it is helpful to look at how Schreiner articulates a different type of connection 
between lifeforms in the relationship between human and vegetal. As a child, alone and 
feeling ‘wicked’ due to his loss of faith in God, Waldo observes the leaves of the prickly-pear 
and sees that ‘they glinted, and glinted, and glinted, just like his own heart – cold, so hard, 
and very wicked’. After crying, these ‘wicked leaves, blinked, and blinked, and blinked at 
him’, resulting in him revealing to them his ‘secret’: that ‘I hate God!’ (AF 42). Waldo feels 
160 
 
some form of sympathy from the prickly-pear and therefore feels emboldened to 
communicate what he has withheld from his fellow humans. Likewise, in the ‘Times and 
Seasons’ chapter, the child, sitting amongst bushes, kisses them ‘and they love us back: it 
seems as though they were alive’ (AF 139). The child experiences some form of kinship 
between himself and the plants.  
 
If these could be read as childish experiences of sentiment or imagination, this is dismissed 
when the adult Waldo has a similar experience. As he returns to the farm after his journey, 
on descending into a ravine for water: 
There was one among the trees on the bank that stood out against the white 
sky. All the other trees were silent; but this one shook and trembled against the 
sky. Everything else was still; but those leaves were quivering, quivering […] I 
could not go away […] Does it seem strange to you that it should have made 
me so happy? It is because I cannot tell you how near I felt to things that we 
cannot see but we always feel (AF 262).   
 
Although Waldo is older and more cognisant of material connectivity, he continues to 
experience the vegetal as communicating with him in a different way. It is an experience he 
cannot rationally explain; he can only recognise its effect. 
 
To consider this vegetal/human relationship further, it is instructive to return to Bennett’s 
conceptualisation of an onto-sympathy between vegetal and human that I discussed in 
Chapter Three (see p.79). Although Bennett’s onto-sympathy analyses an ‘overlap between 
materialities’, it is worth looking at some of the reading she explores in this context, 
particularly that of Thoreau’s Walden (1854). He writes that ‘sympathy with the fluttering 
alder and poplar leaves almost takes away my breath’ and that ‘every little pine needle 
expanded and swelled with sympathy and befriended me. I was so distinctly made aware of 
the presence of something kindred to me’. Although Bennett recognises how Thoreau 
locates the ultimate source of this in the divine, she makes a deliberate decision to leave this 
161 
 
behind to focus upon how this is materialised.144 In contrast, I would like to stay with 
Thoreau’s more mystical ideas and acknowledge their closeness to Schreiner’s. Both 
Schreiner and Thoreau recognise a sympathy and kinship between vegetal and human, and 
both also articulate the presence of something that is non-material. 
 
In a letter, Schreiner recalls her own experience of such a presence in the natural world in a 
childhood visionary experience: 
When I was a little child of five and sat alone among the tall weeds at the back 
of our house, this […] perception of the unity of all things, and that they were 
alive, and that I was part of them, was as clear and overpowering to me as it is 
today. It is the one thing I am never able to doubt. 
 
Although she goes on to record her struggles with ‘reconciling this direct perception […] with 
what I was taught’, she stresses that she could not leave it behind: 
I believe scientific knowledge harmonized with my feeling, but I could not shake 
myself free from it if they did not; there would only be discord, as there was of 
old between facts as known to my intellect, and my inevitable perceptions.145 
 
There are similarities between this mystical experience and the relationship between vegetal 
and human that Schreiner articulates in African Farm. In both, Schreiner articulates a 
continuing belief in something within the world of materiality that she can access through 
feelings but cannot fully express. Moreover, she purports the importance of feelings as that 
which can access this noumenal. 
 
In African Farm, Schreiner asserts that allied with a loss of faith is a loss of the validity of 
feelings as a route to understanding. She claims that those who have faith ‘feel Him, we feel 
– that is all!’ (AF 146). But through experiences such as Waldo’s perceived kinship between 
 
144 Jane Bennett, ‘Vegetal Life and Onto-Sympathy’, in Entangled Worlds: Religion, Science, and New 
Materialism, ed. by Catherine Keller and Mary-Jane Rubenstein (New York, Fordham University Press, 2017), 
pp.89-110 (p.90-94). References to Henry David Thoreau from Walden and Civil Disobedience, ed. Owen 
Thomas (New York, Norton & Co., 1966), pp.87-89.  
145 Olive Schreiner, letter to John T Lloyd, 29th October 1892, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 25th February 
2023]. 
162 
 
vegetal and human, and his dreams, Schreiner implies that it is possible to bypass the 
rational intellect and therefore gain a glimpse, however tentative, of an underlying reality that 
restores a meaning to life that is more than material. In an image in which she contrasts 
dreams and rational tangibility, reason is represented through images of disease and 
entrapment. It is a ‘little cankered kingdom of the tangible’, which is surrounded by ‘bars’ that 
are ‘set close about us’ that ‘our wings […] are struck against’. Dreams, however, are 
portrayed as space and freedom. They embrace an ‘infinite peace’ and enable us to ‘glide 
between the bars into the great unknown beyond’ (AF 291).  
 
Schreiner’s idea of ‘the great unknown beyond’ can only be expressed through analogy as 
the point is that it is ineffable. Schreiner’s image of connectivity that comes at the end of the 
‘Times and Seasons’ chapter also allows for the unknowable. In this tree of life image, 
Schreiner suggests that the ‘deep union’ that can be observed in materiality has an 
underlying metaphysical basis: 
How are these things related that such deep union should exist between them 
all? Is it chance? Or, are they not all the fine branches of one trunk, whose sap 
flows through us all? […] This thing we call existence; is it not something which 
has its roots far down below in the dark, and its branches stretching out into the 
immensity above, which we among the branches cannot see? Not a chance 
jumble; a living thing, a One. The thought gives us intense satisfaction, we 
cannot tell why (AF 153, emphasis in original). 
 
Unlike Hardy in The Woodlanders who sees material and metaphysical explanations of 
reality as opposing, Schreiner unites them in this image which has similarity to Waldo’s 
perception of ‘Universal Unity’ that I considered earlier. The ‘Universal Unity’ image stresses 
the return to a unified whole after death whereas the tree of life image, coming as it does 
after the child’s struggle to reconcile materiality and a metaphysical, emphasises the union of 
these separate spheres. Like other mythological and religious ‘tree of life’ images, it also 
physically embodies the connection between material and metaphysical, with its ‘roots far 
down below in the dark’ and its ‘branches stretching out into the immensity above’. Moreover, 
the ’intense satisfaction’ it engenders suggests that such an understanding can restore 
163 
 
meaning to the material world. Bryan Magee contends that Schopenhauer’s philosophy 
suited the needs of those who, having lost their faith, embraced a scientific outlook and yet 
who could not accept that everything could be scientifically explained.146 This reflects the 
value of Schreiner’s combined material and metaphysical image to reinstate meaning.  
 
Schreiner’s use of a vegetal image embeds the metaphysical realm within a living material 
reality. The tree is not a mere dead image; the ‘One’ is a ‘living thing’. Life processes are 
present in the flowing sap that connects all lifeforms, redolent of the lifeforce. Like 
Schopenhauer’s metaphysical will, the metaphysical becomes an intrinsic part of material 
life, rather than existing in a separate idealist sphere.  
 
Schreiner’s image of the tree also explains why for her, humankind cannot fully grasp the 
unity behind the whole. As humanity is embedded within the branch system, we cannot 
perceive the whole from the outside: it is ‘too mighty for our comprehension’ (AF 154). Karen 
Barad’s linking of ontology and epistemology can extend this understanding. Barad states 
that ‘we are a part of that nature that we seek to understand’ (KB 26). She develops this idea 
to make the point that minds themselves are material phenomena that emerge through the 
sort of processes that Schreiner is illustrating in this image (KB 361). The mind that thinks is 
therefore as entrapped within the materiality and processes in Schreiner’s tree as is the 
body. We cannot perceive through a ‘godlike approach whereby we may know [the world] as 
it is “absolutely in itself”’ (KB 125). The mind is not able to observe or understand from a 
position external to the world of materiality and humanity can only resort to metaphor to 
tentatively articulate such ideas, as Schreiner does through this image. Furthermore, through 
expressing this ‘thought’ as a series of questions rather than statements and concluding with 
‘we cannot tell why’, Schreiner resists any definitive explanation. 
 
 
146 Bryan Magee, The Philosophy of Schopenhauer, revised edn. (Oxford, Clarendon Press, 1997), p.429. 
164 
 
After the understanding expressed in the tree of life image in the ‘Times and Seasons’ 
chapter, Schreiner asserts that as we now ‘walk in the great hall of life’ so ‘all is meaningful’ 
(AF 154). At the end of the novel, Waldo himself appreciates that ‘life was a rare and very 
rich thing’ (AF, 299). Both the universal child of the ‘Times and Seasons’ chapter and Waldo 
recognise the meaningfulness to be found in life after they come to an understanding of the 
metaphysical unity behind material connectivity. African Farm ends with the statement, ‘but 
the chickens were wiser’ (AF 390). Although ostensibly this alludes to the fact that the 
chickens realise that Waldo is dead before anyone else, perhaps Schreiner calls the reader’s 
attention here to the point that wisdom and meaning resides in being.  
 
 
165 
 
Chapter Five - ‘Long unbroken lines of connection’: The 
Entanglement of Theory, Context, and Matter in Schreiner’s From 
Man to Man 
 
Between spirit that beats within me and body through which it acts, between 
mind and matter, between man and beast, between beast and plant and plant 
and earth, between the life that has been and the life that is, I am able to see 
nowhere a sharp line of severance, but a great, pulsating, always interacting 
whole.  
(Olive Schreiner, From Man to Man, p.181). 
 
Narrative form is intrinsically ecopolitical: it carries messages of unity and 
equality or isolation and discrimination that run underneath the texts' thematic 
surface.  
(Justyna Kostkowska, Ecocriticism and Women Writers, p.165). 
 
 
From Man to Man, published posthumously in 1926, represents some of Schreiner’s most 
mature thinking on many of the concerns that she had previously explored in her other work, 
including African Farm. Her key theme here is connectivity. What are portrayed as separate 
issues in African Farm: evolution, feminism, relations between the sexes, and societal 
critique are, in From Man to Man, intertwined. In African Farm, Schreiner uses two 
protagonists, Waldo and Lyndall, to respectively voice her evolutionary and feminist 
messages, whereas in From Man to Man, the character of Rebekah addresses both of these 
concerns and this adds to the sense of connectivity. For Schreiner, humanity is ‘a solidarity 
and a whole with all its parts reacting on one another’ (FM 188). This bears some 
resemblance to Hardy’s conception in The Woodlanders of a ‘great web of human doings’, 
but for Hardy the ‘web’ exemplifies the entanglements between people whereas Schreiner’s 
human ‘solidarity’ portrays humanity as a temporal continuum. 
 
In From Man to Man, Schreiner presents humanity as in a process of continuous 
evolutionary progression, a stark contrast to the implication in Hardy that humanity is over-
developed and civilisation detrimental. Schreiner portrays progress and continuity from the 
perspective of a long-term evolutionary framework. Humankind is embedded within, and part 
166 
 
of Timothy Morton’s ‘ecological time and geological time’. Morton suggests that such 
timescales are ‘difficult to grasp intuitively’ but Schreiner’s representation of life as a long-
term continuum makes these timescales, and humanity’s participation in them, noticeable.147 
By positioning the individual within this evolutionary progression, Schreiner emphasises 
one’s responsibility to contribute towards it.  
 
Connectivity is also a structural and formal principle. Although Schreiner uses two 
protagonists, the sisters Rebekah and Bertie, for me it is Rebekah who is the more important 
to Schreiner’s project as she acts as a connecting device between the realist narrative and 
the theoretical sections. As the abstract sections are written or articulated by Rebekah, they 
become more contextualised in the realist narrative than are the abstract sections of African 
Farm and add to, rather than detract from, the cohesiveness and unity of the novel. I argue 
that Schreiner’s emphasis on how the material context and Rebekah’s own corporeality 
influences the theoretical, so it feminises and adaptively strengthens it as theory becomes 
entangled with materiality and dividing lines are blurred.  
 
Rebekah is both a practical and theoretical botanist and the representation of the vegetal is 
at the heart of the novel. In the realist narrative she practices botany in her farming, 
gardening, and scientific experiments. In the theoretical sections her botanical knowledge is 
employed in vegetal ontological analogies through which she foregrounds the importance of 
the collective, the continuity of life, and the values these perspectives engender. Through 
Rebekah, Schreiner reinforces the importance of context in another sense with Rebekah 
having to adapt to the context of the patriarchal environment. Rebekah therefore links 
abstract and realist form, theory and practice, and alterity and adaptation to that alterity, 
demonstrating how material contexts are agentially entangled with characters, events, and 
theory. Moreover, as Schreiner represents Rebekah’s theory as inextricably influenced by 
 
147 Morton, p.42. 
167 
 
her life experience, I read the abstract sections as reflective of plant modules rooted in and 
influenced by alterity. Rebekah is also plant-like in her own adaptations to the patriarchal 
environment, her theories changing in response to conditions. 
 
This chapter is divided into four parts. I begin by exploring Schreiner’s use of form as it is 
pivotal to an understanding of the connected narrative of From Man to Man. With Rebekah 
as creator of the abstract sections, I argue that Schreiner successfully amalgamates 
experimental and realist narrative, brokering a new form in which realist context impacts on 
theory, and vice versa, creating a cohesiveness across the novel. I also assert that through 
foregrounding the material processes involved in the creation of the theoretical sections, 
Schreiner blurs the boundaries and connects theory and practice, and mind and matter, 
through a femininely divergent and adaptive practice. In Part II, I examine how Schreiner 
uses a long-term evolutionary framework to conceptualise humanity as a continuous 
collective progressive body. I explore how she articulates this through a series of vegetal 
analogies which she also uses to critique the hierarchical nature of hegemonic society. I 
recognise how Schreiner’s emphasis on the importance of evolutionary adaptation to 
environment is related to the importance of context in the formal narrative strategy. In Part III 
I focus upon the character of Rebekah and how she is used to create thematic and formal 
connections across the novel. I demonstrate how Rebekah is plant-like in how she entangles 
theory and practice, thinking and acting. I contend that through her adaptation to the 
environment, Schreiner reinforces the agency of the entanglement between material context 
and individual. In the final part of the chapter, I consider Schreiner's evocation of a lifeforce 
and a creative force, constructing them as analogous continuous drives that are derived from 
mysterious and unexplainable sources. I explore how Schreiner’s lifeforce connects all 
beings as well as connecting materiality and the noumenal. In my analysis of Schreiner’s 
creative force, I examine how human progress has been facilitated through the 
entanglement of materiality and mind. 
 
168 
 
My focus on the natural world, particularly the vegetal, responds to the challenge of Jade 
Munslow Ong and Dorothy Driver that scholarly criticism on From Man to Man would benefit 
from considering these aspects of the novel. In an interview article in The Journal of 
Commonwealth Literature (2021) that considers current perspectives on From Man to Man, 
Munslow Ong suggests that through exploring Schreiner's interest in the natural world, 'new 
conclusions might emerge around the intersection of Schreiner's feminism, anti-imperialism, 
and free thought with the currently underexplored aspects of her environmentalism'.148 My 
focus on connectivity in this chapter does indeed explore how Schreiner connects the 
position of the natural world with the position of women and other oppressed groups in 
society. Munslow Ong also observes that there is little ecocritical analysis on this novel and 
recommends 'further research on the social and functional roles of plants in Schreiner's 
writings'.149 This chapter both rectifies this omission and extends it. My approach to Munslow 
Ong’s plant-based challenge is ontological whereas Driver’s approach is locational and 
claims that Schreiner's references to indigenous plants are indicative of South Africa as the 
locus for her 'vision of social change'.150 While having no intention to undermine this point, 
my ontological focus sees Schreiner’s use of plant imagery and analogy as making a wider 
point.    
    
In my 2020 article on From Man to Man, I used an ecocritical and ecofeminist methodology 
to examine Schreiner's evolutionary-based model of interconnectivity.151 I argued that 
Schreiner was writing against categorisation, binaries, and hierarchies, as well as the 
appropriation of evolutionary theory to support these, and that instead, she articulates the 
 
148 Jade Munslow Ong in Sanja Nivesjö and Heidi Barends, ‘Current Perspectives on Olive Schreiner’s From Man 
to Man or Perhaps Only – ‘, The Journal of Commonwealth Literature, 56(1) (2021), 44-60 (p.58). (In this article, 
Nivesjö and Barends engage three Schreiner scholars, one of which is Jade Munslow Ong, in a discussion about 
current and future perspectives in relation to Schreiner’s From Man to Man.) 
149 Jade Munslow Ong in Nivesjö and Barends, p.58. 
150 Driver, p.61. (In the ‘Notes’ at the end of Nivesjö and Barends’s interview article, they acknowledge that 
Driver takes up the challenge to consider Schreiner’s use of plants in From Man to Man in the article in this 
same issue of The Journal of Commonwealth Literature.) 
151 Hilary Bedder, ‘Oliver Schreiner’s From Man to Man: Alternative Models of Reality and the Problematics of 
Human Reasoning’, English in Africa, 47(2) (2020), 99-118. 
169 
 
values of unity and equality. Examining From Man to Man from the perspective of the 
vegetal here extends the conclusions of my article and Schreiner scholarship. The vegetal 
approach I develop in this study creates the capacity to explore wider issues in this novel 
and across Schreiner’s work. Through this lens, I recognise how her work is embedded in 
late nineteenth-century discourses centred around evolutionary materiality and the 
metaphysical, how she extends the novel form to decentralise the human and the individual 
and configures life as a vegetal-like continuum in which we are all embedded.  
 
In the previous chapter, I used a vegetal ontological model to create a new reading of 
African Farm. Although this approach is also productive for exploring From Man to Man, in 
this chapter I also examine Schreiner’s own use of these ontological models. Michael 
Marder’s work is a useful lens for exploring Schreiner’s conceptions of vegetal being as she 
calls into service, like Marder, the vegetal’s collective being, and its continuous reiterations 
that are both independent and yet reliant upon the whole plant for their viability. Marder’s 
approach is also productive for considering how Schreiner contextualises the abstract 
sections in this novel where I view them as vegetally dependent upon the external narrative.  
 
Reading Schreiner through Marder and New Materialist critics suggests an affinity between 
their deconstruction of boundaries between theory and practice. However, a New Materialist 
approach is useful for extending an understanding of how Schreiner articulates 
entanglements across all matter, and between beings and alterity. Through reading 
Schreiner’s agentic connections between theory and context, and mind and matter, through 
New Materialist approaches, I argue that these intra-actions result in a reconfiguring of the 
world and even Rebekah herself. In analysing Schreiner’s emphasis on the materiality of 
Rebekah’s writing practice and theoretical writings, Karen Barad’s discussion of the 
differences between performative and representational approaches offers a particularly 
useful approach. 
 
170 
 
Schopenhauer’s development of his philosophy of the will to an evolutionary theory is useful 
for exploring Schreiner’s evolutionary lifeforce that connects all beings, spirit and body, and 
mind and matter, across vast periods of time. Schopenhauer is also useful to my 
examination of Schreiner’s creative force. Schopenhauer suggests that the artist is able to 
overcome the individualistic focus of the will to express the universal and this is helpful to an 
understanding of Schreiner’s exploration of the artistic urge to create and share universal 
truths. 
 
I. Entanglements between Theory and Practice  
 
In From Man to Man, Schreiner employs an experimental form, disrupting the realist 
narrative with lengthy theoretical sections. These sections outline the values and theoretical 
positions that underpin and are played out in the realist narrative. For clarity in what follows, I 
list these theoretical sections in the order in which they appear:  
• 
in chapter seven, an almost fifty-page polemic about evolution and connectivity, that 
includes a critique of hegemonic society;   
• 
in chapter seven, an allegory in which the 'Spirit of the Ages' instructs fettered 
humanity to strive for freedom;   
• 
in chapter eight, an almost fifty-page letter from Rebekah to her husband that 
addresses the problems in their relationship;   
• 
in chapter twelve, an anti-racist allegory;  
• 
in chapter twelve, the story of evolution and the development of humankind as told to 
Rebekah’s children.  
 
These theoretical sections are all written or voiced by Rebekah and are therefore more 
embedded in and pertinent to the realist narrative than are the abstract sections in African 
Farm, both in terms of their positioning and relevance. Rebekah's polemic opens out of her 
retreat to her study after assisting Bertie to get ready for a party and reviewing her own 
171 
 
intellectual proclivity and development. Rebekah's letter to her husband is predicated upon 
her discovery of his adultery. The anti-racist allegory and subsequent story told to her 
children are framed as a response to her son Frank's assertion that he would no longer walk 
with his step-sister, Sartje, as he had been teased for 'walking with a nigger girl!' (FM 417). 
Such contextualisation shows Schreiner more fully utilising the potential of the experimental 
technique she had developed in writing African Farm to create a cohesive model of 
connectivity across the theoretical and realist parts of the novel.  
 
I read these contextualisations of the abstract sections as ontologically vegetal in the sense 
that the theoretical sections can be viewed as plant-like and the realist narrative the external 
environment in which they grow. Marder uses Deleuze and Guattari’s conceptualisation of 
the rhizome to develop his notion of plant-thinking as contextual. Deleuze and Guattari 
suggest that for plants 'there is always an outside where they form a rhizome with something 
else'.152 In recognising how this rhizomatic model allies with his concept of plant-thinking, 
Marder emphasises the importance of the interconnections between the plant and alterity. He 
extends this through his contention that what is important is not 'who or what does the 
thinking' but 'when and where does thinking happen?' and in his recognition that 'all radically 
contextualised thought is a worthy inheritor of vegetal life' (MM 169). Reading Schreiner’s 
use of form from this perspective, vegetal ontology becomes a formal structural device.  
 
If this contextualisation is read through Jane Bennett's New Materialist concept of the 
assemblage, Schreiner’s context becomes part of the agency that generates the theoretical 
sections. Agency emerges from the collaborative assemblage of context and Rebekah. The 
anti-racist allegory and the story that follows, is precipitated by her son's racism but also 
rooted within the necessity for Rebekah to communicate her values to her children. 
 
152 Gilles Deleuze and Felix Guattari, A Thousand Plateaus: Capitalism and Schizophrenia (London, Continuum, 
2003), p.11. 
172 
 
Schreiner emphasises the importance of these contexts by inserting contextual reminders 
within the theoretical sections. The story to her children is interrupted by comments like 'the 
boys leaned forward watching her intently' (FM 426). In the letter to her husband, Rebekah 
draws the reader back to the moment by frequent references to 'tonight'.   
 
The contextual relationships between the abstract sections and the realist narrative are also 
emphasised through Schreiner's explanations of the processes involved in their creation. 
These are both long-term and current. The letter to her husband detailing Rebekah’s 
unhappiness with their marriage is both a result of that specific evening and her long-term 
disillusionment with their relationship. The polemic in chapter seven emerges from a long 
gestation which emphasises its rootedness in her childhood and adult life. As Rebekah takes 
her book to write in from her drawer that evening, the reader learns that 'in the drawer below 
were six or seven such books' (FM 176) that stretch back to her childhood. Schreiner lists 
the contents of these exercise books, and these include creative fragments, passages 
copied from books, discussions on abstract questions, diary entries, and plans for the future. 
As her polemic emerges from these other writings, the boundaries between these forms 
become blurred.  
 
Schreiner foregrounds the material process of the production of writing itself, intertwining the 
corporeality of writing and writer. In the exercise books, the handwriting is sometimes 'filled 
with the sharply pointed writing of a very small child' and later 'with the larger, more rounded 
handwriting of a young girl from twelve to twenty' (FM 176). Within the polemic itself, 
Rebekah's handwriting mirrors her thought processes. She 'wrote quickly in a large 
sprawling hand, because she had much to say' (FM 183). When she is questioning her ideas 
'she began to write slower and slower […] after a time, began slowly drawing with her 
outstretched hand the pictures of faces down one edge of the page she had written on’ (FM 
186).  
 
173 
 
The corporeality of the process of Rebekah’s writing is further emphasised through 
Schreiner’s blurring of the boundaries between different modes of being while she is 
producing what becomes the polemic. Rebekah is not just writing, she is also thinking, 
speaking, and walking, and which of these she is enacting at any one moment is often 
unclear. After reviewing what she has written, Rebekah begins to write. But as she argues 
with an imaginary interlocuter, Schreiner parenthetically interrupts the polemic to comment 
on how she 'walked quicker and quicker' (FM 204) and then ‘stretched out one hand with the 
palm extended' (FM 206). Later she 'was whispering so loudly to herself that, in the next 
room, you would have thought she was speaking to someone beside her' (FM 209). So, 
although the polemic continues on the page, the reader is often unaware as to which mode 
of action or creativity Rebekah is performing at any one time. At the end of the polemic and 
the following allegory, Schreiner tells us that Rebekah 'had been walking nearly three hours' 
(FM 225). Not writing or thinking or speaking but 'walking'. In fact, her walking has produced 
on the carpet 'a mark like a footpath where the nap had been worn off, running right around 
the desk' (FM 175). 
  
The point is that Schreiner’s emphasis on the different modes of creation involved in the 
polemic shows the impact of the material and corporeal on its content. Barad’s distinction 
between representationalism and performative approaches is useful here. For Barad, 
representationalism enshrines a distinction between the representation and that which it 
purports to represent (KB 46). In contrast, a performative understanding of discursive 
practices, including those of thinking, observing, and theorising, views them as ‘practices of 
engagement’ with the material world (KB 133). They are ‘mutually implicated in the dynamics 
of intra-activity’ and are themselves ‘material (re)configurings of the world’ (KB 151-2, 
emphasis in original). Read through Barad, it is a performative approach that Schreiner 
embraces here. Her focus on the physicality of Rebekah’s creative processes means that 
they are entangled with her discourse, blurring the boundaries between theory and practice, 
abstract and concrete, and mind and matter. Corporeality affects the theory, but in turn the 
174 
 
theoretical sections of the narrative alter the reader’s reception of the realist narrative. 
Furthermore, Rebekah herself does not just creatively construct or partake in the discursive; 
through the long-term processes of her discursive creation, she is herself reconfigured and 
therefore participates differently in the world. In this way, the theoretical sections of From 
Man to Man participate in and change the realist narrative and it is their entanglement that 
creates the meaning. 
 
This is further emphasised by examining how Rebekah’s domestic circumstances affect the 
polemic. While writing: 
she thought suddenly she heard the baby stir. She threw her pen down on the 
table and took up the lamp, went into the next room and bent over his cot […] 
Then she tucked the cover in at the back of his neck and went back, closed the 
door softly behind her and began to walk up and down. If the advance of a 
nation or a race must always ultimately be stayed (FM 192). 
 
Rebekah deals with the interruption and continues with her polemic. Her writing and thought 
processes are regularly broken up by such domestic duties: 'she had been sitting up for a 
night, to make poultices for her boy who had bad earache, and, between whiles as he slept, 
she had written' (FM 177). Rather than mere contextual reminders, through these 
interruptions Schreiner shows how domesticity influences the direction of the polemic itself.     
 
Rather than reading these domestic interruptions as problematic, I contend that they are, 
perhaps paradoxically, beneficial. As they break Rebekah's chain of thought, they cause her 
to reflect: 'she had had to go and make a poultice, and she had branched off on a side line 
when she came back, to question if' (FM 177), and later, 'here she had been interrupted 
again. When she came back she had gone on to seek more narrowly after the cause' (FM 
178). In her article about the importance of motherhood in From Man to Man, Ruth Knechtel 
cites Ann Heilmann’s view that 'these moments represent how "marriage and motherhood 
175 
 
have taken a heavy toll on her creative energy"'.153 However, I argue that these interruptions 
strengthen Rebekah's polemic. Munslow Ong views Rebekah’s movements from darning to 
text as moving from feminine to masculine tasks.154 Indeed they are, but they become part of 
the causal assemblage of the polemic itself, from which emerge the counterarguments, 
questions, and the consideration of alternative perspectives, all of which create a stronger 
rather than weaker argument. It becomes femininely divergent rather than masculinely linear 
or didactic. If these ideas are read through Marder’s suggestion that the vegetal’s context-
dependent life contrasts with the ‘context-free abstract rationality’ of Western thought, the 
context-dependency of Rebekah’s polemic suggests not just a feminine subversion of 
masculine argument modes but a more expansive countering of dominant Western modes of 
thinking.155 
 
Considering the importance of contextualisation to Rebekah’s polemic highlights further 
connections between Marder’s ontological vegetal and New Materialist approaches. 
Returning to my notion of reading the theoretical sections as vegetal, Rebekah's argument 
that branches off on a side-line and considers a variety of societal factors and perspectives, 
is analogous to the plant producing new modules, as an adaptive response to the 
environment. They allow Rebekah to incorporate alterity (as the vegetal incorporates light, 
air, soil) to widen and adapt her argument. Likewise, reading this from a New Materialist 
perspective, that the polemic emerges from the intra-actions between itself and its context, 
suggests that intra-actions can themselves be seen as adaptive responses to externality. 
Rebekah does not intentionally branch off on side-lines, but such intentionality is the result of 
a complex network of agencies and material conditions to which Rebekah adapts. 
 
 
153 Ruth Knechtel, ‘Olive Schreiner’s Pagan Animism: An Underlying Unity’, English Literature in Transition, 
1880-1920, 53(3) (2010), 259-282 (p.272). 
154 Munslow Ong, p.113. 
155 Michael Marder, ‘Plant Intentionality and the Phenomenological Framework of Plant Intelligence’, Plant 
Signalling and Behaviour, 7.11 (2012), 1365-1372 (p.1370). 
176 
 
Schreiner’s use of narrative form in From Man to Man models interconnectivity.  
Astrid Bracke suggests that much criticism of the novel form with respect to ecocriticism 
rests upon viewing it as little more than story, neglecting any analysis of its narrative 
devices.156 If we consider the 'story' of the realist narrative in From Man to Man in isolation, 
much of the connectivity across the novel is overlooked. Justyna Koskowska contends that 
'narrative form is intrinsically ecopolitical: it carries messages of unity and equality or 
isolation and discrimination that run underneath the texts' thematic surface'.157 In From Man 
to Man, Schreiner foregrounds the connections between matter and mind, individuals and 
types of humanity, and humankind and the non-human, and the causal entanglements 
between the theoretical sections and the realist narrative participate in this project. 
 
Schreiner also connects the theoretical sections themselves by repeating ideas and attitudes 
across them. As in African Farm (see p.139), reiterations are not mere repetitions but 
contextually relevant. Rebekah’s theories about the development of human societies in the 
polemic in chapter seven and in the story to her children in chapter twelve, both foreground 
the importance of shared progress. In the polemic, Rebekah argues that 'permanent human 
advance must be united advance!' so societies must 'Bring up your rears!’ (FM 191-2). But 
this message is slightly different in the story where Rebekah states that 'when I think of all I 
have and all I know, the only feeling I have is – "Pass it on!"' (FM 435). In the story 
Rebekah’s instruction to 'pass it on' is intended to create a responsibility in her children, 
whereas the 'bring up your rears' of the polemic is part of a theoretical analysis of why 
societies collapse. Likewise, in the polemic and allegory that follows it, Schreiner addresses 
the position of women. But it is only in the letter to her husband in chapter eight that these 
ideas become personalised and converted into potential practical actions. The different 
 
156 Astrid Bracke, ‘Why neither realism nor the novel are dead: Genre and Form in the Anthropocene’, 
unpublished keynote paper delivered at the conference ‘Association for the Study of Literature and the 
Environment (UK and Ireland) postgraduate conference’ (online, 4th September 2020), p.4. 
157 Kostkowska, Justyna, Ecocriticism and Women Writers: Environmentalist Poetics of Virginia Woolf, Jeanette 
Winterson, and Ali Smith (New York, Palgrave MacMillan, 2013), p.165. 
 
177 
 
contexts mean that Rebekah needs to, and is enabled to, adapt her language and ideas so 
that they have relevance and become increasingly practical.  
 
II. The Progressive and Collective Evolutionary Body in Schreiner’s 
Vegetal Analogies  
 
As well as embedding connections within the formal structure of From Man to Man, 
Schreiner also uses Rebekah’s theoretical sections to articulate theories of connectivity. I 
have already explored how in African Farm Schreiner represents the collective or whole as 
of greater importance than the individual or part. In From Man to Man she extends this 
understanding by approaching it through a long-term evolutionary perspective. In both the 
polemic in chapter seven and the story to her children in chapter twelve, Rebekah articulates 
a progressive interpretation of evolutionary theory. She represents the individual as part of 
an extended continuity of beings and their importance in the role they play in the progressive 
development of humanity. To this end, Rebekah uses numerous vegetal analogies that use 
ontological plant strategies to represent her view of the long-term continuity of life and the 
role of the individual within the whole.    
 
In her story about evolution to her children in chapter twelve, Rebekah uses vegetal growth 
to illustrate the continuousness of life: ‘so short a time ago that perhaps the lichens which 
are growing on the rocks that have fallen from Table Mountain were growing exactly as they 
are to-day – our forefathers, yours and mine, were savages’ (FM 431). She suggests that the 
reason that the long-term ancestry of humanity is difficult to grasp is less to do with the 
length of time, but due to the individual manifestations of humans. The lichens, in contrast, 
represent continuity. While individual humans have come and gone, the lichens have stayed 
the same. Of course, the lichens growing on the rocks will be the descendants of previous 
lichens, but the continuous nature of vegetal growth obscures this fact and Rebekah can 
178 
 
therefore use the capacity of plants to grow and reproduce seemingly continuously to 
illustrate life as a long-term continuum. 
 
It is not simply the individual manifestations of humans that make a continuous species 
history difficult for us to grasp. In his influential essay on the Anthropocene and climate 
change, Dipesh Chakrabarty claims that as humans we can only understand ourselves 
collectively, as a species, from an intellectual viewpoint rather than experientially.158 Our 
individualistic subjective experience forestalls recognition of human continuity. In fact, the 
lesson that Waldo learns in African Farm is that a perspective framed by one’s own 
existence is limited (see p.147). But through the comparison with lichen, Rebekah overrides 
subjective experientiality and aims to enable her children to perceive humanity as a long-
term continuous body that bears resemblance to that of the vegetal.   
 
In the story, Rebekah links herself as gardener to her conception of humankind as a 
progressive continuum:  
sometimes even when I am walking in my garden […] the feeling comes to me, 
and I want to say – “To all the gardeners that have been before me […] 
Thanks!” And sometimes as I work there I feel as if they were working beside 
me and the garden belongs to them and me. And sometimes I think perhaps in 
years to come […] some woman working in a garden […] will stretch out her 
hand and say – “To all the gardeners that have been before me-” and I, so long 
dead in the dust, will live in her heart again' (FM 433).  
 
Rebekah feels herself part of the long-term continuum that connects past, present, and future 
and enables progress. In fact, through imagining the gardeners of the past alongside herself, 
and herself within the gardener of the future, she almost dissolves any division between the 
individuals of the past and the present, thereby emphasising humanity’s oneness across 
time. This passage also illustrates how Schreiner characterises the relationship between 
theoretical and concrete not just through the influence of the concrete on the theoretical 
 
158 Dipesh Chakrabarty, ‘The Climate of History: Four Theses’, Critical Inquiry, 35(2) (2009), 197-222 (p.220). 
179 
 
sections of the novel, but within the theoretical sections themselves. For Rebekah, the use of 
material practices familiar to her children enables her to root her theory within the concrete. 
But it also demonstrates how the concrete is part of the assemblage that contributes to the 
theory itself.  
 
Rebekah extends her vision of society as a continuum by explaining to her children the debt 
that they owe to previous generations. This time her analogy is that of an ant heap:  
Millions of ants have worked at them for years, and slowly and slowly they have 
grown a little and a little higher. Sometimes I have fancied, if a little ant should 
come on the top of one of these heaps, and should rear himself on his hind legs 
[…] and say, “My ant heap, that I have made! […]” and that then suddenly a 
gust of wind should come! The ant heap would still be there, the ant heap on 
the top of which he chanced to be born […] but he would be gone for ever’ (FM 
433-4). 
 
The extreme number of ant generations needed to create the ant heap and the ant who 
assumes it is all his own achievement, pointedly human-like in his rearing up on his hind 
legs, ridicules the idea that the contribution of any one individual to the development of 
society is paramount. The endeavour is instead long-term and continuous, and Rebekah’s 
evocation of the reliance upon a series of individuals recalls the Hunter allegory of African 
Farm. Although Rebekah uses the analogy of the ant heap to make her point here, an ant 
heap is not dissimilar to the plant body. Francis Hallé points out that many plants exhibit 
colonial modes of being through modular growth and Schreiner uses these colonial 
characteristics as a corrective to humanity's tendency to valorise the achievements of the 
individual and downplay their reliance upon the achievements of the past.159 As Hallé points 
out, colonialism is a mechanism 'incompatible with the idea of the individual' and 'founded on 
the sacrifice of the individual' in favour of long-term survival.160   
 
 
159 Hallé, pp.254-261. 
160 Hallé, p.261; p.268. 
180 
 
Schreiner does not deny the individuality and the individual stories of the characters in From 
Man to Man, but places them within an understanding of humanity as a long-term collective, 
demonstrating that individuality and connectivity are not mutually exclusive. Her use of a 
long-term evolutionary temporality enables her to perform what Benjamin Morgan refers to 
as 'perceptual scale shifts'.161 This is shown when, after the discovery of her sexual violation, 
Bertie goes to stay in an area surrounded by mountains that were 'still bearing the marks […] 
of what had been the shores of seas and lakes, the remains of whose mammoth amphibious 
beasts lay still within the stratified shale' (FM 314). The trauma of the individual Bertie is 
placed within a topographical representation of deep time. Without minimalizing Bertie's 
trauma, Schreiner reminds us of the position of the human individual within huge timescales, 
emphasising Bertie’s agentic incapacity. Later, as Griet awaits Bertie's arrival home, she 
listens 'as for all the long generations of the past her foremothers had listened […] for the 
coming step of the antelope that meant food, or of the enemy that meant war’ (FM 342). The 
narrative places Griet's actions within a lineage that connects her to her ancient ancestors 
and suggests the continuing struggle of the individual in the face of wider agencies. Through 
this use of the long-term, Schreiner extends the scope of the novel form, countering 
criticisms of its limited capacity to envision longer timescales, placing the stories of 
individuals within a wider framework.  
 
Rebekah uses plant analogies to embed her values and articulate a critique of societal 
inequalities and Social Darwinist-type theories, as well as to put forward a vision of a society 
in which individuals work together for the long-term good of the collective. Marder’s 
suggestion that the plant contributes to a ‘lived destruction of metaphysics’ through 
deconstructing many binaries such as ‘the one and the many’ (MM 11) is a useful lens for 
understanding Rebekah’s use of plant ontology to embed the values that correspond with 
collective being. They enable her to model her view that the individual is an integral part of a 
 
161 Morgan, p.45. 
181 
 
whole or collective and to critique hegemonic society's atomisation of individuals. Morgan 
makes the point that the scales we think with are evaluative and laden with judgement.162 
Rebekah’s use of the long-term situates the individual within a principle of continuity and 
reinforces the values embedded in an awareness of collective and continuous being. 
 
Rebekah recognises plant ontology as in some ways preferable to that of humanity. In the 
polemic, she seeks to understand 'why, when a nation or a race or a dominant class has 
reached a certain point of culture and material advance, has it always seemed to fall back 
from it' (FM 187). In questioning whether there is an 'immutable law' causing this, she uses a 
vegetal growth analogy, wondering 'is it absolutely futile to hope that humanity can ever 
advance as the fern palm grows, beautiful frond beyond beautiful frond opening one out of 
the other as it mounts up higher and higher?' (FM 188). She acclaims the vegetal’s 
continuous growth as a model of progressive continuity and the success to be gained 
through working as a collective, implicitly critiquing humanity’s individuality.  
  
Rebekah reinforces her point by making specific reference to the reliance of plant modules 
upon the whole plant. She suggests that 'an abnormally nourished class, unsupported by any 
vital connection with the classes beneath them' would resemble: 
the long, thin, tender, feathery, green shoots which our small rose trees 
sometimes send out in spring, rising far into the air, but which we know long 
before the summer is over will have broken and fallen; not because they have 
grown to a height which no rose tree can ever attain […] but because they have 
shot out too far before their fellow-branches to make permanence possible (FM 
191). 
 
Although each individual shoot has the capacity to grow independently (in Marder's terms, it 
has its own 'intentionality'), it does not have the capacity to be fully independent from the 
whole plant from which it obtains its support and nourishment. Rebekah points out that 
although powerful classes in society might rise further than the rest of society, they will be 
 
162 Morgan, p.47. 
182 
 
unable to consolidate these advances as they remain part of that society. Her explicit lesson 
is that 'sectional growth is no permanent growth' and that society must progress together and 
‘bring up your rears!’ (FM 191). Rebekah’s potential allusion to the parable of the sower here 
suggests that she is reflecting upon the responsibility of those who hear the word, to spread 
the kingdom of God in order to engender progress. Read in this way, Schreiner’s analogy is 
one of responsibility rather than critique.  
 
As her argument continues, Rebekah’s conception of humanity continues to gesture towards 
the ontological plant whose modules function individually and yet have need of the whole 
organism to survive. Her conclusion that humankind is 'intimately in its nature a solidarity and 
a whole with all its parts reacting on one another' (FM 188) bears resemblance to the plant 
body in which modules, for Marder, are 'both members of a plant and independent entities in 
their own right' (MM 84). Her representations of humanity continue to be plant-like. She 
asserts that 'the individual is part of an organism which cannot reach its full unfolding quite 
alone' (FM 193) and that 'each man is but a cell in the human organism' (FM 194). For 
Rebekah, each human (or plant module) requires the whole society (or plant) to achieve its 
potential.163  
 
Rebekah also uses a vegetal analogy to argue against Social Darwinist eugenic-type 
theories:   
Out there in my garden […] there are flowers of all kinds growing – tall queen-
lilies and roses and pinks and violets and little brown ranunculuses – and I love 
them all. But if the tall queen-lilies were to say, “We must reign here alone, all 
the others must die to give place to us,” I do not know, but I think I might say, “Is 
it not perhaps then best you should go?”(FM 438, emphasis in original).  
 
She suggests that the flowers that view themselves as superior, might assume that they are 
able to survive alone, and that other species are inferior and therefore expendable. Driver 
 
163 In the following chapter, I explore further how, in her allegories, Schreiner’s notion of humanity as one 
body contributes to meaning.  
183 
 
reads this analogy as a 'rejection of exclusivity and supremacy', viewing the 'brown’ 
ranunculuses and the 'many-hued' other flowers as reflective of the indigenous peoples who 
represent 'South Africa's potential'.164 Her interpretation suggests that the species perceived 
as lesser contain promise for the future. I extend this to read the analogy as one that presses 
into service the evolutionary mechanism of variation. This is made clearer when reading how 
Rebekah extends her analogy:  
And perhaps the next day there might come a blighting frost; the rose bushes 
would live on and the little violets hidden at their roots, and even the little brown 
ranunculuses sheltered by higher branches, would be alive; but the lilies – the 
tall white queen-lilies – would all be dead! (FM 439). 
 
The environment - the 'blighting frost' - destroys the queen-lilies. In evolutionary terms, it is 
the varying responses of different species to environmental conditions that mean that the 
most adaptable thrive when the environment changes and it is this adaptability that overturns 
the hierarchy. The queen-lilies that towered over the others become the most vulnerable and 
the diminutive species’ adaptability proves more successful.  
 
Rebekah’s argument recognises the importance of externality and therefore heteronomy as 
opposed to autonomy and the hierarchies that enables. The important lesson that she is 
trying to teach her children results from the racist comment made by one of them and her 
analogy counters Social Darwinist appropriations of evolutionary theory that validate the 
social hierarchies that enable and perhaps encourage racism.165 Her aim is to show that 
adaptability, and therefore variation, is essential to evolutionary progress and that these 
mechanisms neutralise hierarchies by making all lifeforms (and therefore all types of people) 
of value. For Schreiner, hierarchy is a human construct and, in evolutionary terms, invalid. 
Furthermore, hierarchy assumes human autonomy whereas in this analogy Schreiner 
 
164 Driver, p.73. 
165 In my article of 2020 (Hilary Bedder, ‘Oliver Schreiner’s From Man to Man: Alternative Models of Reality 
and the Problematics of Human Reasoning’, English in Africa, 47(2) (2020), 99-118), I explore in more detail 
how in From Man to Man, Schreiner explicitly counters the Social Darwinist agendas of writers such as Herbert 
Spencer and how this ‘distorts and misrepresents evolutionary theory’ (p.108). I also show how Schreiner 
counters these through the framework of mutualism. (See pp.108-112.)  
184 
 
illustrates the role of alterity, making humans as vulnerable to change as the heteronomous 
plant. Although this heteronomy is easily recognised in the plant due to an immediate 
response to the frost, for humanity, these responses must be longer-term and therefore 
potentially misapprehended.  
 
Such a role for the environment in evolutionary theory is analogous to my argument for the 
importance of context to the theoretical sections of From Man to Man. Alterity influences the 
success and progress of the species as it does the content of the theoretical sections of 
From Man to Man. In the way that they counter notions of autonomy, both bear resemblance 
to Marder’s heteronomous plant and New Materialism’s emphasis on the role played by 
entanglements. Moreover, in a double move, Rebekah’s story that is itself initiated by 
context, uses the argument that context is important and relevant to progress.  
 
The importance of variation and all types to Schreiner’s values is corroborated through 
Darwin’s Variation of Plants and Animals under Domestication (1868) being the only Darwin 
(and in fact scientific) book named out of the numerous books in Rebekah’s study (FM 
172).166 This is emphasised when in her polemic in chapter seven, Rebekah explicitly argues 
for the importance of all types of people. She wonders whether the ‘primitive man individually 
and structurally as well as socially, may, in some future aeon’ have a ‘restorative function’ in 
society (FM 205), emphasising his value in terms of societal progress. Rebekah reinforces 
his value when she wonders if there might be in the ‘little untaught savage the root out of 
which ultimately the noblest blossom of the human tree shall draw its strength' (FM 197). By 
representing society as a tree, Rebekah suggests that all types of people potentially 
contribute to its future progress. To the same end, she also critiques female oppression:      
 
166 In The Variation of Animals and Plants under Domestication, Darwin considers the workings of variation in 
evolutionary theory. Incidentally, in From Man to Man, Schreiner misnames this book, calling it Variation of 
Plants and Animals under Domestication rather than Variation of Animals and Plants under Domestication. It is 
tempting to reflect upon whether this is indicative of the importance she places on plants in From Man to 
Man, but this thought must remain speculative.   
185 
 
What has humanity not lost by the suppression and subjection of the weaker sex 
[…] we have a Shakespeare; but what of the possible Shakespeares we might 
have had, who passed their life from youth upward brewing currant wine and 
making pastries […] stifled out without one line written […] what statesmen, what 
rulers and leaders, what creative intelligences have been lost to humanity' (FM 
219-20). 
 
She argues that through repressing women society is held back. Moreover, Rebekah does 
not merely voice female oppression, she enacts it: she is one of the ‘creative intelligences’ 
potentially ‘lost to humanity’.  
 
As well as using vegetal analogies to articulate her understanding of the importance of all 
types of peoples, in a reverse move Schreiner also asserts the value of all types of 
vegetation. Although moss and lichen might be considered insignificant, for Schreiner they 
carry a weight of meaning and connectivity across From Man to Man. In chapter eight, 
Rebekah collects moss and lichen from a variety of locations to examine under her 
microscope (FM 242). Investigating them leads Rebekah to ponder on the different types of 
mosses at her childhood farm (FM, 243), thereby linking the narrative back to her old home. 
In the letter to her husband, Rebekah recalls her recent visit to the farm and ‘the long moss 
still hanging from the tree in the kloof’ (FM 286). Meanwhile, in London, as Bertie's feet sink 
into the luxurious carpet, it recalls to her the ‘moss by the rocks in the kloof’ (FM 353). I have 
already noted how Rebekah uses lichen in her polemic to articulate long-term temporality, 
and in chapter three Schreiner similarly recognises the moss and lichen on the rocks at the 
old farm that had fallen from the mountain centuries before (FM 113). Images of moss and 
lichen are used then to connect places, time, and ideas across the novel: South Africa and 
London; Rebekah and Bertie; the contemporary world of the novel and deep time, memories 
of the old farm and scientific research, contributing to the overall unity of the novel. When 
Rebekah begins to collect moss and lichen to examine under her microscope it is because 
‘all the little things of life were of much interest to her again’ (FM 242). Across the novel, 
Schreiner shows that lifeforms that might seem unimportant are, in fact, of equal importance 
to those that could be perceived as of more value.  
186 
 
 
III. The Plant-like Rebekah  
 
Schreiner uses her main character, Rebekah, to thematically and formally further the 
connectivity across From Man to Man. Rebekah not only voices all the abstract sections and 
is therefore realist character and contributing writer, but through this structural device she 
entangles abstract and concrete, and mind and matter. Thematically she links science, 
feminism and the relations between the sexes, evolutionary theory, societal critique, and 
creativity. Furthermore, it is through Rebekah that the Prelude is linked to the main book. 
The Prelude concerns a day in the life of the child Rebekah and in a letter written at a much 
later date than the first drafts of From Man to Man, Schreiner says that she realises that the 
Prelude is ‘a picture in small, a kind of allegory, of the life of the woman in the book!!’.167 
Reading the Prelude we see many of the characteristics of the adult Rebekah in the child: 
nascent scientific investigations, making up stories about the natural world, and intervening 
in a predatory scenario. 
 
It is as a theoretical and practical botanist that Rebekah links many themes across the book. 
Her knowledge enables her to use plant ontology as analogy. I have already noted her 
observations and experiments on lichen and moss, but she also keeps records on the length 
of time for germination of the seeds she plants (FM 176). She is a practical and skilled 
gardener and farmer. After she has left her childhood farm, her father missed his ‘wise little 
daughter’ as he no longer had anyone ‘to advise with over his grafts and flutes’ (FM 93). On 
her own farm, it is ‘her own hands [that] had planted and grafted trees and vines’ (FM 156). 
Mrs Drummond praises her garden in Cape Town as ‘she always seems to have more 
flowers and sweeter smelling than anyone else!’ (FM 167).   
 
167 Olive Schreiner, letter to Mrs Frances Smith, October 1909 in The Letters of Olive Schreiner, 1876-1920, ed. 
by S.C. Cronwright-Schreiner (London, T. Fisher Unwin Ltd, 1924), p.291. 
 
187
