Rebekah's life is also entangled with the vegetal in other ways: locationally, botanically, 
symbolically, and structurally. Her association with mimosa will serve to illustrate. Her 
childhood farm is surrounded by mimosa. She carries out experiments, collecting gall 
growths from it ‘to see whether the galls on the different species of mimosa are all quite 
alike, or whether they are different’ (FM 123). She characterises herself as mimosa, 
resprouting after damage (FM 121-2) (an image I explore in more detail later in this chapter). 
In one of her stories in the Prelude, mimosa and human seem structurally akin. The child 
Rebekah opens a mimosa pod and finds that ‘there, lying inside it, like the seeds lie inside 
the pod of a mimosa tree – was a little baby’. When she tries to lift out the baby, she finds 
that ‘it was tied to the pod like the mimosa seeds are, with a little curled-up string’ (FM 47).  
 
Schreiner also presents Rebekah as gaining shelter and emotional sustenance from trees. 
Her married home in Cape Town is ‘tucked away’ and ‘buried’ among oaks, sheltering her 
from a judgemental society (FM 147). At times of emotional crisis, she finds comfort in these 
trees. After she discovers her husband with a girl, she runs up the mountain through the 
woods (FM 276), and, after he dismisses her letter, she recognises the ‘green and golden 
glory of the light’ through the young oaks (FM 300) which enable her to regain a sense of 
hope.  
 
Trees also inspire Rebekah’s thinking, again entangling material context and mind. She tells 
Mr Drummond that as a child thoughts ‘nearly always came’ when she would ‘lie under a 
pear tree in the garden’ (FM 468). They also inspire her writing as an adult: it is under the 
pine trees that she reconsiders some of the arguments that appear in the polemic. In 
suggesting that vegetal materiality can affect the mind, Schreiner suggests that the agency 
that creates Rebekah’s thought becomes ‘confederate’ and Rebekah’s polemic can be read 
as a Bennetian co-production of the entanglement of materiality and mind, problematising 
human autonomy (JB, 23). Reading through Barad takes us further. Barad claims that 
188 
 
‘existence is not an individual affair. Individuals do not preexist their interactions; rather, 
individuals emerge through and as part of their entangled intra-relating’ (KB ix).  Reading 
Rebekah through this lens suggests that her self, as revealed in her writing and thinking, is 
created through these intra-actions. Rebekah ‘emerge[s] through, their intra-action’, further 
dismissing human autonomy and the notion of a separate self (KB 33). 
 
Rebekah’s entanglements with the vegetal also make her plant-like. Marder maintains that 
his concept of ‘plant-thinking’ includes the idea that ‘human thinking is, to some extent, de-
humanised and rendered plant-like […] by its encounter with the vegetal world’ (MM 10); to 
some extent, a comparable argument to Barad’s that intra-actions create new phenomena. 
Rebekah's botanical enmeshment engenders in her plant-like characteristics and behaviour. 
I have already explored how Rebekah entwines theory and practice and, from Marder’s 
perspective, this makes her plant-like. For Marder, as ‘plant-thinking is plant-doing’, the 
vegetal eliminates ‘the classical opposition between theory and practice’ (MM 181). In the 
realist narrative, Rebekah also enacts the theory espoused in the abstract sections. In her 
polemic, she emphasises the importance of motherhood and nurturing to the survival and 
development of the young; in her role as mother she enacts this care, even going as far as 
to adopt the child, Sartje, borne of her husband's adultery with the servant girl. Likewise, in 
the polemic, Rebekah critiques how society suppresses women; in the realist narrative she 
works to overcome this situation. Like Marder’s plant, for Rebekah, thinking is being is doing.   
 
Rebekah’s ethics are also plant-like. Through enacting the values generated by her theory, 
Rebekah's ethics and compassion are rooted within an understanding of connectivity and 
unity. This is shown in the love and service she offers to others, for example, in her care of 
Sartje. This reflects an understanding of the plant-like indivisibility of life. For Marder, as plant 
being is collective and has an integral relationship to alterity, it offers an ethical model which 
negates the boundaries between other and self (MM 52-3). In this sense, Schopenhauer’s 
philosophy in its relation to ethics is also plant-like. Laurence Wright asserts that as in 
189 
 
Schopenhauer’s noumenal world all are related through the will, this is the basis for 
compassion and altruism. In fact, Wright suggests, these values would not make sense if we 
were separate beings.168 The ethical actions of Rebekah spring from her recognition that all 
are connected and that the boundaries that separate us are negated when life is viewed as a 
plant-like continuum. 
 
It is useful to read Rebekah as plant-like when exploring her relationship to her environment, 
particularly in relation to her gender. In the previous chapter, I considered Lyndall’s struggle 
to thrive within a patriarchal environment. I now want to consider if the women in this novel 
are portrayed as similarly at odds with their environment, particularly as they may be more 
reliant upon it than the male characters, having less scope for self-determination. 
 
In her visit to her childhood farm in chapter three, Schreiner presents Rebekah as ‘like a 
creature returning to its old habitat and resuming its old instincts and habits’ (FM 118). 
Although she is framed as a ‘creature’, her need for a sustaining environment can be read as 
plant-like, reliant as plants are on their surroundings. Schreiner suggests that Rebekah’s old 
home, where she was originally rooted, is more conducive to her than is her married home in 
Cape Town. In her adult study, Rebekah seems to have tried to recreate elements of her 
childhood home. The wooden cabinet that had held her childhood fossils and insects is 
transplanted into her adult study, as are all her books and her microscope. She has 
replanted herself into an environment where she can try to flourish.  
 
In terms of female responses to the environment, it is productive to compare Rebekah and 
Bertie. As sisters, they can be viewed as two plant modules that have developed from the 
same plant and yet are different. Bertie's response to adversity (admittedly extreme 
adversity) is to flee from the scene of the violation and then continue to do so as her 
 
168 Wright, p.29.  
 
190 
 
damaged reputation catches up with her. But it does her no good – her life in London is 
sinister and unsavoury. She fails to re-root herself anywhere. In contrast, Rebekah's 
response to difficulties is to adapt to her environment and in this respect, she is the more 
plant-like.   
 
Bertie's relationship with the vegetal is problematic throughout the novel. Her tutor initiates 
the creation of a wildflower garden in the bush with Bertie, but this is a ruse.  It is in this 
unfamiliar space of the bush, away from her home, that he violates her. Subsequently, the 
wildflower garden is destroyed, the reader assumes as a response by Bertie. Her 
subsequent relationships with the vegetal are either overblown or unnatural. After being 
involved in a conversation with Mrs Drummond about her education and tutor ‘there was a 
stain of red on each cheek deeper than the red of the damask rose in Rebekah's garden’ (FM 
168). In London her associations with the vegetal are artificial: ‘the soft double-piled carpet 
had pink roses in it’ (FM 353); her fan is illustrated with daisies (FM 355); the ‘Jew' buys her 
extravagant ‘flowers, lilies and orchids that cost sovereigns a bunch’ (FM 356); and she 
names the kittens he buys her after the pictures on their bowls: Holly, Larkspur, and Rose 
(FM 385). Bertie's relationship with the natural vegetal seems broken.  
 
Returning to Rebekah's adaptive response to her environment, it would perhaps be easy to 
infer that adaptation is a passive strategy, but it is not so, neither is it for plants. Merlin 
Sheldrake quotes the scientist Barbara McLintock: that plants are ‘extraordinary “beyond our 
wildest expectations” […] because a life lived rooted to one spot has coaxed them to evolve 
countless “ingenious mechanisms” to deal with challenges that animals might avoid by 
simply running away’.169 Plants also grow in response to their environment as well as 
contributing to that environment. As Marder writes, ‘place dynamically emerges from the 
 
169 Sheldrake, p.75-6. 
191 
 
plant’s living interpretation of and interaction with its environment’.170 Rather than passivity, 
plant sessility engenders agency. In this respect, Rebekah's response to her environment is 
active. She not only adapts to the environment; she attempts to alter it to create a place 
better suited to her needs. In fact, McLintock's explanation of the differing responses of 
plants and animals perhaps encapsulates Rebekah and Bertie's contrasting strategies: Bertie 
as the animal that runs away; Rebekah as the plant that develops ‘ingenious mechanisms’ in 
order to survive in that place. 
 
Schreiner shows Rebekah’s increasing agency in relation to changing her environment as 
the novel develops. The turning point comes in chapter eight. Although her husband has 
refused to read her letter, when she fails to meet him on his return home, he is forced to visit 
her study and forced to perch on the edge of her desk. As she passes him a note that 
explains his options, ‘he had an unpleasant sensation as he looked at her that she was 
growing physically taller and larger at the moment’ (FM 304). Like a plant, she grows in situ, 
in her study, as she begins to progress to a more independent and sustaining life. 
 
It is Rebekah's botanical knowledge and farming ability that gives her the capacity for 
independence. One of her active adaptive responses to Frank's infidelity is the purchase of 
her own farm. It subsequently ‘supplied the household with fruit and vegetables’ (FM 156) 
and eventually pays all of Rebekah's expenses, as well as Sartje's (FM 444). One of the 
options she offers to Frank on deciding that their marriage is irretrievable, is that she can be 
independent because ‘I have my farm and I can work’ (FM p.288). In her letter Rebekah tells 
of how finding the advertisement for the farm is what saves her, making the emancipatory 
statement that ‘I could not study, and I dared not feel: but I could still [work]; it was my hold 
on freedom and life; and it has been so to me ever since’ (FM 283).   
 
 
170 Michael Marder, Marder, ‘Plant Intentionality and the Phenomenological Framework of Plant Intelligence’, 
Plant Signalling and Behaviour, 7.11 (2012), 1365-1370 (p.1367). 
192 
 
Rebekah does not therefore simply adapt to her environment, or context, but changes it. In 
Baradian terms, rather than simply taking her place in the world, she is involved in ‘bringing 
forth new worlds, of engaging in an ongoing reconfiguring of the world’ (KB 170, emphasis in 
original). Like Marder’s plant, in her sessility, she is active. She becomes part of the 
entangled ‘confederation’ of agency in the world rather than being passively controlled by 
externality (JB 23). In this sense, her adaptive entanglements are the equivalent in the realist 
narrative to the responses of her discursive work to context in the theoretical sections of the 
novel.   
 
Her progress is also shown in the increasing efficacy of her voice. Although Schreiner's use 
of form has enabled the reader to hear Rebekah's voice, for much of From Man to Man her 
voice is not heard by the other characters: her polemic is unread and Frank throws away her 
letter, also unread. But Schreiner grants Rebekah a second opportunity in the penultimate 
chapter to articulate her conception of an evolutionary continuum and societal progress in the 
story she tells her children, so it does gain a hearing and potential influence. Then in the final 
chapter, her meeting with Mr Drummond offers her the intellectual discourse and 
companionship for which she has been yearning. As he now agrees to deliver one of the 
fossils she has found to where it might be seen, through him she may finally gain an 
opportunity to have an impact upon the wider world. 
 
Early in the novel, Rebekah anticipates her own and Bertie's differing responses to adversity 
through plant analogies, comparing her own resilience and adaptability to the mimosa and 
Bertie's fragility to the aloe: 
Some women with complex, many-sided natures, if love fails them and one half 
of their nature dies, can still draw a kind of broken life […] The world of the 
impersonal is left them: they can still turn fiercely to it, and through the intellect 
draw in a kind of life – a poor, broken, half-asphyxiated life […] but still life. But 
Bertie […] only one life possible, the life of the personal relations; if that fails 
them, all fails. If you chop down the stem of a mimosa tree, years after you may 
come and find from the bottom of the old dead stem sprouts have sprung, which 
193 
 
will even bear flowers […] but an aloe has one flower, once; if you cut that 
down, nothing more comes (FM 121-2).  
As mimosa, Rebekah can survive even in inauspicious circumstances. Bertie, as aloe, has 
only one chance. For Rebekah, love does indeed fail her, but in this analogy, she 
underestimates her future self. Rather than living a ‘poor, broken, half-asphyxiated life’, by 
the end of the novel, Rebekah, with her ‘complex, many-sided nature’, has created a richer 
life than she has predicted. Her plant-like ability for active adaptation means she has grown 
new ‘sprouts’ and these ‘bear flowers’ in the form of her increasing independence and 
achievement.  
 
In the final chapter of the novel, Rebekah and Mr Drummond discuss two modes of life, 
framed through the metaphor of being ‘hedged’. Their discussion begins with Rebekah's 
response to Mr Drummond's praise of her garden. She says that ‘there’s a pleasure […] if 
you have a very small space, in seeing how much will live in it’, to which Mr Drummond 
replies:  
I’ve sometimes thought that a life itself might be lived more satisfactorily a little 
hedged about with narrow conditions which compelled one to expand oneself in 
that circle – there’s such a thing as being dissipated with too large an horizon 
and too much liberty. 
Rebekah counters, ‘if the hedges are too close round, they may kill the plants’ (FM 458-9). 
This exchange encapsulates the different environmental conditions within which women and 
men live. Women's lives are plant-like: they are forced to be sessile and ‘hedged’, while 
men's lives are more animal-like with a large ‘horizon’ and ‘liberty’. Although Rebekah points 
out the difficulties of living a ‘hedged’ life, her ‘narrow conditions’ have ‘compelled one to 
expand oneself in that circle’, and this describes her adaptive plant-like response to external 
conditions. Although narrow hedges risk killing the plants, her adaptations demonstrate how 
her agency has enabled her to ‘see how much will live’ within the restricted life of a women.  
 
 
194 
 
IV. Lifeforce and Creative Force  
 
In her exploration of two forces that connect and are continuous over time, Schreiner frames 
Rebekah as a connecting device. Rebekah perceives a natural lifeforce and a creative force, 
and I argue that these representations share many features in common. She envisages 
these forces as an energy that connects beings over time, that are manifested in materiality 
and yet are mysterious and unexplainable. In this part of the chapter, I examine Rebekah’s 
understanding of the lifeforce that she articulates in her polemic, and then move on to 
consider how, in conversation with Mr Drummond, Rebekah’s notion of the creative force 
shares many corresponding features.  
 
Rebekah’s conception of a lifeforce is outlined in her polemic in chapter seven:  
between man, plant, bird, beast and clod of earth, everywhere the close 
internetted lines of interaction stretch; nowhere are we able to draw a sharp 
dividing line, nowhere find an isolated existence […] between the life that 
moved in the creature that ploughed in the mud of the lake-shores three million 
years ago and the life which beats in my brain and moves in my eyes here in 
the sunshine today, I can see long unbroken lines of connection. Between spirit 
that beats within me and body through which it acts, between mind and matter, 
between man and beast, between beast and plant and plant and earth, between 
the life that has been and the life that is, I am able to see nowhere a sharp line 
of severance, but a great, pulsating, always interacting whole. So that at last it 
comes to be, that, when I hear my own heart beat, I actually hear in it nothing 
but one throb in that life which has been and is (FM 180-1).  
 
There are similarities between this image and Schreiner’s representation of ‘Universal Unity’ 
(AF 290) in African Farm. But in this conceptualisation, Schreiner emphasises the 
connections across life rather than the necessity to find a reason for them. The evolutionary 
context of the polemic perhaps supplies the rationale. The ‘internetted lines of interaction’ 
and ‘long unbroken lines of connection’ serve to negate ‘isolated existence’ caused by any 
‘sharp dividing line’, the long sentences reinforcing this point. Schreiner’s evolutionary 
connections across lifeforces displace any notion of human exceptionalism, but, more 
radically, she also connects ‘spirit’ and ‘body’, and ‘mind and matter’. In her evocation of the 
long-term connections across time, with the ‘life’ in the creature of ‘three million years ago’ 
195 
 
the same as that in the brain of Rebekah today, she also connects matter and mind across a 
vast period of time. In fact, Schreiner embeds a further connectivity when we recall that 
Rebekah has herself uncovered fossils of such ancient creatures.   
 
But Rebekah struggles to fully understand or explain this connecting force, as Schreiner had 
in African Farm. In the polemic she goes on to consider a physiologist who might study a 
small drop of blood or shred of tissue through a microscope because ‘it may turn into the key 
which may unlock for us the meaning of part of that great universal life of which it is an 
integral fragment’ (FM 181). Later in the polemic, she uses the vegetal to consider it. She 
compares this force with the impact of pruning which she suggests is an ‘entirely subsidiary 
process’ that ‘fails utterly to account really for the tree, whose essential life and essence lie 
in its power of growth’ which she sees as ‘mysterious’: the ‘fundamental mystery of one bud 
becoming a flower, for one grain of matter in the soil or particle of gas in the air being 
transformed into bark, for the kernel, and reality of life’ (FM 221). Schopenhauer's use of the 
vegetal can perhaps shed light on Schreiner's own attempt to use it to account for this force. 
He employs it to consider the 'blind irresistible urge’ of the will, as in the plant it is clearly 
seen unobscured by intellect or consciousness (WWR1 275). His interpretation is helpful to 
an understanding of Rebekah’s ‘fundamental mystery’ as metaphysical, additional to, rather 
than part of, materiality and therefore out of reach of a full understanding. 
 
In conversation with Mr Drummond in the final chapter, Rebekah returns to her concern 
about the fact that ‘we know nothing’ of ‘the moving power’ in life. She says, ‘I wish they 
gave it a larger name’ to which Mr Drummond replies that ‘the name circumscribes it’ (FM 
471). One might surmise that this unhelpful name is ‘God’. Although Schreiner rejected a 
conventional Christian faith, in her letters she continues to use the word ‘God’ to articulate 
the mysterious force at the heart of life. She writes that ‘I have never been able to conceive 
of God, and man and the material universe as distinct from one another’ and that ‘if I must 
put it into words I would say the Universe is one, and it lives:- or if you would put it into older 
196 
 
phraseology, I would say; - there is nothing but God’.171 So although Rebekah perceives a 
metaphysic at the heart of connectivity, Schreiner is hesitant to use the word ‘God’ and 
instead leaves the connecting force unexplained. She can only refer to it as a ‘great, 
pulsating, always interacting whole’, characterising it only as an energy or drive.  
 
In chapter thirteen, in a discussion on creativity with Mr Drummond, Rebekah draws 
attention to the closeness between her understanding of the lifeforce and a creative force 
that is transmitted through artifacts. Using her small Hercules statue as an example, she 
states that: 
after all these hundreds, perhaps almost thousands, of years it has lain buried, 
whilst the man, whose being it first lived in, has for ages been Italian dust […] it 
has sprung out into life again […] with the joy, the comprehension of it, that 
throbbed in his brain when he saw it, beating here again in mine (FM 475). 
 
Although the artist himself is dead, the artwork he has created has the capacity to 
communicate to Rebekah, over innumerable years, something of the ‘being it first lived in’. 
This seems analogous to the lifeforce, manifested in materiality but an additional force; the 
likeness emphasised through the statue bearing resemblance to a buried fossil in which the 
lifeforce had once existed. Through her re-use of the word ‘throb’ and the link between the 
creator’s brain and hers, Rebekah calls further attention to the similarity to her earlier 
description of the lifeforce. 
 
Rebekah asserts that this force engenders in the artist an imperative to create. His ‘will is 
bound’ and there is a ‘restless pressure which forces him […] to his work’ (FM 472, emphasis 
in original). Through comparing this imperative to the bee, Schreiner allies the force in the 
artist and in nature. The bee ‘makes her perfect polygonal cell in the first year of her life’ and 
‘if you ask her why, she says she must – it is so, that's all!’. For the bee ‘her whole cell is 
 
171 Olive Schreiner, letter to John T Lloyd, 29th October 1892, Schreiner’s emphasis, 
 https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 25th February 2023]  
 
197 
 
meaningless unless you realise the storing of the honey and the bees that have yet to be 
born whom she has never seen’ (FM 470). The bee works, even though unknowingly, for the 
continuity of the species, and Rebekah suggests that the internal impetus that compels the 
artist to create, makes him part of an analogous continuity.    
 
Schopenhauer is useful for understanding Schreiner's explanation of the relationship 
between artist and life-force. He contends that in the artist, rather than the will operating to 
support the survival of the individual, the intellect is in service to the universal will. As Helen 
Zimmern explains, in the artist, there is a kind of ‘self-annihilation’. This characterises how 
Schreiner's artistic force compels the artist to ‘defy their reason’ and create. Like the bee, the 
artist is in service to the force. Moreover, when Rebekah suggests to Mr Drummond that the 
artist is one ‘in whom some of the accumulated life of his race […] is stored’, she argues that 
the artist has access to a collective understanding. This also reflects a Schopenhauerian 
understanding. For Schopenhauer, in the artist ‘the veil of Maya is rent’. He can access the 
universal will and is ‘no longer under the illusion that hinders him from recognising himself in 
all other existence’.172 For Schreiner, the artist is ‘an eye in the great human body’, being 
one who can offer this vision of ‘the great body of life’ to others (FM 470-6).   
  
Like the lifeforce, Rebekah suggests that the creative force has a mysterious source. 
Although it is manifested within the material, both Rebekah and Mr Drummond suggest that 
it has an external source. In relation to his writing, Mr Drummond says that ‘all the pictures in 
the thing ran out before me with a flash one after the other […] It was exactly as though 
someone quite external to myself had shown them me suddenly’. Rebekah claims that it 
arrives from ‘something beyond ourselves’ (FM 467), and that although it is ‘made of the 
man's substance […] yet he feels in a way that it is something not himself but shown to him’ 
 
172 Zimmern, p.229. 
198 
 
(FM 471). This bears relation to Rebekah’s conceptualisation of the vegetal's mysterious 
compulsion to grow. In neither does reason or the self intervene. 
 
As well as the Hercules statue, Schreiner suggests that books transmit the mind of their 
creators over time. Looking at the books in her study, Rebekah sees that ‘behind each was 
hidden the mind of some human creature which at some time had touched her own’ (FM 
175), and when Rebekah reads a book written by an Ancient Greek composed ‘two 
thousand years’ ago, she surmises that ‘it might have been composed this morning’ (FM 
177). She suggests that books overcome the limited temporality of the individual mind, as 
Waldo had experienced in African Farm.  
 
In African Farm, Schreiner considered the materiality of books. She also does this in From 
Man to Man but extends this through allying their materiality to the progress of humanity. In 
the story to her children in chapter twelve, as part of her attempt to explain to them the debt 
they owe to the past so that they don’t behave like the little ant on the ant-heap, Rebekah 
explains how essential books have been in the development of humanity. Books, she tells 
them, had been ‘sometimes written on stone tables, sometimes on mud tablets, sometimes 
on leaves or bark or skin’ and that they contain ‘the thought and knowledge of mankind [that] 
might be stored and each generation begin where the last left off’. She emphasises the 
importance of the matter from which books have been made and asserts that it is this that 
has enabled humanity to store and pass on ‘thought and knowledge’. Through the 
entanglement of mind and matter, materiality participates and contributes to the development 
of ‘civilisation’ (FM 427).   
 
Rebekah further extends her notion of the dependence of humanity’s progress on materiality 
by allying books and the development of writing to the material evolutionary processes of 
deep time:  
199 
 
If I tear one sheet of paper out of this little book and there were some being 
wise enough to tell me its whole story, with all the lines and dots and marks 
upon it, and what they mean and how they came to be, and how the leaf itself 
came into being, the whole story of the human race on earth would have to be 
told me (FM 430).  
 
Through describing writing as ‘lines and dots and marks’, Rebekah represents it as concrete 
before it has useful meaning. Extending this materiality further back, to ‘how the leaf itself 
came into being’, shows human progress to be dependent upon a materiality that is older 
than humanity itself. Human development becomes positioned within an evolutionary 
timescale and dependent upon non-human lifeforms. Rebekah works to ensure that her 
children are under no illusions about their own, or human, autonomy in relation to the 
development of civilisation.   
 
Schreiner extends her focus on the reliance of humanity upon materiality by connecting 
books to metaphorical roots: ‘This little book! – this little book! – it has got its roots down, 
down, deep in the life of man on earth; it grows from there’ (FM 430). Schreiner links books, 
and therefore humanity, to vegetal growth processes that are fixed within the earth. The 
metaphor encourages us to read ‘earth’ as both planet and soil, embedding humanity's 
history within the darkness of the earth, rather than in the ideal sphere of the heavens. 
Marder points out that Western metaphysics begins with the ‘inversion of the earthly 
perspective of the plant […] and the correlative devaluation of the literal plant’ and contends 
that a vegetal perspective challenges this view (MM 57-59, emphasis in original). I read 
Schreiner here as suggesting that the entanglement of humankind's progress and materiality 
means that we, like the vegetal, are earth-bound life-forms. 
 
From Man to Man is itself an artwork and can therefore manifest Schreiner's notion that 
through materiality, art can transcend its time. In talking to Mr Drummond Rebekah states 
that: 
 
200 
 
ages ago in Ancient Chaldea there might have been some thinker whose 
thought seemed to have no relation to the men about him […] yet, recorded on 
a mud tablet and preserved through the years and coming to the light today, it 
might be found most closely connected with our needs and new life, might find 
its own here today and live again, though the man who made it might be 
forgotten (FM 476). 
 
Through From Man to Man, something of the mind of Schreiner is transmitted to us today, 
perhaps finding new resonances in the twenty-first century. She suspects that the man 
himself who recorded his thoughts on the mud tablet might be forgotten; my hope is that 
Schreiner herself is not.  
 
 
201 
 
Chapter Six - The Continuous Collective Body of Humanity in 
Schreiner’s Contemporary Allegories 
 
the past is dead, it lives only for us to learn. The present, the present only, is 
ours to work in, and the future ours to create.  
(Olive Schreiner, Trooper Peter Halket, p.37) 
 
it grew so bright I could not see things separately; and which were God, or the 
man, or I, I could not tell; we were all blended.  
(Olive Schreiner, Dreams, p.119) 
 
 
Schreiner frequently turned to the allegory form to convey her ideas. In this chapter I explore 
her use of this form and go on to consider three of her allegories in more detail. I have 
already considered the Hunter allegory embedded in African Farm and elements of 
Rebekah’s allegories in From Man to Man, but I now turn to the separately published 
allegories. Two collections of her allegories were published in her lifetime: Dreams (1890), 
and Dream Life and Real Life (1893). A further collection, Stories, Dreams, and Allegories, 
was published posthumously in 1923. Schreiner also wrote an allegorical novella, Trooper 
Peter Halket of Mashonaland (1897) (hereafter Trooper Peter) which I consider in this 
chapter. Although allegory is a long-established literary form, Schreiner re-purposes it to 
critique aspects of the contemporary world, namely patriarchal, capitalist, and colonialist 
hegemonies, to offer alternative ways of living, and inspire readers to contribute to change.    
 
In this chapter I argue that the allegory form allows Schreiner to straddle narrative and 
abstract forms, as she does throughout her work, and it enables her to express her ideas 
without the need for many narrative contingencies. I assert that through this form she can 
clearly articulate her notion of the importance of the collective in relation to the individual, 
characterising humanity as a continuous collective body. To examine this body, I 
conceptualise it as bearing resemblance to the long-term continuous vegetal body and 
consider how she locates meaning in this body and in the individual’s contribution to its 
202 
 
progress. The ethics this engenders is collective and reading this through the vegetal, I 
establish the similarity of vegetal ethics to other relationship-based ethical systems. Through 
this long-term body of humanity, Schreiner merges the material and the metaphysical, 
corporeality and the ideal, both within humanity and in the new world that is created.  
 
In what follows I begin by exploring Schreiner’s use of the allegory form to express her 
collective vision. I then examine three of her allegories in detail. In Part I, I read Schreiner's 
evocation of the struggle for female freedom in ‘Three Dreams in a Desert’ through her 
presentation of womankind as a vegetal-like continuous body, that links past, present, and 
future, and that, through struggle, creates a new future. I contend that she situates meaning 
within the collective continuous body of womankind and that individual sacrifice and struggle 
becomes meaningful in relation to that body. I further argue that she roots enlightenment in a 
merging of the corporeal and the spiritual. In Part II, my analysis of ‘The Sunlight Lay Across 
My Bed’ focuses upon Schreiner’s representation of light as corporeal. I assert that through 
locating transcendence within the body, she portrays humanity as non-dualistic and a 
participant within an ecological web of creation. I consider how her critique of capitalism 
centres on its notion of the separate individual and how she contrasts this to a socialist 
Heaven in which all is interconnected. Finally, in Part III, I interrogate the allegorical novella, 
Trooper Peter through exploring how Schreiner's collective ethics are rooted in her portrayal 
of the long-term nature of Christ's kingdom. My analysis reveals the similarity between 
vegetal, gospel, and Schopenhauerian ethics, in their acceptance of difference. I 
demonstrate how in this allegory Schreiner asserts the importance of manifesting these 
ethical values in the here and now. I also examine her use of a tree as symbol of 
connectedness and a future South Africa, in which black and white peoples are integrated.  
 
One might ask why Schreiner refers to these pieces of writing as ‘dreams’, even using this 
as the title of her first collection. She uses this term in some of the titles: ‘Three Dreams in a 
Desert’, ‘A Dream of Wild Bees’, and in many of them the allegory is framed through a 
203 
 
dream. This reflects the fact that many of them seem to come to her in the form of dreams, 
visons, or flashes of inspiration. She explains in a letter that she has been ‘writing out a 
dream’,173 and that the Prelude of From Man to Man, (that she later refers to as an allegory) 
‘in an instant […] flashed on me’.174 Indeed, throughout her life she seems to have 
experienced mystical experiences, visions, or dreams. It is in writing them out that she calls 
them allegories, referring to Dreams as ‘my allegory book’.175 I suggest that in the allegory 
form she finds a mode that facilitates the expression of these dreams or visions.  
 
Growing up in a Protestant missionary household, Schreiner would have been aware of the 
tradition of allegorical extra-biblical writings and interpretations, such as Bunyan's The 
Pilgrim's Progress (1678). In her allegories she follows Bunyan in her use of biblical imagery 
and rhythms, and the inclusion of biblical quotations or paraphrasing. Her use of dream 
framing and everyman-type characters who learn from wiser interlocuters is also Bunyan-
esque. I argue that using such allegorical features and tropes allows her to work within the 
narrative form but without the need to develop individual characters. In the previous two 
chapters I explored how Schreiner problematises individuality within the novel form. I 
suggest that in allegory she finds a form that gives her capacity to focus on the collective 
rather than the individual. Characters are instead everyman/everywoman-type figures. The 
allegory form enables her to situate her contemporary societal critique within a wider 
tradition that embraces a spiritual and ethical universality. It also releases her from the 
delimited temporality and spatiality necessary to other narrative forms. In fact, it is this wider 
temporal and spatial framework that she uses in the abstract sections of African Farm and 
From Man to Man to leave behind the exigencies and limitations of the novel form to create a 
wider vision. This places her contemporary societal critique within a wider framework of 
 
173 Olive Schreiner, letter to Havelock Ellis, 21st Nov 1887, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 25th February 
2023]. 
174 Olive Schreiner, letter to Mrs Francis Smith, October 1909 in The Letters of Olive Schreiner, 1876-1920, ed. 
by S.C. Cronwright-Schreiner (London, T. Fisher Unwin Ltd, 1924) p.291, emphasis in original. 
175 Olive Schreiner, letter to William Philip Schreiner, Sept 1891. https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 30th 
March 2023]. 
204 
 
progress, viewing individual struggle as part of humanity’s continuous fight against 
hierarchical power structures.  
 
Allegorical form also facilitates Schreiner to explore abstract ideas within an engaging 
narrative structure. In her introduction to Woman and Labour (1911), Schreiner states that 
‘while it is easy clearly to express abstract thoughts in argumentative prose, whatever 
emotion those thoughts awaken I have not felt myself able adequately to express except in 
the other form’, this ‘other form’ being allegory.176 Schreiner implies that emotional 
engagement is necessary to fully make her arguments. In the allegoric form she finds a way 
to engage readers in her analysis of contemporary issues and to embed her call for change.  
 
I conceptualise Schreiner’s portrayal of humanity in the allegories as a continuous and 
progressive historical body. Although in many ways this is similar to the portrayal of 
humanity in African Farm and From Man to Man, in the allegories she is able to foreground 
and extend this perspective as the form absolves her from the necessity to develop 
individual characters, situate meaning in the individual consciousness, or represent progress 
within the small scale. Using the lens of Marder, I read this continuous body and its 
associated collective ethics, adaptive strategies, and non-dualist ontology as bearing affinity 
with a vegetal mode of being. I also read Schreiner's own use of the vegetal in these 
allegories as symbolic of the continuous body of humanity.  
 
Not only does Schreiner situate progress in this continuous body but also meaning. Marder 
extends his conceptualisation of vegetal growth as a spatial encoding of time through his 
assertion that the ‘meaning of vegetal being is time’ (MM 95). Applying such a perspective to 
Schreiner's humanity suggests that human meaning and purpose is located within the 
continuous body. Rather than meaning derived from individual existence or existential 
 
176 Olive Schreiner, Woman and Labour (London, Virago Ltd, 1978), p.16. 
205 
 
search, for Schreiner meaning comes from the individual’s relationship to that body and their 
contribution, often through struggle or even sacrifice, to humanity's shared progress. This is 
illustrated at the end of the ‘The Hunter’ allegory (published as part of African Farm and re-
published in Dreams) where, after spending much of his life cutting steps in the mountain for 
others to reach ‘Truth’, the hunter realises that ‘where I lie down worn out, other men will 
stand […] by the steps I have cut they will climb’ (D 73). 
 
Her emphasis on the importance of the collective embeds an ethics based on responsibility 
towards others. Across the allegories she advocates the importance of an ethical 
responsibility towards others as a way of working together to create a better future. She 
contrasts this stance to one of egoic individualism upon which are predicated, she contends, 
capitalist and colonialist ideologies. In fact, in response to her publisher's plan to publish a 
cheap edition of Dreams, she states ‘If I dedicated it to the public for whom I intended it, I 
should dedicate it (“To all Capitalists Millionaires and Middle-men […] and all high and 
mighty persons”)’, making clear that her societal critique is aimed at the values of the 
powerful who maintain hierarchical hegemonies.177 
 
For Schreiner, otherness is reconceptualised as unity that extends to the non-human. 
Reading her collective ethics through the perspectives of Schopenhauer, Marder, and Barad 
draws attention to how in the negation of individuality, they all extend their philosophies to 
purport an ethics based on connection. Schopenhauer contends that it is only in the 
phenomenal that separate individuals are perceived whereas in the noumenal all are 
connected. Marder views a ‘vegetal democracy of sharing and participation’ (MM 52) with 
vegetal beings problematising the binary between individual and whole. Barad asserts that 
her notion that individuals emerge from intra-actions rather than preceding them means that 
we ‘are always already responsible to the others with whom or which we are entangled’ (KB 
 
177 Olive Schreiner, letter to T. Fisher Unwin, 26th Sept 1892, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 24th January 
2022]. 
206 
 
393). Furthermore, in Trooper Peter, Schreiner's portrayal of Christ's kingdom embraces a 
similar stance.   
 
As in her other work, in the allegories Schreiner evinces a non-dualistic understanding of 
humanity in which mind and body merge. The body therefore becomes an integral part of 
progress, sacrifice, and unity. In their introduction to the Broadview Press edition of Dreams 
(2020), Barbara Black, Carly Nations, and Anna Spydell suggest that ‘just as allegory 
conjoins the literal and the symbolic, the work feels simultaneously embodied and 
disembodied […] the continual drifting from sleep to wakefulness suggests a world that is 
separate, yet intricately connected to our own'’178 Schreiner uses embodiment to link the 
framing dreamer/narrator to the dream itself in both ‘Three Dreams’ and ‘Sunlight Lay’, but I 
extend this understanding of Schreiner’s use of embodiment. I argue that through it is 
through placing importance on the corporeality of her characters, that she extends allegory 
form itself. It is the bodies of the women fighting against subjugation in ‘Three Dreams’ that 
must suffer. In ‘Sunlight Lay’ it is human bodies that enable participation in the whole 
ecosystem. Furthermore, in both these allegories, by embodying enlightenment itself, 
Schreiner undermines any notion of a dualistic separation between physical and mental 
spheres. My vegetal approach to these allegories offers a useful perspective in this respect 
in that it models a form of life that is inherently non-dualistic, having no separate inner 
sphere, and in which, at least for Marder, meaning and even thinking can be situated in the 
material.     
 
For Schreiner, the ideal of the future is concrete and must be established on earth. Her 
allegories can be read as a ‘call to arms’, demonstrating the need for action in the material 
reality of the present to lead to an ideal future that is also located within the tangible, rather 
 
178 Olive Schreiner, Dreams, ed. by Barbara Black, Carly Nations, and Anna Spydell (Peterborough (Ontario), 
Broadview Press, 2020), p.47. 
 
207 
 
than noumenal, world. In ‘Three Dreams’ the future ‘Land of Freedom’ is ‘heaven’ on ‘earth’ 
(D 84-5). The dreamer/narrator of ‘Sunlight Lay’ must return to the real world from Heaven to 
put into action what he has learnt in his journey through Hell and Heaven. In Trooper Peter, 
Peter Halket converts his newly found Christian beliefs into action to attempt to affect 
change for the South Africa of the future. As such, Schreiner articulates a sense of 
responsibility to participate in creating a better future for humanity.  
 
Her use of the allegory form means that she embeds her message in a form that can 
transcend the moment and speak to the future. Indeed, inherent in the allegorical form is the 
idea that it contains a greater meaning than the immediate situation it narrates. The 
dedication in the original edition of Dreams is ‘to a small girl-child, who may live to grasp 
somewhat of that which for us is yet sight, not touch’ (D 57). These allegories are indicative 
of her writing as part of a wider project which, alongside her political activism in causes such 
as women’s suffrage, racial equality, and the creation of a more equitable society, 
participates in striving for a more just society.  
 
I. ‘Three Dreams in a Desert: Under a Mimosa-Tree’: The female body as 
route to enlightenment 
 
‘Three Dreams in a Desert: Under a Mimosa-Tree’ (hereafter ‘Three Dreams’) is an allegory 
about womankind’s struggle for emancipation within a patriarchal society. In this allegory the 
narrator, falling asleep under a mimosa-tree, has three dreams, which represent the past, 
present, and future respectively of womankind. In the first dream the narrator sees two huge 
immobile figures in the sands of the desert. The woman figure lays on the sand, subjugated 
by the ‘Age-of-dominion-of-muscular-force’, the ‘burden of subjection’ and the band of 
‘Inevitable Necessity’. In the second dream, a woman, who is now mobile, asks the figure of 
‘Reason’ how to get to the distant ‘Land of Freedom’. He tells her that she must struggle 
‘down the banks of Labour’ and ‘through the water of Suffering’, and that any ensuing 
208 
 
individual sacrifice will contribute to the eventual emancipation of women and the freedom of 
the entire human race. In the final dream, ‘heaven […] On earth’ is reached. 
 
It is therefore an allegory of female progress. Jacqueline de Vries suggests that nineteenth-
century feminists ‘believed their movement paralleled other great evolutionary trends in 
history’ and was seen as part of a ‘teleological story of cumulative progress’.179 In ‘Three 
Dreams’, Schreiner represents the female journey as part of a wider journey of human 
progress. She not only places it within a framework of deep time but also shows female 
struggle as benefitting humankind as a whole.  
 
In this allegory, Schreiner presents womankind’s cause as having resonance with a 
spiritualised journey. Womankind needs to move out of the desert, through the ‘water of 
Suffering’, wearing only ‘one white garment’ (D 82-3) to lead on and eventually arrive at 
‘heaven’ (D 85). These and other religious tropes are reflective of, as Rose Lovell-Smith 
suggests, ‘the link between religion and political activism’ for feminists at the turn of the 
century.180 de Vries asserts that Suffragettes themselves ‘harnessed a distinctive feminist 
spirituality’ in their campaigns. She argues that they ‘fashioned militancy as a spiritual quest’, 
and co-opted ‘religious language, symbols and ideas.’181 Although this allegory could be 
read as secular in terms of its context and aims, Schreiner brings into play religious tropes to 
embed the necessary struggle and progress within a wider quest for meaning and purpose.  
 
If the Suffragettes responded to the religious undertones of ‘Three Dreams’, they also 
recognised its contemporary concrete reality. Constance Lytton, writing about the reading of 
this allegory by Suffragettes in Holloway prison, states that it ‘seemed scarcely an allegory. 
 
179 Jacqueline de Vries, ‘More than paradoxes to offer: Feminism, history and religious cultures’, in Women, 
Gender and Religious Cultures in Britain, 1800-1940, ed. by Sue Morgan and Jacqueline de Vries (London, 
Routledge, 2010), pp.188-210 (p.190). 
180 Rose Lovell-Smith, ‘Science and Religion in the Feminist Fin-de-Siècle and a New Reading of Olive 
Schreiner’s From Man to Man’, Victorian Literature and Culture, 29(2) (2001), 303-326 (p.308).  
181 de Vries, p.199.  
209 
 
The words hit out a bare literal description of the pilgrimage of women. It fell on our ears 
more like an ABC railway guide to our journey than a figurative parable’.182 As well as 
reading it as allied to a spiritual ‘pilgrimage’, through describing it as analogous to the ‘ABC 
railway guide’, she also finds in it a materialised reality. Moreover, it is not surprising that the 
Suffragettes found resonance in this allegory. Schreiner situates the late nineteenth-century 
struggle for female emancipation within a longer-term context of women’s fight for freedom 
and self-determination. The first dream reflects the situation they are working to defeat, and 
the second, their own struggles. The optimistic portrayal of the future in the third dream 
would have provided some motivation to them to continue their fight while suffering from 
their prison privations. 
 
I argue that the mimosa-tree of the title is both allegoric symbol and literal part of the 
framing. It is underneath the mimosa that the narrator shelters from the African sun and 
experiences the three dreams. But Schreiner’s inclusion of the mimosa-tree in the title of the 
allegory is suggestive of a greater importance. I offer a reading in which the mimosa-tree 
gestures towards the Bodhi tree underneath which the Buddha experienced his awakening. 
Through the allusion to the Bodhi tree, Schreiner incorporates another signifier of the 
spiritual into her allegory. For the narrator/dreamer, the visionary awakening is spiritualised 
and yet what is learnt must be realised in the world of the real, as the Buddha’s own 
awakening was transformed into action. 
 
Reflective of other uses of trees in religion and myth, Schreiner's mimosa-tree is a symbol of 
continuous growth, echoing her use of the mimosa to represent Rebekah's continuous 
regeneration and resilience in From Man to Man (see p.192-3). The vegetal's spatial 
expression of its continuous growth means that it can bear the past in its ‘extended being’ 
 
182 Constance Lytton, Prisons and Prisoners, Some Personal Experiences (London, William Heinemann, 1914) 
(repr. in Dreams, ed. by Barbara Black, Carly Nations, and Anna Spydell (Peterborough (Ontario), Broadview 
Press, 2020)), p.138. 
210 
 
(MM 155) and is therefore a spatial expression of time (MM 96). From this perspective, 
Schreiner’s mimosa-tree becomes an embodiment of time, and the narrator shelters within 
this continuity of time. It enables the narrator to experience time itself and to perceive the 
history of womankind as continuous and progressive. 
 
Marder's extension of vegetal spatiality into meaning or purpose – ‘vegetal life is laden with 
meaning in all its spatiality, materiality, and finitude’ (MM 76) - is a useful approach for 
considering Schreiner's presentation of collective womankind. If Schreiner's collective 
continuous body of humanity is read as ontologically vegetal, then meaning and purpose can 
be located within it. Progress, created through continuous struggle, is that of the whole body. 
For each individual who contributes to this progress, meaning adheres to their contribution to 
the long-term emancipatory achievement.  
 
The struggle is portrayed in the second dream. The woman enquires of ‘Reason’ how to 
cross the river to journey to the ‘Land of Freedom’. There is neither a bridge nor a track. 
‘Reason’ explains that these need to be created, and uses the analogy of locusts crossing a 
stream:  
first one comes down to the water-edge, and it is swept away, and then another 
comes and then another, and then another, and at last with their bodies piled up 
a bridge is built and the rest pass over (D 84).  
 
Schreiner suggests that the body of the individual that must be sacrificed in aid of progress is 
of no more importance than the individual locust who dies for the sake of the swarm. Like the 
modules of a plant that are continuously lost – leaves, shoots - their contribution might even 
be forgotten, as the locusts who are swept away and might not even have contributed to the 
bridge, ‘are heard of no more’ (D 85), and yet their lives have meaning in relation to that 
contribution. Reading this through Schopenhauer’s notion that viewing individuals as 
separate phenomena is illusory as in the noumenal we are all connected by the will, 
suggests that the sacrifice of the individual in favour of the whole body of humanity is 
211 
 
reasonable. Schreiner's use of the symbolic and unnamed figures of allegory to represent 
these anonymous women further elevates the meaningfulness of their lives.  
 
Through her use of a long-term temporality, Schreiner implies that female subjugation is 
aberrant in that it breaks the natural continuity of progress. Such natural continuous 
progression is symbolised by the mimosa-tree and echoed in the progress of the sun across 
the sky as the dreams progress. It is also reflected in Schreiner’s own note that clarifies that 
the steep banks of the river are caused by ‘the course of ages the river has worn its gigantic 
bed’ (D 82). In contrast to these natural progressions, womankind’s progress has reversed. 
In the first dream, ‘woman’ has been laying in the sand for so long that ‘the oldest, oldest, 
oldest man living has never seen her move’ and ‘the oldest, oldest book records that she lay 
here. But by extending the temporality back further: before then ‘are found the marks of her 
footsteps’ and these are ‘side by side with his’ as she ‘once wandered free […] with him’ (D 
79), Schreiner shows women’s previous freedom and equality with men. Her immobile state 
is presented as an unnatural interruption and her subjugation politically and deliberately 
motivated rather than an ‘Inevitable Necessity’ (D 80). 
 
But in showing that man had agency in his subjugation of women, Schreiner also asserts 
human agency per se, suggesting that women must use their agency to liberate themselves 
and create a different future. This allegory bears some resemblance to the ‘Spirit of the 
Ages’ allegory in From Man to Man (FM 223-5).183 In that allegory, the ‘Spirit of the Ages’ 
tells a woman who represents ‘Humanity’ and is immobile in the desert, and who blames an 
anonymous ‘they’ for her plight, that ‘no deliverer will ever come. You, you, yourself must 
save yourself’ (FM 224).  
 
 
183 In From Man to Man, after writing the ‘Spirit of the Ages’ allegory, Rebekah puts it away in a drawer but 
wonders if ‘some day the little allegory would enlarge itself and she would write it in fit words to make others 
see the picture’. She expects not but also notes that ‘the little picture she would never forget’. I suggest that 
on a meta-level this allegory does become enlarged and that ‘Three Dreams in a Desert’ is its development. 
212 
 
In ‘Three Dreams’, as Schreiner locates the agency to progress within corporeality, I 
approach it through a New Materialist lens. Barad suggests that through agential matter, the 
world can be created or reconfigured. For Barad, ‘matter’s dynamism is generative […] in the 
sense of bringing forth new worlds […] Bodies do not simply take their places in the world’ 
(KB 170). From this perspective, it is through the continuous temporal body that a new 
‘temporality and spatiality can emerge in this processual historicity’ (KB 141). Read in this 
way, the new world that humankind arrives at in the third dream has been created rather 
than reached. In the second dream, the woman only ‘thinks’ she sees ‘trees and hills’ in the 
distance; in the final dream, they have made those hills a reality. 
 
Schreiner suggests that such struggle is essential for growth and progress. No one will give 
woman freedom. Instead, ‘she must help herself. Let her struggle till she is strong’ (D, 81, 
emphasis in original). In this respect, Schreiner likens womankind to the vegetal who must 
continually struggle to adapt and grow and will die if does not do so.184 Moreover, it is this 
adaptability of the mimosa that Rebekah recognises in From Man to Man (see p.191-2), 
suggesting womankind’s adaptability through struggle is also represented in the framing 
mimosa. In contrast, in the motionless woman of the first dream, Schreiner suggests that 
plant-like sessility is unnatural in that she has relinquished many of the characteristics that 
make her human. Described by the narrator as a ‘creature’ with her legs ‘crushed under her', 
she is prone rather than an upstanding biped and her inability to raise her neck to see 
implies a loss of cerebral capacity (D 79-81). Her passivity represents the lack of agency of 
the woman who does not struggle to create progress.   
 
Schreiner also foregrounds the importance of corporeality by entwining progress towards 
enlightenment with embodiment, countering dualistic notions of the separation of mind or 
spirit, and body. At the end of the first dream, woman gains enlightenment in association 
 
184 Hallé, p.97. 
213 
 
with regained embodiment. Once the burden has been removed from her, her body 
‘quivered, and a light came into her eyes’ as ‘a thought has come to her’. She ‘raised her 
head’ and when there is ‘light in her eyes’ the ‘creature staggered onto its knees’ (D 81-2). 
By the second dream, woman has become upright and mobile. Schreiner reinstates the body 
as an integral element of humanity and humanity's progress. As such, hers is a radical 
feminist reworking of the Puritan allegory of Bunyan, which demotes the body, particularly 
the female body. For Schreiner, it is female bodies that create the bridge over which will 
pass the ‘entire human race’ (D 85) to get to the ‘Land of Freedom’. 
 
In the final dream, the land that has been created is one of unitedness: male and female, 
real and ideal. Woman and man now walk ‘hand in hand’ (D 85). This bears similarity to their 
relationship before the interruption of the ‘Age-of-muscular-force’, when their footsteps were 
‘side by side’ and they ‘wandered free’ together (D 79). Normal progress has been resumed, 
and the material and ideal united. Schreiner inserts no interruption to progress through a 
move to the purely spiritual realm, but ‘heaven’ is created ‘on earth’ (D 85) and the spiritual 
continuous with the tangible. As woman in the first dream finds no place in heaven or earth 
for her, looking ‘at the earth, and at the sky, and knew there was no hope for her’ (D 81), 
through struggle and sacrifice, humankind’s existence in the future combines these two 
realms in an image of utopian unity. 
 
II. ‘The Sunlight Lay Across My Bed,-’: from darkness into light 
 
In ‘The Sunlight Lay Across My Bed'’ (hereafter ‘Sunlight Lay’) Schreiner allegorises the 
contrasting ethics of capitalist and socialist societies. It was originally published in The New 
Review in two parts, in April and May 1890, the first part headed ‘Hell’ and the second 
‘Heaven’, such a division emphasising the two contrasting societies portrayed. 
Subsequently, it was published as part of the collected allegories, Dreams, in November 
1890.  
214 
 
 
In this allegory the narrator dreams that God takes his soul to visit the capitalist society of 
Hell. In this hierarchical society, those with most power feast on the blood produced by 
crushing the bodies of the oppressed in a wine-press. In a second dream, God takes the 
narrator to three levels of a socialistic Heaven respectively. In the first Heaven, humans are 
a source of light that is shared for the benefit of all, including the non-human. In the second 
and third Heavens, the increasing light enables the narrator to perceive a world in which all 
things are one. At the end, the narrator returns to the world of reality with an altered 
perspective. I argue that although the allegory is set in the divine spheres of Hell and 
Heaven, corporeality remains important. 
 
In ‘Sunlight Lay’ Schreiner further extends her notion of the non-dualistic nature of humanity. 
In ‘Three Dreams’, the non-dualistic representation of corporeality is one in which mind and 
body together become aware and engender progress. However, in ‘Sunlight Lay’, Schreiner 
is more radical in that she locates transcendence itself within the body. In the first Heaven, 
humans emit a light that is corporeal: ‘all their body gives the light; they dare not cover any 
part’ (D 113). Rather than situated in a separate mental or spiritual sphere, the light is 
spread across the whole physical body. Viewing this through the lens of Marder suggests 
that Schreiner gestures here towards a vegetal-like humanity. For Marder, a major contrast 
between humanity and the vegetal, is the human possession of a separate interiority. In 
contrast, the vegetal is pure exteriority and the human body in ‘Sunlight Lay’ seems to have 
some similarity to this. Furthermore, for Marder, the vegetal is a living critique of the dualistic 
separation between soul and body, and depth and surface (MM 11). Through locating the 
transcendent light within corporeality, Schreiner diametrically opposes dualistic notions of 
humanity and reformulates humanity’s transcendental nature as embodied.  
 
Marder maintains that the materiality and non-conscious existence of plants is devalued in 
Western thinking, but in this allegory, Schreiner promotes the importance of corporeality and 
215 
 
reasserts humanity's roots within material existence. In Return of the Native, Hardy presents 
abstract mental light as at odds with the materiality of the body. Schreiner’s model of 
humanity is one in which materiality and a transcendent light are not mutually antagonistic. 
Such a perspective bears resemblance to her models of connectivity in African Farm and 
From Man to Man in which materiality and metaphysics are mutually entwined.  
 
Such corporeality also embeds humanity within the world of all beings. Rather than being 
separated through the possession of interiority, Schreiner's humankind participates within 
the ecological web of all beings. Through emitting light, humanity provides the energy for 
plants upon which the ecosystem runs, even replacing the sun. The narrator, noticing the 
increasing brightness, asks God ‘if the sun were not going to rise’, and God replies ‘no; we 
are coming to where the people are’; it is humanity's light that is ‘shining on the plants that 
they may grow’ (D 113). 
 
Reading in a less materialised and more symbolic mode, the light of humanity enables 
humankind to become one with all other lifeforms as they share the light of the spirit. Such a 
representation of the divine in nature is an incarnational understanding. In Victorian Parables 
(2012), Susan Colon draws on Rebecca Styler’s understanding that for Victorian literary 
women there are ‘wider tendencies towards an “incarnational” Christianity’, in which the 
division between secular and sacred is blurred’.185 I read Schreiner’s suggestion that through 
humanity's sharing of their corporeal light, all creation is enabled to participate in the light of 
transcendence, as both incarnational and material. The light is divine but located in the 
materiality of the human who has attained a heavenly non-egoic largesse. This bears some 
relation to Emma Mason’s understanding of Christina Rossetti’s notion of grace. Mason 
suggests that for Rossetti, through grace, human life could ‘“expand”’ and ‘welcome God 
 
185 Susan E. Colon, Victorian Parables (London, Continuum, 2012), p.32. 
216 
 
back into the world’.186 However, as for Schreiner it is the corporeality of the human that 
enables this light to expand into the world, she roots this transcendence more overtly in 
materiality. 
 
Schreiner represents light as that which reveals a perception of the unity of all things: human 
and non-human, organic and inorganic. As Schreiner’s narrator passes up through the levels 
of the Heavens, he is increasingly able to perceive himself as part of a unity. In the first 
Heaven, the sharing of the light creates a connection between everything. In the second 
Heaven, light reveals an underlying pattern behind phenomenal reality. Then in the highest 
Heaven the light shows the narrator a oneness across everything: ‘it grew so bright I could 
not see things separately; and which were God, or the man, or I, I could not tell; we were all 
blended’ (D 119). The narrator sees the noumenal unity behind the world of phenomena. 
Mason suggests that for Christina Rossetti, in a state of grace humanity can ‘perceive itself 
as one in a participatory and interdependent body that is at once material and also divine’.187 
In ‘Sunlight Lay’, God allows the narrator to catch a glimpse of his own participation in such 
a unity in the world of the divine, but this is momentary. Once it is dark again, he resumes 
the recognition of things as separate. He must return to Earth to learn to recognise unity in 
materiality as he is not yet in a state of grace. 
 
Such an underlying unity makes sense of the sharing paradigm in Heaven. For 
Schopenhauer, our notion of separate beings is a misunderstanding as in the noumenal all 
beings are connected. He asserts that ‘genuine virtue’ can only come from the ‘intuitive 
knowledge that recognises in another’s individuality the same inner nature as in one’s own’ 
(WWR1 367-8). Schreiner shows people in Heaven acting upon such an understanding. In 
the first Heaven, people touch the ‘maimed stumps’ of ‘our brother’, a disabled man, and 
 
186 Emma Mason, ‘Praying Kin: Christina Rossetti and the Unity of Things’, in Anticipatory Materialisms in 
Literature and Philosophy: 1790-1930, ed. by Jo Carruthers, Nour Dakkak, Rebecca Spence (Cham, Palgrave 
MacMillan, 2019), pp.169-182 (p.173-4). 
187 Mason, p.174. 
217 
 
their light enables these stumps to ‘shine brighter than anything in Heaven’. In the second 
Heaven, the men who are labouring to dig up stones, share their finds: ‘when one found a 
stone he passed it on to his fellow, and he to another, and he to another. No man kept the 
stone he found’ (D 115-6).   
 
Schreiner contrasts Heaven and Hell through differing conceptions of the relationship 
between people. In the light of Heaven there is a recognition of oneness: in the darkness of 
Hell, an awareness of separation. This creates, in Hell, an egoic mode of being, predicated 
upon viewing fellow inhabitants as other. In Heaven, as for the plant which incorporates 
difference within itself, difference is positive and strengthening rather than a deficiency. In 
Hell, otherness is represented through the positioning of a curtain that hides the horrors of 
the wine-press from the powerful who are feasting on the wine. Those who recognise and 
call out their suffering are also destroyed; others learn to remain silent. Even those 
oppressed join in the struggle for survival by crushing those who have sunk below them in 
the wine-press. Merlin Sheldrake makes the point that ‘modern capitalist thought is founded 
on the idea of rational individuals acting in their own interest’, and that ‘without individuals, 
everything comes crashing down’.188 Although Schreiner’s stance in African Farm and From 
Man to Man is one that is critical of individuality and in From Man to Man she presents an 
ethics based on a understanding of connectivity, I read this allegory as her most direct 
analysis of the reasons for and consequences of individualism. Her critique of capitalism in 
‘Sunlight Lay’ is rooted within the value placed upon individual self, which facilitates the 
development of hierarchical systems.   
 
In Hell, the instrument used to oppress those without power is the body. It is through their 
blood, produced through crushing them in the wine-presses, that those without power are 
used and destroyed. Schreiner potentially alludes here to the wine-press of Revelation and 
 
188 Sheldrake, p.237. 
218 
 
its resultant blood which represents the wrath of God.189 But my reading of Schreiner’s wine-
press and blood is more corporeal and, as such, I see it as gesturing towards the Eucharist. 
Carly Nations, also reading it as alluding to the Eucharist, suggests that the blood/wine in 
‘Sunlight Lay’ is ‘not simply an embodiment of sacrifice, but […] the body itself, sacrificed for 
the enjoyment of others’.190 I extend this to recognise that it is through the body that 
Schreiner contrasts Heaven and Hell. In Heaven, the body unifies all beings; in Hell, the 
body is used to oppress and destroy the other: the narrator is horrified to realise that the 
wine drunk by those feasting is created from the bodies of others: ‘they drink it – their own!’ 
(D 107, emphasis in original). But, of course, they who drink it, see it as the blood of the 
other, who are not ‘their own’.  
 
Schreiner also suggests that unlike the society in Heaven in which resources are shared, the 
decadent society of Hell treats the environment as other. This can be read through Marder's 
contention that humanity's interiority ‘emasculates its integral connection to the environment’ 
(MM 168). Where the feasting houses are now in ruins, the landscape of Hell is barren and 
arid, the earth laid to waste and ‘sodden’ with the blood of those destroyed (D 110). Such 
environmental degradation is indicative of a capitalist, perhaps even colonialist, society that 
through the exploitation of resources, including people, eventually destroys itself. Looking 
upwards to access the holy, they do not recognise that God is in the world of materiality, as 
Schreiner reinforces through stating that ‘out in the sunshine, God lay’ (D 106). Moreover, 
responses to the environmental other in Hell and Heaven can be traced in the uses to which 
fruits are put. In Hell, people use it to destroy one another. Women bite into fruit to poison it, 
so that ‘another may not eat’, with the result that ‘in Hell all men fear’ (D 103-4). In Heaven, 
the people ‘shone long’ (D 114) to ripen the fruit which is then shared amongst them.    
 
189 ‘And the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast it into the 
great winepress of the wrath of God. And the winepress was trodden without the city, and blood came out of 
the winepress …’ (Revelation 14.19-20). 
190 Carly Nations, '"Made Alive": Olive Schreiner's Dreams and the Embodied Vision of Equality', Nineteenth 
Century Gender Studies, 17(2) (2021), p.14-15.  
219 
 
 
Schreiner recognises the importance of alterity to growth and articulates the importance of 
the struggle from material darkness into a more spiritual light. The figure in the highest 
heaven, has had to climb from the ‘lowest Hell’, and it is through this that ‘its limbs grew 
larger’ and ‘drops fell from its eyes’. Schreiner represents such striving through a vegetal 
analogy: the figure realises that ‘Hell is the seed ground from which Heaven springs. He 
sees the sap ascending’ (D 118). I read this through Marder’s notion that both spiritual and 
intellectual enlightenment in Western thought puts into analogic service vegetal movement 
from the darkness of the earth towards the light of the sun. Schreiner's vegetal image 
suggests that enlightenment not only connects the realms of darkness and light, and 
material and transcendence, but also recognises the necessity of darkness to seed light. But 
Marder also suggests that such analogies serve to undermine the importance of beginning in 
darkness and striving towards the light. For Marder the enlightenment analogy ‘represses 
the unconscious remainder it cannot do away with […] leaving the darkness of mere life 
behind’ and therefore ‘undercuts the conditions of its own existence’ (MM 177). I assert that 
Schreiner's image reaffirms the necessity for the originating darkness and that true 
enlightenment requires the opposing darkness from which to grow. This manifests in the 
allegory with the necessity for the narrator to return to the real world to make his own journey 
from his material roots. This is the reason why God refuses each of his pleas to remain in 
Heaven, telling him that he must ‘go back to earth: that which you seek is there’(D 119, 
emphasis in original). As the allegory ends with the narrator firstly perceiving the morning 
light as a ‘dull grey’, but then becoming cognisant that ‘across my bed a long yellow streak of 
pale London sunlight was lying’ (D 120), he begins his own enlightenment journey back in 
the world of the material; the point to which the title alludes. 
 
 
 
220 
 
III. Trooper Peter Halket of Mashonaland: from ideals to action 
 
Trooper Peter was written during 1896/7, contemporaneous with the events it depicts. It 
concerns Cecil Rhodes’ Chartered Company’s attempted invasion of what became 
Rhodesia, the Jameson Raid, and the wider speculative colonialism of Rhodes and his ilk in 
South Africa. It was published as a stand-alone novella and was an overt attempt to 
intervene into contemporary politics. As Schreiner states in a letter to her brother: ‘it is to […] 
the great British public […] that my little book is addressed […] They must know where the 
injustice and oppression really lies’.191  
 
We might question to what extent Trooper Peter is an allegory or a novella. Schreiner 
herself, in explaining its dreamed inception, refers to it as an allegory: ‘as I opened my eyes 
there was an allegory full fledged in my mind! A sort of allegory story about Matabele 
land’.192 Matthew Blackman, in considering its formal strategies, assesses whether it is a 
realist novel or an allegory, recognising Schreiner’s use of elements from both forms. He 
views her use of character, plot, and a plurality of voices as realist, but sees its ‘powerful 
proclamatory voice’ as reminiscent of allegory.193 Although I am not convinced that the 
weighing up of the relative importance of these two forms in relation to Trooper Peter is 
helpful, it does draw attention to how Schreiner blurs the boundaries between these forms. 
In Trooper Peter, elements of the realist setting are also symbolic, and this includes the tree 
that I discuss in detail. Schreiner uses a novelistic plurality of voices and yet Christ’s 
allegorical voice and his ethical position remain paramount. As Blackman recognises, in 
Trooper Peter, the figure of Christ is both ‘word of God’ but also incarnate.194 I argue that the 
crossovers between realist novella and allegory that Blackman observes, rather than being 
 
191 Olive Schreiner, letter to William Philip Schreiner, December 1896, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 6th 
April 2023]. 
192 Olive Schreiner, letter to Betty Molteno, 20th August 1896, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 28th March 
2023]. 
193 Matthew Blackman, ‘Bakhtin and Trooper Peter Halket of Mashonaland: Beyond Allegory and Realism’, 
English in Africa, 48(1) (2021), 33-52 (p.34). 
194 Blackman, p.45. 
221 
 
problematic are part of Schreiner’s very project. In her allegories, Schreiner combines 
concrete reality and noumenal to emphasise the interconnections between these spheres, 
reminding her readers that abstract values and beliefs must influence concrete action. 
 
Blackman further argues that in Trooper Peter ‘it is difficult to discern a singular allegorical 
and ethical message’.195 On the contrary, I assert that Schreiner’s ethical stance is clear and 
that it is that of the gospels. However, through my analysis of the ethical framework of this 
allegory, I recognise how it bears a similarity to that which extends from Marder’s vegetal 
and Barad’s agential realism. It is also that embedded in Schopenhauer’s hidden 
connectivity.  
 
In the first chapter of this allegory, Peter Halket, a guileless young trooper employed in 
Rhodes’ Chartered Company, having become separated from the rest of his company, 
spends a fearful night alone on a kopje.196 During the night Christ appears to him in the 
guise of a stranger. Halket proudly reveals to Christ the brutal destruction that he and his 
fellow colonialists mete out to the natives, their homes, and their land. Christ tells Halket 
about his own ‘company’ (his kingdom) and its ethical principles. Halket becomes converted 
and Christ instructs him to carry out tasks that would contribute to the establishment of the 
kingdom. Halket feels unable to complete any of these tasks except for Christ’s final 
instruction: ‘in that small spot where alone on earth your will rules, bring there into being the 
kingdom today’ (TPR, 187).197 The second and final chapter articulates Trooper Peter’s 
completion of this task. After failing to persuade the Captain to offer mercy to a captured 
native who has been condemned to death, under the cover of darkness, Halket releases the 
native, saving his life. Halket himself, caught in the act, is shot dead.  
 
195 Blackman, p.49. 
196 A ‘kopje’ is a small hillock. 
197 Schreiner explains in a letter that in the dream in which this allegory comes to her, ‘I just as I opened my 
eyes saw Peter Halket on the kopje and heard the voices talking’ (Olive Schreiner, letter to Henrietta Schreiner, 
25th December 1901, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 28th March 2023]. 
222 
 
 
As in ‘Sunlight Lay’, in Trooper Peter Schreiner contrasts two ethical positions: this time they 
are those of speculative colonialism and the ethics of the gospel. Schreiner’s explanation of 
gospel ethics bears similarity to a Schopenhaurian compassionate ethics and a 
Marderesque ethics rooted in ‘vegetal democracy’ (MM 52). Peter Halket explains to Christ 
his colonialist ethics: that ‘we don't come out here to work […] we've come here to make 
money’ (TPR, 83).  Individual gain is the aim - entirely rational when viewed from a point of 
view centred on self. In a Schopenhauerian sense, he is acting for the benefit of his 
individual will. However, Schopenhauer maintains that this is a misunderstanding of our 
underlying connectedness, and reading from the perspective of Helen Zimmern’s recognition 
that Schopenhauerian ethics are ‘in unison with those of the New Testament, and might be 
defined as its scientific expression’, indicates that such Schopenhauerian ethics are also 
reflected in this allegory. 198 Christ’s explanation to Halket of the ‘unknown that lies beyond 
us we know of no otherwise than through its manifestation in our own hearts’ (TPR, 132), is 
suggestive of such hidden connectedness. It is through such teachings that Halket's ethical 
stance eventually becomes converted to one that incorporates a recognition of similarity and 
therefore compassion for his fellow beings.   
 
As Christ puts modular vegetal growth into service in arguing for the acceptance of 
difference, the similarity of a vegetal ethics to that of the gospel can be recognised more 
overtly than that of Schopenhauerian ethics. Christ asserts his kingdom’s acceptance of 
difference when he states that it encompasses ‘men of every race and from every land’, 
including those of ‘every religion, Buddhists, Mahomedans, Confucians, Freethinkers, 
Atheists, Christians, Jews’ (TPR 103). He goes on to symbolise unity in difference through 
tree growth, instructing Halket to tell the English and the Boers, that ‘you should stretch out 
your arms to welcome each stranger who comes to live and labour with you. You are the 
 
198 Zimmern, p.154. 
223 
 
twin branches of one tree; you are the sons of one mother’ (TPR 168). The branches 
symbolise the unity between the offspring of one being. But for Marder, vegetal being is not 
merely multiple, it problematises and makes meaningless the entire multiple/whole binary. Its 
modules are neither fully separate nor fully unified. Reading Christ's image of the tree 
through this image implies that his kingdom likewise negates the entire concept of 
individuality. He suggests that the two white races are not separate and, in recognition of 
their combined interests, should share the land and its resources.   
 
Barad’s dismissal of the separate nature of the individual might be useful in this context, 
particularly as she extends her conceptualisation of matter as secondary to relationships or 
intra-actions, to an ethical position. She suggests that rather than individuals possessing 
ethical responsibilities towards one another, the ethical obligation exists prior to this. For 
Barad it is ‘not a commitment that a subject chooses but rather an incarnate relation that 
precedes the intentionality of consciousness’ (KB 392). Reading Schreiner’s gesture towards
