the lack of separate individuals in her use of the tree through Barad, the ethical perspective 
becomes primary, requiring no conscious decision-making. Ethical responsibility is an 
obligation that operates from a position of there already being an underlying ‘relation of 
responsibility to the other’ (KB 391) that is not even really the other but already inter-related. 
This seems closely related to the point that Christ is teaching Halket: that he has an a priori 
responsibility towards everyone. There is no other. 
 
But Christ's message that the two white races should share the land is not without 
problematic racist overtones as it rather bypasses colonisation’s fundamental injustice to the 
native black people of South Africa. However, Schreiner goes on to use another tree as 
symbol of the integration of black and white races; an appropriate image in the light of the 
fact that as vegetal being, the tree, is itself symbolic of an integrated mode of being with a 
heteronomous relation to alterity that neither denies otherness nor appropriates it. In the 
camp, the condemned black captive is tied to a white tree. Schreiner describes the tree and 
224 
 
the black captive as integrated as ‘the black man hung against the white stem, so closely 
bound to it that they seemed one’ (TPR 249). Black and white, human and tree, merge. 
Schreiner extends such an image of integration further. When Halket is shot while releasing 
the native from the tree, it becomes the place where ‘a black man and a white man's blood 
were mingled’ (TPR 259).   
 
Schreiner goes on to articulate a further affinity between the captive’s body and the tree. He 
is wasted with limbs that ‘had fallen away, making the joints stand out’, hair that is ‘wild and 
thick with neglect’ and skin that ‘was rough with want and exposure’ (TPR 249-50). The tree 
is ‘stunted’, ‘gnarled and knotted’, with ‘misshapen branches’ (TPR 195). Both have suffered 
through the other of a tough environment. However, the heteronomous tree has managed to 
adapt and survive; the black man has not. Alterity for him is a colonial society, diametrically 
opposed to him as other.  
 
 
Schreiner uses a long-term temporal framework in which to examine Halket’s ethics and 
suggest the longevity of the kingdom. As such, I read this tree as a visual representation of 
Christ’s kingdom that stretches into the past. Reading it through Marder’s notion of vegetal 
spatiality as the embodiment of time, it serves as a symbol of continuity, growth, and 
survival. Its contorted and stunted nature inscribes its growth and survival within a harsh 
environment, as has Christ’s kingdom. But Schreiner’s Christ lengthens the temporality of his 
kingdom further back into deep time. He purports the existence of the kingdom before any 
notion of ‘Christianity’, even before the development of humanity itself, dating from ‘when 
these hills were young, and these lichens had hardly shown their stains upon the rocks’, its 
‘fore-dawn’ recognised within the care and love of animals for their young and their mates 
that are now extinct (TPR 145; 148). Mike Kissack and Michael Titlestad recognise how 
Schreiner endows Christ with an ‘extensive historical panorama’ and an ‘evolutionary 
225 
 
perspective’ which contrasts to the ‘limited historical perspective’ of Halket’s.199 Such a 
perspective is, of course, consistent with Schreiner’s other writings. But in relation to this 
allegory, this means that the ethics associated with the kingdom are shown as eternal and 
fundamental, and in contrast, Halket’s appear short-term and opportunistic. Through her use 
of the long-established allegory form, Schreiner reinforces this perspective, formally situating 
Christ’s ethics within a long-term moral framework.   
 
The tree’s spatiality is also suggestive of the survival of Christ’s kingdom into the future. Its 
‘white stem and outstretched arms’, along with the stones beneath it that mark Halket’s 
grave, is the final image of the abandoned camp that the men see as they leave at the end 
of the allegory (TPR 264). It therefore becomes an image of endurance and survival into the 
future. As a symbol of the integration of black and white, it also embodies a future post-
colonial unified society. The combined bloods of black and white man under the tree will be 
utilised by the roots in a future ‘material “resurrection”’ (MM 67). Kissack and Titlestad 
problematise Halket's sacrifice, asking if it is ‘a recapitulation of the impasse and futility of 
such an unrealistic gesture in the world of political conflict’. As another trooper states, ‘There 
is no God in Mashonaland’ (TPR 261). Their solution is to read it through what they term 
Schreiner's ‘“Mystical Christianity”’ which focuses upon ‘the merits of considering oneself a 
conduit through which the divine nature can express itself’, as opposed to a ‘“Church and 
Biblical” Christianity’ which focuses upon authority and obedience.200 The first of these is 
more representative of Schreiner's interpretation of Christ, but my response to the potential 
futility of Halket's sacrifice is inscribed in the image of the surviving tree that symbolises the 
continuance of the kingdom through its embodiment of time.  
 
 
199 Mike Kissack & Michael Titlestad, 'Olive Schreiner and the Secularisation of the Moral Imagination’, English 
in Africa, 33(1) (2006), 23-46 (p.38). 
200 Kissack & Titlestad, p.41-2. They state that their use of the terms and ideas of ‘Mystical Christianity’ and 
‘Church and Biblical Christianity’ are taken from Linda Woodhead, Christianity: A Very Short Introduction 
(Oxford, Oxford University Press, 2004), p.46; p.71. 
 
226 
 
I read this tree as symbolic of the message of the crucifixion. With its ‘white stem and 
outstretched arms’ (TPR, 264), this observation pointedly made after Halket’s Christ-like 
sacrifice, it is similar to a print that Halket remembers from his old school-house of Christ 
‘hung with his arms stretched out and the blood dropping from his feet’ (TPR 25). It therefore 
not only embodies the divine kingdom but is also a reminder of the necessity for sacrifice in 
aid of the creation of a better future. Christ explicitly articulates this message to Halket. In 
contrast to Halket’s assertions that a Christian is one who believes in Heaven, Hell, God, 
Christ, and the Bible (TPR 99-100), through the voice of the preacher whose story he tells 
Halket, Christ instructs that ‘the past is dead, it lives only for us to learn. The present, the 
present only, is ours to work in, and the future ours to create’ (TPR 121). Our only efficacy is 
through action in the present in support of the future. This is a similar message to that of 
‘Three Dreams’, both reinforcing the Baradian notion that the material future is ours to 
create. Christ makes his message more pertinent to Halket when he instructs him to act so 
that ‘the day shall come, when the stars, looking down on this little world, shall see no spot 
where the soil is moist and dark with the blood of man shed by his fellow man’ (TPR 149). 
He foreshadows Halket’s sacrificial death under the tree and the mingling of his blood with 
that of the black captive. Furthermore, the tree is not only an image of sacrifice, but a spatial 
representation of how the past (through growth) impacts upon the future; a lesson perhaps 
that it offers humanity. 
 
Schreiner stresses the importance to humanity of belonging or rootedness and implies that 
the lack of belonging on the part of the colonialists emboldens their destructive impulses. 
Halket acts towards the land of South Africa and its people as if he belongs. Saying he will 
teach the ‘bloody niggers’ a lesson, the narrative voice comments that this is his:  
pugilistic spirit rising, firmly reseating himself on the South African earth, which 
two years before he had never heard of, and eighteen months before he had 
never seen, as if it had been his mother earth (TPR 92-3). 
 
227 
 
Although he asserts himself on ‘earth’ that is not his ‘mother earth’, Schreiner suggests that a 
‘mother earth’ sense of rootedness is of importance to humanity, perhaps as much as it is for 
the vegetal. But the colonialist’s method of creating rootedness in South Africa is destructive 
and exploitative, whereas the vegetal’s heteronomous mode of being means that it does not 
assert itself over its environment but is an integral part of it (MM 168). The vegetal can even 
create place and although Halket might try to do so through the use of force, he cannot 
‘reseat’ himself on South African earth. This is a far more developed understanding of the 
effects of colonialisation than Schreiner presents in African Farm. Whereas in African Farm 
Schreiner presents characters who suffer from a rootlessness which I read partially caused 
by colonialisation, in Trooper Peter, published fourteen years later, she directly addresses 
this issue as a consequence of colonialism. 
 
But Halket does eventually find belonging and meaning in relational ethics rather than in a 
fraudulent and untenable relationship to the South African land. His need for a ‘mother earth’ 
is also expressed through his frequent thoughts of his mother. While alone on the kopje his 
thoughts turn to her and although he often brushes it off, he is aware that in South Africa he 
does not live up to her moral standards. But he recognises his mother in Christ, telling him 
that ‘when your eyes look at me it seems to me as if she was looking at me’ (TPR 72). It 
seems that in Christ he regains the sense of belonging that he cannot find in the land. His 
relationship with Christ becomes his ‘mother earth’, symbolised by the tree that is 
representative of Christ, also symbolising a protective mother with her ‘branches, like arms, 
stretched out on either side’ (TPR 195).   
 
Schreiner’s use of the allegorical form enables her to create a wider vision in which to 
express a moral responsibility towards others and the future. This is well illustrated in 
Trooper Peter. Halket's meeting with Christ on the kopje converts his mode of thinking. In 
calling into question Halket’s colonialist beliefs and the odious actions to which they have 
led, Christ asks Halket ‘which of those souls you have seen on earth is to you greatest?’, to 
228 
 
which the confused Halket replies, ‘we weren't talking of souls at all’ (TPR 87). Christ 
deepens his thinking into the realm of the ideal and broader ethical responsibilities in the 
world of the material. This causes Halket to deepen and widen his perspective, moving away 
from the small, personal, and individual, towards a longer-term and wider collective moral 
responsibility towards others and towards the future.  I contend that as readers of these 
allegories, she also leads us towards this wider perspective as a locus for our actions. 
 
In deciding whether to publish this allegorical attack on Rhodes and his values, Schreiner 
explains her decision-making in a letter to her brother. She writes that: 
at last the matter decided itself within me. Don’t think I mean anything 
supernatural takes place; though that decision which one’s nature gives when 
one tries to silence the lower and purely personal interest and to allow the 
higher elements in it to adjudicate among themselves is what the ancients 
called the ‘voice of God’; and is so to a certain extent; because it the highest 
and for each soul within itself, the most ultimate injunction it can ever receive.201 
 
This seems to me to sum up something of the message of her allegories: that one should 
follow the moral injunction that asks one to act through a sense of responsibility towards 
others. Once again, Schreiner hedges her bets about the noumenal. She suggests that the 
decision is not ‘supernatural’ but allies the influence to the ‘voice of God’, again taking up a 
position that neither fully embraces a religious faith nor fully denies it in favour of materiality.  
 
 
 
201 Olive Schreiner, letter to William Philip Schreiner, 29th June 1898, https://oliveschreiner.org [accessed 24th 
March 2023], emphasis in original. 
 
229 
 
Conclusion  
 
Human thinking is, to some extent, de-humanised and rendered plant-like, 
altered by its encounter with the vegetal world.  
(Michael Marder, Plant-Thinking, p.10) 
 
In his recent book, The Nation of Plants (2021), the botanist Stefano Mancuso, in a ‘playful 
exercise’, imagines plants writing a ‘Constitution’ for their own nation which they present to 
the ‘United Nations General Assembly’. Mancuso writes that the ‘articles’ that make up this 
constitution ‘were suggested to me by plants themselves’. They are based on a plant mode 
of being: life as a continuous whole, a non-hierarchical and collective organisation, a 
heteronomous relationship with alterity, and protecting the environment for future 
generations.202 In Plant-Thinking, Marder treads a somewhat similar path. He uses a 
‘method drawn from the plants themselves’ (MM 7), attempting to ‘abandon the familiar 
terrain of human and humanist thought and to meet vegetal life […] at least halfway’ (MM 
10). Although my own interdisciplinary approach incorporates philosophical botany, New 
Materialism, post-humanist, evolutionary, and econarratological perspectives, I have tried to 
keep at the centre, the mode of life of plants. 
 
Relationships are key to the work of Hardy and Schreiner. Using the relationship-centred 
philosophical botany of Michael Marder, the continuous will of Schopenhauer, and the 
connectivity across matter embraced by the New Materialism of Jane Bennett and Karen 
Barad, this thesis has shed light on this aspect of Hardy and Schreiner’s work. Relationships 
are evident in their representation of the individual plant, communities of vegetation, and the 
entanglements and connections between the vegetal and other lifeforms. My approach 
identifies how Hardy and Schreiner explicitly and implicitly use the vegetal as a model of a 
relational mode of being. For Mancuso, relationships dominate his ‘nation of plants’. He 
recognises the importance for the sessile plant of ‘building stable and cooperative 
 
202 Stefano Mancuso, The Nation of Plants, trans. by Gregory Conti (London, Profile Books, 2021), pp.10-12. 
230 
 
communities with other individuals with whom you have to share your living space’ and 
reminds us that ecology is about relationships.203 Hardy and Schreiner’s texts abound with 
the language of webs, networks, and connections, across which ‘internetted lines of 
interaction stretch’ (FM 180), and the values they embed are based upon responsibility 
towards others and denounce human solipsism.  
 
I have demonstrated how Hardy and Schreiner use evolutionary perspectives to create long-
term frameworks in which life is portrayed as a continuum. In his novels, Hardy shows the 
uninterrupted growth of the vegetal creating and re-creating place, embedding vitality, 
adaptation, and change within a framework of immutability. In his poetry, he highlights how 
for the vegetal, transience becomes part of the continuing life of the plant. Schreiner, in her 
novels, uses vegetal analogies to contribute to her evolutionary framework, using plants as 
symbols of long-term uninterrupted development and models of the relationship of the 
individual to long-term progress. I read the long-term body of humanity in her allegories as 
similarly vegetal-like. For Schreiner, this human body participates in progressive evolutionary 
development, however, Hardy presents humanity as problematically over-developed. The 
approach I have developed in this thesis responds to these aspects of their work, cognisant 
of how their incorporation of evolutionary temporalities into the novel expands its form. 
 
Merlin Sheldrake asserts that ‘all life forms are in fact processes, not things’.204 I have argued 
that in Hardy and Schreiner’s work the vegetal is represented as in a state of constant 
change rather than stasis. Hardy characterises its energy or lifeforce as an inherent element 
of the materiality, whereas Schreiner envisages it as a metaphysical force supplementary to 
the material. I situate their divergent views in the late nineteenth-century era of debates over 
 
203 Mancuso, p.138. 
204 Sheldrake, p.60, emphasis in original. 
231 
 
material and metaphysical epistemologies. Yet even for Schreiner, materiality is the location 
of vitality and agency, and so for both of them, matter matters. 
 
In representing life as a material continuum, Hardy and Schreiner dismantle notions of a 
hierarchical Great Chain of Being and present all lifeforms, including the vegetal, as of value. 
But while identifying these connections, they also conceptualise the vegetal as other. Like 
Mancuso in The Nation of Plants and Marder in Plant-Thinking, their use of the vegetal 
reminds us that there is an alternative ontology and that ours is not the only way to live. 
Schreiner overtly reflects this in her plant analogies, and Hardy does so in his poetry in which 
he suggests that the vegetal might, in fact, be better adapted to the conditions of life on Earth 
than is humanity with its burdensome consciousness and focus on self.  
 
Hardy and Schreiner conceptualise humankind as non-dualist beings, mind and body being 
inseparable. Across Hardy’s work, it is when reason and consciousness dominate that 
humanity misunderstands its true nature and suffers from the ensuing existential concerns. In 
African Farm Schreiner makes a similar point and across her work she validates other modes 
of perception that embrace feeling and the body. For both writers, human agency, motivation, 
and meaning are corporeal, and it is through a recognition of such embodiment that our 
kinship with other lifeforms is evident. Matter matters for us too.  
 
Although this is not intended as a comparative thesis, examining these two authors through 
a similar lens has been insightful, particularly with respect to where they differ, helping to 
create new understandings of the position of materiality in the late-nineteenth and early- 
twentieth-centuries. The editors of Anticipatory Materialisms in Literature and Philosophy 
articulate their intention to read forwards rather than backwards, cognisant of how the 
literature of the nineteenth-century was deeply invested in notions of material agency and 
232 
 
autonomy that anticipate twenty-first century New Materialism.205 My readings of Hardy and 
Schreiner centre them in their own era, in which material and metaphysical epistemologies 
were in debate, while arguing for their relevance to the environmental sensibilities of the 
twenty-first century.  
 
My study contributes to the current ‘plant-turn’ in the humanities, which continues to expand 
across academia, and in non-fiction and literary texts, suggesting that we are starting to 
recognise something important about the vegetal mode of being. It also further extends post-
human discourse in that it decentres the human and questions our exceptionalism. The lens 
I have developed could effectively be applied to other work by Hardy and Schreiner. Hardy’s 
Under the Greenwood Tree with its arboreal setting would be interesting to compare with 
The Woodlanders. There are many Hardy poems which address the vegetal to which I could 
not do justice in one chapter of this thesis, but which would benefit from further study, 
perhaps alongside the vegetal poetry of his friend and mentor, William Barnes. This 
approach could usefully be employed to examine other allegories by Schreiner that embrace 
the vegetal, as well as her early novel, Undine.  
 
Although my current work focuses on the work of Hardy and Schreiner, it would be a useful 
approach to consider the representation of the vegetal across a wider range of texts and 
authors. It could be used to examine the environmental and ecological values embedded 
within texts, to analyse the representation of entanglements across human and other 
lifeforms, and to assess how meaning, agency, and knowing is widened out to other lifeforms 
including the vegetal. As my lens extends to the representation of humanity in relation to the 
role of the individual, ethical responsibility, and the role of the human mind and human 
corporeality, it offers further potential for exploring how other writers address these features.  
   
 
205 Anticipatory Materialisms in Literature and Philosophy: 1790-1930, ed. by Jo Carruthers, Nour Dakkak, and 
Rebecca Spence (Cham, Palgrave MacMillan, 2019), pp.1-7.  
233 
 
In terms of the nineteenth-century, my approach offers a useful lens to consider how Emily 
Brontë uses the moorland setting and how she locates agency and vitality in Wuthering 
Heights, as well as to examine how in Shirley, Charlotte Brontë centres botanical knowledge 
within many of the minor female characters. Reading Elizabeth Barrett Browning’s Aurora 
Leigh from this perspective might suggest new ways to understand her representation of the 
natural world and how it influences the character of Aurora. Turning to the twentieth century, 
my methodology would provide a valuable perspective for analysing the representation of 
the vegetation of the North American plains in the work of Willa Cather, as well as for 
exploring how Sylvia Townsend Warner applies her plant knowledge across her novels, short 
stories, and poetry, particularly in her extended poem, Opus 7 in which the nurturing of 
plants becomes subversive. In more recent work, analysing how writers such as Annie 
Proulx and Richard Powers represent the role of the arboreal in North America would gain 
from my approach. I also offer a useful lens for considering some of Ursula Le Guin’s 
science fiction, in which she uses the vegetal to suggest better ways of living in the world, 
particularly in her novel The Word for World is Forest, and her short story, ‘Direction of the 
Road’, written from the perspective of a tree. 
 
The editors of the 2020 edition of Schreiner’s Dreams comment that ‘avowed millennials, we 
felt an immediate bond with Olive Schreiner, as she confronted so many of the issues that 
resonate in our lives today’ (D 7). Although they do not indicate so, I would include in this her 
representation and use of the vegetal and recognition of the value of all lifeforms. I contend 
that Hardy’s work, particularly his knowledge, description, and use of the vegetal, as well as 
his understanding of the importance of relationships and entanglements across all beings, 
also speaks to us today.  
 
I suggest that Hardy and Schreiner’s awareness of the importance of the vegetal is a 
salutary lesson for our times. It is perhaps an irony that while there is a ‘plant-turn’ in 
discourse, we have become a species of urban environments, divorced from the everyday 
234 
 
experience of the green world. Hardy and Schreiner were aware and knowledgeable about 
the plant world, having experienced rural childhoods, and our vegetal-inflected language – 
‘roots’, ‘branching’, ‘offshoots’, ‘hedged’, ‘stems from’, ‘field of’, ‘grounded in’ – demonstrates 
the intimate entanglement of our culture with the vegetal.  
 
Our attitude to plants perhaps reveals us as zoocentric, not merely anthropocentric. We 
continue to conceptualise the world in terms of individual beings and things, rather than 
through relationships. Francis Hallé suggests that our language fails to acknowledge the 
different ontology of plant-life: ‘our language is not appropriate for plants. They do not know. 
They use nothing. They have no needs, projects, or goals. We speak an animal language 
that adapts poorly to the truth about plants’.206 The assertion by others that Hardy’s Egdon 
Heath is a character because Hardy endows it with a face, a voice, and agency is indicative 
of this. Marder suggests that ‘human thinking is, to some extent, de-humanised and 
rendered plant-like, altered by its encounter with the vegetal world’ (MM 10). My hope is that 
my work contributes to changing the reader’s thinking in this respect.
